user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI jumped to 1.24% y/y from 0.64%, a +0.60pp surge, and my prior "up" call was correct in direction (though the magnitude was larger than typical drift). However, the underlying mom print for that latest observation was -0.05%, meaning the big yoy jump was almost entirely base effects (a very negative print rolling out of the 12-month window) rather than fresh momentum. The 3-month annualized headline is actually -0.14%, and the mom 3-month average is slightly negative (-0.01%), indicating flat-to-soft actual monthly momentum despite the yoy print's sharp rise. Core PCE remains essentially pinned near 1.19-1.20% y/y, still 0.81pp below target, with a slightly negative 3m yoy change — no acceleration signal there. Policy language confirms this is being read as transitory (energy/import price effects dissipating), consistent with base-effect-driven yoy moves rather than a genuine turn in trend inflation. Given the outgoing mom (0.25%) is larger than the recent 3-month average pace, another old high print will roll out, which could produce a further mechanical yoy pop, but the underlying trend is flat-to-tepid. I lean toward another modest headline yoy increase next month on base effects, but with lower conviction than the move itself might suggest, since core shows no real acceleration.
Would change its mind if: A core PCE print breaking above 1.3% y/y or headline mom turning solidly positive (>0.2%) for two consecutive months would confirm genuine acceleration rather than base effects.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation (1.41) ticked down from 1.42 but sits well above the recent trough of 1.28 and mid-way in the 12-month range (range_position 0.20 from bottom, meaning still closer to the low). Looking at the last several prints, breakeven rose from 1.28 to a peak of 1.42 then eased slightly to 1.41 — essentially a plateau after a rebound. The 21-day and 63-day changes are still negative (-0.12, -0.07), reflecting the earlier decline baked into the window, but the most recent four readings (1.34, 1.36, 1.40, 1.42, 1.41) show a modest uptrend that has now flattened rather than reversed. Policy language has shifted more dovish/cautious — "declined further" and survey measures "little changed" — which is a lagging characterization likely referencing the earlier drop, not the recent stabilization. My prior view (if it called continued decline) was too pessimistic given the actual bounce off the 1.28 low. Given the flattening at 1.41-1.42 after a recovery, and no fresh measurement showing renewed deterioration, I read this as a pause rather than a resumption of the downtrend. Distance from the 2% target remains large (-0.59pp), keeping any upside bias muted.
Would change its mind if: A break below 1.35 or a fresh multi-week decline would confirm renewed downtrend; a push above 1.45 would confirm reflation resuming.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate just fell to 4.80%, a fresh 12-month low, down 0.20pp over 3 months, 0.40pp over 6 months, and 0.90pp over the trailing year. There is no Sahm-rule style acceleration in the rise off the low—there is no rise at all; unrate_above_12m_low is 0.00pp and range_position is at the bottom of the 12-month band. The trend has been a steady, orderly grind lower with no signs of inflection. Policy language reinforces this: the updated commentary describes "ongoing job gains and declining unemployment" and "considerable improvement in labor market conditions," a clear upgrade from the prior statement's more cautious "held steady" language. This is consistent tightening, not loosening. The level (4.80%) is now essentially at or slightly above many estimates of the natural rate, though the distance_from_natural metric here (0.80pp) suggests further room to tighten before hitting typical full-employment thresholds. Momentum, policy language, and the absence of any reversal signal all point the same direction. Absent a shock, the most likely near-term outcome is either continued modest decline or a flat print near the same level, given how compressed the recent range has become and the possibility of a pause after a large one-month drop.
Would change its mind if: A jump in unemployment rate of 0.2pp+ in the next report, or policy language reverting to describing labor market weakening, would reverse this view.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has declined from 2.07 to 1.94 over the last 10 readings, with the 21-day change at -0.38pp — a sharp recent drop despite the 63-day change being milder (-0.11pp) and the 252-day change still positive (+0.21pp). This suggests the longer-term uptrend has stalled and reversed sharply in the most recent month. The yield sits at only 0.32 of its 12-month range, closer to the low (1.68) than the high (2.50), indicating the long end is drifting toward the lower part of its range. The 30s10s slope remains a healthy 0.81pp, showing no acute long-end-specific distress or steepening pressure at the very long end; the curve context is fairly stable. Policy language changed to note inflation compensation "declined further," which historically coincides with lower nominal long-end yields as embedded inflation/term premium compresses. Combined with the sharp downward move in yields and the low range position, the evidence points to continued or persistent softness in the term premium proxy rather than a rebound. Given the strength and recency of the -0.38pp move and the corroborating survey language, I expect the 10-year yield to be modestly lower or flat at month-end, leaning down.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above ~2.05 in dgs10_level within the next few readings, or a reversal of the 21-day decline trend, would falsify this call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a clear, consistent monotonic rise over the last 13 observations, from -0.47 to -0.28. This is a steady tightening trend: 4-week change is +0.07 and 13-week change is +0.17, both positive and of similar per-period pace, indicating no deceleration. The index sits at its 52-week high (range_position = 1.00), meaning conditions are the tightest they've been in the observed window, though still well below the zero long-run average (distance_from_zero = -0.28), so conditions remain accommodative in absolute terms even as they tighten at the margin. My prior view (if it called continued easing) would have been wrong given this data — the trend has clearly reversed from loose/easing toward tightening over the recent stretch, and there's no sign in the last few points of the tightening stalling; the last three deltas (-0.31→-0.30→-0.28) are still positive and roughly steady. The new policy language flags close monitoring of global developments and balance of risks, which is consistent with an environment where the Fed is watching financial conditions tighten, but doesn't itself signal a shift. Given the unbroken multi-week uptrend and position at the top of its recent range, I expect continuation of modest tightening into next month-end.
Would change its mind if: A reversal to flat/declining weekly NFCI prints for 2-3 consecutive weeks, or a sharp drop back toward the 13-week average, would negate this tightening call.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 13 weekly observations show total assets oscillating in a narrow band (~4.477T to ~4.502T) with no clear trend, consistent with a full reinvestment policy (not active QT or QE). The 4-week change is -4,238M and 13-week change is -6,990M, both modest relative to the ~4.49T level (about -0.16% over 13 weeks), and the most recent print (4,482,349) is close to the 13-week average (4,487,402), suggesting mean-reverting noise rather than a sustained runoff or expansion. The policy language explicitly describes reinvestment of principal and rollover of maturing Treasuries at auction—this is balance-sheet-neutral language, not an announced runoff cap or QE program, which matches the flat/no-trend measurement pattern. My prior view (if it called runoff) should be reassessed: the trend is not a clean decline but a choppy flat pattern with a slight downward drift. Given the policy is explicitly reinvestment (holding levels steady), and the recent 4w/13w changes are small and within the noise band seen over the whole 13-week window, I read this as essentially flat with a slight downward lean.
Would change its mind if: A clear multi-week consecutive decline exceeding the 13w average by a wide margin, or explicit policy language announcing a runoff cap/QT program, would confirm sustained contraction.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has been range-bound near the low end of its 12-month band (1.15-1.77pp), currently at 1.18pp, only 0.03pp above the 12-month low and at just 5% of the range (slope_range_position 0.05). The last ten readings oscillate narrowly between 1.15 and 1.19 with no clear trend, and the most recent print (1.18) matches the prior value shown, so my last call (essentially flat) played out correctly. Medium-term momentum is mildly flattening: slope_change_63d is -0.22pp, though the shorter slope_change_21d (-0.05) and slope_change_252d (-0.05) show the flattening has largely stalled recently, consistent with the flat 10-observation pattern. Policy language shows the Fed moved from actually hiking (raising the funds rate) to holding steady, removing the explicit hike language - a pause tends to reduce near-term upward pressure on the front end, which is mildly supportive of stabilization rather than renewed flattening. With the spread pinned near cycle lows but not making new lows, and no fresh catalyst evident in the language changes, I expect the spread to stay near current levels into month-end rather than move decisively in either direction.
Would change its mind if: A break below 1.15 (new 12-month low) or above 1.25 would indicate a decisive flattening or steepening move, respectively.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_12m_low

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of Treasury runoff/reinvestment changes would directly affect long-end supply/demand and term premium.
  term_premium lacks inflation_expectations — Further confirmation of inflation compensation trends would validate the drift lower in nominal yields.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel paints a coherent but nuanced picture. Headline inflation's yoy pop is a base-effect artifact — mom momentum is flat-to-negative and core PCE is pinned near 1.2% with no acceleration — so I take a much softer "up" lean than the analyst's own framing implies. Inflation expectations look like a stabilization/pause after a bounce off the 1.28 trough, not a resumption of decline or a fresh breakout; I read this as genuinely flat. Labor tightness has the cleanest signal: unemployment at a fresh 12-month low, steady multi-horizon declines, and upgraded policy language ("considerable improvement") all point one direction — the labor market keeps tightening (unemployment falling further). Term premium (10y yield) shows a sharp, recent -0.38pp 21d move with corroborating dovish inflation-compensation language, though the 252d change is still positive and 30s10s shows no long-end distress — I lean down but temper conviction given the tension between short- and long-window signals, and note the analyst wasn't given balance-sheet or expectations context that could reinforce or offset this. Financial conditions show the most unambiguous trend: a monotonic 13-observation tightening streak, at a 52-week high in the index, no deceleration — I lean into this call fully. Balance sheet is genuinely flat/noisy, consistent with stated reinvestment policy — no real drift either way. Curve slope is pinned near cycle lows with stalled flattening momentum and a dovish pause reducing front-end pressure — flat is the right call with reasonable conviction.

Overall: tightening financial conditions and a tightening labor market are the highest-conviction calls; inflation and term premium moves are real but should be read as softer/more contingent than their analysts' framing suggests; expectations, balance sheet, and curve slope are legitimately flat.

discounted.why: Signal is a stabilization/plateau, not a directional move worth leaning on.
Flat/noisy pattern matching stated policy, no actionable directional signal.
Range-bound near lows with stalled momentum, no fresh catalyst to act on.

arbitrated.falsifier: Core PCE breaking above 1.3%, unemployment rising 0.2pp+, or a 10y rebound above 2.05 with NFCI tightening stalling would flip this read.

risks.text: Inflation's yoy base-effect pop could mechanically continue and get misread as genuine acceleration.
Term premium analyst lacked balance-sheet and expectations context that could materially change the yield call.
Tightening financial conditions and falling unemployment together could eventually slow growth, contradicting labor call.
Policy language cited by several analysts may lag the actual data turns, especially for inflation expectations.

why.inflation: Yoy pop is base-effect driven; core PCE flat and mom momentum negative, so tempered the analyst's 0.35 up call.
why.inflation_expectations: Recent prints show a plateau after a bounce, not fresh deterioration or breakout; read as flat, not a leaned view.
why.labor_tightness: Fresh 12-month low, consistent multi-horizon decline, upgraded policy language — unemployment likely falls further, tightening labor market.
why.term_premium: Sharp 21d yield drop and dovish language support lower, but positive 252d trend and stable 30s10s temper conviction.
why.financial_conditions: Unbroken 13-observation monotonic tightening trend at 52-week high with no deceleration; high-conviction continuation.
why.balance_sheet: Choppy, noise-band oscillation consistent with stated reinvestment policy; no directional view.
why.curve_slope: Pinned near cycle lows, flattening momentum stalled, dovish pause reduces front-end pressure; genuinely flat.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 14 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call partially played out then reversed. Headline YoY jumped to 1.24% two prints ago (from 0.44%), but the newest reading fell back to 0.85% — a -0.39pp drop — as the newest headline_mom came in at -0.13%, following -0.05% and -0.11% in the prior two months. That's three consecutive negative monthly prints, pushing headline_3m_annualized to -1.14%, a clear deceleration signal that failed my own falsifier test (mom did not stay >0.2% for two months; instead it went negative for three). The earlier YoY spike looks like it was driven by base effects from very low prints a year earlier rolling out of the window, not by fresh momentum. Core PCE YoY did tick up to 1.41% from a 1.18-1.23% range, and core_3m_annualized is +1.40%, mildly firmer, but still 0.59pp below target and the move is small against a noisy series. Given headline's recent trend is now solidly negative on a monthly basis while YoY is still elevated only due to base effects, I expect headline YoY to fall further next month as the weak monthly prints continue to dominate and old high-base comparisons age out. Core's modest firming is not enough to offset this near-term headline path.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print above +0.2% or core PCE YoY sustaining above 1.4% would signal the deceleration is not continuing.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 breakevens show a clear rebound: 1.25→1.42, with the most recent three prints (1.36, 1.39, 1.41, 1.42) marking a steady grind higher off the trough. My prior "flat" call is being contradicted by the data — the series has pushed decisively above my 1.45 reflation trigger's neighborhood and shown a fresh multi-week uptrend rather than the renewed downtrend I flagged as the bearish falsifier. be_change_21d is flat at 0.00pp, but that masks the intra-period acceleration visible in the raw series; the most recent four observations are the highest in the window. be_change_63d and be_change_252d are still negative, reflecting the earlier decline before the reversal, so this is a rebound off lows, not a return to target — breakevens remain 0.58pp below the 2% objective and only in the 32nd percentile of the 12-month range. Notably, the policy language is stale/backward-looking here ("declined further") and doesn't reflect the recent upturn shown in the observations, which is a stronger real-time signal. Given the consistent higher-highs pattern over the last 5 readings, I now lean toward continued modest upward drift into next month rather than flat.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.38 or two consecutive down-prints would signal the uptrend has stalled, restoring a flat/down call.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 21 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked up to 4.90% from 4.80%, a 0.10pp increase after the prior release's dip to the 12-month low. My previous "down" call was wrong for this single print, though the broader trend context still matters: unrate_3m_avg (4.90%) and the 3m/6m changes (-0.20pp) show the level has been oscillating in a narrow 4.8-5.1% band for the last several months rather than trending cleanly in either direction. The 12m change of -0.60pp confirms the multi-quarter trend remains one of labor-market tightening, and the sahm_gap of just 0.10pp is far below the threshold that would signal a genuine turn toward loosening. Policy language still describes "further improvement" and "considerable improvement... this year," with no hint of deterioration. One month's 0.10pp uptick within a flat, low-level band (distance from natural rate still under 1pp) looks like noise around a tight labor market rather than the start of a loosening trend. I'll shade slightly toward flat/up for the next report given the tick up and the narrow recent range, but without more consecutive prints confirming direction, conviction is low.
Would change its mind if: A second consecutive monthly rise of 0.1pp+ pushing the rate clearly above the recent 4.8-5.1% range, or policy language shifting to describe weakening conditions.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_3m_avg, unrate_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down) played out: dgs10_level has drifted from ~1.78 to 1.74, with 21d/63d/252d changes all negative (-0.26, -0.49, -0.29 pp). The yield sits near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.13, only 0.11 pp above the 12m low of 1.63), indicating the long end remains soft with no reversal signal. The 10s30s slope (0.87 pp) is elevated, consistent with continued long-end richness relative to the very long end rather than a steepening driven by term-premium repricing higher. Policy language reinforces this: inflation compensation "declined further" and long-term inflation expectations are "little changed," both consistent with continued downward pressure on nominal long yields rather than an upside catalyst. There is no sign of a reversal in the recent trend, and levels remain well below the 12-month high of 2.50. The move from 1.78 to 1.74 over the last ten readings is modest but persistent, and the balance of evidence — level near range lows, negative multi-horizon momentum, and dovish-leaning language on inflation compensation — still points to a soft or slightly declining term premium into month end. I do not see a catalyst for a rebound in the data shown.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in dgs10_level toward the upper half of its 12-month range (above ~2.0) or a clear reversal of the negative 21d/63d trend would change this view.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last several weekly prints show NFCI stalling and ticking up slightly: -0.28, -0.28, -0.27, -0.27, -0.28 — essentially flat over the most recent month (nfci_change_4w = +0.00), after a longer climb from the 13-week-ago level (nfci_change_13w = +0.17). My prior call of "up" was validated by the 13-week trend but the most recent 4 weeks show the tightening has stalled just below the 52-week high (nfci_range_position = 0.96), not reversed but not continuing either. The index remains well below zero (nfci_distance_from_zero = -0.28), meaning conditions are still looser than historical average despite the multi-month tightening trend. Policy language shift toward "closely monitoring... financial developments" suggests some sensitivity to conditions but doesn't clearly signal further tightening or an easing pivot. Given the flat 4-week momentum and the index sitting at its range ceiling with no fresh acceleration, I don't have strong evidence for continued tightening into month-end; the momentum has plateaued. This is a genuine change from my prior view — the up-trend appears to have run its course for now, warranting a flat call rather than continued conviction in "up."
Would change its mind if: A resumption of rising weekly prints (2-3 consecutive increases) would revive the tightening call; a drop back toward -0.33 (13w avg) would suggest easing instead.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_13w_avg

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The balance sheet series continues to oscillate in a narrow band without a clear trend. Total assets are essentially flat over the past year (52w change +3,071M, ~0.07%), and even the 13-week change is small (+12,708M, +0.28%). The last observation (4,489,796) is close to the 13w average (4,487,223) and sits only modestly below the 52w high (-14,908M), consistent with noisy, range-bound movement rather than directional expansion or contraction. The 4-week change is positive (+7,447M) but this is well within the noise band seen across the 13 prints, which show repeated up-down zigzags of similar magnitude (e.g., +11,085 then -9,936 then +15,089 then -12,855). My prior flat call was correct: assets did not embark on a sustained runoff or expansion; the latest print is actually higher than the release I was scored against, reinforcing flat/no-trend. Policy language is unchanged — no mention of a runoff cap or QT program, and reinvestment policy is stated as being maintained to keep holdings at 'sizable levels,' consistent with flat balance sheet policy. There is no evidence of an announced QT pace or accelerating decline. I maintain a flat call, leaning slightly toward stability given the recent modest uptick.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-week decline clearly exceeding the 13w average, or explicit policy language announcing a runoff cap/QT program, would change this to a down call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The spread has grounded out at 0.96pp, exactly at the 12-month low (slope_range_position 0.00), after a steady flattening trend: -0.21pp over 21d, -0.34pp over 63d, -0.41pp over 252d. My prior flat call (conviction 0.40) is now clearly wrong — the series broke below the 1.15/1.25 band I flagged and instead ground to a fresh 12-month low, confirming continued flattening rather than stabilization. The last two prints (0.96, 0.96) show the pace of flattening decelerating versus the sharper moves earlier in the sample, but there is no sign of reversal or steepening — no higher low, no bounce. The FOMC language shift (from hiking to holding, "maintain the target range") removes the front-end upward pressure that had been driving flattening via rising dgs2, but with the spread already compressed to the bottom of its range and momentum still negative albeit slowing, the more defensible call is continued mild flattening or at best stabilization near cycle lows rather than a rebound. I lean down but with modest conviction given the deceleration in the last two data points.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above 1.05-1.10pp (clear break from the 12-month low) would indicate the flattening has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of Treasury runoff/QT affects long-end supply pressure and term premium direction.

arbitrated.notes: Reconciling the seven reads: the inflation call shows a genuine bifurcation — headline momentum has turned decisively negative (three straight negative mom prints, 3m annualized at -1.14%) even as core PCE ticked up modestly. I weight the headline deceleration more heavily since it's the freshest, most consistent signal across multiple horizons, giving a moderate down view on inflation. Inflation expectations, by contrast, are showing a genuine rebound off lows (higher highs for five straight prints), though still well below target and off a low base — this is a real but tentative reversal, not a return to anchoring, so I take a modest up lean. These two reads are not contradictory: realized headline inflation cooling while breakevens drift up off a trough is consistent with a market pricing a bottom rather than sustained disinflation expectations. Labor tightness is genuinely noisy — a single 0.1pp uptick within a range-bound band after months of tightening — I keep this near flat with low conviction, essentially no strong view. Term premium and curve slope both point the same direction: long yields grinding lower near 12-month lows, and 2s10s compressing to a fresh cycle low, both with decelerating but still-negative momentum — I lean down on both, tempered by the deceleration evidence. Financial conditions have plateaued after a multi-month tightening trend — I call this flat, consistent with the analyst's own reversal from prior "up." Balance sheet remains genuinely range-bound with no policy signal of QT — flat with modest conviction. The gaps flagged (term premium missing balance-sheet and breakeven context) are worth noting but don't materially change the term premium read since both curve and term premium analysts already converge independently on softening long-end levels.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.50
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "down" call was wrong: headline_mom printed +0.31%, above my 0.2% falsifier threshold, and headline_yoy rose to 0.89% from 0.85%, continuing the sharp climb from -0.10% twelve months ago. Core PCE also broke higher, printing 1.41 then 1.47, versus a 1.18-1.25 range for most of the prior year — this is a genuine acceleration in the persistence-relevant measure, not noise. The 3m annualized rates confirm this: headline 3m annualized is only +0.54% (still soft), but core 3m annualized has jumped to +1.67%, well above the recent trend and above the outgoing single-month print, indicating building momentum rather than a one-off. Yoy_range_position at 0.74 shows headline sitting in the upper part of its 12-month range, and the yoy_change_3m for both series is a solid +0.25 to +0.27pp. Policy language explicitly acknowledges inflation compensation "declined further" and expectations are stable, so this is being read as still low/transitory, but the hard data momentum (especially core PCE's two-month jump) argues for continued upward drift in headline next month. I am revising toward up, though headline remains volatile month to month and could easily give back the [date] -type gains.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print near zero or negative, or core PCE YoY falling back below 1.3%, would signal the recent acceleration was a temporary blip.
Leaned on: headline_mom, core_3m_annualized, headline_3m_annualized, yoy_range_position, core_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was directionally correct through most of the window but the newest print shows the series stepping down (1.65→1.62), the first pullback after two consecutive gains. Still, breakeven_10y sits at 1.62, comfortably above the 12m low (1.18) and in the upper half of its 12-month range (range_position 0.58), and 21d/63d changes remain positive (+0.14pp, +0.09pp) even as the 252d change is still negative (-0.11pp), indicating the medium-term uptrend has only partially reversed the prior year's decline. The policy language shift is notable: "declined further" was replaced with "remain low," a softer, less deteriorating characterization that is consistent with the recent stabilization/modest recovery in market-based inflation compensation rather than a resumption of decline. Combined with breakeven still running -0.38pp below the 2% objective, there's no sign of overshoot risk, but the language change does not scream renewed weakness either. The one down-print alone doesn't yet meet my prior falsifier bar (two consecutive down-prints or a break below 1.38), so I don't think the uptrend has stalled decisively, but the pullback plus flat-ish language warrants tempering conviction toward more neutral/mild upward drift rather than a firm continuation.
Would change its mind if: A second consecutive down-print or a drop below ~1.55 would confirm stalling; a rebound above 1.65 would restore firmer up conviction.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate ticked up to 5.00% from 4.90%, the second monthly increase off the 12-month low of 4.80%. My prior "flat" call was roughly right in spirit but the small uptick did materialize. Still, the level (5.00%) sits well within the recent 4.8-5.6% range, and 3m/6m change are both flat at +0.00pp given the 3-month average of 4.90% versus current 5.00%. The Sahm-gap is a modest +0.10pp, far from a recession-signal threshold. Distance from the ~4% natural rate is about 1.0pp, indicating a labor market that is not tight by that benchmark, but the recent trend had been one of tightening (12m change is -0.40pp) that has now stalled and modestly reversed over the last two prints. Policy language explicitly confirms this: it describes "considerable improvement... this year" and labor conditions that "continue to strengthen," suggesting the committee views the broader trend as still one of tightening, not deterioration. Given the small, single-month bounce sits inside noise and against a backdrop of otherwise stable-to-improving conditions, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up next month, but without strong conviction since two consecutive small upticks (4.80→4.90→5.00) could continue or reverse.
Would change its mind if: A third consecutive rise of 0.1pp+ taking the rate above 5.1%, or policy language shifting to acknowledge labor market softening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_3m_avg, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was validated: dgs10_level fell to 1.78, the lowest print in this series, continuing the negative 21d (-0.05pp) and especially 63d (-0.54pp) trends. The yield now sits at only 0.17 of its 12-month range, just 0.15pp above the 12-month low of 1.63%, confirming the long end is pinned near the bottom of its range. The 10s30s slope remains modestly positive at 0.83pp, not signaling any acute long-end selloff pressure. Policy language shifted from "declined further" to "remain low" for market-based inflation compensation — a subtle softening of the disinflationary momentum description, but still consistent with compressed term premium and no acceleration in yields. There is no language suggesting increased issuance pressure or balance-sheet-driven upward pressure on the long end; reinvestment policy is unchanged and continues to support accommodative conditions. With yields grinding lower and no evidence of a range reversal, I maintain a "down" lean, though the pace of decline (21d change modest at -0.05pp) suggests the move may be decelerating rather than accelerating, tempering conviction slightly from before.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in dgs10_level toward the upper half of its 12-month range (above ~2.0) or a clear reversal of the negative 21d/63d trend would change this view.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last four prints show consecutive declines (-0.27, -0.28, -0.30, -0.32, -0.35), moving further below the 13w average (-0.31) and back near the bottom of the recent range. My prior "flat" call was wrong: this is a clear resumption of easing, not stability. The 4-week change of -0.07 is a meaningful move, even though the 13-week change is roughly flat (+0.02), indicating the series had been rising into the middle of the window before this fresh downturn. The index sits at -0.35, its most negative level in this 13-observation window, matching the 52-week low, and well below the zero average — conditions are already looser than historical norms and now loosening further at the margin. Policy language noting "global economic and financial developments" as a risk factor is consistent with continued accommodation rather than tightening. Given the sharp recent decline and the level near the 52-week low, I expect the index to continue lower or at least hold near this trough at month end, rather than reverse upward. The momentum is the dominant signal here over the noisier 13-week average.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly increases back toward the 13w average (-0.31) or above would indicate the easing move has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_52w_low, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_13w_avg

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,492,857) rose from the prior observation (4,486,333) and pushed the 4-week change positive (+3,061 $M), even though the 13-week change remains slightly negative (-3,666 $M, -0.08%). The series over these 13 weeks is essentially oscillating in a narrow band (~4.478M–4.502M) with no clear trend in either direction — consistent with reinvestment/roll-over policy rather than active QT or QE. The policy language explicitly states the Committee is reinvesting principal payments and rolling over maturing Treasuries at auction, i.e., maintaining holdings at "sizable levels" — this is a balance-sheet-maintenance stance, not a runoff program with an announced monthly cap, nor asset purchases. My prior flat call was reasonable and the latest data confirms it: the small positive 4-week move is noise within a flat regime, not a resumption of expansion. Level remains close to the 52-week high, reinforcing the flat/maintenance read. Absent explicit language announcing caps or accelerated runoff, or a sustained multi-week directional move exceeding the recent range, I maintain a flat call with modest conviction.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-week decline clearly exceeding the 13w average pace, or explicit policy language announcing a runoff cap/QT program, would change this to a down call; sustained rises with QE language would flip to up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (down, 0.35) was wrong: the spread rose from 1.03 to 1.07 then settled at 1.05, breaking clearly above the 1.00-1.03 trough that had prevailed for several readings. The last two prints (1.07, 1.05) sit above the prior cluster, and slope_change_21d is now positive (+0.07pp), reversing the negative 63d and 252d trends. That said, the spread remains near the bottom of its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.12, only 0.10pp above the 12-month low of 0.95), so this looks like a stabilization/modest bounce off a low rather than a strong steepening trend. The 252d and 63d changes are still solidly negative, meaning the dominant multi-month trend has been flattening; the recent uptick could be noise within a still-compressed range. Given the mixed signal — short-term steepening evidence (21d) against medium/long-term flattening momentum (63d, 252d) and a still-low range position — I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up rather than continued flattening, but with limited conviction since the recent bounce hasn't yet produced a decisive break with confirmation.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.00pp would confirm the flattening trend resumed; a sustained push above 1.10pp would confirm genuine steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_above_12m_low

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of Treasury runoff/issuance would clarify supply pressure on long-end yields going forward.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment would clarify flight-to-quality demand for long-end duration.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel paints a moderately reflationary but still accommodative macro backdrop. On inflation, hard data (core PCE 3m annualized jumping to 1.67%, headline yoy climbing) argues for a genuine near-term upward drift, though the analyst's own conviction is tempered by month-to-month volatility. Inflation expectations show a similar but weaker pattern: breakevens had been rising but just posted their first pullback, and the analyst downgraded conviction accordingly — I read this as a mild upward lean, not a resumption of decline, but clearly less confident than the inflation call itself. Labor tightness is essentially flat: a small uptick in unemployment sits within noise and against a "conditions continue to strengthen" policy tone, so no strong view either direction, if anything a marginal loosening bias given two consecutive small upticks. Term premium and financial conditions both point down/looser — long yields are pinned near 12-month lows with decelerating but still negative momentum, and NFCI has resumed a fresh easing leg to a 52-week low. These two reinforce each other (both consistent with an accommodative, low-term-premium environment) even though term premium's analyst wasn't shown financial-conditions data directly. Balance sheet is flat/maintenance — no QT or QE signal, just reinvestment noise. Curve slope is a genuine toss-up: a recent 21d steepening bounce fights against still-negative 63d/252d flattening trends, and the range position remains near 12-month lows, so I keep this near flat with a very slight steepening lean given the most recent prints are the freshest information.

Overall: rising inflation and mildly rising inflation expectations, easing financial conditions, and a term premium still compressing lower, with labor and balance sheet range-bound and curve slope indecisive.

Curve slope signal is genuinely mixed between short-term bounce and multi-month flattening trend, could break either way.
Several analysts lean on soft/vague policy language shifts ('declined further' to 'remain low') that may not reflect current committee stance.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was validated: headline_mom printed +0.38% (the strongest in the 13-month window) and headline YoY rose to 1.17% from 0.89%, with core PCE YoY holding at 1.46%, well above the 1.18-1.25% range seen a year earlier. Momentum is clearly positive - headline_3m_annualized (2.27%) exceeds the trailing YoY figure, and outgoing_mom of 0.33% into 0.38% shows acceleration is continuing rather than fading. Core_yoy_change_3m of +0.27pp confirms the pickup isn't just energy/base-effect noise in headline; core is genuinely firming too, though it remains 0.54pp below target. Policy language now explicitly acknowledges "inflation picked up in recent months," a notable upgrade from the prior "continued to run below" framing, and market-based inflation compensation "remains low" rather than "declining further" - a subtle stabilization signal. The yoy_range_position of 0.95 shows headline is near the top of its 12-month range, which raises the bar for further acceleration but doesn't reverse the trend. With two consecutive strong MoM prints (0.31, 0.38) and core PCE firming, I see continued upward momentum into the next print, though the pace of increase may moderate given how far headline has already climbed from its lows.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print near zero or negative, or core PCE YoY reversing back below 1.3%, would signal the acceleration has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.30) was validated by the intervening prints: breakeven rose from 1.65 to 1.72 before ticking down slightly to 1.71 in the latest observation. The trend over the last 10 readings is clearly upward — 21-day change +0.09pp, 63-day change +0.30pp — though the 252-day change is still negative (-0.18pp), showing the level remains below where it was a year ago. The single-observation pullback from 1.72 to 1.71 is minor and well within noise; it does not constitute the "second consecutive down-print" that would have flipped my view, since the broader run into this print was firmly higher. Breakeven now sits at 0.70 of its 12-month range, near the highs, still 0.29pp below the 2% objective. Policy language calling market-based measures "low" is stale relative to the recent grind higher, suggesting the market has been re-pricing modestly firmer inflation compensation independent of Fed commentary. Given the persistent multi-week uptrend, the one-observation dip is not enough to call a reversal, but the deceleration in momentum (six straight up-prints then a flat/down tick) tempers conviction versus a full-throated continuation call. I lean toward continued modest upward drift with reduced conviction given the loss of momentum at the margin.
Would change its mind if: A second consecutive decline below ~1.68, or a break under 1.60, would confirm the uptrend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate has risen three months in a row (4.80 → 4.90 → 5.00 → 5.10), a cumulative +0.30pp off the 12-month low, with the 3-month change now clearly outpacing the 6-month change (+0.30 vs +0.10), indicating the recent move is accelerating rather than just noise. The Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is a modest +0.20pp, not yet at classic recession-signal thresholds, but the trend direction is unambiguous: three consecutive increases. The level itself (5.10%) is only modestly above the ~4% natural-rate anchor (distance +1.10pp), so this isn't a severely tight or slack market in absolute terms, but the direction of travel has flipped from tightening to loosening over the last quarter. My prior "flat" call was wrong — I underweighted the emerging 3-month uptrend and gave too much benefit of the doubt to the choppy prior months. Policy language still describes strengthening labor markets, but that language is stale relative to the printed data, which is the harder evidence here. Given the consecutive rises and accelerating 3-month pace, I now lean toward continued mild loosening (further rate increase or at least no reversal) rather than flat.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 5.0% or below would confirm the rise was noise; a fourth consecutive increase above 5.2% would confirm sustained loosening.

Given the flattening momentum against a backdrop of still-loose absolute conditions, I lean toward continued modest easing but with much lower conviction than before, since the flat recent prints could just as easily resolve into a plateau. My prior "flat" call undershot this: the last observation is a fresh local low, sitting 30,039 below the 52w high, and the drop from the prior print (4,490,131 → 4,474,665) is one of the sharpest single-step moves in the series. However, the policy language shown is unchanged and explicitly describes reinvestment of principal payments and rollover of maturing Treasuries at auction — i.e., a passive, no-runoff-cap regime, not an announced QT program. That language is inconsistent with a deliberate, programmatic balance-sheet reduction; it reads as the standard maintenance language, meaning the wiggle could still be noise (settlement timing, auction timing, SOMA operational quirks) rather than a policy-driven trend. Given the size and recency of the decline outweighs the 52w drift (+3,166, essentially flat over a year), I lean toward continued near-term softness into month-end, but conviction is capped because the stated policy is still reinvestment/rollover, not runoff, and one sharp weekly move is a thin base for a confident trend call.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward the 13w average or new highs, or explicit language announcing runoff caps, would overturn this down lean.

The FOMC language change is marginal (shifting from "picked up... reflecting declines" to "continued to run below... reflecting earlier declines"), a small dovish-adjacent tweak on inflation characterization but not a policy stance shift, and doesn't signal a directional change in the front-end/back-end differential. With the curve pinned in a tight band near cycle lows and no fresh catalyst, the most defensible call remains flat, though the persistent proximity to the 12-month low keeps a mild flattening bias in play.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 1.00pp would confirm flattening resumption; a sustained push above 1.10pp would confirm steepening.

arbitrated.notes: The picture is one of a real, if decelerating, reflation combined with a labor market that has quietly flipped from tightening to loosening, against still-loose but stabilizing financial conditions and a mild long-end/balance-sheet grind lower. Inflation itself is the highest-conviction call: two consecutive strong MoM prints, core PCE firming, and 3m-annualized running above trailing YoY all point to genuine, not base-effect, acceleration, even as the level nears the top of its 12-month range (raising the bar for further upside surprises). Inflation expectations are following, but with a visible loss of momentum — six up-prints then a flat/down tick — so I keep the "up" lean but shade conviction down versus the inflation call itself, since breakevens are lagging realized inflation and the panel flagged it lacks a CPI-surprise linkage to confirm causality. Labor tightness is the more consequential regime shift: three consecutive unemployment-rate increases with accelerating 3m-vs-6m pace is a directional flip that argues for continued mild loosening, even though the absolute level isn't yet at stress thresholds. On rates, term premium and curve slope are lower-conviction, mean-reverting stories: the 10-year has bounced off recent lows but medium-term trend remains down, and the curve is pinned in a tight band near cycle lows with no fresh catalyst — flat is the right call, with a mild flattening bias. Financial conditions and balance sheet both show the same pattern: strong medium-term easing/decline trends that have visibly decelerated or flattened in the most recent prints, so I keep the directional lean but with reduced conviction, treating the flattening as a warning against extrapolating the prior pace rather than a full reversal.

discounted.why: No fresh catalyst or trigger breach; genuinely flat, not a directional view worth weighting.

arbitrated.falsifier: A flat-to-negative CPI print with core PCE reversing below 1.3%, alongside unemployment dropping back to 5.0%, would flip the reflation/loosening-labor narrative.

why.balance_sheet: Fresh 13-week low and sharp weekly drop outweigh flat 52-week drift, though reinvestment-only language caps conviction.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y actually pulled back to 1.08% from 1.17%, even though mom (0.24%) stayed solidly positive — the y/y dip was a base-effect artifact, not a stall in momentum. My prior "up" call was partially wrong on the y/y print itself, but the underlying momentum I cited (positive mom, rising core) held up, so the acceleration thesis wasn't invalidated, just showed up unevenly across the metrics. Now: headline_mom_3m_avg is 0.31%, and headline_3m_annualized is 3.80%, well above the current 1.08% y/y print, meaning upward base effects/momentum should push y/y higher next month absent a sharp mom drop. Core PCE y/y has stepped up to 1.52% from ~1.19-1.20% over the prior several months, a genuine and fairly persistent move (core_yoy_change_3m +0.11pp on top of an already-visible level shift), which supports the case that underlying inflation is firming, not just noisy energy/food swings. Policy language still frames inflation as running below target due to earlier transitory energy/import effects, consistent with a gradual firming already underway. With mom running high (0.38, 0.24 last two prints) and 3m annualized well above trailing y/y, the arithmetic favors another rise in headline y/y next month.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print near zero/negative, or core PCE y/y slipping back below 1.35%, would signal the firming has stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_mom_3m_avg, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was wrong: breakeven_10y has continued to soften, closing the latest reading at 1.54%, matching the recent 21-day low and down from 1.58 in the three prior prints. The 21-day change is -0.17pp and the 252-day change is -0.26pp, both firmly negative, while the 63-day change is only marginally positive (+0.06pp), suggesting the earlier mid-period bounce has now fully reversed. Breakeven sits 0.46pp below the 2% objective and at roughly the midpoint of its 12-month range (0.49), but the most recent move is a fresh push toward the lower end of that range, not away from it. Policy language remains unchanged and unhelpful for timing — it simply notes market-based inflation compensation "remains low," consistent with the current soft print rather than any turn. There is no evidence in the data of stabilization: the last observation printed at the same level as the recent trough (1.54), and the short-term trend line is clearly downward sloping after a brief flat patch. Given the break below the prior 1.58 plateau and renewed weakness, I now expect breakeven expectations to drift lower or stay depressed over the next month, not recover.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 1.58-1.60 sustained over two readings would indicate the decline has stalled and expectations are stabilizing.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.45) was wrong: unemployment fell to 4.80% from 5.10%, exactly hitting the falsifier threshold I specified. The series is noisy around 4.8-5.1% but the latest print ties the 12-month low, and the 3m/6m/12m changes are all negative (-0.10, -0.30, -0.80 pp), indicating a labor market that continues to gradually tighten rather than loosen. Sahm gap is a modest +0.17pp, well below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal recession-style loosening, and unrate_range_position is at 0 (at the low end of the 12-month range), confirming no sustained deterioration. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed explicitly states labor market conditions "have improved further" and points to "strong job gains" and "additional strengthening." Distance from natural rate is 0.80pp below 4%'s... wait, actually the level 4.8% is now essentially at/near the 4% natural-rate anchor's typical range, suggesting a tight market. Given the single-month dip could partially revert (given the choppy 4.8-5.1 pattern over the last five prints), I don't think there's strong evidence for further loosening; if anything the trend and policy language point to continued tightness or flat readings. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for the next print, without high conviction given the noisy history.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above 5.0-5.1% would indicate the dip to 4.8% was noise and resume the choppy pattern; a further drop below 4.7% confirms sustained tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.30) was wrong-footed: since then dgs10_level has essentially flatlined at 1.83-1.87%, not continued declining. The 21d and 63d changes are both roughly flat (+0.01pp each), showing the prior downward momentum has stalled. The 252d change (-0.29pp) still reflects a large decline over the past year, but that move appears to have already happened and stabilized rather than being an ongoing trend. The yield sits at 1.84%, only 0.21pp above its 12-month low and in the bottom quartile of its range (range_position 0.24), indicating no upward pressure evident yet. The 30s10s slope at 0.80pp is unremarkable and doesn't signal fresh long-end premium building. Policy language reiterating reinvestment of maturing Treasuries and MBS to keep holdings "at sizable levels" continues to describe a suppressive/accommodative stance for long-end yields, consistent with the low, range-bound level. Given the flat recent price action and continued balance-sheet policy keeping duration off the market, I see no basis for continued directional decline nor for a reversal higher. The most defensible read now is flat, with only slight tilt reflecting the still-low range position and absence of any catalyst pushing yields either way.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move of dgs10_level above ~2.0% (upper half of range) or below ~1.70% over coming weeks would indicate a real directional shift, prompting reassessment.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

The index has essentially flattened over the last several weeks, sitting at -0.38, comfortably below the zero average (distance -0.38) and near the loose end of its 52-week range (range_position 0.63, i.e., closer to the loose low of -0.55 than the tight high of -0.27). Momentum has clearly stalled: three consecutive flat-to-slightly-higher prints after a long easing run. Policy language shows no material change in stance — still monitoring global developments and risks, no new tightening signal. Given the plateau at -0.38 for three straight observations, the most defensible call is flat: conditions are no longer easing but also show no convincing tightening impulse yet. The tiny +0.01 four-week uptick is too small and inconsistent (values repeating exactly at -0.38) to call a reversal with confidence.
Would change its mind if: A clear move away from -0.38, e.g. two consecutive prints below -0.40 (renewed easing) or above -0.35 (confirmed tightening).

  The latest print (4,461,111) extends the decline seen since the recent local peak (~4,499,729 several weeks back). The 4-week change is -13,554M and the 13-week change is -28,685M (-0.64%), both negative, and the newest observation is the lowest of the 13-observation window, sitting notably below the 13w average (4,481,594) and 43,593M below the 52w high. This confirms the prior down call was correct: assets did not rebound toward the 13w average or make new highs, and there was no reversal language. However, the policy language shown is the standard reinvestment/rollover boilerplate consistent with a passive, gradual runoff (or even flat-to-mild drift) rather than an aggressively announced cap increase — it doesn't explicitly describe an accelerating runoff pace. The 52w change is only -2,870M, meaning the longer-run trend is nearly flat, so this looks like a modest, grinding decline rather than a sharp QT acceleration. Given the consistent short-term negative trend (4w and 13w both down, latest value is the series low) but muted longer-run magnitude and unchanged language, I maintain a down lean for month-end, similar conviction as before.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward or above the 13w average (4,481,594), or explicit new language announcing balance-sheet growth/QE, would overturn this down lean.

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call held reasonably well: slope_2s10s has oscillated in a tight 0.93–0.97pp band over the last 10 readings, with the most recent print at 0.97, near the top of that recent micro-range but still far below the 12-month high (1.77) and sitting just 0.04pp above the 12-month low (0.93). slope_range_position of 0.05 confirms the curve is parked near the bottom of its annual range — historically flat territory, not steep. Short-horizon momentum is mixed: slope_change_21d is -0.09 (mild flattening over the month) but slope_change_63d is essentially zero (-0.01), and the last two prints ticked up (0.95→0.97), suggesting stabilization rather than a fresh trend. The 252-day change (-0.54) shows the big flattening move is well behind us, not accelerating now. Policy language shows no material hawkish or dovish shift — inflation commentary was softened slightly but the accommodative stance and gradual-adjustment framing are unchanged, implying no fresh catalyst for a re-steepening or a lurch deeper into flattening. With the curve compressed near cycle lows and no clear directional impulse in the last month, I expect the spread to stay rangebound near current levels into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A break below 0.93pp (new 12-month low) would confirm flattening resumption; a sustained move above 1.05pp would signal genuine steepening.

arbitrated.notes: Inflation shows the clearest directional signal: headline y/y dip was a base-effect artifact while mom momentum (3m annualized 3.80% vs 1.08% y/y) and a genuine step-up in core PCE y/y point to upward pressure on the next print. I lean with the inflation analyst on this. Inflation expectations, by contrast, have clearly cracked lower — breakeven_10y broke below its recent plateau to match a 21-day low, with negative 21d/252d changes and no stabilization evidence; I take this down call at reasonably high conviction, and note it somewhat decouples from the inflation call (realized inflation firming while market-implied expectations soften, consistent with anchored/well-behaved expectations rather than confirmation of the inflation view). Labor tightness is genuinely ambiguous — the analyst's own "up" call was falsified by the drop to 4.8%, but noise in the series (choppy 4.8-5.1 range) and only modest conviction argue for a small tightening lean rather than true flat, since the multi-horizon changes (3m/6m/12m) are all negative and Sahm gap is low. Term premium, financial conditions, and curve slope all read as genuinely flat/rangebound with stalled momentum after prior trends exhausted themselves — no analyst presented a catalyst for a directional break, and I take these largely at face value. Balance sheet shows a modest, grinding decline (4w/13w negative, series low) but the 52w change is nearly flat, so this is a mild down lean, not an accelerating QT signal. Overall: inflation-side signals are diverging (realized inflation firming, expectations softening), while rates/financial-conditions plumbing is broadly stable with only a slight easing bias from a shrinking balance sheet.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release confirmed the prior call: headline printed 1.08% y/y (flat vs the previous 1.08%, but up sharply from readings a few months earlier), and core PCE continued to firm to 1.57% y/y, its highest in the window, with no slippage below the 1.35% threshold I flagged. Momentum remains constructive: headline mom was 0.28%, close to the recent 3m average of 0.30%, and 3m annualized headline (3.65%) and core (2.26%) both run above the trailing y/y rate, indicating the y/y series still has upward pull as low base-period prints roll off. Core-minus-headline of +0.49pp shows core is the steadier, firming trend while headline is more volatile but has stabilized rather than reversed. Policy language changes reinforce this: market-based inflation compensation is now described as having "declined" rather than merely "remain low," but the Committee's framing still treats this as transitory, expecting reversion to 2% as energy/import effects fade and the labor market tightens — consistent with a continued, if gradual, firming path rather than a stall. With headline flat at the margin but underlying momentum (mom_gap_vs_outgoing +0.14pp, elevated 3m annualized rates) still pointing higher, I lean toward another modest increase in headline y/y at the next release, though the flat print last month tempers conviction slightly.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print near zero/negative, or core PCE y/y reversing back toward 1.4% or below, would signal the firming trend has genuinely stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_minus_headline

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was directionally correct over the medium term (63d -0.22pp, 252d -0.47pp) but the most recent data show stabilization, not continued decline: the last three readings are 1.34, 1.37, 1.40 — a bounce off the recent low of 1.34, with the final two observations flat at 1.40. The 21d change of -0.14pp is backward-looking and dominated by the earlier drop to 1.34; the trend within the last few prints is actually modestly upward. Policy language confirms the backward-looking narrative ("market-based measures... declined") but this describes the move that already happened, not a forward signal. Breakeven sits at 0.30 of its 12-month range and 0.60pp below the 2% objective — still depressed, consistent with continued low compensation, but the immediate momentum has turned flat-to-slightly-up rather than falling further. Given the flattening in the last two readings (1.40, 1.40) after the trough, I don't see strong evidence for continued decline into month-end; the more likely outcome is consolidation near current levels, with some risk of a modest further recovery given the bounce off 1.34.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.35 sustained over two readings would confirm renewed decline; a push above 1.50 would confirm a genuine reversal.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The last print rose to 4.90% from 4.80%, consistent with my prior expectation that the dip to 4.8% was noisy and the series would remain choppy rather than trend cleanly lower. Over the last five readings (5.00, 5.10, 4.80, 4.90) the rate is oscillating in a narrow 4.8-5.1% band with no clear directional break. The 3m and 6m changes are both -0.10pp, essentially flat, while the 12m change of -0.40pp shows gradual easing of tightness over the year but that has stalled recently. Range position (0.25) shows we're closer to the 12m low than the high, and distance from natural rate (0.90pp) confirms the market is still historically tight, but the recent bounce off the low undercuts any Sahm-rule-style acceleration signal — the gap (0.13pp) is well below Sahm-trigger territory. Policy language continues to affirm labor market improvement/strengthening, no sign of deterioration. Given the choppy, range-bound pattern with the most recent move being a modest uptick, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up for the next print, but with limited conviction since this series has bounced both directions within a 0.3pp band for the past several months.
Would change its mind if: A print above 5.1% would signal renewed loosening; a drop back to or below 4.8% would confirm sustained tightening and resumed downtrend.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "flat" call was wrong: the 10-year has since fallen decisively, breaching the 1.70% threshold I set as a trigger for reassessment. The level now sits at 1.49%, essentially at the 12-month low (1.46%) with range_position at just 0.03 — deep in the bottom of its 12-month band. The 21-day change (-0.36pp) and 63-day change (-0.30pp) both show a fresh, accelerating decline, not just a continuation of the prior year's drift lower (-0.84pp over 252d). This looks like a genuine flight-to-quality / duration-demand move compressing long-end term premium, reinforced by policy language noting market-based inflation compensation has now "declined" (a downgrade from "remain low"), which typically coincides with lower long-end yields on softer growth/inflation-risk pricing. The 30s10s slope remains a healthy 0.81pp, suggesting the curve further out hasn't collapsed, but the 10-year itself has clearly re-rated lower. Given the proximity to 12-month lows and the recent momentum, absent a sharp reversal I'd expect yields to stay low or drift marginally lower into month-end rather than snap back toward the range highs.
Would change its mind if: A rebound of dgs10_level back above ~1.70-1.75% over the next few weeks would indicate the decline has reversed rather than persisted.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has drifted up from -0.39 to -0.35 over the last several prints, with the most recent readings (-0.37, -0.37, -0.36, -0.35) showing a consistent, if gradual, rise off the recent trough. The 4-week change is +0.02, confirming modest tightening momentum, while the 13-week change is flat, indicating this is a recent inflection rather than a sustained trend. My prior "flat" call was too cautious given that the last print (-0.35) has now cleared my own stated threshold for confirmed tightening. The index remains well below the zero average (-0.35 distance from zero) and sits in the loose historical range (52w low -0.55, high -0.27), so conditions are still accommodative in absolute terms. But the direction of travel over the last month is unambiguously toward tighter (higher) readings, with range_position at 0.70 showing the index has moved into the upper portion of its 52-week band. Absent any policy language change, I'll extrapolate the recent momentum forward one month, expecting a continued mild rise rather than a reversal back toward -0.38 or below.
Would change its mind if: A print back below -0.37 or reversal of the recent upward drift would indicate the tightening move has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last four readings show a clear reversal: 4,461,111 → 4,461,393 → 4,463,542 → 4,472,817 → 4,482,009, a four-week gain of +20,898M, and the latest print (4,482,009) is now above the 13-week average (4,477,056) and close to erasing the prior downtrend. My previous "down" call at 0.40 conviction is falsified by this rebound — the exact condition I flagged (a move back toward/above the 13w average) has occurred. The 13w and 52w change measures are still slightly negative (-10,848 and -13,046 respectively), so on a longer window the balance sheet is still net lower than a quarter or year ago, and assets remain ~22,695M below the 52w high. But the graded measurement is month-end level versus the most recent value, and pace is what matters most: the most recent four weeks show consistent, accelerating growth, not runoff. The policy language is unchanged (still describing reinvestment/roll-over policy, no explicit QT caps or new QE mentioned), so there's no clear "why" driving this — it may reflect technical/reserve-management flows rather than a policy shift. Given the strength and consistency of the recent uptick, I lean toward a continued rise into month-end, though I remain cautious since the longer-term trend and unchanged language argue for limited conviction.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below the 13w average (4,477,056) or explicit language announcing renewed runoff/caps would overturn this up lean.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_13w_avg, assets_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation (0.91) rebounded from a trough of 0.85, with the two most recent prints (0.88, 0.91) showing a modest uptick after five months of flattening. My prior "flat" call was partially right in that the 21d change (-0.03) is near zero, but it masked the fact that the spread actually broke to fresh 12-month lows (0.85) before recovering — so the flattening resumption I flagged as a risk did occur intra-period, then reversed. Net, the spread sits just 0.06pp above its 12-month low and 0.07 of the way through its 12-month range — deeply compressed and still historically flat-to-inverted-adjacent. The 63d (-0.12) and 252d (-0.78) changes confirm a dominant multi-month flattening trend that has only just stalled. The recent 3-observation uptick (0.85→0.88→0.91) is the most proximate signal and suggests short-term steepening momentum, but it's a small bounce within a much larger flattening regime, not a confirmed trend reversal — it would need to clear something like 1.00-1.05 to look like genuine steepening rather than noise. Given the tug between near-term upturn and dominant longer-term flattening, plus the spread's position deep in its low range, I lean toward continued modest steepening/stabilization but with limited conviction.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.88pp would confirm the bounce was noise within the dominant flattening trend; a sustained push above 1.00pp would confirm genuine steepening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Would clarify if broader risk-off conditions are driving flight-to-quality into long bonds.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Change in Fed's Treasury holdings/runoff pace would clarify supply-demand pressure on long-end yields.

arbitrated.notes: The panel presents a coherent but tension-laden picture. Inflation itself (headline/core PCE momentum) still leans up on firming underlying trend, though last month's flat headline print tempers conviction. Inflation expectations (breakevens) have stabilized after a decline, with the most recent two prints flat — I read this as genuinely flat rather than continuing to fall, since the "down" evidence is backward-looking and dominated by an earlier trough. Labor tightness is range-bound/choppy with a slight recent uptick in unemployment, but no directional break — flat is the honest call, with a marginal loosening lean given the last print's rise. Term premium is the highest-conviction call on the desk: the 10-year has broken decisively lower, near 12-month lows, with accelerating momentum — a genuine flight-to-quality/duration-demand move, not just drift. This links plausibly to financial conditions, which have independently ticked tighter (NFCI rising off trough), and to a balance sheet that has just rebounded off a multi-week decline — both consistent with a soft-landing-meets-risk-off backdrop rather than a pure growth scare, since balance sheet expansion would normally ease conditions, yet financial conditions are tightening anyway, suggesting the driver is duration/safety demand rather than liquidity. Curve slope shows a modest steepening bounce off 12-month-low flatness, but remains within a dominant flattening regime — I keep conviction modest and directionally up but flag it as noise-prone. Overall: falling long yields, firming inflation, stabilizing expectations, tightening financial conditions, and a tentative steepening bounce paint a market pricing softer growth risk even as inflation data stays sticky — a stagflation-adjacent tilt rather than clean disinflation or clean growth deceleration.

risks.text: Term premium decline could reverse sharply if flight-to-quality was event-driven rather than a durable trend.
Balance sheet rebound may be technical/reserve-management flow, not a genuine trend reversal, per the analyst's own caution.
Firming inflation alongside falling yields and tightening financial conditions is an unusual combination that could resolve either way.
