why.financial_conditions: NFCI drifting up off trough, cleared stated tightening threshold, consistent with risk-off/duration demand.
why.balance_sheet: Sharp 4-week rebound above 13w average, though longer trend still net lower; moderate conviction on reversal.
why.curve_slope: Bounce off 12m-low flatness suggests modest steepening, but still deep in flattening regime — noise risk.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release's headline mom printed -0.05%, essentially flat/slightly negative, exactly the kind of print I flagged as a reason to doubt continued firming — headline y/y fell to 0.87% from 1.08%, a -0.30pp swing over 3 months. My prior "up" call was wrong for this most recent print. However, looking beneath the single-month noise: core PCE y/y actually ticked up further to 1.55% from 1.57%/1.52%, still up 0.09pp over 3 months and near a 13-month high, suggesting underlying persistence hasn't reversed — my stated falsifier (core PCE back toward 1.4% or below) has NOT been met. The core-minus-headline gap (+0.68pp) reflects energy/food giving back some of the earlier pop that drove headline higher, consistent with policy language attributing the shortfall to energy and import price effects rather than core dynamics. Headline 3m annualized (+1.87%) is below core 3m annualized (+2.10%), implying headline's dip is more a base/volatile-component effect than a broad deceleration. With core momentum intact and headline having overshot on energy and now normalizing, I expect headline y/y to stabilize or drift modestly higher next month as the very-low outgoing comparison base and firming core reassert. Conviction is limited given the outright monthly print was weak.
Would change its mind if: A second consecutive negative/near-zero headline mom print, or core PCE y/y falling back below 1.5%, would confirm genuine broad deceleration rather than energy noise.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_minus_headline

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 breakevens show a choppy but stabilized pattern: 1.49,1.44,1.46,1.52,1.51,1.50,1.50,1.49,1.52,1.49. The prior call was flat, and the newest print (1.49) sits right in the middle of the recent range, confirming that call was reasonable — no sustained break below 1.35 nor a clean push above 1.50 materialized; the series oscillates around 1.49-1.52. The 21-day change is +0.09pp (mild recent firming), but 63-day and 252-day changes are still negative (-0.22, -0.26), showing the medium-term trend remains soft even as short-term momentum ticks up. Range position of 0.53 confirms breakevens are near the middle of their 12-month range, not trending decisively either way. The policy language shift from "declined" to "remain low" is a subtle softening in the diagnosis — no longer describing an active decline, but still explicitly flagging depressed inflation compensation, consistent with a bottoming/stabilizing rather than reversing pattern. Distance from the 2% target is still notable (-0.51pp), meaning expectations remain well anchored below objective, but the acute deterioration seems to have stalled. Given the sideways chop and the lack of a decisive breakout in either direction, I read this as flat with a slight upward tilt from the improved 21-day momentum, but not enough to call a reversal.
Would change its mind if: A sustained push above 1.55 over two readings, or a drop back below 1.40, would confirm a genuine directional break from this range-bound pattern.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 4.80% matches the 12-month low, confirming my prior flat call was reasonable and the falsifier condition for tightening (drop to/below 4.8%) was met. The series has been choppy in a narrow 4.80-5.10 band for 13 months with no sustained trend: 3m change is -0.30pp (improving) but 6m change is flat at 0.00pp, and 12m change is only -0.40pp. Sahm-gap is negligible at +0.03pp, well below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal recessionary loosening. The range position sits at 0 (at the low end), and distance from natural rate is 0.80pp, indicating a labor market modestly tighter than the ~4% natural rate benchmark but not tightening further at pace. Policy language shifted from "strengthening" to "improvement has slowed" and "job gains have diminished," which is consistent with a plateauing rather than a directional move. Given the oscillation between 4.8 and 4.9 in recent prints and lack of sustained momentum, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up next print, as mean-reversion off a 12-month low within a tight band is common, though the level itself is comfortably tight.
Would change its mind if: A print above 5.0% would signal loosening momentum; a print at/below 4.7% would confirm a genuine new downtrend.
Leaned on: unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_change_6m, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield continued its decline, closing the window at 1.46% versus prior readings near 1.52-1.59%. My previous "down" call was correct and the trend has intensified rather than reversed: 21d change is a modest -0.04pp but the 63d (-0.37pp) and 252d (-0.80pp) changes show a sustained downtrend, and the most recent print (1.46%) is a fresh low relative to the trailing ten observations. The yield now sits just 0.09pp above its 12-month low and at only the 9th percentile of its 12-month range, signaling the long end remains firmly pinned near cycle lows rather than showing any reversal. The 10s30s slope at +0.72pp is stable, suggesting the move is a broad downward shift in the belly-to-long curve rather than a steepening/flattening distortion. Policy language shows inflation compensation measures "remain low" (downgraded from "declined"), consistent with continued anchoring of longer-term yields and no sign of a term-premium-driven selloff. Absent any tick back toward 1.70-1.75%, I see no evidence of reversal — if anything the decline has gained a bit of fresh downward momentum this period. I maintain a down call, with conviction slightly higher than last time given confirmation of the trend and the fresh low print.
Would change its mind if: A rebound of dgs10_level back above ~1.65-1.70% over the next few weeks would indicate the decline has reversed rather than persisted.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest two prints ticked down to -0.36 from -0.35, matching my stated falsifier for the prior "up" call — the tightening drift has stalled and modestly reversed over the last 4 weeks (nfci_change_4w = -0.01). That said, the 13-week trend is still slightly positive (+0.03), and the index sits in the upper part of its 52-week range (range_position 0.61), well above the 52w low. So the picture is mixed: short-term momentum has flattened/eased slightly, while the medium-term trend and range position still lean toward the tighter side of the recent band. NFCI remains deeply negative (-0.36), meaning conditions are still much looser than the historical zero average — any near-term wiggle is small relative to that gap. Given the flattening of the most recent two observations and the failure of the uptrend to extend further, I no longer see conviction for continued tightening. The move looks like a plateau near -0.35/-0.36 rather than a clear continuation in either direction. I'm shifting off the "up" call given the stall, but I don't yet see enough evidence of a genuine reversal to call "down" with confidence either — this reads as consolidation.

Given the magnitude of the pullback in the newest print and the multi-horizon consistency of the decline, I'm shifting from up to down. With the spread pinned near 12-month lows and no measurement showing renewed steepening, the dominant trend remains flattening, and I should not have leaned up last time.
Would change its mind if: A clear push above 0.90pp sustained over several observations would signal the flattening trend has genuinely reversed into steepening.

Inflation is genuinely mixed — headline mom just printed flat/negative and y/y decelerated, but core PCE continues to grind higher toward a 13-month high, and the analyst's own falsifier (core back below 1.4%) was not triggered. I read this as a mild upward lean in underlying inflation with the headline dip being a noisy/energy-driven aberration, but conviction is capped by the weak monthly print. Inflation expectations are range-bound with only a modest short-term uptick against a still-soft medium/long trend — essentially flat with a marginal upward tilt. Labor tightness sits at a 12-month low but within a well-worn band with no momentum in either direction — flat, mean-reversion risk if anything. Financial conditions have stalled after an "up" attempt reversed on the latest two prints; this reads as consolidation, not a real signal — true flat. Balance sheet has flipped convincingly from up to down, with consistent negative changes across 4w/13w/52w horizons and a print near cycle lows — a genuine, if not high-conviction, decline in the Fed's balance sheet. Term premium and curve slope are the highest-conviction calls: yields and the 2s10s spread are both pinned near 12-month lows with consistent negative momentum across all horizons, and no evidence of reversal despite the analysts' own priors calling for a bounce. The panel flagged that term premium and balance-sheet calls were made without cross-referencing financial conditions, which tempers how much I can attribute the yield decline to duration/term-premium compression versus balance-sheet-driven supply/demand — I keep this as a soft caveat rather than a reason to fade either call.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline mom came in at +0.18% after -0.05%, and headline yoy ticked to 1.06% from 0.87% — so my prior "flat" call was roughly right in direction but the print actually firmed rather than stayed neutral; my stated falsifier (a second weak mom print) did not occur, so the deceleration thesis is not confirmed. Looking at the full picture: headline yoy has been range-bound near 12-month highs (yoy_range_position 0.85, near the top of a 1.23pp range), and core PCE yoy has climbed steadily from ~1.2% to 1.60%, up 0.08pp over 3 months with core_3m_annualized at 1.69% — modest but persistent upward momentum, not deceleration. Headline_3m_annualized (1.66%) is close to core's pace, so the two measures agree directionally now, which strengthens confidence versus prior periods where they diverged. Core is the better read on persistence per mandate, and it shows continued gradual firming from a low base, still 0.4pp below target. Policy language is essentially unchanged apart from a firmer read on inflation compensation. Given base effects (last month's comp was a soft -0.05 print), and the recent mom average of 0.14%, next month's yoy is more likely to hold near current levels or edge up modestly rather than reverse. I lean toward a mild upward drift continuing.
Would change its mind if: A negative or near-zero headline mom print combined with core PCE yoy stalling or falling below 1.5% would indicate the firming trend has stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (1.47) ticked up from the recent local low of 1.46 seen in the two prior readings, consistent with the +0.03pp 21-day change. However, this is well within the noisy range that has persisted: 10 observations span only 1.46-1.49, and the 63-day and 252-day changes remain negative (-0.07 and -0.14pp), showing the medium-term trend is still soft even as the very short-term wiggle is up. My prior "flat" call was correct — breakevens neither broke above 1.55 nor below 1.40, staying range-bound as expected. Policy language reinforces this: the statement shifted from "declined" to "remain low" for market-based inflation compensation, which is a subtle acknowledgment that the earlier decline has stabilized rather than reversed — not a signal of rising expectations, just arrested weakness. Breakevens remain 0.53pp below the 2% target with range_position at 0.54 (roughly mid-range of the 12-month band), giving no clear directional edge. Absent a break from the 1.46-1.49 pattern, I expect near-term stability. I'll maintain a flat call, perhaps with slightly higher conviction given the confirmed range-bound behavior over the past month.
Would change its mind if: A sustained push above 1.55 over two readings, or a drop back below 1.40, would confirm a genuine directional break from this range-bound pattern.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 4.90% came in between the prior 4.80% and the 5.0-5.1% range seen earlier, consistent with my prior "flat" call being roughly correct—the print stayed within the recent noisy band and did not confirm the loosening trigger (>5.0%) or the downtrend trigger (≤4.7%). Looking at the fuller series: unemployment has oscillated in a narrow 4.80-5.10% band for over a year with no clear trend. The 3m change is a modest +0.10pp, 6m change is flat, and 12m change is actually -0.20pp, indicating no sustained deterioration. The Sahm-rule-style gap is a negligible +0.07pp, far below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal recessionary loosening. Range position at 0.33 (of the 12-month range) shows the rate sits closer to the low end than the high end. The updated policy language now describes the labor market as having "strengthened" with "strong" [date] job gains and "some increase in labor utilization," reversing the prior statement's language about slowing improvement. This confirms the labor market is not loosening—if anything, sentiment has firmed. Combined with the rate ticking up only slightly from the 12-month low, this is consistent with continued flatness, not a new up-trend.
Would change its mind if: A print above 5.0% would signal renewed loosening momentum; a print at/below 4.7% would confirm a genuine new downtrend.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, conviction 0.60) was largely borne out over the 63d/252d window (yields fell 0.23pp/0.61pp), but the most recent 21-day trend shows a modest reversal: +0.03pp, with the last three prints (1.62, 1.57, 1.57, 1.58) essentially flat-to-slightly-up after the trough. The 10-year sits at 1.58%, still low in its 12-month range (range_position 0.21, only 0.21pp above the 12m low of 1.37%), well below the 12m high of 2.36%. This argues the multi-quarter decline has largely run its course and stabilized rather than continuing to fall sharply. The 10s30s slope (0.65pp) and 10s2s slope (0.78pp) are unremarkable and don't signal fresh long-end pressure. Policy language shows a subtle dovish tilt on inflation compensation (from "declined" to "remain low"), consistent with continued accommodative long-end conditions and reinvestment policy unchanged, which caps upward pressure. Given the yield is stable near the low end of its range with only a marginal recent uptick, I read this as consolidation rather than a fresh trend. I'm scaling back conviction on "down" since the sharp decline has clearly stalled, and now lean toward flat given the tension between the modest recent uptick and still-low range position.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move back above ~1.70% over coming weeks would signal renewed upward pressure; a drop back toward 1.50% or below would confirm the decline resumed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has been extremely stable near -0.37 for the last several readings, essentially flat since the prior release. My prior falsifiers were a sustained break below -0.37 or a push above -0.33; neither occurred — the index sat right at -0.37 for the last four observations, so the prior flat call was correct. The 4-week change is a negligible -0.01 and the 13-week change is +0.01, confirming no real momentum in either direction. The index remains well below zero (distance from zero -0.37), indicating financial conditions are looser than the historical average, and it sits roughly mid-range (range_position 0.51) between the 52-week low (-0.47) and high (-0.27), showing no directional pressure from either extreme. Policy language is unchanged, reiterating accommodative intent, consistent with no shift in stance. With such a tight, low-volatility band over 13 weeks and no acceleration in either direction, the most defensible call remains flat. There is no evidence of an inflection forming toward tightening or renewed easing beyond noise-level fluctuation.
Would change its mind if: A move below -0.39 or above -0.33 sustained for 2+ weeks would confirm a directional break from this tight range.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_13w_avg

  The previous "down" call was wrong: assets rose to 4,473,840, the highest print in the 13-observation window, up +9,342 over 4 weeks and +12,729 over 13 weeks. This is above the 13w average (4,470,043) and represents the exact rebound condition I set as my falsifier. The series shows a choppy but net-upward drift over the last several prints (4464498→4466793→4468929→4466476→4473840), and the 52w change, while still slightly negative (-1,265), has essentially flattened out, with the level now only ~31,000 below the 52w high. Policy language is unchanged: it describes reinvestment of maturing securities and rollover at auction, consistent with maintaining holdings at "sizable levels" — this is boilerplate reinvestment language, not an active runoff announcement (no explicit monthly cap or QT language is present in what I'm shown), so it doesn't contradict the recent uptick. Given the balance sheet has now printed above its own 13w average with positive 4w and 13w momentum, I'm flipping to a modest "up" lean, though I hold conviction moderate given the noisy week-to-week swings (e.g., large single-week reversals like 4482009→4466482) and the still-negative 52w trend.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below the 13w average (4,470,043) or resumed net declines over next few prints would overturn this up lean.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct in direction over the recent window but the latest prints show tentative stabilization: after falling from 0.82 to 0.76, slope_2s10s has ticked back up to 0.77 then 0.78 in the last two observations. Still, the broader trend remains firmly flattening: slope_change_252d is -0.69pp, slope_change_63d is -0.14pp, and slope_change_21d is -0.10pp — all negative, confirming the dominant multi-horizon trend is flattening. The spread sits at 0.78pp, just 0.02pp above its 12-month low of 0.76pp, with slope_range_position at 0.03 — essentially at the bottom of its 12-month range. This is a curve that has compressed dramatically (range width 0.75pp, high of 1.51pp) and shows no sign of a genuine reversal; the small two-tick uptick looks like noise/consolidation near cycle lows rather than a trend change. The 10s30s at 0.65pp is also compressed, consistent with broad long-end flattening pressure rather than isolated 2s10s dynamics. Policy language remains dovish/gradualist with no hawkish acceleration that would steepen via front-end selloff, though one dissent (George) favoring a hike could add marginal front-end pressure, mildly flattening further if realized. Given proximity to 12-month lows and negative medium/long-term momentum, I lean toward continued flatness-to-slight-flattening rather than a reversal, but the recent 2-tick bounce tempers conviction.
Would change its mind if: A sustained push above 0.85-0.90pp over multiple observations would signal genuine steepening reversal rather than noise near cycle lows.

arbitrated.notes: Inflation is the clearest signal on the panel: core PCE yoy has firmed for three straight months (1.2%→1.60%) with core 3m annualized above trailing yoy, and headline yoy sits near a 12-month high with headline and core now converging. Labor tightness is flat/range-bound with Sahm gap negligible and language firming ("strengthened") — no loosening signal, supports continued gradual normalization pressure rather than a labor-driven disinflation story. Term premium and curve slope both show multi-quarter declines that have stalled into consolidation near 12-month lows, with tiny recent upticks that both analysts explicitly discount as noise — I read both as flat, though the fact that both independently show the same stall-near-lows pattern with a slight upward tick gives a very mild upward skew if inflation continues to firm and balance-sheet growth continues. Financial conditions are genuinely flat, tight range, no signal. Balance sheet flipped to up on real momentum (new high in window, above 13w avg), but I'd weight this less since it's noisy week-to-week and policy language is unchanged boilerplate — a modest up lean only. The panel's cross-link concern (term premium missing balance-sheet/FCI context) is worth flagging but doesn't change my flat call since the balance-sheet uptick is modest and FCI shows no risk-off impulse.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.55) was correct: headline CPI y/y rose from 1.55% to 1.69%, and my stated falsifier (a near-zero/negative mom print plus core PCE flattening below 1.65%) did not materialize — outgoing mom was +0.23% and core PCE actually ticked up to 1.69%. Momentum remains constructive: headline mom_3m_avg (0.23%) exceeds the outgoing print's prior trend, and headline_3m_annualized (2.76%) sits well above the current y/y rate (1.69%), implying further upward drift in y/y readings as base effects roll off low prior-year prints. Core PCE has climbed steadily from ~1.2% a year ago to 1.69%, though its 3m annualized pace (1.83%) is decelerating relative to its recent y/y print, suggesting the core trend may be leveling off even as it approaches target. Headline and core are now converging (core_minus_headline ≈ 0), with headline no longer being suppressed by energy relative to core — a sign the disinflationary energy drag is fading, consistent with the policy language's "transitory effects... dissipate" framing. Given continued positive mom prints and the y/y series sitting at a 12-month high, I lean toward continued acceleration, though the deceleration in core's 3m annualized pace tempers full confidence.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print at or below zero, or core PCE yoy reversing below 1.60%, would signal the upward momentum has stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, yoy_range_position, core_minus_headline

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The breakeven series continued its steady grind higher: 1.66→1.73 over the last ten readings, with the last two prints flat at 1.73 after a run of consecutive gains. My prior "up" call at 0.55 conviction is validated — the breakout thesis held, with no reversal toward 1.54 and no resumption of choppy range-bound action. be_range_position sits at 1.00, meaning the series is printing at its 12-month high, and be_change_21d/63d/252d are all positive and of similar magnitude (~0.2-0.27pp), indicating the move is not just a short-term blip but a sustained multi-month drift higher. However, the pace has clearly decelerated: the last two observations are identical, suggesting the rapid ascent is stalling right at the high. Distance from the 2% target remains meaningfully negative (-0.27pp), so expectations are still below objective, which historically caps upside momentum without fresh catalysts. The policy language is stale/unchanged and describes a low-breakeven regime that no longer matches current levels, so it carries little updating power here. Given deceleration at a new high after a genuine trend, I lean toward continued modest upward drift, but with reduced conviction versus last time given the flattening at the top.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.68 over the next two readings, or renewed flatlining/reversal, would signal the uptrend has exhausted.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print is 4.90%, down from 5.00% the prior month and within the recent 4.80-5.10% range that has persisted for over a year. My prior flat call (0.35 conviction) held up reasonably well: the rate did not break above 5.1% nor below 4.7%, staying inside the established band. The 3-month change is +0.10pp (slight loosening), but 6-month and 12-month changes are both negative (-0.20pp, -0.10pp), indicating no sustained deterioration. The Sahm-gap of +0.13pp is well below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal recessionary loosening. Range position of 0.33 shows the rate sits in the lower-middle of its 12-month range, not at an extreme. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed's updated statement now says the labor market "has continued to strengthen" and unemployment is "little changed in recent months," with "solid" job gains — an upgrade from the prior statement's more tentative "some increase in labor utilization." This is consistent with a market oscillating in a tight, low-4s-to-low-5s band without a clear trend in either direction. Given the choppy but range-bound data and no acceleration signal, I maintain a flat call, though the small uptick in the 3-month trend keeps me from going lower in conviction.
Would change its mind if: A print above 5.1% (new 12-month high) would signal renewed loosening; a print at/below 4.7% would confirm a fresh downtrend.

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has continued climbing since my last read: from 1.75 at the start of this window to 1.84 now, with the level pushing above 1.70 - the threshold I set to confirm a real upward trend rather than noise. My prior "up" call is validated. Momentum remains positive across horizons that matter for a one-month view: +0.28pp over 21 days and +0.33pp over 63 days, though the 252-day change is still negative (-0.26pp), showing the longer downtrend has only recently reversed. The yield sits at 0.47 of its 12-month range (1.37-2.36), squarely in the middle - not stretched in either direction, which leaves room for continuation. The 10s30s slope at 0.74pp is fairly steep, consistent with rising term premium at the long end rather than a broad parallel shift, which reinforces this as a term-premium (not just level) phenomenon. The last print (1.84) ticked down slightly from 1.86, a minor pause, but the underlying trend over the past month is still clearly upward. Policy language is unchanged and describes continued reinvestment supporting accommodative conditions, but that hasn't stopped the recent climb, so it's not decisive against the move.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below ~1.75% would signal the recent rise was noise; a push toward the 12-month high near 2.0%+ would confirm strong continuation.

The recent two-week decline (-0.35 to -0.37) suggests short-term momentum is toward easing, but given the small magnitude of moves (a few hundredths of an index point) and the lack of any new policy language signaling a shift, I don't see strong conviction for a continued move either direction. I'll lean toward a modest easing continuation given the most recent two-point drop, but treat this as a soft lean given the noisy, range-bound history.

  The prior "down" call was wrong for the most recent 4-week window: assets rose net +2,324M over 4 weeks, and the last two prints (4,467,382 then 4,454,326) actually show a decline in the final week after a jump the week before—so the short-term trend is choppy, not a clean rebound. Looking at the fuller picture: 13w change is -10,172M and 52w change is -35,013M, both still negative, and the level sits noticeably below the 52w high (-47,369M). The latest single observation (4,454,326) is below the 13w average (4,464,524.69), which does not meet the rebound condition I set previously. The policy language remains unchanged—still describing a reinvestment/roll-over regime aimed at holding, not overtly expanding, holdings—consistent with a flat-to-slightly-declining path rather than active QE. Net picture: the multi-month runoff trend still dominates, but the pace has clearly decelerated and the very latest weekly move was down again. I'll shift from a confident "down" toward a weak lean, since the balance sheet looks close to flat with a slight downward bias into month-end.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly increases pushing the level back above the 13w average (~4,464,525) would flip this to an up call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_13w_avg, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The slope series has moved from ~0.93 at the start to 0.98 currently, with the last two prints at 1.00 and 0.98 - a modest pullback from a fresh recent high but still well above the earlier cluster around 0.90-0.93. My prior flat call's falsifier (sustained move above 0.90pp) has now been met: slope_change_21d (+0.15pp) and slope_change_63d (+0.14pp) both confirm a steepening trend over the last one to three months, even as the 252-day change remains negative (-0.39pp), showing this is a reversal within a longer flattening regime rather than a continuation of it. slope_range_position at 0.31 shows the spread is still in the lower third of its 12-month range, so there's room to steepen further before hitting range extremes. The two-observation dip from 1.00 to 0.98 is minor and could be noise rather than a turn. Given the consistent short- and medium-term steepening signal, and that my prior flat call should have already flipped to up on this data, I now lean toward continued modest steepening, though the last print's small pullback tempers full conviction.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.93pp over the next few observations, or slope_change_21d turning negative, would signal the steepening has stalled.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===

  term_premium lacks inflation_expectations — Rising breakevens would clarify whether the yield rise is real-rate or inflation-premium driven.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was validated: headline CPI y/y rose from 1.55 to 1.68/1.69, and core PCE y/y climbed further to 1.78, both well above the falsifier thresholds I set (no sub-zero MoM print, no reversal below 1.60% core PCE). Momentum has clearly been positive over the past year: headline y/y is up 0.63pp over 3 months and sits at the top of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position=1.00, right at the 12m high of 1.69). However, the most recent monthly prints show deceleration in the flow data: outgoing_mom is only 0.12%, down from a 3-month average of 0.20%, and the last two MoM readings (0.23, 0.12) are cooling from the 0.26-0.38 stretch earlier. The 3m annualized headline pace (2.49%) is still above the y/y rate, consistent with some further drift higher, but the deceleration in monthly increments tempers that. Core PCE continues to grind higher and remains 0.22pp below target, so no target-based pressure to decelerate. On balance, momentum has moderated but not reversed — headline's 3m annualized pace still exceeds the y/y print, implying another modest increase is more likely than a decline, but conviction is lower than last time given the softening MoM trend.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print at or below zero, or two consecutive sub-0.15% MoM prints, would signal momentum has stalled and flip me to flat/down.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: breakeven_10y rose to 1.94 from 1.86, its 12-month high, and the range_position sits at 1.00 — the series is now printing new highs within its recent range. Momentum is intact across horizons (21d +0.21, 63d +0.48, 252d +0.34), all positive, with the most recent print the strongest single-step move in the sample. Policy language corroborates the market read: the statement was explicitly updated to acknowledge market-based inflation compensation "have moved up," a direct confirmation that the Committee itself now sees this shift as real rather than noise. Breakeven is essentially back at the 2% objective (distance -0.06pp), removing the anchoring-below-target concern that existed a few readings ago. The single dip to 1.85 mid-series shows some two-way volatility, but the latest print reverses that and extends to a fresh high, undercutting any near-term reversal thesis. With confirmation from both price action and policy text, I maintain an up call, and raise conviction modestly given the corroborating statement language and the new high — though I stop short of high conviction since one only reading confirms the breakout and monthly moves in breakevens can retrace quickly.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below ~1.86 over the next two readings, or a return to policy language describing compensation as flat/low, would signal the uptrend has stalled.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print came in at 4.70%, a fresh 12-month low, which is exactly the downside threshold I flagged as a falsifier for "flat." My prior call was wrong to the extent it didn't anticipate this drop, though the series had been oscillating in a narrow 4.7-5.1% band for a year, so a flat call had reasonable grounds going in. Now the picture has shifted: 3m change is -0.20pp and 12m change is -0.40pp, both pointing down, and the unrate is sitting at its low with zero distance above the 12m low. Sahm-gap is a modest +0.17pp, well below the 0.5 threshold that would signal a genuine recessionary turn, so this isn't a Sahm-rule alarm signal but rather a continuation of gradual tightening. Fed language is essentially unchanged, still describing the labor market as "continued to strengthen," consistent with gradual improvement rather than any shock. Given the fresh low and negative momentum across 3/6/12m windows, my base case shifts toward continued tightening (rate flat-to-down) rather than the mean-reverting flat call I made last time. I'll lean down, though modestly, since one month at a cycle low can easily be followed by a tick back up given the narrow historical range.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 4.9% (mid-range) would suggest reversion rather than continued tightening; a print above 5.0% would confirm renewed loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.45) was correct: the 10y has continued rising and now sits at 2.37%, exactly at its 12-month high (range_position = 1.00). Momentum remains firmly positive: +0.51pp over 21d, +0.80pp over 63d, though the 252d change of only +0.15pp shows this is a recent acceleration rather than a year-long trend. The yield is pinned at the top of its 12-month range with no sign of reversal in the last few prints (2.32→2.30→2.37). The 30s10s slope at 0.65pp is modest, not suggesting an outsized long-end premium blowout beyond the broader curve move, but the level and momentum both point to continued upward pressure. Policy language shifted to note market-based inflation compensation "has moved up," consistent with the term premium/long-end pressure narrative. With the yield breaking to a fresh 12-month high rather than just testing it, I'm raising conviction modestly from last time. Risk is mean-reversion after such a sharp 63-day move, but nothing here signals stalling. I'll call up with fairly firm conviction given the fresh high and sustained momentum, tempered by the recognition that a break above range highs can sometimes exhaust momentum near-term.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below ~2.15-2.20% would signal the breakout to new highs was exhausted; continuation above 2.40% would confirm further term-premium rise.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_12m_high, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down/easing, conviction 0.30) was correct and the trend has strengthened. NFCI has moved from -0.35 four weeks ago to -0.42 now, with nfci_change_4w at -0.05 and nfci_change_13w at -0.06 — momentum is accelerating rather than stalling, since most of the 13-week decline occurred in just the last month. The index is now sitting near its 52-week low (-0.44), with nfci_above_52w_low at only +0.01 and nfci_range_position at 0.08 (near the bottom of its 52-week range). This is a clear, persistent easing trend, not a one-off wiggle: six of the last seven readings show consecutive declines. Policy language remains unchanged, still describing accommodative conditions, which is consistent with and does not contradict the measured easing. There's no sign of reversal or plateau in the data — no observation has ticked back up toward -0.35 as would be needed to negate the easing lean per my prior falsifier. Given the consistency and acceleration of the move, plus proximity to historical lows, I expect conditions to continue loosening modestly into month end, absent a shock. I'd temper conviction only by the flatness in the earliest part of the window, which shows this is a fairly gentle, grinding easing rather than a sharp break.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.38 sustained for 2+ weeks would negate this easing lean and signal stabilization near the 13-week average.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_above_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last release shows fed_assets at 4,465,782, up from 4,453,633 the prior week — a second consecutive weekly increase, and the level now sits above the 13w average (~4,460,798) and above the level I set as my flip condition (~4,464,525). The 4w change is now clearly positive (+11,456), reversing the prior mild downtrend. My prior "down" call at 0.25 conviction was weak and is now contradicted by the data: this looks like a small reversal or noise-driven uptick rather than continued runoff. However, the policy language is unchanged and describes a reinvestment/roll-over framework consistent with either flat holdings or gradual runoff under a cap — it doesn't explicitly signal a new expansion. The 13w and 52w changes remain negative, so the broader multi-month trend is still slightly down, but recent weekly momentum has flipped up. Given the mandate to weight recent pace more heavily, and that my own stated falsifier condition has been met, I'm revising toward flat-to-up rather than sticking with down. Conviction is modest because a two-week bounce could easily be noise within a still-declining longer trend, and policy language gives no indication of a deliberate shift away from runoff.
Would change its mind if: A renewed weekly decline pushing fed_assets back below the 13w average (~4,460,800) would revert this to a down call.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is validated by this release: slope_2s10s jumped from 1.21 to 1.26pp, the largest single-step move in the series, confirming the steepening bias rather than stalling. Momentum indicators are broadly positive: slope_change_21d +0.26pp and slope_change_63d +0.50pp both show steepening over the past one-three months, though slope_change_252d is still slightly negative (-0.04pp), meaning the year-over-year trend only recently flipped from flattening to steepening. The spread sits at 0.82 of its 12-month range (position 0-1), close to the 12-month high of 1.37pp and well above the 12-month low of 0.76pp — this is a curve that has been persistently steepening rather than chopping sideways. The 10s30s slope at 0.65pp confirms the long end is not inverting against itself, consistent with broad-based steepening rather than a front-end-specific artifact. FOMC language shows continued signaling that a hike "case has strengthened," which typically pressures the front end more than the long end, supportive of further steepening. Given the strong recent acceleration and high range position, I lean toward continued steepening into month-end, but with the caveat that the series has chopped over 1.21-1.27 repeatedly this year, so a pause or partial reversal is plausible.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below 1.21pp over the next few observations, or slope_change_21d turning negative, would signal the steepening move has stalled or reversed.

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of Treasury runoff/reinvestment changes directly affects long-end supply and term premium.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-on/risk-off flows could indicate flight-to-quality demand suppressing yields.

arbitrated.notes: The panel paints a fairly coherent picture of a reflating, steepening, easing-financial-conditions regime, though with divergent conviction levels and a few internal tensions worth flagging. Inflation momentum is real but decelerating at the margin (MoM cooling even as y/y hits highs), so I trim conviction versus the analyst's own framing. Inflation expectations are the strongest, cleanest signal — breakevens at fresh highs, multi-horizon momentum aligned, and corroborated by explicit FOMC language acknowledging the move, which is a rare cross-check I weight heavily. Term premium (10y yields) is similarly clean and self-reinforcing with the breakeven move and curve steepening — three drivers (inflation_expectations, term_premium, curve_slope) all point the same direction with mutual corroboration via 30s10s/10s30s consistency checks, which raises my confidence in each individually. Labor tightening (falling unemployment continuing) fits a strengthening-economy backdrop that is somewhat in tension with an aggressively easing financial-conditions/dovish balance-sheet story, but a modestly tightening labor market plus rising term premium plus rising breakevens is actually a coherent "growth + inflation firming, curve steepening on term premium not policy panic" narrative. Financial conditions easing is well-evidenced (accelerating, near 52w low, six of seven readings down) and I keep it at solid conviction. Balance sheet is the weakest call — only two weeks of reversal against a still-negative 13w/52w trend, and unchanged policy language; I discount conviction meaningfully versus the analyst's own 0.35, treating it as closer to noise. The flagged cross-driver gap (term premium missing balance-sheet/financial-conditions context) is worth noting but doesn't change direction since both would if anything reinforce a steepening long end via easier conditions and ambiguous supply signal.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "up" call played out: headline rose from 1.68% to 2.05% y/y, and my falsifier condition (a stalled or negative MoM print) did not occur — the latest MoM was 0.25%, above the trailing 3m average of 0.20% and above the outgoing print of -0.05%. So momentum did not stall; it reaccelerated. However, looking ahead one month, the base effect matters: headline_yoy is now at its 12-month high and the 3m-annualized headline pace (2.45%) is only modestly above the yoy level, while core PCE's 3m-annualized pace (1.23%) is well below its yoy (1.72%), and core_yoy_change_3m is flat (+0.02pp) — core is decelerating in momentum terms even as headline yoy has been pulled up by energy/base effects. Core-minus-headline is now -0.33pp, meaning headline is running hot relative to core, likely an energy-driven pop. Policy language confirms this is being read as a genuine but still-below-target increase, not a runaway trend. Given headline's strong recent MoM (0.25%) versus the prior year's comparison month, another firm print is plausible, but the deceleration in core's momentum urges caution about extrapolating another sharp yoy jump. I lean modestly toward continued firmness but tempered.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print below 0.15%, or core_yoy_change_3m turning negative, would signal the recent acceleration has stalled and flip me toward flat.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, core_minus_headline, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.

=== inflation ===

Headline 3m annualized is running at 3.14%, more than a point above the y/y rate, signaling building momentum, not stalling. My falsifier (mom below 0.15% or core_yoy_change_3m negative) did not trigger — mom came in at 0.40% and core_yoy_change_3m is still positive at +0.08pp. However, core PCE tells a more measured story: 1.77% y/y, essentially flat over the last several prints (1.70→1.69→1.78→1.72→1.77), with core_3m_annualized only 1.16%, actually below core's own y/y — implying core momentum is cooling even as headline is being pushed up, likely by energy/food base effects given core_minus_headline has swung to -0.74pp. Since headline is the requested measurement, and it's now clearly accelerating with strong momentum and no distance-from-target ceiling (headline still below 2% target isn't shown, but core sits at -0.23pp), I lean up again, though I weight core's stabilization as a reason not to go higher in conviction.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print below 0.15%, or headline_3m_annualized falling back near/below the y/y rate, would signal the acceleration has stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, core_minus_headline

Given the magnitude and persistence of the prior uptrend, and that breakeven is now essentially at the 2% target distance (+0.05pp), I lean toward continued strength or at most a shallow pause rather than a reversal. Financial conditions are unambiguously easing — fresh 52-week lows, monotonic across all windows, accommodative policy language unchanged — this is the highest-conviction call on the panel. Balance sheet is genuine noise around a plateau — no policy or measurement basis for a trend, flat with low conviction. Curve slope has stalled near cycle steep levels after being wrong on the prior "down" call; 21d flat, 63d/252d steepening, hike now delivered — I read this as flat/consolidation with a slight flattening skew, but not enough conviction to lean either direction confidently, especially since this driver wasn't checked against term premium or inflation expectations which could clarify the direction.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 13 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose again to 2.81% from 2.51%, continuing a steady climb from 12-month lows near 0.87%. However, momentum signals are now mixed rather than clearly accelerating. The latest monthly print (0.16%) is below the 3m average (0.27%) and well below the outgoing print (0.31%), and headline_3m_annualized (3.31%) has decelerated versus recent quarters even though it's still above the y/y rate. Core PCE continues a slow, steady grind higher (1.86% y/y, only +0.08pp over 3m annualized pace of 1.72%), still below target, showing persistent but modest underlying inflation with no sign of acceleration or deceleration — just steady drift toward 2%. The y/y increase is now dominated by base effects and energy/import price normalization per policy language, not fresh momentum. Given the deceleration in the latest MoM reading and the cooling 3m annualized pace toward the y/y rate, my trigger condition for reconsidering is partially met. I lean toward continued mild upward drift in headline y/y (base effects still working through), but with less conviction than before given the loss of monthly momentum.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print near/below 0.10%, or headline_3m_annualized dropping below the current y/y rate, would confirm the acceleration has ended.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  Last release's "up" call (0.45 conviction) is mixed against the newest print: breakeven ticked up to 2.02 from 2.01, but that follows a dip to 2.00, so the series has been choppy (2.03, 2.00, 2.01, 2.02) rather than trending. The 21d change is now negative (-0.06pp), while 63d (+0.13) and 252d (+0.63) remain positive — a picture of a longer uptrend that has stalled/paused over the last month, not reversed. Range position is high (0.92 of 12m range), meaning breakevens sit near their highs, but the pace of further increase seems to have flattened. The policy language actually turned less hawkish on this driver: the prior statement said inflation compensation "moved up considerably," while the current statement drops that phrase, describing compensation as simply "remain low" with survey expectations "little changed." This is a downgrade in the narrative of upward momentum, suggesting the committee no longer sees a considerable rise in market-based measures. Combined with the flat-to-slightly-negative recent price action and the softer language, I read this as a pause rather than continuation of the uptrend, though levels remain elevated versus 12m lows and near target. I'm shifting toward flat, with a slight upward tilt given still-positive 63d/252d momentum and proximity to target.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 2.06 (12m-ish recent high) or below 2.00 sustained for multiple prints would confirm renewed trend rather than continued chop.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print came in at 4.60%, below my prior "flat" range and at the 12-month low—so my previous call was wrong in direction of resolution, though the magnitude was modest and consistent with continued gradual tightening rather than a regime shift. The series shows a clear downtrend: 3m change -0.10pp, 6m and 12m change -0.30pp each, and the rate now sits exactly at the 12-month low (unrate_above_12m_low = 0.00, range_position = 0.00). Sahm gap is a negligible +0.07pp, far from recession-signal territory, confirming no loosening momentum. Distance from the ~4% natural rate is 0.60pp, meaning the market is still historically tight but has been grinding tighter, not looser. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed's own characterization shifted from "the labor market has continued to strengthen... unemployment rate has declined" to "job gains remained solid and the unemployment rate stayed near its recent low"—consistent with a market that troughed rather than reversed. Given three consecutive months at 4.70 before ticking to 4.60, the momentum is modestly tightening, not flat. I'll lean toward continued tightness with a modest downward (further decline) call, though flat is also plausible given the small size of the move and possible bottoming near the low.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.7% would confirm loosening/reversal off the low; a print at or below 4.5% would confirm accelerating tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: the 10-year has drifted lower over the last ten readings, from 2.47 down to 2.36, with a -0.13pp move over 21 days and a break below the 2.40% level I flagged as the downtrend trigger. The 63-day change is flat, and the 252-day change (+0.65pp) still shows the level well above where it was a year ago, but the recent trend is clearly softening. Range position at 0.80 shows yields still sit in the upper part of the 12-month range, so this is a pullback from elevated levels rather than a new low, tempering how much weight I put on the recent decline. The 30s10s slope (+0.61pp) is modest, not signaling aggressive steepening pressure from long-end issuance/duration supply. Policy language changed only on inflation compensation wording (dropping "moved up considerably," now "remain low"), which is a modest disinflationary-expectations signal - this reduces upward pressure on nominal long yields rather than supporting a further one. Given the break below 2.40, the flat 63-day trend, and the softer inflation-compensation language, I lean toward flat-to-down for month-end rather than continuing to expect a resumption of the uptrend.
Would change its mind if: A push back above ~2.45-2.50% would signal resumed uptrend; a break below ~2.30% would confirm stronger downtrend and raise conviction.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continued to grind lower (easier) since the prior release, moving from -0.49 to -0.50, consistent with my prior "down" call. The 4-week change is -0.01 and the 13-week change is -0.07, both negative, confirming the easing trend is intact, though the pace of easing has clearly decelerated versus recent history. The index sits at the very bottom of its own 52-week range (range_position 0.00), at the 52-week low, well below the zero average, indicating financial conditions are firmly looser than historical norms. The trajectory has been remarkably steady and monotonic-almost every observation ticks down slightly-suggesting a persistent, low-volatility easing drift rather than a reversal. Policy language remains unchanged, reiterating accommodative intent, which supports continuation rather than a turn. There is no evidence of stabilization or upward inflection; my falsifier condition (a move back above -0.42 or a positive 4-week change) has not been met. Given the flattening momentum, I moderate conviction slightly versus last time, but the direction still points down for month-end, as the series has given no sign of bottoming.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.42 sustained for 2+ weeks, or a positive 4-week change, would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,468,702) is the second-highest of the 13-week window, up sharply from the prior reading and pushing the 4-week change to +15.9B — the largest short-term swing in this series. However, the 13-week change is only +2.9B (+0.07%), and the 52-week change is still slightly negative (-21.1B), so the level remains range-bound within the ~4,446–4,471B band I identified last time. The move has not yet broken decisively above the top of that band (52w high is 4,499,729, still ~31B above). Policy language is unchanged: reinvestment/rollover language explicitly designed to keep holdings at "sizable levels," consistent with a flat-to-reinvestment stance rather than active QE or QT — there's no runoff cap language here at all, just full reinvestment, which mechanically tends to keep the balance sheet roughly flat with some noise from auction timing and settlement dates. My prior "flat" call was directionally fine given the 13w figure, but the 4-week acceleration is large enough to lean modestly toward "up" for the near-term month-end read, while the longer trend still looks flat rather than a clear expansion regime. I'll raise conviction slightly on "up" given the recent acceleration, but keep it modest since the multi-week trend hasn't broken the range convincingly.
Would change its mind if: A pullback below ~4,456B next reading would confirm this was noise within the band, not a real uptrend; a push above 4,471B would confirm expansion.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been overtaken by events: the spread has broken decisively below 1.20 (last three prints 1.19, 1.16, 1.14), with slope_change_21d at -0.13pp and slope_change_63d at -0.10pp — both confirming the exact break condition I set for changing my mind. This is a clear, persistent flattening trend, not noise: the last five observations are monotonically non-increasing after a period of stability. The spread now sits at 1.14pp, well off the 12-month high (1.34) and closer to the low (0.76), with slope_range_position at 0.66 — still mid-range but moving toward the low end. The 252-day change remains positive (+0.15), showing the longer-run trend is still steepening, but the recent 21d/63d momentum has clearly reversed and is now the dominant near-term signal. 10s30s at 0.61 shows the long end itself isn't inverting, suggesting the flattening pressure is concentrated in the belly/front-end differential rather than a long-end selloff. With no reversal signal in the last three prints and momentum solidly negative, I expect continued modest flattening into month end, though the still-comfortable range position argues against a sharp acceleration.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 1.19-1.20pp with slope_change_21d turning positive would signal the flattening has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader financial conditions tightening/easing would clarify flight-to-quality vs. risk-on pressure on long yields.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Details on pace of Treasury issuance and Fed balance sheet runoff would clarify supply pressure on the long end.

arbitrated.notes: Inflation is drifting up but losing monthly momentum — the y/y print keeps climbing on base effects while MoM and 3m-annualized decelerate, so I keep a modest positive lean rather than a strong one. Inflation expectations look like a stalled uptrend: breakevens are choppy near highs, 21d momentum has turned negative even as 63d/252d stay positive, and Fed language softened on "moved up considerably" to "remain low" — net flat with only a marginal upward tilt. Labor market continues to grind tighter: unemployment sits at a 12-month low with negative 3/6/12m changes and no Sahm-rule loosening signal, so tightness is still increasing (lower unemployment). Term premium reversed its prior uptrend call — 10s have broken below the flagged 2.40 trigger, 63d change is flat, and softer inflation-compensation language removes upward pressure on nominal yields; I read this as flat-to-slightly-down rather than up. Financial conditions remain the cleanest, most persistent signal: NFCI is at a 52-week low, monotonically easing for weeks, with no stabilization evidence — clear continued easing (index falling further). Balance sheet is more ambiguous: a sharp 4-week acceleration is real but sits inside a well-established flat range, with 13w/52w changes near zero; I lean mildly up but with limited conviction given the recon-siliation between short and long windows. Curve slope is the strongest directional call — three consecutive prints below 1.20, negative 21d/63d momentum, and a decisive break of the prior range ceiling all point to continued flattening in the near term, even as the very long-run trend still steepens. Overall the macro mix is: inflation grinding up slowly, easier financial conditions, tighter labor market, and a flattening curve — a classic late-cycle tension between easy financial conditions and slowing growth/inflation-momentum signals.

discounted.why: Signal too noisy/ambiguous between short-term acceleration and flat longer-term trend to weight heavily.

arbitrated.falsifier: A term_premium break back above 2.45-2.50%, curve slope reclaiming 1.19-1.20 with positive momentum, or NFCI stabilizing above -0.42 would flip this easing/flattening read.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "up" call was wrong: headline yoy fell from 2.81 to 2.44 as the outgoing 0.38% m/m print (from a year ago) rolled off and was replaced by a weak -0.05% m/m reading. That -0.05 print is exactly the kind of soft number I flagged as a falsifier. Momentum has clearly cooled: the 3-month average m/m is now 0.17%, well below the outgoing 0.38%, and headline 3m annualized (2.09%) sits below the latest yoy (2.44%), signaling the yoy rate should keep drifting down over the next print unless m/m reaccelerates. Core PCE continues a slow, steady grind higher (1.87 vs 1.86 vs 1.72 a few months back), still 0.13pp below target, showing underlying persistence but no fresh acceleration. Policy language shifted to "moving close to" 2%, with core still "somewhat below" — consistent with gradual, not sharp, firming. Given headline's high base-effect drag and the negative recent m/m print, I expect headline yoy to decelerate again next month, though core's steady climb argues against a sharp reversal. I'm weighting the momentum/base-effect math over the longer trend given the one-month horizon.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print back above ~0.25-0.30% would suggest deceleration has stalled and yoy could stabilize or tick back up.
Leaned on: headline_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_pce_yoy, core_distance_from_target

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (1.97) sits right in the middle of a choppy range that has persisted for ~10 readings (1.95-2.00), with no sustained break either direction. My prior "flat" call holds up: breakeven ticked around within a tight band and the 21d change (-0.04pp) is modest while 63d is essentially flat (+0.03pp), confirming continued chop rather than a new trend. The longer 252d change (+0.32pp) shows an established uptrend over the past year, and the series sits 0.85 of the way up its 12-month range, near the highs — so the broader drift has been upward even though the last month has stalled. Distance from the 2% target is negligible (-0.03pp), meaning inflation expectations are essentially anchored at target, which itself argues against a big directional move either way. Policy language changed only marginally (dropping "only" before gradual increases) — a hawkish-leaning tweak to the rate path narrative, but the inflation-compensation language is verbatim unchanged, low and little-changed. That language stasis reinforces that market-based inflation expectations aren't seeing a fresh catalyst. Given the tight recent range and unchanged qualitative characterization, I still don't see enough evidence for a directional call this month — the series is oscillating near the top of its range without breaking out.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 2.00 or below 1.94 sustained for multiple prints would confirm a renewed trend rather than continued chop.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (down/loosening, 0.35 conviction) was wrong: the newest print fell to 4.40%, a new 12-month low, not a rise above 4.7%. The series shows a clear tightening trend: unemployment fell from 4.7% to 4.4% in the last reading, with 3m and 6m/12m changes all negative (-0.30, -0.60, -0.60 pp). The rate sits at its 12-month low (unrate_above_12m_low = 0), sahm_gap is a modest +0.17pp (no recessionary spike), and range_position is 0 (at the bottom of its 12-month range). Distance from natural rate is now only 0.4pp, meaning the market is already tight and getting tighter. Policy language corroborates: the Fed shifted language from "labor market has continued to strengthen... unemployment rate has declined" to "job gains remained solid and unemployment rate stayed near its recent low" — consistent with a tight, stable-to-tightening labor market, not loosening. Given the accelerating decline and fresh new low, the momentum favors another flat-to-down print, though three-tenths drop in one month is unusually large and could partially reverse/stabilize. I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-down call rather than continued sharp decline, since a single-month 0.2pp drop is often followed by stabilization.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 4.6-4.7% would confirm reversal/loosening; a print at or below 4.3% would confirm continued rapid tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35

The prior call of flat was correct: yields have not pushed back above the 2.45-2.50% trigger nor broken below 2.30%, staying inside a tight band. The range position (0.82) still shows the yield sitting in the upper portion of its 12-month range, consistent with the balance-sheet reinvestment/runoff policy language staying unchanged and not signaling fresh upward pressure. The 30s10s slope (+0.62pp) is stable, suggesting no material shift in long-end term premium relative to the very long end. Policy language shows only a minor wording tweak on inflation expectations, not directly bearing on term premium mechanics. With no new catalyst — no acceleration in issuance signals, no flight-to-quality move, no breakout in either direction — the driver looks range-bound. I maintain a flat call, keeping conviction modest given the driver has been essentially directionless for three consecutive readings and lacks a clear trigger for a month-end move.

Given the last two readings show a step-up in level (4,478,236 then 4,469,618) versus the preceding several months, and given the recent trend in 4w/13w changes, I lean toward continued modest expansion or at least stabilization, but the pullback from the peak tempers confidence.

Given the deceleration in the pace of flattening (21d milder than 63d) but the underlying trend and hike still pointing toward continued front-end pressure, I lean toward continued mild flattening but with less conviction than before, since the tape has essentially stalled near 1.13-1.14 the last few prints.

arbitrated.notes: Overall picture is a late-cycle economy where labor is tightening further, headline inflation is cooling on base effects even as core PCE grinds slowly higher, and market-based signals (breakevens, term premium, financial conditions) are stuck in tight, directionless ranges. The strongest, most mechanically grounded calls are labor tightening (new 12-month low, negative 3/6/12m changes, corroborated by Fed language) and headline inflation's near-term deceleration (soft m/m print, base-effect math favoring lower yoy). Curve flattening is real but decelerating — I keep it negative but trim conviction since the last several prints have stalled in a narrow band. Term premium, inflation expectations, and financial conditions all sit in genuine chop with no breakout signal and modest conviction analysts themselves — I keep these near-flat/low-conviction rather than manufacture direction. Balance sheet is the shakiest: a plausible reversal from runoff to modest expansion, but the analyst's own falsifier band brackets the current print, and it's the one series most detached from the others (no corroborating policy language). I give it a small positive lean but treat it skeptically. No analyst was given cross-driver context (inflation lacked financial conditions, term premium lacked balance sheet and financial conditions), so I can't stress-test correlations between the rates/liquidity drivers as much as I'd like — that's a real gap given curve flattening, term premium flatness, and balance sheet expansion should in principle interact. I'm treating each driver's call largely on its own momentum evidence rather than assuming consistency across them.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The previous call (down) was directionally correct: headline yoy fell from 2.44 to 2.18 as the outgoing high monthly print (0.40) rolled out and was replaced by a soft 0.12 m/m reading. That confirms the deceleration thesis for that release. Looking ahead to the next print, momentum has clearly cooled: headline_mom_3m_avg is just 0.08%, headline_3m_annualized is only 0.95%, well below the 2.18% yoy level, implying further downward pressure as old higher-base months roll off. However, the outgoing month being dropped next period was a modest 0.23% (from earlier in the run), not a large one, tempering how much further yoy could fall. Core PCE tells a more resilient story — it dropped from 1.87 to 1.68, a larger single-month drop than its trend, but core_yoy_change_3m of only -0.09pp shows core has been broadly flat/stable near 1.6-1.7%, still running below target with little momentum either way. Policy language shift confirms this: statement moved from "still below" target to "moving close to" 2%, but explicitly flags core "little changed" and "somewhat below" — consistent with a stalling, not accelerating, core trend. Given weak recent m/m momentum and low base effects rolling off, I lean toward another modest decline or flat print in headline yoy, though the sharp core PCE drop injects some uncertainty about whether the last headline print was noisy.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above ~0.30-0.35% would signal reacceleration and could push yoy back up toward 2.3-2.5%.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_mom_3m_avg, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation (1.92) continues a modest uptick within a recent series that had dipped to 1.84 before climbing back. My prior "flat" call was reasonably accurate in that the series has stayed in a tight 1.84-1.92 band with no decisive breakout, but the most recent three prints (1.90, 1.91, 1.92) show a small but persistent grind higher, and the 21d/63d changes of -0.06/-0.16 reflect the earlier dip more than the current trajectory. Breakevens remain 0.08pp below the 2% objective, and range_position at 0.78 shows the series sitting in the upper part of its 12-month range (1.34-2.08), well off the lows. Policy language dropped "remain low" framing slightly and kept "little changed" characterization for survey expectations, suggesting no strong catalyst either way from communication. Given the recent three-print upward drift and proximity to the top of the range without a confirmed breakout above 2.00, I lean toward a mild upward drift continuing into month-end rather than a reversal back toward the 1.84-1.85 lows. Conviction remains modest since the move is small and could easily stall.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below 1.88 or a stall exactly at 1.92 for multiple prints would undercut the mild-upward-drift read.

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 4.40%, unchanged from the prior month and matching the 12-month low. My previous flat call was reasonable: the rate did not reverse back toward 4.6-4.7% as one risk scenario suggested, nor did it break meaningfully below 4.4% into new-low territory. The 3m/6m/12m trends all show continued gradual declines (-0.30, -0.50, -0.70 pp respectively), and the Sahm-gap of +0.07pp is far from any recession-signaling threshold, confirming there is no loosening underway. The range position at 0.00 (sitting at the 12-month low) shows the labor market remains tight, at 0.40pp below my natural-rate anchor of 4%. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed's updated statement describes the unemployment rate as "little changed in recent months" (softer characterization than "near its recent low" previously), and continues to describe labor market strengthening alongside a rate hike — consistent with a still-tight, stable labor market rather than one deteriorating. With two consecutive prints at 4.40% and no acceleration in either direction, I read this as a genuine plateau at a cyclical low rather than the start of a new leg down or a reversal upward. I'll maintain a flat call, slightly more confident given the stabilization is now confirmed by a second flat print.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 4.6% would confirm reversal/loosening; a print at or below 4.2% would confirm renewed rapid tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_low

My prior "flat" call was largely correct in direction — yields drifted in a narrow 2.24-2.35% band since then rather than breaking decisively either way, though my downside trigger (break below 2.30%) has now just barely been touched. The 21d and 63d changes are modestly negative (-0.10, -0.20 pp), suggesting a mild cooling from the prior uptrend, while the 252d change (+0.35pp) shows the broader year-long trend is still up. Range position at 0.74 shows yields remain in the upper-middle of the 12-month range, not at extremes. The 30s10s slope is steady at 0.67pp, showing no dramatic steepening or flattening pressure from long-end issuance/supply dynamics. Policy language is essentially unchanged, with a minor edit that doesn't signal any new stance on longer-term financial conditions or asset purchases. With balance-sheet reinvestment policy holding steady and no fresh catalyst, I see the long end as directionless but with a slight softening bias given the recent 21d/63d declines.
Would change its mind if: A push above ~2.35% would signal resumed uptrend; a sustained break below ~2.20% would confirm a firmer downtrend and raise conviction.
