arbitrated.notes: Inflation is cooling on multiple fronts: headline decelerated more than expected, 3m annualized momentum (headline and core) runs well below yoy, and core PCE is sitting right at target rather than overshooting. Combined with policy language shifting to neutral "near 2 percent" phrasing, I side with the inflation analyst's down call, though only moderate conviction since the mom_gap trigger technically didn't fire and core PCE ticked up slightly. Inflation expectations are pulling back from a local peak (2.12→2.08) but the longer 252d trend remains up and the level is still 8bp above the 2% objective with range position elevated — this reads as a pullback within an uptrend, so I take a mild down lean but respect the analyst's own hedging. Labor tightness is genuinely flat — unemployment plateaued at 3.8% with zero 3m change and no loosening signal; I have no strong directional view here beyond flat. Term premium: the analyst backed off its prior up call given yield drift lower and flat 21d/63d changes, and I agree this is more consistent with continued softening in expectations and easier financial conditions than fresh long-end pressure — modest down lean. Financial conditions show a clean, steady easing trend (NFCI grinding lower, closer to 52w low, no reversal signs) — high-conviction down call. Balance sheet runoff is the cleanest, most mechanical trend: steady, unbroken decline with no policy change signaled — high conviction down. Curve slope shows a tentative steepening bounce off cycle lows but the dominant multi-month trend is still flattening and range position is near the bottom decile — I temper the analyst's up call to a small lean rather than a clear call, since one print isn't a trend break. Overall, the macro picture is one of gently disinflating pressure, easing financial conditions, continued balance-sheet drawdown, and a labor market that has stopped tightening further but hasn't reversed.

discounted.why: Genuine plateau with no momentum either direction; analyst's own flat call taken at face value.

arbitrated.falsifier: A headline CPI mom print above 0.30% with core PCE breaking above 2.1%, alongside NFCI reversing above -0.55 and balance sheet stabilizing above its 13w average.

risks.text: All calls are one-month-ahead extrapolations from short windows; a single surprising print could reverse several drivers at once.
Financial conditions, term premium and breakevens easing together could all reflect one common risk-off shock rather than independent trends.
Curve steepening bounce could be an early regime shift the panel is underweighting given its recency.

why.labor_tightness: Plateau at 3.8% with zero 3m change and no loosening signal — genuinely flat, no directional edge.
why.term_premium: Yield drifted below recent high with flat/negative 21d,63d changes; softer breakevens and easier FCI reduce upside pressure.
why.financial_conditions: Steady multi-week easing, near 52w low, no reversal signs — clean directional trend, high conviction down.
why.balance_sheet: Unbroken mechanical runoff, pace steady-to-slightly-faster, no policy language suggesting pause — firm down call retained.
why.curve_slope: Tentative steepening bounce off lows, but dominant 63d/252d trend still flattening and range position near bottom decile — tempered lean.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: headline CPI y/y fell from 2.85 to 2.64 to 2.33 over the last two prints, a substantial deceleration, and my falsifier condition (mom>0.30% plus core PCE clearly above 2.0%) did not trigger — outgoing mom was just 0.08% and core PCE actually ticked down to 1.90 from 2.00. The 3-month annualized rates confirm the cooling: headline running at only 1.87% annualized and core PCE at a soft 0.93% annualized, both well below the trailing y/y levels, meaning the y/y figures likely have further room to fall or at least stabilize lower before mean-reverting. Core PCE sits essentially at target (1.90, -0.10pp from 2%) and has been flat-to-softening over 3 months, arguing against any acceleration narrative. Policy language shift from "moved close to" to "remain near" 2 percent is consistent with a stabilization rather than a reacceleration story. Given the sharp two-month drop in headline y/y and soft recent monthly prints, I lean toward continued softness or flat readings next month rather than a rebound, though the pace of the recent decline (0.31pp in one month) may partially reflect base effects that could attenuate.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print above 0.30% combined with core_pce_yoy rising back above 2.0% would signal renewed acceleration and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call of "down" (0.35 conviction) was wrong: the series has since pushed further up, not down, breaking well above the 2.11-2.12 level I flagged as the negation threshold. The last 10 readings show a plateau near 2.14-2.16, essentially flat over the most recent stretch (21d change +0.02pp, 63d change +0.03pp) after a larger run-up over the past year (+0.27pp over 252d). Breakevens are sitting near the top of their 12-month range (range_position 0.89, only 0.02-0.04pp off the 12m high of 2.18), and are +0.14pp above the 2% target — a modest but persistent overshoot. Policy language describes expectations as "little changed, on balance," consistent with the recent flattening in the short-term data, though the level itself reflects the earlier upward drift. Given the near-term data show stabilization rather than continued acceleration or reversal, and the series has been oscillating in a narrow 2.14-2.16 band for the last several readings, I don't see strong evidence for a directional move in either direction over the next month. The elevated range position argues against further upside being the base case, but there's no sign of a rollover either.
Would change its mind if: A break above 2.18 (new 12m high) would signal renewed uptrend; a drop below 2.10 would confirm a reversal lower.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (flat, 0.30) undershot the direction of travel: the newest print fell to 3.70%, a fresh 12-month low, extending the series' gentle downtrend rather than plateauing at 3.8%. Over the trailing observations the rate has moved from 3.80/3.80 to 3.70, and the 3m/6m/12m changes are all negative (-0.30, -0.30, -0.60 pp), with the 3-month average (3.77%) still above the latest print, confirming continued month-over-month tightening rather than stabilization. The Sahm-style gap is trivial (+0.07pp) and range position is at 0 (sitting at the 12-month low), so there is no evidence of the rapid-rise dynamic that would flag loosening. Distance from an assumed ~4% natural rate is now -0.30pp, indicating the labor market is arguably tighter than normal, not looser. Policy language reinforces this: the updated FOMC statement upgrades activity from "solid" to "strong" and changes "the unemployment rate has declined" to "has stayed low" — consistent with a labor market holding firm at a tight level rather than deteriorating. Given the unbroken low-water mark and no directional inflection, I lean toward continued tightness with unemployment holding near or slightly below 3.70% next month, though the move size is small enough to keep conviction moderate.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 3.8-4.0% would signal the plateau/reversal; continued readings at or below 3.7% confirm ongoing tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y sits at 3.05, near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.93; only 0.77pp above the 12m low, and just 0.06pp below the 12m high of 3.11). Momentum has cooled slightly over the very short run (last two prints 3.06→3.05) after peaking at 3.10, but the broader trend remains firmly up: +0.16pp over 21d, +0.20pp over 63d, and a large +0.74pp over 252d. My prior "flat" call was wrong in direction of magnitude — the breakout above the 2.90-2.95 zone I flagged as the trigger for an upward call has clearly happened and persisted, with yields now consolidating at cycle highs rather than reversing. The 30s10s slope at +0.14pp is modestly positive, consistent with continued long-end pressure rather than curve flattening that would signal a top. With yields pinned near the 12-month high and no sign of a flight-to-quality reversal, the path of least resistance is still a modestly rising or at least persistently elevated term premium. The last two observations show a slight pullback, tempering conviction, but this looks like consolidation near highs rather than a reversal.

Call: flat, conviction 0.35

  The NFCI series has essentially flattened at the front end: the last four readings are all -0.60, and the 4-week change is -0.00, meaning the easing trend that dominated the 13-week window has stalled. The 13-week change of -0.05 confirms conditions did loosen over the past quarter, but nearly all of that move happened earlier in the window — the most recent weeks show no further movement. My prior call was for continued easing (down) with modest conviction; that call is now only partially validated — the level did drift slightly lower earlier, but the latest four readings show stabilization exactly as I flagged as a possible reversal signal. With the index sitting at -0.60, near its 52-week low (-0.62) and well below the zero average, conditions remain loose in absolute terms, but momentum has died. Range position at 0.20 confirms we're near the loose end of the recent band, but the plateau in the last month suggests the easing impulse has exhausted itself for now, absent new drivers. Given no fresh policy language addressing this driver, I see no basis for expecting a fresh leg of easing or a reversal to tightening. The most defensible near-term call is flat, with low-moderate conviction given the flat 4-week trend dominates over the stale 13-week momentum.
Would change its mind if: A move away from -0.60 in either direction over 2+ consecutive weeks, or fresh policy language explicitly addressing credit/financial conditions, would revise this view.

The falsifier from last time (a rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a pause) did not occur. Everything points to continued balance-sheet runoff at a steady pace into next month-end, so I maintain a confident down call, essentially unchanged from before, since the trend is clean and monotonic with no countervailing signal.

The 252-day change of -0.60pp shows the dominant trend over the past year has been aggressive flattening, and the last few weeks are just noise around a low plateau near 0.23-0.27. Policy language shifted to an actual hike (to 2-2.25%) with continued "further gradual increases" guidance, which typically pressures front-end yields up and can keep the curve flattening pressure alive. Given the tiny 21d uptick but negative 63d/252d trends and rock-bottom range position, I don't see convincing steepening evidence. I'll call this closer to flat-to-slightly-down, with low-moderate conviction, acknowledging my prior call missed the continued flattening bias.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.35) was wrong: headline CPI y/y rose from 2.33 to 2.49, not lower, driven by a firm 0.23% m/m print. The latest reading shows headline reaccelerating on a monthly basis (mom rose from 0.21 to 0.23, above the 3m average of 0.21), while the y/y series had dipped to 2.33 two prints ago before bouncing back to 2.49 — the 3m change is still -0.36pp but that's dominated by the earlier dip, not the current trend, which is upward over the last two months. Core PCE is the better persistence read: it's essentially flat/near target at 1.96%, within noise of the 2.00 peak two months back, and core_3m_annualized (1.31%) is soft, suggesting underlying core momentum has cooled even as headline ticked up. This divergence — headline reaccelerating on a monthly basis while core annualized momentum runs below target pace — is genuinely mixed. Policy language shifted to an actual hike (not just hawkish talk), and unchanged commentary still describes inflation as "near 2 percent," consistent with stabilization rather than a clear trend either way. Given headline's recent two-month uptick and mom_gap_vs_outgoing being only slightly negative, I lean toward a modest continuation of headline firmness next print, but conviction is low given core's softness and the whipsaw in recent y/y prints.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.15% combined with core_pce_yoy falling back under 1.9% would confirm deceleration and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous flat call was wrong in direction terms: breakeven_10y has drifted down through my own stated falsifier (a drop below 2.10 confirming reversal lower). The series fell from 2.10-2.11 to a 2.05-2.06 plateau, with be_change_21d -0.09pp and be_change_63d -0.08pp both negative, while the last four prints (2.06, 2.06, 2.05, 2.05) show the decline has stalled and flattened at a new, lower level. The 252-day change is still +0.18pp, so the longer trend remains mildly up, but the recent momentum has clearly reversed and stabilized lower rather than continuing to accelerate downward. Distance from the 2% target is now only +0.05pp, essentially anchored, and range_position of 0.63 shows breakevens are still in the upper half of the 12-month range, not collapsing. Given the plateau over the last four observations, I read this as a completed small step-down that has now leveled off rather than an ongoing decline. That argues for flat over the next month, but with lower conviction than before given I misjudged the prior move — the risk is skewed slightly toward further softness given the recent negative 21d/63d momentum hasn't fully reversed.
Would change its mind if: A move back above 2.09 would confirm reversal higher; a break below 2.03 would confirm continued downtrend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked up to 3.80% from a 12-month low of 3.70%, matching the prior "down" call's falsifier condition of a bounce back into the 3.8-4.0% band. The series has been grinding lower over 12 months (-0.40pp) and even 6 months (-0.20pp), but the most recent two prints (3.70 then 3.80) suggest the tightening trend may be flattening or bottoming rather than continuing. The 3-month change is exactly flat (0.00pp), and the level (3.80%) remains 0.20pp below the natural rate, so the labor market is still tight by any historical standard, but the marginal direction is no longer confirming further tightening. There's no Sahm-rule-style acceleration (gap only +0.07pp, well below the 0.5pp trigger), so this is not a loosening signal yet — just a stall. Policy language still describes "continued strengthening" and low unemployment, with no hint of labor deterioration. Given the flat 3-month trend and the tiny uptick off the cycle low, I read this as a plateau: the rapid tightening phase has likely ended, but there's insufficient evidence of a genuine reversal upward. I'm shifting from "down" to "flat" given the loss of clear downward momentum.
Would change its mind if: A print at or below 3.7% would reconfirm tightening; two consecutive prints above 3.9-4.0% would confirm a genuine loosening reversal.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y yield printed 3.15 in the latest reading, up from 3.12 prior and continuing a modest recovery from the 3.08 trough. My prior "up" call was directionally correct over the 21d and 63d windows: dgs10_change_21d (+0.06pp) and dgs10_change_63d (+0.15pp) both confirm a mild upward drift, though the move has been choppy rather than a clean trend. The yield sits at 0.91 on the 12-month range position — near the top of its range, 0.83pp above the 12-month low and only 0.08pp below the 12-month high — indicating the long end remains elevated relative to its own recent history, consistent with a still-firm term premium. The 30s10s slope (+0.24pp) is positive and not flattening, which argues against a reversal into falling term premium; a genuine flattening/inversion would be needed to signal that the up-move has exhausted itself. The 252d change (+0.78pp) shows the larger trend is still firmly higher over the past year. With no offsetting flight-to-quality signal and policy language on inflation expectations unchanged, I see a continuation of gradual upward drift in the long end, though the recent choppiness (3.08→3.12→3.15) tempers conviction that this is a strong, decisive move rather than range-bound noise near the top of the 12-month band.
Would change its mind if: A clear break below ~2.95 with 30s10s flattening/inverting would confirm the up-move has reversed into a falling term premium.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

  The NFCI series bottomed near -0.60 several weeks ago and has since ticked up for three consecutive readings (-0.59, -0.58, -0.56), lifting the 4-week change to +0.03 while the 13-week change remains essentially flat at -0.01. This is a modest but genuine reversal in momentum from the recent low, consistent with my stated trigger of a sustained move away from -0.60. The index remains deeply negative (-0.56, well below the zero average) and near the loose end of its 52-week range (range_position 0.47, near the middle actually, but up from the low), so conditions are still historically easy overall. However, the direction of travel over the last month is toward tightening, not easing — a small but consistent uptick rather than noise. My prior flat call was reasonable given the plateau at -0.60, but the last three prints show the plateau breaking to the upside. With no policy language to corroborate or contradict, I'll lean toward a continued mild uptick (tightening) into month-end, but conviction is limited since the move is small in magnitude and 13-week trend is still flat.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back toward -0.59/-0.60 over the next 2+ weeks, or policy language reaffirming easy/accommodative financial conditions, would revert this to flat/easing.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

Call: down, conviction 0.72
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

  The latest print (4,173,070) continues the decline from the prior observation (4,175,446), confirming the previous call was correct. Total assets have fallen in 10 of the last 12 weekly observations, with assets_change_4w at -19,839M and assets_change_13w at -104,611M, both consistent in sign and magnitude with an ongoing, steady QT pace. The pace has if anything been remarkably consistent — roughly -5,000M/week on average over the trailing month, not accelerating or decelerating sharply. The level sits well below the 13w average (4,208,519) and far below the 52w high (-285,482M), reinforcing the runoff trend rather than any stabilization. There is no statement language available this period to confirm or contradict a change in the reinvestment/cap policy, so I rely purely on the measurement trend, which is unambiguous: assets are still declining at a steady, moderate pace. Absent any sign of deceleration in the weekly prints or new statement language on tapering the runoff cap, the base case for month-end is a continuation of this gradual decline. This is not an accelerating drawdown, but it is a clear, persistent one.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has been essentially pinned in a narrow 0.26-0.30pp band for the last 10 readings, currently at 0.28pp, matching the prior release. My previous "flat" call is validated: the spread neither pushed above 0.30 nor dropped toward 0.18-0.20. The 21d change is a negligible +0.01pp, though the 63d change is mildly negative (-0.05pp) and the 252d change shows a much larger flattening move (-0.51pp) — consistent with continued gradual Fed tightening (target range raised again this release) compressing the curve over the past year, but that trend has stalled recently. The spread sits at only 0.10pp above its 12-month low and just 17% into its 12-month range (0.18-0.78pp), indicating the curve remains historically flat/compressed with limited room before approaching inversion territory, but no fresh momentum in either direction over the last month. The continued gradual-rate-hike language reinforces the front-end anchor that has driven flattening, but the recent stabilization in the spread suggests this force has plateaued for now rather than accelerating. Absent a break outside the recent 0.26-0.30 micro-range, I see no basis for a directional call this month.
Would change its mind if: A push above 0.30pp with positive 21d/63d changes confirms steepening; a drop below 0.20pp confirms resumed flattening toward inversion.

arbitrated.notes: Cross-checking the seven reports, the picture is one of a Fed still tightening at the margin, a long end drifting modestly higher, and inflation signals genuinely split between headline reacceleration and soft core momentum. On inflation, headline CPI mom/yoy has turned up over two prints while core PCE annualized is soft — I keep a mild up lean but temper it given the core divergence and the analyst's own low conviction and recent miss. Inflation expectations look better described as anchored/flat after a step-down that has now plateaued (four flat prints, distance from target only +5bp), though residual negative 21d/63d momentum keeps me from calling it fully stable — flat with a slight easing skew. Labor tightness has stopped tightening (3m change flat, small bounce off cycle low) but hasn't reversed — genuine plateau, no view on direction, essentially flat. Term premium: 10Y drifting higher (3.08→3.12→3.15), range position near top of 12m band, 30s10s not flattening — a real, if choppy, up move, reinforced by the fact that QT (balance sheet shrinking steadily) is a supply-side tailwind for higher yields even though that analyst wasn't consulted directly on term premium. Financial conditions have inflected off the NFCI trough for three straight prints — modest tightening bias, consistent with the higher-yield story and not contradicted by flight-to-quality. Balance sheet is the highest-conviction call on the panel: steady, unambiguous QT continuation, no deceleration signal. Curve slope is pinned in a micro-range with stalled momentum — no directional edge, flat. Overall the coherent narrative is: continued QT + firming financial conditions + drifting-higher term premium, with inflation and labor data still ambiguous/plateauing rather than clearly turning.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release confirms the down call: headline CPI YoY fell to 2.00% from 2.15%, continuing the descent from the 2.85% peak — now at the 12-month low, with yoy_range_position at 0. The 3m annualized headline pace has collapsed to 0.93%, well below the trailing trend, and headline_yoy_change_3m is -0.33pp, confirming deceleration in headline terms. However, the most recent monthly print (0.07% m/m) rebounded from the prior month's -0.07%, and core PCE actually ticked up to 2.01% YoY, back above target and above my stated flip threshold (0.95%... actually above 1.95%). Core_3m_annualized at 2.13% and core_yoy_change_3m at +0.11pp show core is firming, not decelerating — this is the more persistent read and it argues against further headline declines given core is the leading indicator of underlying trend. The divergence (headline down, core stable-to-up) suggests the headline decline is likely being driven by energy/food base effects rather than broad disinflation. With core sitting almost exactly at the 2% target and reaccelerating slightly, and headline_mom picking up from its dip, I expect headline CPI to stabilize or tick modestly higher next month rather than continue falling. Policy language is unchanged on inflation being "near 2 percent," reinforcing a steady-state read rather than a disinflationary trend.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent headline_mom print below 0.10% combined with core_pce_yoy falling back under 1.95% would confirm broad disinflation resuming.

The 21-day (-0.23pp) and 63-day (-0.43pp) changes both confirm a persistent downtrend, not noise — this is a clear break below my prior downtrend-confirmation threshold of 1.91, so the falsifier condition for a directional call has been met. Market-implied inflation expectations are now 0.29pp below the 2% objective, the widest undershoot in this window, and the trend is still accelerating on the 63-day lookback relative to the 252-day (-0.23pp), suggesting the decline is concentrated in the most recent quarter rather than a stale year-old move. Policy language is stale/backward-looking ("little changed, on balance") and does not reflect this recent slide, so it offers no offsetting signal. With no sign of stabilization in the last three prints (1.74, 1.73, 1.71 — three consecutive lower closes) and breakevens sitting at the floor of their range, the momentum favors continuation or at least no reversal by month end. I'm calling this down with moderate-high conviction, tempered by the fact that a 10-observation series can reverse quickly and I lack information on what is driving the move.
Would change its mind if: A print back above ~1.80 (reversing the recent lows) or flattening of be_change_21d toward zero would undercut the downtrend call.

This continues a pattern: unemployment troughed at 3.70% and has since ticked up two of the last three months (3.70→3.80→3.80→3.90), producing a +0.20pp 3-month change while the 6m and 12m changes remain slightly negative. The Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is now +0.20pp, a mild but non-trivial rise off the cycle low. Range position is 0.50, meaning the rate sits mid-range between the 12m low and high — not extreme, but the trend within the last quarter is clearly upward rather than flat. My prior "flat" call was too sanguine: the print came in at the top of my tolerance band, and the 3m trend shows consistent upward drift rather than noise around a stable level. Policy language still describes the labor market as "continued to strengthen" with unemployment having "declined" over a longer look-back, but that language lags the most recent uptick and doesn't capture the last month's reversal. Given the level is still historically low (near/below the 4% natural-rate anchor) but the short-term momentum has flipped upward for two consecutive prints, I lean toward further mild loosening next month rather than reversion to the 3.70-3.80 lows.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.80% would confirm reversion to the low-range trend; a print above 4.0% would confirm accelerating loosening.

  The 10y has continued to grind lower, closing the run at 2.69% versus 2.81/2.77/2.72 in the prior prints — the previous "down" call was correct and the trend has not reversed. Over 21d and 63d the yield is down 0.37pp, a clear and consistent decline with no sign of stabilization in the last three observations (2.81→2.77→2.72→2.69). The 12m range position sits at 0.35, meaning the yield is now in the lower third of its year's range, well off the 3.24 high and closer to the 2.40 low — consistent with falling term premium rather than reflation/issuance pressure. The 30s10s slope is modestly positive at +0.33pp, not flagging any long-end-specific steepening event that would suggest new term premium building at the very long end. Inflation-expectations language is unchanged/benign, offering no offsetting upward pressure. With momentum, range position, and slope all pointing the same direction, and no measurement showing stabilization yet, I extend the down call, though I temper conviction slightly since six of ten prints are within a tight 2.7-2.86 band and a bounce off recent lows is plausible before month-end.

That said, the index remains solidly negative (-0.42), well below the zero average, meaning conditions are still looser than historical norms even as they tighten toward that average. The policy language shift to "risks roughly balanced" (from presumably a more one-sided framing) is neutral-to-mildly-tightening in tone, consistent with less urgency for easy conditions, and does not contradict the measured trend. Given the persistence and smoothness of the move, and no measurement suggesting stalling or reversal, I expect the tightening trend to continue over the next month, though the magnitude of weekly moves has been modest (~0.01-0.02/week), so this is a continuation call rather than an acceleration call.

The pace of decline (~$8-10B/week in recent weeks) is consistent with prior months, so I expect a similar magnitude of further decline, keeping conviction high but not maximal given the absence of any explicit confirming statement language this period.

Looking at the full series, the curve bottomed around 0.12 mid-series and has since recovered to 0.20-0.21, a modest steepening off the lows. However, the very short-term momentum (21d change -0.04, 63d change -0.02) is mildly negative, suggesting the last few weeks have seen slight give-back after the recovery, even though the latest print ticked up to 0.21. The 252d change of -0.34pp shows the year-over-year trend remains one of substantial flattening, and the spread sits at only 0.15 of its 12-month range — very close to the 12-month low (0.11) and far from the high (0.78). The policy language shift (removing "further gradual" hedging, actually hiking) reflects continued tightening bias, historically a flattening force at the front end, but the last two prints show stabilization rather than fresh flattening. Given the tug between negative short-term momentum and a stable/slightly firmer recent level, I lean toward roughly flat with a slight downward tilt.

arbitrated.notes: Panel paints a fairly coherent late-cycle-tightening picture with one internal tension. Headline CPI is falling sharply but core PCE is firming and reaccelerating slightly above target, so I discount the headline-only "flat" call modestly — the inflation analyst's own evidence (core_3m_annualized 2.13%, core_yoy_change_3m +0.11pp) argues against continued headline declines and toward stabilization, so I keep this near flat with a small upward lean rather than the stated pure "flat." Breakevens have broken to fresh 12m lows with accelerating (63d vs 252d) momentum — this is the freshest, most decisive series on the panel and I weight it heavily; but I temper conviction slightly because it could be partly a term-premium/liquidity artifact (as the panel itself flags) rather than pure inflation-expectation decline. Term premium (10y yield) is falling in a clean, monotonic trend with supporting range position and benign slope — I keep this close to the analyst's own call, though it's plausible flight-to-quality/financial-conditions tightening (which the panel is separately showing) is contributing, which would mean the "term premium" component per se is weaker than the raw yield decline suggests. Financial conditions tightening (NFCI at 52w high, monotonic 13-week uptrend) and balance-sheet runoff (unbroken multi-week decline, on-cap pace) are the cleanest, most mechanical trends here and I have high conviction in both continuing. Labor tightness is a genuine but modest two-month uptick off cycle lows — real but not yet decisive, so modest conviction. Curve slope is genuinely directionless per the analyst's own admission (tug between recovery off lows and negative short momentum) — I keep this near zero.

discounted.why: Analyst explicitly flags no decisive breakout; evidence is balanced both ways.
Headline and core diverge sharply; net signal too muddled for a directional call.

arbitrated.falsifier: Breakevens rebounding above ~1.80, 10y bouncing above 2.85 with steepening 30s10s, or NFCI/balance-sheet trends breaking their monotonic patterns would flip this read.

risks.text: Breakeven decline and term premium decline may be the same flight-to-quality move double-counted as two separate drivers.
Core PCE reacceleration could broaden, forcing the inflation call sharply higher and undercutting the low-breakeven narrative.
Inflation and labor calls are 16-23 days old and may not reflect the most recent data used by fresher analysts.
Several analysts note policy language is stale/backward-looking, so no early warning of a regime shift is captured here.

why.inflation: Headline falling but core PCE reaccelerating above target; net read is stabilization not further decline.
why.inflation_expectations: Fresh 12m low breakeven with accelerating downtrend is decisive, tempered for possible term-premium/liquidity contamination.
why.term_premium: Clean monotonic 10y decline kept, slightly discounted since flight-to-quality/FCI tightening may be co-driving the move.
why.labor_tightness: Two-month uptick off cycle lows is real but modest and still mid-range; kept below analyst's own conviction.
why.financial_conditions: Monotonic 13-week tightening to 52w high with no reversal signal; kept at analyst's conviction, mechanical and clean.
why.balance_sheet: Unbroken multi-week runoff at steady cap-consistent pace, high conviction continuation, minor discount for no confirming statement language.
why.curve_slope: Analyst's own evidence is genuinely tug-of-war between steepening recovery and negative short-term momentum; no clear lean.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "flat" call was wrong: headline CPI y/y dropped sharply from 2.00% to 1.49%, a full percentage point below its 3-month-ago level, driven by the newest headline_mom print of -0.08% and a weak 3-month annualized pace of -0.33%. This looks like energy/food-driven softness given the gap between headline and core (core_minus_headline +0.55pp). Core PCE y/y, the better persistence gauge, actually ticked up to 2.04% and core_3m_annualized is running +2.09%, still right at target with no deceleration signal. My falsifier condition (mom below 0.10% combined with core_pce falling under 1.95%) was only half-met — mom fell well below threshold but core PCE rose, not fell. So underlying inflation is stable-to-firm while headline is being pulled down by volatile components. For the next CPI print, the key question is whether the sharp negative mom readings (-0.07, +0.07, -0.08) persist or reverse; the recent run of soft/negative prints suggests some continuation risk of a soft headline print, but base effects and the stable core trend argue against further steep y/y declines. I lean toward continued softness but with real uncertainty given core's resilience.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print rebounding above 0.20% while core_pce_yoy holds or rises would signal energy-driven weakness reversing, not disinflation.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_minus_headline

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My previous "down" call (conviction 0.60) has been overtaken by the data: breakeven_10y has printed 1.84, 1.85 in the last two readings, the highest of the 10-observation window, and be_change_21d has flipped to +0.14 pp — precisely the reversal condition I flagged as a falsifier. The longer-horizon changes (63d -0.21, 252d -0.24) still show the prior downtrend, but the most recent momentum is clearly upward, with the series bouncing off its 12-month low (1.68) and now at be_range_position 0.34, still well below the 12m high (2.18) and below the 2% target (distance -0.15pp). The policy language explicitly acknowledges market-based inflation compensation "moved lower in recent months" — confirming the committee is describing the trend I had, but this is backward-looking language layered on top of data that has just turned. Given the two-observation uptick is small in absolute terms and the longer trend was down, I don't have high conviction in a sustained reversal, but the near-term evidence (21d change, latest two prints) points modestly higher for the next month. I'm shifting to a lean-up call, tempered by the fact this could easily be noise around a still-low range.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below ~1.80 or be_change_21d turning negative again would undercut this modest up call and revert to down/flat.

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.00% from 3.90%, continuing a climb from the 3.70% trailing 12-month low reached two readings ago. That's a 0.30pp rise off the cycle low and a +0.20pp move over both 3- and 6-month windows (Sahm gap +0.20pp) — a genuine, if still modest, upward drift rather than pure noise. My prior "up" call is validated by this print: unemployment moved above 4.0%, which I had flagged as confirming accelerating loosening. The 12-month change is flat at 0.00pp, so the longer-run trend is still ambiguous, but the recent trajectory (three consecutive higher readings after the 3.70% trough) is the more informative signal per Sahm-rule logic. The level is now right at the ~4% natural rate estimate (distance_from_natural = 0.00pp), so we're not yet in clearly loose territory, but the momentum is up rather than down. Policy language still describes a "strong" labor market with unemployment "remained low," which lags the turn already visible in the data — statement language reflects past strength, not the recent uptick. Given the confirmed break above 4.0% and the persistent short-term rise, I lean toward continued mild loosening next month, though the range remains narrow (3.70-4.10) and a stabilization back to 3.90-4.00 is plausible.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.90% would suggest stabilization; a print at/above 4.10% (new 12m high) would confirm accelerating loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield continues to drift lower: 2.63 latest vs 2.70-2.75 range in prior prints, with 21d change -0.06pp and 63d change -0.45pp both negative, confirming the downtrend intact. The level now sits just 0.07pp above its 12-month low and at only the 10th percentile of its 12-month range (2.56-3.24), indicating the long end is pressing toward the bottom of its recent band rather than rebounding. My prior falsifier (rebound above ~2.85-2.90 with steepening) did not occur — instead yields fell further, so the prior "down" call was directionally correct and should be reaffirmed, not softened. The 30s10s slope at +0.36pp is modestly elevated but not sharply steepening, and 10s2s at +0.18pp shows no acute front-end distortion feeding into the long end. Absent a reversal signal, the momentum, range position, and recent change all point to continued or persistent downward pressure on the term premium into month-end. Conviction is moderate-high given the consistency of the signals, though the pace of decline (21d only -0.06) suggests the move may be decelerating slightly, arguing against extreme conviction.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above ~2.80-2.85 with 30s10s steepening further or 21d momentum flipping clearly positive would signal renewed upward term-premium pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My previous "up" call was wrong: the series had risen from -0.55 to -0.42 over most of the window, but the last three prints reversed sharply, falling from -0.42 back to -0.44, -0.46, -0.48 — exactly the reversal I flagged as a flip condition. The 4-week change is now -0.06 (easing), even though the 13-week change is still +0.08 (net tightening over the longer window). This is a clear break in the monotonic uptrend, with three consecutive weekly declines pulling the index back toward the lower end of its recent range, though nfci_range_position at 0.69 shows it's still above the midpoint of the 52-week band. Conditions remain historically loose in absolute terms (distance from zero -0.48, well below average), but the momentum has clearly shifted toward easing over the last month. Policy language also shifted toward emphasizing patience and muted inflation pressures rather than balanced risks, consistent with a less tightening-oriented backdrop, reinforcing the measurement-based reversal. Given the sharp and consistent 3-week reversal, I now lean toward further easing (lower nfci) into month-end, though I hold only moderate conviction since the longer-run trend was tightening and three weeks is a short base for a new trend call.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above -0.44 over the next couple of prints would signal the uptrend resumed rather than a genuine reversal.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.78
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,047,052) extends the decline from 4,050,044 the prior week, continuing an unbroken runoff trend. The 4-week change (-28,584) and 13-week change (-126,018, -3.02%) both confirm steady, ongoing balance-sheet contraction at a pace consistent with the announced QT cap. Assets remain well below both the 13-week average (4,090,556) and the 52-week high (4,434,863), with no sign of deceleration or plateauing in the weekly cadence — each of the last several prints is lower than the one before. My prior call (down, 0.75) was correct: there was no reversal or leveling off, just continued methodical drawdown. The statement is silent on this driver, so there's no new policy signal to alter the trajectory — reinvestment caps appear to remain in force, and the absence of any language about pausing or tapering runoff means the default assumption of continued balance-sheet shrinkage stands. Given the consistency and multi-week persistence of the decline, plus no evidence of any technical rebound or dovish shift in redemption policy, I maintain a confident down call for month-end, with conviction similar to before, perhaps slightly higher given the trend's persistence and the lack of any offsetting evidence.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The spread has been range-bound near multi-year lows: last two prints at 0.18pp, up slightly from a 0.15-0.16pp cluster a few observations back. My prior "flat" call holds up reasonably well — there was no clear break below 0.15pp, and the recent tick to 0.18 is a mild stabilization/steepening rather than confirmation of renewed flattening. That said, slope_change_21d (-0.03) and slope_change_63d (-0.09) are still negative, showing the medium-term trend remains one of flattening pressure, even as slope_change_252d (-0.35) shows the dominant trend over the past year has been a large flattening move. The curve sits at just 0.10 on the 0-1 range position — very close to its 12-month low (0.11) and far from the high (0.78), confirming we're near the flat end of the range. The policy language shift toward "patient" and holding rates steady (versus prior hiking bias) plausibly reduces near-term upward pressure on front-end yields, which could modestly support steepening if front-end yields ease while long end holds. But the two most recent observations (0.18, 0.18) show stabilization, not a clear new trend. Given the tension between short-term stabilization and longer-term flattening momentum, I lean flat with a slight steepening tilt from the dovish policy shift.
Would change its mind if: A break below 0.13pp would confirm renewed flattening; a sustained push above 0.22pp would confirm steepening trend.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low

arbitrated.notes:
Headline CPI is being pulled down by volatile components while core PCE holds firm at target — a genuine soft-headline/firm-core split, so I give inflation a modest downward lean rather than a strong one, respecting the core resilience the analyst flagged. Inflation expectations have just turned: two prints and a positive 21d change reverse a multi-month downtrend, but the move is small and sits well below the 12m high, so I treat this as a tentative, low-conviction uptick rather than a regime change. Labor tightness shows a real, confirmed break above 4.0% with three consecutive higher prints off the cycle low — Sahm-style momentum is genuinely up, and I keep that call largely intact. Term premium (10y yields) is pressing toward 12-month lows with consistent 21d/63d declines and no steepening reversal — I keep this as a fairly confident down call. Financial conditions reversed sharply over the last three weekly prints after months of tightening; three weeks is a thin base, so I temper conviction versus the analyst's own moderate stance. Balance sheet runoff is mechanical, persistent, and unambiguous — QT continues on cap with no deceleration, so I keep high conviction here. Curve slope is genuinely range-bound with competing signals (stabilizing 21d prints vs. deeply negative 63d/252d momentum near 12m lows); I keep this as a low-conviction, near-flat call with a very slight steepening tilt, since two data points don't overturn a year of flattening momentum.

Overall the picture is: disinflationary headline noise atop sticky core, a fragile expectations bounce, loosening labor, falling long-end yields and easing financial conditions short-term, continued balance sheet shrinkage, and a curve stuck near cycle-low slope. Nothing here is high-confidence except QT and the labor uptick.

discounted.why: Signals conflict too much (recent stabilization vs long-run flattening) for a confident directional view.

arbitrated.falsifier: Core PCE turning down alongside headline, breakevens dropping back below 1.80, or a rebound in 10y above 2.85 with 30s10s steepening would flip this read.

risks.text: Several calls (financial conditions, inflation expectations) rest on very short (2-3 print) reversals that could be noise.
If headline softness feeds into core via lagged pass-through, inflation call could understate downside.
Statement language lags recent data turns (labor, financial conditions), so policy reaction could surprise either direction.

why.inflation_expectations: Two-print bounce and positive 21d change are real but small versus a multi-month downtrend; tempered the 0.35 up call.
why.labor_tightness: Confirmed break above 4.0% with three consecutive higher prints; kept close to analyst's 0.45 up conviction.
why.term_premium: 10y pressing toward 12m lows, consistent 21d/63d declines, no steepening reversal; kept close to analyst's 0.55 down call.
why.financial_conditions: Three-week easing reversal is real but thin base after months of tightening; discounted analyst's 0.45 conviction.
why.balance_sheet: Mechanical, unbroken QT runoff on cap with no deceleration signal; kept close to analyst's high 0.78 conviction.
why.curve_slope: Two stabilizing prints vs deeply negative longer-term momentum; kept low-conviction near-flat with slight steepening tilt.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 13 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct in direction (headline fell to 1.49%) but the most recent print reversed sharply: headline_mom jumped to 0.30%, well above the 3m average of 0.10% and above my stated reversal threshold of 0.20%, pushing headline_yoy back up slightly to 1.52%. This matches the falsifier condition I set previously (mom rebound >0.20%), though core_pce_yoy dipped to 1.84% rather than holding/rising, so the signal is mixed rather than a clean reversal confirmation. Core remains the better persistence read: core_3m_annualized at 1.98% is right at target, and core_yoy_change_3m is essentially flat (-0.03pp), suggesting underlying inflation has stabilized near 2% rather than continuing to decelerate. Headline's prior decline looks like it was driven by transitory (likely energy) softness that is now partially unwinding, per the 0.30% mom print. Policy language changes reinforce this: the Fed shifted from "further gradual increases" language to a "patient" stance citing "muted inflation pressures" and lower market-based inflation compensation — consistent with inflation running near but not above target, not a runaway trend either way. Given the single strong mom print against a stable core trend, I lean toward a modest headline uptick next release, but conviction is limited since one month's mom volatility is noisy and food/energy swings could reverse again.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent headline_mom print back below 0.10% with core_pce_yoy continuing to soften would indicate the 0.30% print was noise, not a turn.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom_3m_avg, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is validated: breakeven_10y rose steadily from 1.85 to 1.95 over the last 10 observations, with be_change_21d at +0.17pp, the strongest short-run momentum reading in this set. The 63-day and 252-day changes remain slightly negative (-0.01 and -0.17pp), showing this is a recent reversal off a longer decline rather than a sustained uptrend, but the last several prints are consistently higher (1.91→1.92→1.92→1.93→1.95), with no down-ticks. Breakeven sits at 1.95%, still 0.05pp below the 2% objective (be_distance_from_target -0.05pp), and at 0.54 of its 12-month range — mid-pack, not stretched. Notably, the policy language shifted to explicitly flag that "market-based measures of inflation compensation have moved lower in recent months" even while survey measures are stable — this is backward-looking language likely referencing the 252-day decline rather than the recent 21-day pop, and suggests the committee's framing lags the latest uptick. Given the clear positive short-term momentum, proximity to target, and lack of any reversal signal, I maintain a modestly bullish stance on breakeven, though the mixed medium-term readings and cautious policy language argue against high conviction.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back toward 1.90 or below, or be_change_21d turning negative, would undercut this up call.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print came in at 3.80%, down from 4.00% the prior month — exactly the kind of stabilization/reversal that would have triggered my stated falsifier. My prior "up" call was wrong: the market did not continue loosening; it snapped back toward the cycle low. Looking at the full series, unemployment has been oscillating in a narrow 3.70-4.00% band for over a year with no trend — 3m and 6m changes are both flat at 0.00pp, and the 12m change is actually -0.30pp, i.e., net tightening over the past year. The Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is only 0.20pp, well below the 0.50pp threshold that would signal a genuine recessionary turn. Range position (0.33) shows the rate sits closer to its 12-month low than high. The level itself (3.80%) is below the ~4% natural rate estimate, indicating a tight labor market by level alone. Policy language continues to describe the labor market as strong/continuing to strengthen, consistent with no loosening signal. Given the noisy, mean-reverting pattern with no directional momentum, I see no basis for calling either further tightening or loosening with confidence — the most defensible call is flat, expecting the rate to hover near 3.80-3.90%.

Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.

The 4-week change (-0.08) is more negative than the 13-week change (-0.06), indicating the pace of easing has picked up recently rather than stalled. Range position (0.20) confirms conditions sit near the loose end of the past year's range, and distance from zero (-0.56) shows conditions are substantially looser than historical average. My prior call of "down" (easing) was correct — the index continued falling from -0.48 to -0.56 over the tracked prints, and the feared rebound above -0.44 did not materialize; instead momentum strengthened toward easier conditions. Policy language shift toward "patience" and citing muted inflation pressures and global developments is consistent with an environment where financial conditions are not tightening — it doesn't signal a hawkish push that would reverse the easing trend. Given the consistent multi-week decline, accelerating 4-week momentum, and no technical reversal signal, I expect the easing trend to persist into next month, likely printing another low or holding near current lows.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above -0.48 over the next couple of prints would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed toward tightening.

Call: down, conviction 0.80

Assets are now 425,838 below the 52-week high, with no sign of stabilization in the recent data — the last few prints show consecutive declines rather than a leveling off. My prior call (down, 0.78) was correct; the trajectory has, if anything, intensified with this latest sharp step-down. There is no statement language this period to signal any change in pace, reinvestment policy, or a cap adjustment, so I have no qualitative input suggesting deceleration or pause. Absent any policy communication indicating a slowing of runoff or a return to reinvestment, the mechanical trend — reserves draining at or above the recent pace — should continue into month-end. The lack of statement commentary is itself uninformative here since the measured trend is unambiguous and consistent with an ongoing, undisrupted QT program.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.

Call: up, conviction 0.45

My prior "flat" call was too cautious given the 21d change was already +0.05pp; the trend has now continued and strengthened, closing in on the upper end of my stated steepening trigger (0.22pp). The spread remains historically low in absolute terms (range position 0.19, near the 12-month low of 0.11) and still only 0.10pp above that low, so this is a modest bear-steepening within a still-inverted-adjacent range, not a regime shift. But the short-term momentum (21d +0.05pp) has overtaken the medium-term drift (63d -0.02pp, 252d -0.42pp), suggesting the multi-quarter flattening trend has paused or reversed at the margin. The policy language shift toward "patient" on further hikes (removing the "further gradual increases" language) is consistent with markets reassessing the front-end path, which mechanically supports some steepening if front-end yields stabilize while long end holds. Given the spread is now essentially at my steepening threshold and momentum is positive, I lean toward continued modest steepening next month, though conviction is moderate given how compressed the range still is and how quickly this could reverse.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.16pp would signal the steepening move has failed and flattening trend has resumed.

arbitrated.notes: The panel points to a coherent, if moderate-conviction, macro picture: easing financial conditions and accelerating balance-sheet runoff coexisting with a modest firming in inflation and inflation expectations, and a tentative bear-steepening of the curve. Balance sheet is the highest-conviction call — the runoff trend is mechanical, accelerating, and unchallenged by any offsetting signal, so I keep that near full strength. Financial conditions show a clean, accelerating easing trend with no reversal signal and policy language consistent with continued ease, so I keep that call strong as well, though slightly below balance sheet given it's a softer, more sentiment-driven index. Inflation and inflation expectations both lean up but on thinner evidence: the CPI mom reversal is a single print against a still-stable core trend, and breakeven momentum is a recent pop against negative medium/long-term drift — both plausible but not yet confirmed trends, so I moderate conviction versus the analysts' own numbers. Term premium is trickier: the underlying 63d/252d trend is still falling, and the recent uptick in 10y yields is marginal and within a still-low range, so I lean toward maintaining a decline rather than adopting the analyst's flip, effectively softening the "down" call in strength while acknowledging the risk it's turning. Curve slope shows real, strengthening short-term steepening momentum overtaking medium-term flattening, but it's happening from a historically compressed level and could reverse quickly — I keep this as a moderate lean-up. Labor tightness has no clear trend — a genuinely noisy, mean-reverting series — so flat with low conviction is the only defensible read, and I keep it there. Overall this is a "conditions easing while price pressures firm at the margin" regime, consistent with a Fed that has shifted to patience but where underlying growth/inflation dynamics are not yet decisively turning.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: after the 0.30% mom print, the newest headline_mom rose further to 0.38%, and headline_cpi_yoy did tick up from 1.52 to 1.88 — so directionally the "up" call was actually validated this round, not falsified. Looking at the latest data: headline_yoy jumped 0.36pp to 1.88%, driven by two consecutive strong mom prints (0.30, 0.38) versus the prior soft/negative readings. The 3m annualized headline (2.41%) now sits above the current yoy (1.88%), implying base effects and recent momentum are pushing yoy higher next month too. However, core_pce_yoy is telling a different story — it fell to 1.74%, the lowest in the sample, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.27pp, and core_3m_annualized at just 1.60%, well below target. Core is the better read on persistence, and it's decelerating even as headline gets a temporary lift from energy/food-type volatility (mom swung from -0.08 to +0.38, a food/energy-typical pattern). Policy language shifted dovish ("patient," "muted inflation pressures"), consistent with underlying softness. Given headline's own momentum (two strong consecutive mom prints, 3m annualized above trailing yoy), I lean toward another modest headline increase next month, but conviction is tempered by core's clear softening trend and my recent miss on calibration.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print below 0.10% next release, especially alongside continued core_pce_yoy softening, would signal the recent two-month pop was transitory noise.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was wrong: breakeven_10y fell from 1.96 to a low of 1.83 before a small bounce to 1.88 in the latest print. That decline satisfied my own stated falsifier (21d change turning negative, reversal near/below 1.90). The 21-day change is -0.07pp and the 252-day change is -0.20pp, both negative, though the 63-day change of +0.14pp shows the decline is not monotonic and there was a bounce in the last observation. Breakeven sits 0.12pp below the 2% objective, in the lower-middle of its 12-month range (0.40 range position), well off the 12-month high of 2.18 and only 0.20pp above the 12-month low. Policy language changed from describing inflation compensation as having "moved lower in recent months" to "remained low in recent months" — a subtle shift from active declining to a stabilized-at-a-lower-level characterization, which is consistent with the recent uptick from 1.83 to 1.88. This suggests the sharp downtrend may be leveling off rather than continuing to fall, but there's not yet clear evidence of a sustained reversal upward. Given the one-month bounce plus the softer "remained low" language, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up, but with the medium-term trend still negative, conviction is modest.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.85 or be_change_21d turning sharply more negative would undercut this flat/stabilizing call; a push above 1.95 would confirm reversal up.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 3.80%, matching the prior release, so the "flat" call was correct — the earlier tick to 4.00% proved a blip, not a trend, as the series reverted straight back down. Looking at the full run, unemployment has been oscillating narrowly between 3.70 and 4.00 for over a year with no directional drift: 3m change is -0.10pp, 6m change is +0.10pp, and 12m change is -0.20pp — these roughly cancel, indicating noise around a flat trend rather than a building move. The Sahm-style gap (0.17pp above the 12m low) remains well under the ~0.5pp threshold that would flag a genuine loosening turn, and the range position (0.33) shows the rate sitting in the lower-middle of its recent band, not at an extreme. The level itself (3.80%) is comfortably below the ~4% natural-rate anchor, consistent with a still-tight labor market. Fed language continues to describe the labor market as having "continued to strengthen" with "strong labor market conditions," reinforcing that policymakers see no loosening signal. With no rapid-rise pattern and the rate sitting at a 12-month-average level, I expect the unemployment rate to stay near 3.80% at the next report.
Would change its mind if: A print at/above 4.00% (new local high, sahm_gap approaching 0.5pp) would revive the loosening case; a print below 3.70% would confirm continued tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield has continued to grind lower and now sits at 2.41, essentially at the 12-month low (2.39) with range_position at just 0.02 — i.e., the long end is pinned near its cheapest-yield extreme of the past year. The prior call (down, 0.35) was directionally correct: 21d, 63d, and 252d changes are all negative (-0.32, -0.36, -0.44 pp), confirming a sustained decline rather than a reversal. However, the pace of decline has decelerated markedly over the last few prints (2.39→2.39→2.41), suggesting the move may be stalling near this low. The 30s10s slope remains modestly positive at +0.40pp, showing no steepening pressure that would signal rising long-end term premium; if anything a flat-to-slightly-curved long end is consistent with continued grinding lower or stabilization. Policy language shift — market-based inflation compensation "remained low" (vs. previously "moved lower") — suggests less further downward drift in that channel, which could mean the recent decline in yields is losing a source of downward pull. Given the deceleration and proximity to the 12-month low (little room to fall further without new catalysts), I lean toward continued softness but at reduced conviction versus last cycle, with a real chance of flat/stabilization.
Would change its mind if: A move back above ~2.55-2.60 with positive 21d momentum and steepening 30s10s would signal the downtrend has reversed into rising term premium.

Report:
  The prior call (down/easing) was correct in direction: NFCI has continued to fall to -0.60, its 52-week low, from -0.59 previously. However, momentum has clearly decelerated sharply — nfci_change_4w is now just -0.02, and the last three prints (-0.59, -0.60, -0.60) show conditions essentially flatlining after a long easing run. The 13-week change of -0.18 reflects the earlier trend, not the current pace. Range position sits at 0.01, meaning we're pinned at the bottom of the 52-week range with no sign of a bounce, but also no sign of continued acceleration lower. With conditions already deeply negative (-0.60, well below the zero average) and the rate of easing having stalled to near-zero over the last month, the most defensible near-term call is that the index holds near current levels rather than extending materially further, absent new easing impulses. This is a plateau, not a reversal — I see no evidence of tightening momentum, just exhaustion of the easing trend.
Would change its mind if: A print moving decisively away from -0.60 in either direction — below -0.63 would signal renewed easing, above -0.55 would signal tightening.

Call: down, conviction 0.80

  The balance sheet continues its steady runoff. The latest print (3,955,617) is below the prior (3,962,748), extending an unbroken decline over the trailing observations. The 4-week change (-18,973M) and 13-week change (-120,019M, -2.94%) both confirm continued contraction at a pace consistent with ongoing QT operating under the monthly cap. Total assets are now 430,487M below the 52-week high, with no sign of stabilization or reversal in the recent weekly prints. My prior call (down, 0.80) was correct: assets have kept falling every week since, with no clean multi-week rise back above the 13-week average — the level sits firmly below it (3,955,617 vs 4,009,351 average). The statement is silent on this driver this release, meaning there is no fresh policy language confirming or denying a pause in runoff; absent such confirmation, the base case remains continuation of the existing cap-driven runoff pace. Given the consistency and persistence of the decline across all horizons (4w, 13w, 52w), I maintain a high-conviction down call for month end. Nothing in the recent data suggests deceleration or a shift to reinvestment; the trend is monotonic and mechanical, matching a steady-state QT process.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has slipped back to 0.14pp after briefly touching 0.17pp for three readings, confirming that the steepening attempt I flagged last time has failed. My prior falsifier condition (a drop back below 0.16pp) has been triggered, so I was wrong to lean toward further steepening. The spread now sits near the very bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.07, only 0.03pp above the 12-month low of 0.11pp), and both the 21-day and 63-day changes are negative (-0.07pp each), while the 252-day change is sharply negative (-0.38pp), showing a persistent multi-month flattening trend that a brief uptick did not reverse. The curve remains historically compressed at just 0.14pp from flat inversion territory. The 10s30s slope at 0.40pp is comparatively steep, suggesting the flattening pressure is concentrated in the front-to-belly part of the curve rather than being a uniform curve-wide phenomenon, but that's outside my mandate. Given the reversal of the brief steepening pulse and the return to range lows, I now lean toward continued flattening or at best stabilization near these depressed levels, rather than renewed steepening.
Would change its mind if: A move back above 0.17pp sustained for several readings would signal the steepening attempt has resumed and change my call.
