arbitrated.notes: Headline inflation is getting a two-month lift (mom 0.30→0.38, yoy up to 1.88%) that pushes 3m annualized above trailing yoy, but core PCE is decelerating (1.74% yoy, 3m annualized 1.60%) and dovish Fed language corroborates the core story. I read this as headline noise atop a still-soft underlying trend, so I keep a mild "up" lean on headline inflation but temper it, recognizing the analyst's own recent miscalibration. Inflation expectations look to have stopped falling (breakeven bounced 1.83→1.88, statement language softened from "moved lower" to "remained low") but the medium-term trend (63d/252d) is still negative and the bounce is one print — flat with a slight upward tilt is the honest read, low conviction. Labor tightness shows genuine stability: unemployment sitting at 3.80%, oscillating in a tight band for over a year, Sahm gap well below threshold — flat is well supported. Term premium (10y yield) has ground lower but decelerated sharply near the 12-month low; little room left without a new catalyst, so I lean down but modestly, expecting stabilization risk. Financial conditions have plateaued at the 52-week low with 4-week momentum near zero — flat is the right call, not further easing. Balance sheet runoff is mechanical, monotonic, and well-evidenced across all horizons — high conviction down (i.e., balance sheet level falling) continues. Curve slope (2s10s) has resumed flattening after a failed steepening attempt, sitting near 12-month lows with negative momentum across all windows — down is well supported. Overall: no acceleration case anywhere except the transitory headline CPI pop, which I discount given core softness; the dominant forces are QT-driven balance sheet decline, curve flattening, and financial conditions plateauing near easy extremes.

discounted.why: Own deceleration evidence undercuts the 'down' call more than the analyst credits; treated as low-conviction, near-flat.

arbitrated.falsifier: A headline CPI mom print below 0.10% with continued core softening, plus a 10y yield break above 2.55-2.60 with steepening 30s10s, would flip this read toward disinflation-with-rising-term-premium.

risks.text: Headline CPI pop could prove more durable than core suggests, forcing a reassessment of the inflation view.
Breakeven bounce could extend into a genuine reversal, undercutting the flat inflation-expectations call.
A new issuance or risk-off catalyst could snap term premium higher despite recent deceleration, reversing the down lean.
An unannounced pause or slowdown in balance-sheet runoff would immediately break the high-conviction down call.

why.inflation: Headline momentum (two strong mom prints, 3m annualized above yoy) supports modest up lean, but core softness caps conviction below analyst's own 0.40.
why.inflation_expectations: One-print bounce and softened language suggest stabilization, but medium-term trend still negative; kept near flat.
why.labor_tightness: Rate reverted straight back to 3.80%, oscillating band for a year, Sahm gap far below threshold — genuinely flat, no directional view.
why.term_premium: Decline decelerated sharply near 12-month low; some further softness likely but stabilization risk high, so trimmed conviction.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI plateaued at 52-week low with near-zero 4-week momentum; easing trend has exhausted itself, call as flat.
why.balance_sheet: Monotonic, mechanical QT runoff confirmed across 4w/13w/52w horizons with no policy language suggesting pause; kept near analyst's high conviction.
why.curve_slope: Failed steepening attempt reversed; spread near 12-month low with negative momentum across all windows — flattening resumes.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "up" call was borne out: headline_mom printed 0.38% again (not below 0.10%), keeping the recent pop intact and pushing headline_cpi_yoy from 1.88 to 2.00. Two consecutive 0.38% m/m prints put the 3m annualized headline pace at 4.30%, well above the prevailing y/y rate, so the momentum signal still points up for the next release unless there's a sharp deceleration. However, core_pce_yoy continues to soften (1.74 → 1.62), a third straight monthly decline, and core is falling further from target (-0.38pp), which is the more persistent signal per mandate. The core-headline divergence is now stark: headline momentum is accelerating on presumably energy/food-driven components while core underlying trend decelerates. Policy language confirms this pattern explicitly, attributing headline weakness/decline previously to energy and noting core stays near 2%. Given headline_mom_3m_avg (0.35) is running hot and above outgoing_mom, the base effects favor another firm print, likely keeping headline_cpi_yoy flat-to-up. But since core deceleration is the more forward-looking read on persistent inflation, I lower conviction versus mechanically extrapolating headline momentum alone — this is a headline-driven, not broad-based, upward pressure.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print below 0.15% next release, especially with core_pce_yoy stabilizing or rising, would confirm the recent pop is fading and momentum has normalized.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom_3m_avg

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print of 1.95 sits within a tight 1.94-1.98 band over the past 10 readings, essentially flat and consistent with my prior call. My stated trigger for confirming a reversal up ("push above 1.95") was briefly touched (1.96-1.98 mid-series) before settling back to 1.95, so that call held reasonably well — this was a genuine stabilization, not a clean breakout. The 21d (+0.07) and 63d (+0.17) changes show mild upward drift over the medium term, but the 252d change (-0.22) shows the longer trend is still down, and breakeven remains 0.05pp below the 2% objective — essentially at target, arguing against a strong directional call either way. The policy language shift is subtle: "have moved lower" becomes "have remained low," which reads as the Committee now framing low breakevens as a steady-state rather than a recent decline — arguably a slightly less dovish/more stable characterization, not a signal of building inflation expectations. Range position (0.54) is mid-pack, not extreme. Given the flat recent print, offsetting short vs long-term trends, and language suggesting stabilization rather than reversal, I maintain a flat call, marginally firmer than last time given the confirmed stabilization near 1.95-1.96.
Would change its mind if: A break below 1.90 or above 2.00 sustained for several readings, or be_change_21d turning sharply negative/positive, would overturn this flat call.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 3.70%, a new 12-month low, down from 3.80% prior. My previous flat call was reasonably accurate in spirit—the rate had been range-bound between 3.70-4.00%—but the newest reading confirms the labor market tilted back toward tightening rather than staying flat. The 3m change (-0.30pp) and 12m change (-0.30pp) both show renewed downward momentum after a mid-series wobble up to 4.00%. Sahm gap is trivial (+0.07pp), nowhere near recessionary signal territory. Range position at 0.00 (sitting at the 12m low) and distance from natural rate at -0.30pp both indicate a historically tight labor market with no loosening signal at all. The unemployment rate has been noisy but essentially oscillating in a tight 0.30pp band, and this month's move is to the tight edge of that band, not the loose edge. Policy language confirms this: labor market described as "strong," unemployment "remained low," job gains "solid," even as growth commentary softened slightly. There's no evidence here of loosening—if anything the tightening case modestly strengthened. Given the historically narrow band, I don't expect much further downside next month, but the current level and trajectory argue against calling this flat-to-up; I'll lean toward continued tightness with a modest expectation of a slight uptick or flat next print given mean-reversion within the band, but the near-term signal remains tight, not loosening.
Would change its mind if: A print at/above 3.90% (reversing toward range top, sahm_gap rising toward 0.3-0.5pp) would revive the loosening case; a print at/below 3.60% would confirm further tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield closed the last print at 2.51%, down from the recent bounce to 2.54-2.59% but still well within the low end of its 12-month range (range_position 0.14, only 0.12pp above the 12m low of 2.39%). The 63d and 252d changes remain sharply negative (-0.21pp, -0.52pp), confirming the dominant multi-month trend has been falling long-end yields/term premium. However, the 21d change is +0.10pp, and the series shows a choppy pattern (2.60→2.51 with a bounce to 2.59 mid-window) rather than a clean reversal. My prior falsifier (sustained move above ~2.55-2.60 with positive momentum and steepening 30s10s) was partially met intraperiod but the level has since pulled back to 2.51, the cycle low in this window, so the reversal did not hold. The 30s10s slope at 0.42pp is elevated but not clearly trending in either direction based on single-point data. Policy language shift is a minor rewording, not clearly signaling a shift toward higher long-end term premium; it reflects the inflation-expectations driver, outside my scope. On balance, the data still favor continued low/falling long-end premium, with the previous down call vindicated by the pullback to 2.51, though the choppy 21d bounce keeps conviction modest.
Would change its mind if: A sustained close above ~2.60 with positive 21d momentum and a steepening 30s10s slope would signal the downtrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has essentially flattened out at the bottom of its recent range: the last three prints are all -0.61, following a long, decelerating slide from -0.50 thirteen weeks ago. The 4-week change is now just -0.01, versus -0.13 over 13 weeks, showing the easing trend has lost almost all momentum and is bumping against the 52-week low with range_position at 0.00. My prior flat call (with the -0.63/-0.55 bands) was correct in that the print did not break either threshold — it settled right at -0.61, within the flat zone. Conditions remain well below the zero average, meaning financial conditions are still loose/easy in absolute terms, but the pace of further easing has stalled. There's no policy language shift here to suggest a new impulse either tightening or easing. Given the index is sitting flat at its floor for three consecutive readings, the most defensible call for month-end is continued flatness near -0.61, with only a marginal risk of a slight further drift down given the still-negative (if tiny) 4-week change. I'll maintain flat with slightly higher conviction than before given the confirmed stabilization.
Would change its mind if: A print below -0.63 would signal renewed easing; a print above -0.55 would signal a tightening reversal.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low, nfci

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.78
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (3,928,273) confirms continued runoff, extending the series' steady decline. The 4-week change (-27,344) and 13-week change (-118,779) both show ongoing balance sheet shrinkage at a pace consistent with prior months — roughly $25-40B/month, in line with an active QT cap. There is no evidence of deceleration: the newest value sits well below the 13-week average (3,972,455) and far below the 52-week high, with the gap to that high still widening (-429,934). My prior call of "down" at 0.80 conviction was correct; the trend has not reversed or paused. No statement language is available this release to signal any change in reinvestment policy or caps, so I rely purely on the measured trajectory, which remains unambiguously downward. Absent any sign of stabilization — such as a flattening 4-week change or a print holding above the 13-week average — the mechanical trend of steady, roughly linear asset drawdown should persist into next month-end. I maintain a confident down call, keeping conviction high but slightly below max given the lack of fresh policy language this period to confirm the cap is unchanged.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was wrong: the spread has continued to grind higher, not lower. The 10-observation series shows a steady, if gradual, steepening from 0.19pp to 0.24pp, with slope_change_21d (+0.10pp) and slope_change_63d (+0.08pp) both positive, confirming the near-term trend is up. The longer 252-day change (-0.30pp) shows the curve is still much flatter than a year ago, and slope_range_position at 0.30 confirms the spread sits in the lower third of its 12-month range (0.11 to 0.54pp) — so this remains a historically flat curve, just one that has stopped flattening and is inching back toward normal. The move above my prior 0.17pp threshold for several consecutive readings triggers my own stated reversal condition. The 10s30s slope at 0.42pp, well above 2s10s, suggests the front end is the pinch point while the long end retains more term premium — consistent with a Fed on hold ("patient") rather than tightening aggressively, which removes upward pressure on front-end yields and allows gradual re-steepening. With only 10 small increments and low absolute range width, I don't see a dramatic move, but the direction is consistently up.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.19pp sustained for multiple readings, or a resumption of negative 21d/63d changes, would signal renewed flattening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  balance_sheet lacks financial_conditions — Would clarify if funding market stress is forcing an early pause in runoff pace.
  curve_slope lacks financial_conditions — Would clarify whether easier conditions are reinforcing the front-end anchoring behind this steepening.
  curve_slope lacks term_premium — Would confirm whether long-end steepening reflects rising term premium vs. growth expectations.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.45) was wrong: the newest headline_mom printed 0.02%, well below my own 0.15% falsifier threshold, and headline_yoy fell from 2.00 to 1.80. The two strong 0.38% prints that drove that prior "up" call look like a transitory pop rather than a new trend, and the latest data confirms it fading sharply. Core PCE also continues to soften (1.62→1.64, down from a 2.04 peak three prints back), with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.21pp confirming decelerating underlying momentum, and core sitting 0.36pp below target. The 3m annualized core rate (+1.54%) is comfortably below the 12-month yoy print, reinforcing that the recent trend is disinflationary. Policy language corroborates this, now stating both headline and core "have declined and are running below 2 percent" — an explicit downgrade from the prior "core remains near 2 percent" language. Given the very weak outgoing_mom (0.02%) and softening core trend, I expect next month's headline yoy to drift lower or at best hold near current levels, not push back toward 2%. Base effects could cause some noise, but the momentum and policy language both point down.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom rebound above 0.25% next release combined with core_pce_yoy stabilizing or rising would suggest disinflation has stalled and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been overtaken by events: the 21d change is now -0.19pp and 63d -0.22pp, with the series drifting from ~1.81 down to ~1.74 over the last ten readings. This is exactly the kind of sharply negative 21d move I said would overturn a flat call, so I was wrong to hold flat last time — the drift is now consistent enough to call directional. Breakeven sits near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.12, only 6bp above the 12m low of 1.68) and 26bp below the 2% objective, having widened its distance from target over the past year (252d change -0.35pp). The last few prints (1.76, 1.73, 1.75, 1.74) show the decline decelerating and possibly stabilizing just above the 12m low, which tempers conviction that the slide continues sharply lower. Policy language notes market-based measures "have remained low," consistent with no near-term catalyst for a reversal. Given the loss of downward momentum in the very latest observations but the still-negative multi-horizon trend and proximity to lows, I lean toward continued softness rather than a rebound, but not with high confidence given signs of flattening.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above 1.80 sustained for several readings, or be_change_21d turning positive, would overturn this soft-drift call.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 3.60%, the trailing 12-month low, down from 3.70% prior and continuing a run that had been oscillating in a narrow 3.70-4.00% band. My prior "flat" call was too cautious in retrospect — this print confirms further tightening rather than stabilization, meeting the threshold I set (at/below 3.60%) for revising toward continued tightening. The 3m/6m/12m changes all show -0.20pp, and the Sahm gap is a negligible +0.10pp, indicating no loosening signal whatsoever. Distance from the natural rate is now -0.40pp, meaning the market is running notably tighter than a 4% anchor. Policy language reinforces this: the updated FOMC statement upgrades the growth assessment from "slowed" to "rose at a solid rate" while maintaining "labor market remains strong" and "unemployment rate has remained low," consistent with a market that is if anything tightening further rather than turning. Given the level is already at a cycle low and momentum remains modestly negative (falling rate), the most likely next print is either another low reading or a flat hold near 3.60-3.70%, with genuine downside risk to unemployment given the momentum, but mean-reversion off multi-year lows also plausible. I lean toward a modest further tightening or flat outcome, not a reversal.
Would change its mind if: A print at/above 3.80% (back toward range top, sahm_gap rising above 0.3pp) would signal a reversal toward loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: 10y fell further, from 2.22 to 2.14, sitting at the very bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.00, at the 12m low). Momentum remains firmly negative across all horizons (-0.38pp/21d, -0.62pp/63d, -0.79pp/252d), with no sign of stabilization in the last three prints (2.25→2.22→2.14). The 30s10s slope at 0.44pp is modest and not signaling any acute long-end steepening pressure from issuance/supply concerns; if anything the persistent grind lower suggests continued demand for duration/flight-to-quality dynamics dominating over any term-premium-widening forces. There's no policy language suggesting inflation expectations are driving this (they're described as low and stable), which is consistent with the move being a term-premium/duration-demand story rather than an inflation-compensation story. Given the yield is pinned at its 12-month low with unabated negative momentum, the path of least resistance is still lower into month-end, though the pace of decline could moderate given how compressed the level already is relative to the range floor. I'll maintain a "down" call but only modestly firmer than before given the yield is now testing fresh lows with limited room before mean-reversion risk increases.
Would change its mind if: A sustained close above ~2.60 with positive 21d momentum and a steepening 30s10s slope would signal the downtrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series bottomed around -0.61 several readings ago and has since risen steadily to -0.58, the highest level in this 13-observation window. The 4-week change is +0.03 (tightening momentum), while the 13-week change is essentially flat at -0.01, indicating the move is recent and has accelerated over the last month rather than being a longer trend. Conditions remain deeply below the zero average (-0.58 distance from zero), so this is still an accommodative financial-conditions regime in absolute terms, but the direction of travel has clearly shifted from easing to modest tightening. My prior flat call is now looking wrong: the latest print (-0.58) breaches my own stated threshold of -0.55 only partially, but the consistent multi-period uptick from -0.61 to -0.58 is a genuine reversal signal, not just noise. Range position (0.14 of 52-week range) confirms we're still near the loose end historically, but the momentum over the last four readings is unambiguously upward. Given the string of consecutive increases (-0.60, -0.59, -0.58) after a long flat/declining stretch, I lean toward continued modest tightening into next month, though conviction is capped by the fact we're not yet near the upper threshold and absolute level remains very loose.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below -0.60 would signal the recent uptick was noise and easing has resumed; a print above -0.55 would confirm firmer tightening.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.80
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest observation (3,851,444) continues the steady decline from the prior series, confirming the previous down call was correct. Total assets fell ~$9.0B in the last week alone, and the 4-week change (-38,247) and 13-week change (-123,146, -3.10%) both show consistent, uninterrupted runoff. There is no sign of deceleration: each of the last several prints is at or near new cycle lows, well below the 13w average (3,919,230) and 473,462 below the 52w high. The pace of decline (~$30B/month over the recent stretch, consistent with roughly the announced monthly cap) shows no inflection. With no statement language provided this period to signal any change in reinvestment policy or cap adjustment, the base case is simple extrapolation of the current trend: continued balance sheet runoff into month-end. The absence of statement commentary is itself unremarkable here — it does not indicate a pause, and the measurement trend is unambiguous and monotonic across all recent windows (4w, 13w, 52w all negative). I maintain a high-conviction down call, slightly tempered from before only because the missing statement language removes one confirming data point I'd normally weight.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread ended the series at 0.19pp, matching the prior peak in the sequence, after dipping to 0.14 mid-series. The last print shows a bounce (0.16→0.19), but the short-run trend measures remain net negative: slope_change_21d and slope_change_63d are both -0.02pp, and slope_change_252d is a much larger -0.26pp, indicating the year-long trend has been toward flattening even though the very latest tick ticked up. My prior "up" call was only partially vindicated — the spread did rise off its low, but it merely returned to a level already seen two readings ago rather than breaking out, and the 21d/63d changes are still negative, which was my stated falsifier condition for abandoning the bullish-steepening view. The spread sits at only 0.24 of its 12-month range (very close to the low), and remains just 0.08pp above the 12-month low and 0.19pp away from flat — still deeply compressed. This is a choppy, range-bound pattern within a longer flattening trend, not a clear steepening. Given the negative 21d/63d changes persist despite the single-point uptick, I'm downgrading conviction in "up" and leaning toward a more neutral-to-cautious stance, since the evidence is genuinely mixed: last-point bounce vs. persistent negative medium-term trend.
Would change its mind if: A firm break above 0.20pp sustained for 2+ readings with 21d/63d changes turning positive would confirm renewed steepening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of QT/runoff changes would clarify supply pressure on long-end term premium.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-off/flight-to-quality signals would confirm whether duration demand persists.
  balance_sheet lacks financial_conditions — Statement language on reinvestment/caps would confirm whether runoff pace is intentionally steady or nearing adjustment.

arbitrated.notes: The panel points to a coherent late-cycle-disinflation, easing-financial-backdrop picture, with a couple of genuine cross-currents. Inflation: headline and core are both decelerating (weak outgoing MoM, softer core PCE trend, dovish policy language shift), so I keep the "down" lean but temper it slightly given base-effect noise risk. Inflation expectations: multi-horizon breakeven momentum is negative and breakevens sit near 12m lows, but the very latest prints show deceleration/stabilization, so I take a modest down lean, not a strong one. Labor tightness: unemployment at a cycle low with negative 3/6/12m changes and a negligible Sahm gap is unambiguous — this is tightening, not flat, so I override the analyst's own "flat" label and read it as a fall in the unemployment rate (i.e., tightening labor market), which for my "headline measurement rises" convention on labor_tightness (the rate) means down. Term premium: 10y yield pinned at 12-month lows with negative momentum across all horizons — clear down call, undiminished by the missing balance-sheet/FCI context since the yield action is unambiguous on its own terms. Financial conditions: NFCI has turned up off its trough for four straight readings, a genuine if early inflection toward tightening — I keep this as a modest up lean. Balance sheet: runoff is monotonic across 4w/13w/52w windows with no deceleration — high-conviction down call. Curve slope: the medium-term trend (21d/63d) is still flattening despite a single-point bounce; I keep this close to flat/no-view, tilting only very slightly negative. Overall this is a picture of softening inflation and inflation expectations, a tightening labor market, falling long yields and balance sheet, but a modest early tightening in broader financial conditions — an unusual but plausible combination in a still-accommodative regime that is just beginning to firm.

discounted.why: Mixed signal: latest bounce vs. negative medium-term trend leaves genuinely low-conviction, near-flat view.

arbitrated.falsifier: A rebound in headline CPI/core PCE, breakevens back above 1.80, unemployment back to 3.80%+, 10y closing above 2.60%, or NFCI dropping back below -0.60 would flip this read.

risks.text: Single-month CPI/NFCI/curve prints could reverse on noise given how compressed several series are near range extremes.
Tightening labor market and softening inflation is an unusual combination that could resolve either direction next print.
Term premium and balance sheet calls lack cross-driver context (QT pace, risk-off signals) that could sharpen or contradict the read.

why.inflation_expectations: Negative multi-horizon breakeven momentum near 12m lows, but latest prints show deceleration, so only modest downside lean.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment rate at cycle low with negative 3/6/12m changes and negligible Sahm gap signals falling unemployment rate, i.e. tightening.
why.term_premium: 10y yield at 12-month low with unabated negative momentum across all horizons; clear down call despite missing supply/FCI context.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI has risen for four consecutive readings off its trough, a genuine if early tightening inflection despite still-loose absolute level.
why.balance_sheet: Monotonic runoff across 4w/13w/52w windows with no deceleration signal; high-conviction continued decline.
why.curve_slope: Single-point bounce contradicted by persistently negative 21d/63d trend; leaning flat-to-slightly-flatter, low conviction.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "down" call was correct: headline_cpi_yoy fell from 1.80 to 1.67, and the newest monthly print (-0.03% m/m) was the softest in the series, well below the 0.25% threshold I flagged as a reversal trigger. Core PCE also continued its slide, from 1.64 to 1.56, extending a clear downtrend over the last several months (1.84→1.74→1.62→1.64→1.56). Both headline and core 3-month annualized rates (1.48% and 1.59%) sit comfortably below the 12-month y/y levels and below 2%, confirming momentum is disinflationary, not just base effects. The policy language shift is notable: it now states both headline and core inflation "have declined and are running below 2 percent," upgraded from the prior statement that only flagged headline weakness while core was "near 2 percent" — this is a clear acknowledgment of broadening softness. With mom_gap_vs_outgoing only +0.04pp and the latest print negative, there is no sign of a rebound. I see no evidence of stalling disinflation; the trend remains intact and if anything has reasserted itself after a couple of stronger prints mid-series. I'll call another modest step down for headline CPI y/y next release.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print above 0.25% combined with core_pce_yoy stabilizing or rising would suggest disinflation has stalled and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call played out further: breakeven_10y has drifted from ~1.74 to 1.69 over the recent window, with be_change_21d at -0.05pp and be_change_63d at -0.19pp, both negative, confirming the soft-drift-lower thesis rather than overturning it. The last three prints (1.70, 1.68, 1.69) show some stabilization/chop rather than accelerating decline, but no reversal signal — my falsifier condition (sustained rise above 1.80 or positive 21d change) was not met. The policy language shift from "have remained low" to "have declined" is a hawkish-on-disinflation-expectations acknowledgment, confirming the market has genuinely priced lower inflation compensation, not just noise. Breakeven sits 0.31pp below the 2% objective and only 0.08pp above its 12-month low, in the bottom 14% of its 12-month range — a soft, still-negative backdrop. Given the recent flattening in the last three readings, I lean toward continued mild softness or stabilization near current levels rather than a sharp further leg down, but the balance of evidence (medium and long-term momentum both negative, confirmed by policy language) still favors down over flat.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above 1.75 sustained for several readings, or be_change_21d turning clearly positive, would overturn this call.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 3.60% matches the prior month, holding at the 12-month low, so my previous "flat" call was directionally correct in that no reversal materialized — the rate did not rise back toward 3.80%+ as I flagged would change my mind. Unemployment sits 0.40pp below the natural-rate anchor of ~4%, and the trend over 3/6/12 months is modestly negative (-0.20, -0.30, -0.40 pp respectively), indicating a still-tightening or at-worst stable labor market. Sahm gap is negligible (+0.03pp), well below the 0.3pp threshold that would signal loosening momentum. Range position is at 0 (the floor of the 12-month range), and the two consecutive prints at 3.60% suggest the series may be stabilizing at this lower level rather than continuing to fall further, but there's no evidence of loosening either. Policy language reinforces this: the FOMC's updated statement upgraded language from labor market "remains strong" amid slowing growth to labor market "remains strong" with activity rising at a "solid rate" — a more confident characterization, consistent with continued tightness rather than deterioration. With no rise in unemployment and strengthening qualitative language, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-tight, not loosening.
Would change its mind if: A print at/above 3.80% (sahm_gap rising above 0.3pp) would signal a reversal toward loosening; two months of increase would confirm a trend change.
Leaned on: sahm_gap, unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year sits at 2.00%, right at its 12-month low (range position 0.00), confirming last cycle's down call was correct. Momentum remains negative across all horizons: -0.22pp over 21d, -0.41pp over 63d, and -0.87pp over the full year. The yield has been grinding lower with no signs of stabilization — the last print (2.00) matches the cycle low exactly, and the recent bounce to 2.07 quickly reversed. The 30s10s slope at +0.52pp is not unusually steep, suggesting the entire long end is being pulled down together rather than a curve-shape story, consistent with a genuine decline in term premium (falling term/duration compensation) rather than just front-end repricing. The updated policy language noting market-based inflation compensation has "declined" (versus merely "remained low") reinforces that long-end inflation risk premium embedded in the 10y is compressing, which is a direct term-premium signal. There is no evidence of a reversal: no close above prior resistance levels, no positive momentum, no steepening. The bias remains toward further downside or at least stabilization at cycle lows, though the pace of decline has moderated slightly (21d change less severe than 63d/252d annualized pace), so I temper conviction slightly from pure momentum extrapolation.
Would change its mind if: A sustained close back above ~2.30-2.40 with positive 21d momentum and a steepening 30s10s slope would signal the downtrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has drifted gradually upward from -0.61 to -0.57 over the last 13 weeks, with the most recent prints flattening at -0.57 for three consecutive readings. The 4-week change (+0.02) and 13-week change (+0.03) both confirm a mild tightening trend, but the pace has clearly decelerated — the last three observations are identical, suggesting the move is stalling rather than accelerating. My prior call of "up" was directionally correct: conditions did tighten modestly from the trough, consistent with the +0.03 13-week change, though the magnitude was small and my falsifier threshold of -0.55 was not reached. Index remains deeply negative (-0.57), well below the zero average and near the 52-week low, meaning conditions are still historically loose overall even if the marginal direction has been toward tightening. Given the plateau in the last three prints and the absence of new policy language pointing toward tightening (the quoted language is dovish/patient, arguably supportive of continued easing bias), I see limited momentum for further tightening into month-end. The most likely outcome is a continuation of the flat plateau, with only a slight residual upward bias from the multi-week trend.
Would change its mind if: A print below -0.59 would signal renewed easing; a break above -0.55 would confirm resumed tightening momentum.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_13w_avg, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.78
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (3,826,817) continues the steady runoff trend confirmed at the prior release. Total assets fell again from 3,844,016 to 3,826,817, a drop of ~17,199 over the latest week and consistent with the 4-week change of -24,627 and 13-week change of -128,800 (-3.26%). The series has declined in nearly every observation across the 13-week window, well below both the 13w average (3,881,489) and the 52w high (4,291,615), with assets now 464,798 below that peak. There is no sign of deceleration or pause — the pace of decline (~$6-9B/week average) looks consistent with continued balance-sheet runoff at an ongoing monthly cap. My prior call (down, 0.80) was correct: the balance sheet did indeed fall further. No statement language is available this period to indicate any change in reinvestment policy or a slowing/pausing of caps, so I have no reason to expect a reversal. Absent any new language suggesting a taper of runoff pace or a shift to reinvestment, the trend of steady contraction should persist into next month-end. I maintain a confident down call, keeping conviction slightly below prior levels only because the absence of any statement text removes one confirming data point about intent, though the price action itself is unambiguous.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last three readings (0.29, 0.28, 0.27, 0.25) show the spread rolling over after peaking at 0.30, giving a clear three-reading pullback from the recent high. My prior flat call anticipated a possible break higher that did briefly occur (spread reached 0.30, well above the 0.20 threshold I cited), so that call was too cautious - the curve did steepen meaningfully over the period versus the prior low of 0.20. However, the most recent trend is now flattening again: the last four prints are a steady decline (0.30→0.29→0.28→0.27→0.25), a fairly consistent short-term reversal. The 21d and 63d changes are still positive (+0.09, +0.11), reflecting the earlier steepening impulse, but the newest data point is the lowest in five readings, suggesting that impulse is fading. Range position at 0.58 is mid-range, not extreme in either direction. The 252d change is negative (-0.08), indicating the longer-run trend is still toward flatter/more inverted, consistent with the recent pullback. Given the newest observations point down while medium-term momentum is still positive, I read this as a modest flattening bias going into month-end, though not high conviction given mixed signals across horizons.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 0.28 sustained for 2+ readings with 21d change re-accelerating would indicate steepening resumed, not flattening.
Leaned on: slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broad easing/tightening in financial conditions would clarify if flight-to-quality is driving the decline.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of Fed balance-sheet runoff or issuance changes would clarify supply-side pressure on long-end yields.
  balance_sheet lacks financial_conditions — Would clarify if funding stress is prompting any reserve-management interventions offsetting runoff.

arbitrated.notes: Panel is broadly disinflationary and easing-consistent, with cross-checks reinforcing each other. Inflation: soft core PCE and headline momentum, both 3m annualized measures below 2% and below y/y, plus statement language upgrade confirms broadening softness — solid basis for a down call, though conviction tempered slightly since the move is modest (-0.03 m/m) and the analyst's own falsifier (a hot print) is a real near-term risk given prior mid-series bounces. Inflation expectations: breakevens drifting lower but stabilizing over the last three prints (1.70/1.68/1.69) — genuine but low-momentum softness, so a mild down lean rather than a strong one. Term premium: clean, multi-horizon negative momentum at cycle lows, reinforced by the same "declined" language shift feeding through from inflation expectations — this is the most convergent evidence on the panel (inflation, breakevens, and term premium all point the same disinflationary direction), so I keep it near the analyst's own conviction. Balance sheet: unambiguous, mechanical runoff continuing at pace with no offsetting signal — highest-conviction call on the panel. Labor tightness: genuinely flat, unemployment pinned at cycle low with strong qualitative language; no loosening signal, so no directional view beyond flat (net-zero conviction). Financial conditions: tightening impulse has stalled into a plateau — call it flat with a whisper of upward bias, not enough to lean hard either way. Curve slope: recent four-print rollover argues flattening near-term even though medium-term momentum still points steeper; I read this as a modest, lower-conviction down call given the mixed horizon signals. Overall: a coherent disinflation/runoff-driven easing-of-yields narrative, with labor market the one non-confirming, stable driver.

Plateaued trend gives no clear directional signal beyond noise-level drift.

arbitrated.falsifier: A hot CPI/core PCE print combined with breakevens sustaining above 1.75 and 10y yield closing above ~2.30 with steepening would flip this disinflation/lower-yield read.

risks.text: A single hot inflation print could quickly reverse the fragile disinflation narrative given recent mid-series bounces.
Term premium decline lacks financial-conditions and balance-sheet cross-checks, so flight-to-quality vs supply effects are unresolved.
Flat labor market alongside falling inflation and yields is an unusual combination that could signal data lag rather than genuine cooling.
Curve slope call rests on only four prints against still-positive medium-term momentum, raising whipsaw risk.

why.inflation_expectations: Breakevens drifting lower but last three prints show stabilization/chop, so only a mild down lean.
why.term_premium: Multi-horizon negative momentum at cycle lows, reinforced by convergent inflation-compensation language; high-conviction down call.
why.balance_sheet: Mechanical, unambiguous runoff continuing at steady weekly pace with no offsetting policy signal.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment pinned at cycle low with strong qualitative language; genuinely flat, no directional basis.
why.financial_conditions: Tightening impulse has plateaued over three consecutive prints; negligible residual upward bias only.
why.curve_slope: Recent four-print rollover suggests near-term flattening despite still-positive medium-term momentum; low conviction.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline_mom came in at 0.23%, just under my 0.25% reversal threshold, but core_pce_yoy ticked up to 1.66 from 1.56 — the second straight rise after a multi-month decline (1.62→1.64→1.56→1.66). My prior "down" call was directionally right on the headline yoy print itself (1.83, down from 2.00/1.80 a couple prints back... actually it rose from 1.67 to 1.83), so I should acknowledge that the most recent headline yoy actually ticked up, not down. That's a miss relative to my "down" call. The 3m annualized core rate (1.96%) is now essentially at target, and core_yoy_change_3m is +0.04pp — flattening, not falling. Headline mom_3m_avg (0.07%) is soft, but the outgoing print (0.23%) is well above that average, and headline_yoy_change_3m of -0.17pp masks a recent uptick in the last two prints (1.67→1.83). Policy language still describes inflation as "running below 2 percent" with "muted inflation pressures," consistent with a low but not collapsing regime. Given the core uptick and the strong single-month headline print, disinflationary momentum looks to be stalling rather than continuing. I'm shifting away from "down" toward a more neutral-to-slightly-up stance, with modest conviction given the mixed signals.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print back below 0.10% combined with core_pce_yoy resuming its decline would confirm disinflation has resumed and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last release confirms the "down" call was directionally right over the medium term (63d -0.17pp, 252d -0.35pp), and breakeven sits 0.24pp below the 2% objective at 1.76%, near the lower part of its 12-month range (position 0.27). However, my stated falsifier has actually triggered: be_change_21d is +0.07pp and the series has held above 1.75 for all ten recent readings, with the last print (1.76) essentially flat versus the prior (1.78) after a small bounce from 1.77 lows. The short-term trend has stabilized rather than continuing to fall — the last five readings (1.80, 1.78, 1.77, 1.78, 1.76) show a choppy, near-flat pattern rather than a clear downtrend. Policy language shifted from "have declined" to "remain low," which is consistent with a leveling-off rather than a continued decline — the committee itself is now describing a plateau at a suppressed level, not an ongoing slide. Given the mixed short-term signal against the still-negative longer-term trend and sub-target level, I read this as flat with a mild downward lean, not a continuation of the down call at prior conviction.
Would change its mind if: A resumed decline below 1.75 sustained for several readings, or be_change_21d turning clearly negative again, would confirm renewed downward momentum.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position, breakeven_10y

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 3.70% from 3.60%, a one-tick uptick after two months at the 12-month low. My prior "flat" call was directionally fine since the move so far is modest (only +0.10pp above the 12-month low, sahm_gap a negligible +0.03pp), but the single-month uptick means the flat/low-volatility regime may be starting to turn. The 3-month change is still 0.00pp and the 6-month and 12-month changes remain negative (-0.30pp and -0.10pp respectively), so the broader trend over the past year is still one of a tight, low-unemployment labor market with the rate sitting comfortably below the ~4% natural-rate benchmark (distance -0.30pp). There's no confirmed reversal yet — one month's uptick from a cycle low is well within noise for this series, and the range position (0.25) shows we're still in the lower quartile of the past year's range. However, the reversal off the 3.60% low is worth flagging: if next month adds another uptick, that would build a real Sahm-style signal. For now, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up, reflecting the modest uptick without over-reading a single data point.
Would change its mind if: A second consecutive monthly increase (e.g., to 3.80%+) confirming sahm_gap climbing meaningfully above 0.3pp would flip this to a clear loosening call.
Leaned on: unrate_3m_avg, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield sits at 2.02, near the very bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.05, just 0.06pp above the 12m low of 1.96%). The prior call of "down" was correct: yields have continued grinding lower, with the most recent print (2.02) the lowest in the 10-observation window, a fresh leg down from 2.06-2.08. 21d change is roughly flat (-0.01pp) but 63d (-0.50pp) and 252d (-0.96pp) show a strong, persistent downtrend in the long-end premium. The 30s10s slope at +0.51pp is not unusually steep, and 10s2s at +0.13pp is thin, consistent with a still-low term premium rather than a reversal. Policy language reinforces the low-yield backdrop: inflation compensation is described as low/declining, and importantly balance-sheet runoff is being concluded two months earlier than previously indicated — this removes a source of upward pressure on long-end supply/duration, consistent with a lower or stable term premium rather than a rising one. With no signs of steepening momentum or a bounce off the low, I maintain a down call, though conviction is tempered since the yield is already compressed near multi-year lows and could stabilize.
Would change its mind if: A sustained close back above ~2.30-2.40 with positive 21d momentum and a steepening 30s10s slope would signal the downtrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has been essentially pinned in a tight band since the last release: -0.57 to -0.58 over the most recent five prints, with the last two ticking down slightly to -0.58 from -0.57. The 4-week change is a negligible -0.01, and the 13-week change is a small +0.04, indicating that the modest tightening drift seen over the medium term has stalled and, if anything, reversed marginally in the last month. The index remains well below zero (-0.58), meaning financial conditions are still substantially looser than historical average, and it sits low in its 52-week range (range_position 0.18), close to the 52-week low. My prior flat call held: neither threshold I set (below -0.59 for easing, above -0.55 for tightening) was breached, and the data continues to hug the middle of that band. There is no policy commentary to suggest a shift. Given the tiny 4-week move and lack of any breakout from the recent range, I see no clear momentum in either direction. The level remains loose, but level is not the graded metric — the direction of the index is. With flat, low-amplitude oscillation persisting, I maintain a flat call, slightly firmer than before given the extended stability.
Would change its mind if: A print below -0.59 would signal renewed easing; a break above -0.55 would confirm resumed tightening momentum.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (3,803,436) extended the decline from 3,808,110 the prior week, a 4-week drop of -23,381 and 13-week drop of -124,837 (-3.18%), consistent with ongoing runoff. My prior "down" call was correct. However, the key new development is policy language: the Committee will conclude the runoff two months earlier than previously indicated, i.e., balance sheet reduction ends in [date] . This is a clear signal of an approaching halt to QT, but it explicitly confirms the caps remain in effect until that stated end date — it announces a stopping point, not an immediate pause or reversal. Since this release is roughly one month out, and the runoff has not yet been described as paused now, the pace of decline (~$20-25B/4wks) should largely persist into the next month-end unless the [date] cutoff falls within this window. Absent evidence that the announced end-date is imminent (i.e., within the next reading), I expect assets to keep drifting lower, but the magnitude of further decline should moderate as the terminal date approaches, and conviction should soften given the regime change is now visible on the horizon.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print flat or above the prior reading, or explicit statement that runoff has already stopped/reserves are being replenished.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread continued to flatten and did so sharply in the latest print, dropping from 0.21 to 0.13pp — the largest single-step decline in the series and a fresh 12-month low (range position just 0.08, essentially at the floor of the 12-month range of 0.11-0.35). This confirms my prior "down" call was directionally correct, and the move accelerated rather than stabilized. All the trend measures point the same way: 21d change -0.12, 63d change -0.08, 252d change -0.16, all negative, and now the spread sits only 0.02pp above its 12-month low with just 0.13pp of distance from outright inversion. The 10s30s slope at 0.51pp remains comfortably positive, so the flattening pressure is concentrated in the front-to-belly part of the curve rather than a broad long-end collapse, but that's context only. There is no evidence of stabilization or reversal — no reading has ticked up in this window, and the most recent observation is the steepest single-step drop shown. Given the proximity to the 12-month low and the momentum in the 21d change, further flattening or a test of inversion into month-end is the more likely path than a rebound.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 0.20 sustained for 2+ readings with 21d change turning positive would indicate flattening has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_distance_from_flat, slope_12m_low

arbitrated.notes: Cross-driver read: disinflation momentum is stalling (core PCE yoy ticking up two months running, hot single-month headline print) even though inflation expectations have merely gone flat rather than resumed their decline — both point to a floor forming under inflation, not a fresh leg down, but not a breakout either. On the rates side, the term premium (10y) and curve slope (2s10s) tell a consistent and higher-conviction story: yields grinding to fresh lows, curve flattening sharply and accelerating toward the 12-month low, both reinforced by the balance-sheet signal — QT ending earlier removes future duration supply pressure and the current runoff pace continues near-term. That triad (term premium down, curve flattening, balance sheet still shrinking) is the most internally consistent and highest-conviction cluster this meeting, all pointing to a lower, flatter curve environment persisting through month-end. Financial conditions remain genuinely rangebound with no directional edge — take no view. The inflation-side signals are noisier and lower conviction: I give a mild upward lean to inflation given the core PCE inflection and hot headline print, but keep expectations essentially flat since the plateau in breakevens argues against reading too much into one month of stalling disinflation. Labor tightness gets a very slight loosening lean off the single uptick, but this is barely above noise and could reverse next print. Overall: duration-friendly, curve-flattening backdrop dominates; inflation impulse is tentatively stabilizing/turning but not yet confirmed.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release invalidated the prior "up" call: headline_mom printed 0.09%, back below the 0.10% threshold I flagged, and core_pce_yoy ticked down to 1.64 from 1.66, essentially resuming its multi-month drift lower rather than confirming acceleration. I was wrong to lean up last time; the outgoing_mom of 0.21 did not persist into a sustained pickup. Current picture: headline_cpi_yoy at 1.74 sits mid-range (yoy_range_position 0.25), core PCE is 0.36pp below target and has been essentially flat to slightly declining over 3m (core_yoy_change_3m +0.00pp, but the last print rolled over). Core 3m annualized (1.61%) and headline 3m annualized (1.16%) both run below the trailing y/y rates, implying downward drift in y/y prints is more likely than upward. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing of -0.11pp reinforces a cooling monthly pulse. Policy language shift reinforces this: the Committee explicitly cut rates citing "muted inflation pressures" and declining market-based inflation compensation, with language softening from "closely monitor" to actual easing action — a clear signal that the inflation committee itself reads current momentum as disinflationary, not accelerating. I see no evidence supporting an upside surprise next print.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print above 0.25% combined with core_pce_yoy turning back up would signal reacceleration and reverse this down/flat call.
Leaned on: headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, yoy_range_position, core_distance_from_target

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year breakeven series has been essentially pinned near 1.53-1.59 for the last ten readings, with the latest print at 1.55, right in the middle of that tight band. My prior "flat" call at 0.30 conviction was directionally reasonable in that there was no fresh acceleration, but the falsifier I set (be_change_21d turning clearly negative) has technically been met at -0.15pp, and be_change_63d is -0.18pp — both negative, though small in absolute terms given the low level. The range_position of 0.03 shows breakeven is sitting near its 12-month low, and it's 0.45pp below the 2% target, a meaningful undershoot. The policy language shift from "have declined" to "remain low" is notable: it confirms the decline already occurred and characterizes the new level as a persistent, low plateau rather than an accelerating slide. That argues against further material downside from here — the committee's own language suggests stabilization at a depressed level rather than continued deterioration. Given the flat wobbling in the last few prints (1.54-1.59-1.55) and a status-quo characterization rather than a deteriorating one, I lean toward flat, but the persistent negative 21d/63d changes and distance from target keep me from being confident this is fully stable.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break below 1.53 (new 12-month low) or be_change_21d resuming a clearly negative slope would confirm renewed downward momentum rather than stabilization.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked down to 3.60% from 3.70%, matching the 12-month low and keeping the series in a tight, low range (3.60-4.00%). The 3-month change is flat (+0.00pp), the 6- and 12-month changes are both -0.20pp, and the Sahm gap is a negligible +0.03pp — nowhere near the ~0.3-0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine loosening turn. The prior call was flat with modest conviction, contingent on watching for a second consecutive rise; instead the rate fell further, reinforcing that the labor market remains historically tight (0.40pp below the assumed natural rate). There's no evidence of directional deterioration: the series has oscillated in a narrow 3.60-4.00 band for over a year with the most recent print at the low end. Policy language changes are cosmetic (May→[date] reference) and don't alter the "strong labor market" characterization. Absent a fresh shock, the base case is continued flatness at these tight levels, perhaps with a slight upward drift given how low the rate has settled, but no basis for calling a meaningful move either direction next month.
Would change its mind if: A print at 3.80%+ (two consecutive increases) pushing sahm_gap above ~0.3pp would flip this to a clear loosening call.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y sits at 1.50, essentially at the very bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.02, just 3bp above the 12m low of 1.47). Momentum remains negative across horizons: -0.40pp over 21d, -0.57pp over 63d, -1.35pp over the year, though the last two prints (1.47→1.50→1.50) show the decline stalling and flattening rather than accelerating. My prior "down" call was correct — yields kept falling and are now pinned near cycle lows. Going forward, the balance-sheet news (ending SOMA runoff two months early) is a modest technical tailwind for the long end that should, if anything, cap upward pressure on term premium by reducing net Treasury supply pressure from Fed roll-off, consistent with continued low/stable yields rather than a reversal. The 30s10s slope at +0.46pp is unremarkable and doesn't signal a steepening/reversal shock. With the level already compressed against the floor of its range and momentum decelerating, further declines are harder to sustain, but I see no evidence yet of a turn upward. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down, with reduced conviction versus last time since the trend is losing steam near the range bottom.
Would change its mind if: A sustained close back above ~1.90-2.00 with positive 21d momentum and a steepening 30s10s slope would signal the downtrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has moved steadily off its lows over the last several weeks: from -0.58 down to -0.53, a +0.04 rise over 4 weeks and +0.05 over 13 weeks. This is a small but persistent drift toward tighter conditions, not a one-off blip — every one of the last five prints has been higher than the prior one. At my previous release I called this flat with modest conviction, setting -0.55 as the threshold for confirmed tightening momentum; the latest print at -0.53 breaks clearly above that level, so the prior flat call was too cautious and the momentum has now confirmed itself. The index remains well below the zero average (-0.53 vs -0.57 13w avg) and near the loose end of its 52-week range (range position 0.42), so conditions are still looser than historical norms in absolute terms. But the trend is unambiguously toward tightening at the margin, with no sign of reversal in the recent prints. Given the consistency of the move and the break above my own prior trigger, I'm shifting to a modest 'up' call for the next month, while keeping conviction moderate since the absolute moves are still small in magnitude and the level remains historically loose.
Would change its mind if: A print back below -0.55 would signal the tightening drift has stalled or reversed; a drop below -0.58 would confirm renewed easing.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call for continued runoff was correct: the newest print (3,759,946) is below the previous (3,764,866), continuing the steady decline seen across all 13 observations. The 4-week change (-19,156) and 13-week change (-91,498) both confirm an ongoing, fairly steady pace of contraction, roughly consistent with the announced monthly cap pace of recent quarters. However, the policy language has materially changed: the Committee will conclude runoff in [date] two months earlier than previously indicated. This is forward guidance about an end date, not a signal that runoff has already stopped or reversed. Since the observation window still shows active, uninterrupted decline right up to the last print, and no observation yet reflects a slowing or halt, the near-term (one month ahead) trajectory should still show a lower total-assets print, likely at a similar or slightly smaller magnitude of decline as the balance sheet approaches its terminal level. The end-of-runoff announcement is important for future months but doesn't yet change the mechanical monthly draw for the next print. I maintain a down call, tempered slightly given the announced conclusion is approaching and could plausibly overlap with the horizon depending on exact timing.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print flat or above the prior reading, or explicit confirmation that runoff has stopped/reserves are being replenished before month end.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The most recent print shows slope_2s10s ticking up from -0.03 to 0.00, ending a multi-month flattening trend. My prior "down" call was correct through most of the series (0.07→-0.04 over the run) but the latest observation shows stabilization right at the flat line, not further inversion. The 21d/63d/252d changes are all still negative (-0.17, -0.25, -0.18), confirming the broader trend has been flattening, but the last data point breaks that momentum with a move off the 12-month low. Range position is just 0.10 (near the bottom of the 12-month band of -0.04 to +0.35), meaning the curve is about as flat as it's been all year — there's little room left to flatten further before re-inversion, and the bounce to 0.00 suggests the flattening impulse may be exhausting. Policy language shows a rate cut (to 2-2.25%) with dissent toward even more easing, which typically supports front-end anchoring and modest steepening as cuts get priced. Given the sharp reversal in the last print after a long flattening run, and the curve sitting at the low end of its range, I lean toward a stabilization/mild steepening rather than continued flattening, though the trend evidence is not yet overwhelming.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below -0.03 with 21d change resuming negative would confirm flattening has resumed rather than stalled.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===

arbitrated.notes: Across the panel, the macro picture is one of disinflation-adjacent easing with a Fed already cutting rates, set against a modest, persistent uptick in financial-conditions tightness and mechanically continuing balance-sheet runoff. Inflation: the prior "up" call was falsified — headline_mom rolled back under threshold, core PCE resumed its drift lower, and 3m annualized rates run below trailing y/y in both core and headline, plus explicit Fed language citing muted pressures. I read this as leaning down, similar conviction to the analyst. Inflation expectations: breakevens are pinned in a tight, low band with negative 21d/63d changes but the Fed's language shift ("remain low" vs "have declined") suggests stabilization at a depressed level rather than a fresh leg down — I'll call this flat with a slight downside tilt given the still-negative momentum. Labor tightness: unemployment near 12-month lows, Sahm gap negligible, band has held over a year — flat is right, no basis for a directional call either way. Term premium: 10Y pinned near 12-month lows with decelerating downward momentum; balance-sheet runoff ending early is a modest technical tailwind capping any yield-rise, and the analyst wasn't shown financial-conditions or inflation-expectations context that might have reinforced the flight-to-quality bid — I keep this flat-to-slightly-down but with low conviction given analyst blind spots. Financial conditions: five consecutive weekly prints tightening, breaking the analyst's own prior threshold — this is a real, if modest, trend; I lean up. Balance sheet: mechanical runoff continuing uninterrupted through the last print despite an early-conclusion announcement — down call stands but conviction moderated given proximity to the stated end date. Curve slope: multi-month flattening trend but the latest print breaks off the 12-month low with dovish cut/dissent language supporting front-end anchoring — modest steepening lean, tempered by only one data point of reversal.
