Inflation and inflation_expectations corroborate each other — headline momentum decelerated (0.05% mom), core PCE stayed soft, and breakevens broke to a 12-month low with accelerating negative momentum — so I read both as genuine, not noise, though inflation's single soft print keeps that conviction modest while breakevens' clean range-break earns higher conviction. Term_premium's decline is dramatic and consistent (12m low, accelerating), but the panel itself flags that it lacks visibility into financial_conditions and balance_sheet, and financial_conditions independently reports a sharp tightening move to a 52-week high. That's an important cross-check: a flight-to-quality / risk-off tightening episode plausibly explains both the term_premium collapse and the NFCI spike simultaneously, meaning part of the "term premium down" signal may be risk-off flow rather than pure duration demand from lower growth/inflation expectations — I keep the down call but temper it slightly for that overlap risk. Curve_slope's steepening is real (range breakout, 82nd percentile) but sits oddly against a falling 10y and tightening NFCI; I read it as short-end rates staying anchored/cutting while long end is comparatively sticky, consistent with an easing-expectations-driven steepener rather than a term-premium-driven one — so I don't discount it, but note the tension. Labor tightness and balance sheet are lower-conviction, data-supported but muted signals with no strong catalyst; I keep them modest.

discounted.why: Flat call with modest conviction offers no clear directional edge for reconciliation.
Whipsaw data, no policy language anchor, low conviction — too noisy to weight heavily.

arbitrated.falsifier: A rebound in breakevens above 1.55 with 10y yields back above 1.35-1.40 and NFCI stalling below -0.53 would flip this read toward stabilization rather than a disinflation/risk-off regime.

risks.text: Term premium decline and NFCI tightening may share a common flight-to-quality cause the panel can't isolate.
Inflation call rests on one soft mom print that could be noise rather than genuine deceleration.
Steepening curve alongside falling 10y and tightening NFCI is an unusual combination that could reverse quickly.
Sharp single-step moves in breakevens and NFCI raise odds of partial mean-reversion next month.

why.inflation: One soft mom print plus soft core PCE, but single-print evidence and short 14-day-old formation temper conviction
why.inflation_expectations: Clean range breakout to 12m low with accelerating declines across 21/63/252d; strong technical confirmation
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment at cycle low, no loosening signal, but flat call itself implies little net directional view
why.term_premium: Sharp, accelerating decline to 12m low, but likely partly flight-to-quality overlap with NFCI tightening; conviction trimmed
why.financial_conditions: NFCI surged to 52-week high with fast, one-directional 4-week move; genuine trend shift, not mean reversion
why.balance_sheet: Two consecutive weekly declines off recent high, but whipsaw pattern and no policy language keep conviction low
why.curve_slope: Confirmed range breakout to 82nd percentile, consistent with easing-expectations-led steepening despite falling 10y

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The last print confirms the down call: headline_cpi_yoy fell sharply to 1.49% from 2.34%, with headline_mom collapsing to -0.45%, driven almost certainly by an energy/food shock (headline_3m_annualized is now -0.85% vs core's +2.40%). This is a large divergence, and given the policy language referencing "public health" and "global developments" alongside declining market-based inflation compensation, this looks like a demand/energy-price collapse rather than a core disinflation trend. Core PCE actually ticked up to 1.70% from 1.59%, near the top of its recent range, and core_yoy_change_3m is +0.20pp — mild firming, not weakening. I am weighting core here as the persistence signal, but headline is what I'm asked to forecast, and headline is dominated by the volatile components right now. A single -0.45% mom print is a level shock; unless energy prices are cratering further, the very negative 3m-annualized headline pace is unlikely to repeat, mechanically pushing yoy comparisons in either direction depends on base effects I can't fully assess without the underlying energy path. Given the shock's likely partial persistence into next month's yoy base and no clear turn in core, I lean toward continued softness in headline yoy relative to prior levels, but with much lower conviction than before since core is actually stabilizing/firming, not falling.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom rebound above +0.20% or core_pce_yoy pushing further above 1.75% would signal the shock was transient and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous down call was correct: breakeven_10y fell from 1.07 to 0.87 over the last two prints, confirming the decline continued rather than reversing. The series shows a clear late peak at 1.07 followed by two consecutive declines (0.94, 0.87), and all three change windows remain firmly negative (-0.54pp/21d, -0.88pp/63d, -0.96pp/252d), indicating the disinflationary-expectations move is still in force, not stabilizing. Breakeven sits 1.13pp below the 2% objective and only 0.37pp above the 12-month low, in the bottom quartile of its 12-month range (range_position 0.25). The policy language change is corroborating: the statement was updated to say market-based inflation compensation "have declined" (from "remain low"), explicitly acknowledging the further drop — a meaningful escalation in tone that confirms this isn't noise. Survey-based expectations are described as little changed, so this move is specifically a market-pricing phenomenon, consistent with breakeven-specific weakness rather than a broader expectations shift. Given the fresh two-period decline, the still-negative momentum across all horizons, and the explicit acknowledgment in policy language, I expect this trajectory to persist over the next month, likely with continued or at least sustained low readings rather than a snapback toward target.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above ~1.05 with be_change_21d turning positive would signal the decline has reversed and re-anchoring toward target is underway.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print jumped to 4.40% from a stable 3.50-3.60% band that had held for roughly a year, a massive +0.80pp 3-month move and +0.90pp 6-month move. My prior "flat" call was wrong — I anticipated a possible loosening threshold breach but this single-month spike is far larger than the 3.80% sustained trigger I set, and the Sahm-gap of 0.33pp is approaching (though not yet past) the classic 0.5pp recession-signal threshold using this particular gap construction. The unrate_range_position of 1.00 shows this print is the high of the past year, a sharp break from a tight, flat regime. Such a large one-month jump is unusual and could partly reflect noise or measurement idiosyncrasy, but the magnitude (0.9pp in a single print) is historically the kind of move that tends to have follow-through rather than immediately reverse, since labor market deterioration typically continues once it starts (Sahm-rule spirit). The policy language still describes the labor market as "strong" and unemployment as "remained low," which is now stale relative to the new print — the FOMC language likely predates or does not yet reflect this jump. Given the sharp break from a 12-month low and the size of the move, I expect further loosening (higher unemployment) over the next month rather than reversion.
Would change its mind if: A next print back near 3.6-3.8% would indicate the 4.40% was a one-off aberration rather than genuine trend break.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: the 10y has continued falling and now sits at 0.70, essentially flat over the last two prints (0.72→0.70→0.70) after a sharp multi-month decline (-1.20pp over 63d, -1.69pp over 252d). The yield sits at only 8% of its 12-month range, just 0.16pp above the 12m low, confirming deep flight-to-quality/low-term-premium conditions. The 21d change of -0.40pp shows the downward momentum has decelerated but not reversed, and the last two observations are identical, suggesting the decline is stabilizing near a floor rather than reversing. The policy language reinforces this: explicit large-scale Treasury and MBS purchases (at least $500bn and $200bn respectively) are a strong technical/portfolio-balance force compressing long-end term premium, and inflation compensation is now described as having "declined" (vs. merely "remain low"), consistent with lower embedded term premium. The 30s10s slope at +0.65pp is elevated, which could reflect long-end curve steepening pressure from heavy long-bond issuance concerns, but this is outweighed by the dominant flight-to-quality/QE-purchase dynamic. Net: little momentum for a bounce, and active policy support for continued suppression. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down, with lower conviction than before since the yield seems to have found a near-term floor around 0.70.
Would change its mind if: A move back above ~1.00-1.10 with positive 21d momentum, or a steepening 30s10s alongside rising yields, would signal renewed upward term-premium pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.55) was correct and the trend has strengthened, not stalled. NFCI has moved from -0.13 to +0.18 over the last observation, extending a sharp, uninterrupted tightening streak: +0.61 over 4 weeks and +0.72 over 13 weeks. The index has now crossed from below-average to above-average financial conditions (distance from zero +0.18), sitting at its 52-week high and at the top of its range (range_position 1.00). Momentum shows no sign of flattening — the pace of increase in the most recent prints is actually accelerating slightly (e.g., -0.29→-0.13→+0.03→+0.18 are roughly even steps, still firmly upward). Policy language accompanying this move describes emergency liquidity and credit-support facilities (repo, swap lines, discount window, QE increases), which is the kind of extraordinary intervention typically deployed in response to acute market stress/tightening — consistent with and reinforcing the measured tightening rather than contradicting it. Given the consistency, magnitude, and acceleration of the move, along with policy actions confirming stress-driven tightening, I expect the index to continue rising into next month-end, though the presence of massive Fed liquidity backstops could eventually cap or reverse the move, warranting moderate rather than extreme conviction.
Would change its mind if: A print flattening near +0.18 or reversing below +0.03 over the next release would signal the tightening move has stalled or is being offset by liquidity facilities.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_high, nfci

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.90
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call (0.25 conviction) was wrong: the last two prints show an enormous surge, from ~4.31T to 4.67T to 5.25T, a jump of roughly $1.1T in just four weeks (assets_change_4w +1,095,641M), decisively breaking above the prior 52-week high with two consecutive massive increases. This is not noise around a runoff cap — it is emergency-scale QE. The policy language confirms this explicitly: the Committee has committed to increasing Treasury holdings by at least $500B and agency MBS by at least $200B "over coming months," plus reinvesting all principal payments into agency MBS. That language directly explains the balance-sheet mechanics behind the last two weeks' spike and signals more expansion is programmed in, not a one-off. Given the stated $700B minimum in additional purchases has likely only begun to be executed (assets are only up ~$1.3T over 52 weeks with almost all of that in the most recent month), the pace should continue upward into month-end. This is an unambiguous, high-conviction expansion signal — both the measurement trend and the explicit stated commitment point the same direction.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print showing assets flat or declining, or statement language announcing tapering/halting of the $500B/$200B purchase program, would reverse this call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation ticked flat at 0.47pp for three consecutive readings after a run-up from 0.46 to 0.54, then a pullback to 0.47. My prior "up" call was directionally right versus the trough (0.46) but the most recent three prints show the steepening has stalled, not continued — slope_change_21d (+0.21) and slope_change_63d (+0.15) still reflect the earlier move, but the latest flat readings suggest that momentum has plateaued rather than extended. The curve sits at 0.71 of its 12-month range, closer to the high (0.68) than the low (-0.04), so there is room to steepen further, but the immediate signal is stabilization. Policy language shows an emergency cut to the zero lower bound, which mechanically anchors the front end (2y) near zero while the long end has more room to move with growth/inflation expectations — a setup that historically supports gradual steepening as front-end is pinned. But with three flat prints in a row, I don't see fresh evidence of continued steepening this month; the trend that justified my prior call has paused. I'll shift to a modest lean rather than a confident continuation.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward 0.30-0.35pp or below, or 21d change turning negative, would confirm renewed flattening rather than stabilization.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The last print confirms a severe disinflationary shock: headline CPI collapsed to 0.31% y/y from 1.49%, with mom crashing to -0.79% (following -0.45% prior month). This is consistent with an acute demand/price shock (likely energy-driven given the magnitude) rather than gradual disinflation. My prior "down" call was directionally correct and understated — the drop was far larger than typical momentum would suggest. Core PCE, the better persistence gauge, is far more stable at 1.53% y/y, still below the 2% target but only modestly changed (core_yoy_change_3m near zero). The core-minus-headline gap of +1.21pp is unusually wide, confirming the divergence is concentrated in volatile headline components (energy/food), not underlying price pressure. Policy language shifted to emphasize "weathered recent events," consistent with an unusual shock rather than steady-state disinflation. Given headline is now near a 12-month low with two consecutive sharply negative mom prints, the shock looks acute and possibly ongoing (base effects, continued energy weakness) rather than a one-month blip. Absent evidence of energy price stabilization, base effects from such a low print could cause continued y/y weakness or a mechanical rebound next month is genuinely uncertain — but the raw momentum (mom_gap_vs_outgoing -0.42pp, 3m annualized -4.69%) still points down before any bounce-back arithmetic kicks in.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom rebound above +0.20%, or stabilization in energy-sensitive components, would signal the shock is reversing rather than persisting.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, core_minus_headline, headline_yoy_12m_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (down, 0.55) was falsified by the data: breakeven_10y has risen from 1.01 to 1.07 over the last two prints, with be_change_21d now +0.20pp — exactly the rebound condition I specified as my falsifier. The last three readings (1.17, 1.14, 1.14, 1.07) show a peak and partial pullback, but the level remains well above the series lows near 0.94-0.96 and above the 12m low by 0.57pp. Medium-term trend (63d -0.57, 252d -0.89) is still negative, so this isn't a clean reversal to the upside — it's more like stabilization after a sharp decline, with recent chop. Breakeven sits at 0.39 of its 12-month range, still well below the 2% objective (-0.93pp distance), consistent with policy language noting market-based inflation compensation has declined. Given the 21d rebound conflicts with the 63d/252d downtrend, and the last two prints (1.14, 1.07) show renewed softening after the 1.17 peak, I read this as directionless chop rather than a resumed decline or a confirmed uptrend. I'll shift toward flat, acknowledging the reversal broke my prior thesis but the most recent tick (1.07) is a step down from 1.14, tempering enthusiasm for further upside.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 1.17 with be_change_21d and be_change_63d both turning positive would confirm renewed upward re-anchoring toward target.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate just exploded from 4.40% to 14.80%, a monthly jump of over 10 points after a prior jump from 3.50% to 4.40%. This is an unprecedented, historically massive labor-market shock, not a data aberration or gradual softening. The Sahm-gap of +4.07pp already captures the earlier move, but the newest print obliterates any notion of stability — this is a collapse in labor tightness, not a mild loosening. My prior view (up, 0.55) anticipated continued deterioration off the 3.50 low, and this was directionally correct but understated the magnitude enormously; I did not anticipate a move of this scale. Given the level is now 10.8pp above the natural rate and the 3-month change is +11.2pp, the market is in freefall. For the next print, the overwhelming base rate for such extreme dislocations is continued elevated readings or further increases, since shocks of this magnitude rarely reverse in a single month — labor markets are sticky on the way back down. I expect the unemployment rate to remain elevated or tick higher still, though the pace of increase may moderate since it's hard to keep accelerating from already-extreme levels. Direction: up, with high but not absolute conviction given uncertainty about whether the shock is stabilizing.
Would change its mind if: A next print back toward 10-12% or lower, showing the spike was a one-month peak and the labor market is already recovering rapidly.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_12m_high, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y sits at 0.64%, essentially at the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.05, only 10bp above the 12m low of 0.54%). Momentum is mixed: 21d change is -0.06pp (mild further decline) but the last two prints ticked up slightly (0.62→0.63→0.64) after a low of 0.58, so the multi-month decline (-0.93pp over 63d, -1.90pp over 252d) appears to be stabilizing rather than continuing to accelerate. The prior view called for further declines and that was roughly correct in trend terms, though the very latest data shows a small stabilization/uptick, not a resumption of the sharp downtrend. Policy language shifted from a specific, large fixed-amount purchase commitment ($500bn Treasuries + $200bn MBS) to an open-ended, flow-based "amounts needed to support smooth market functioning" formulation — this is somewhat less mechanically supportive of duration-suppression than a large explicit total, though it's still an accommodative stance. The 30s10s slope (0.64pp) is moderate, not showing aggressive steepening that would signal upward term-premium pressure. Given the yield is pinned near multi-year lows with only tentative stabilization and no clear steepening signal, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up given the plateau, but conviction is low given the mixed recent ticks.
Would change its mind if: A resumption of decline toward/through the 12m low (0.54%) with negative 21d momentum, or a sharp steepening of 30s10s alongside rising yields, would clarify direction.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s, dgs10_above_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print is 0.24, down from the peak of 0.31 two readings ago and below the prior 0.29 reading — the first back-to-back decline after a sharp multi-month tightening run (13w change +0.84). My prior call of "up" was correct through the peak but the most recent two prints (0.29 → 0.24) show the tightening impulse stalling and reversing modestly, consistent with the threshold I set for reconsidering. Policy language has shifted decisively toward large-scale asset purchases (Treasury +$500bn, MBS +$200bn), discount window easing, and explicit commitments to support credit flow — these are exactly the kind of liquidity facilities that would push NFCI back down after a spike. Conditions remain far tighter than average (distance from zero +0.24, range position 0.92 — near the top of the 52-week range), so the level is still elevated, but momentum has clearly flipped: 4-week change is now much weaker than the 13-week trend, and the two most recent prints are declining. Given the scale of the announced interventions explicitly aimed at financial conditions/credit flow, I expect continued easing pressure into next month, though from a still-elevated base.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above 0.29-0.31 would indicate renewed tightening overwhelming the liquidity facilities and reverse this call.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.85
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.90) was correct: total assets rose again to 6,573,136 from 6,367,887, a 4-week gain of ~+1.32T and 13-week gain of ~+2.43T, both far above prior periods, confirming the balance sheet is still in aggressive expansion (QE), not deceleration. The policy language has shifted from the fixed, capped $500B Treasury/$200B MBS purchase program to open-ended, uncapped purchases "in the amounts needed to support smooth market functioning" — this is not a taper signal; it removes the explicit ceiling and ties purchases to market conditions, which historically has meant continued large-scale buying rather than a wind-down. There is no language about tapering, reducing pace, or reinvestment caps. Given the measured pace of increase remains extremely high and the statement confirms open-ended purchases continue, I expect total assets to be higher again at month end. The magnitude of the last few weekly increases has been large and accelerating (668k, 586k, 557k, 271k, 285k, 205k in recent multi-week jumps within the 13-week window), suggesting momentum, though the very last increment (6573136-6367887=205249) is smaller than the peak jump, hinting pace may be moderating slightly even as direction stays up.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print showing assets flat or declining, or statement language announcing tapering/halting/capping of ongoing purchases.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was reasonably supported at the time but the last two prints (0.43, 0.44) show the spread ticking back up after the mid-series dip to 0.38-0.39, so the near-term trend is mildly re-steepening rather than genuinely flat. The 21d change is still slightly negative (-0.03pp), reflecting the dip earlier in the window, but the most recent two observations reverse that and sit at the high end of the recent range. Longer-horizon measures (63d +0.28pp, 252d +0.20pp) confirm a steepening trend over the past several months, and the spread now sits at 0.67 of its 12-month range (position 0-1), closer to the 12m high (0.68) than the 12m low (-0.04). The curve is only modestly inverted-to-flat in absolute terms (0.44pp above the 12m low), consistent with continued gradual steepening from deeply inverted levels. Policy language shift — holding rates steady after cuts, plus continued QE purchases to support market functioning — suggests anchored front-end rates while long-end yields have more room to drift with growth/inflation expectations, which tends to support further steepening rather than renewed flattening. I'm revising off flat toward a modest up lean given the two-print uptick and the supportive multi-horizon trend, though the flat 21d change keeps conviction limited.
Would change its mind if: A move back below 0.40pp with 21d change turning more clearly negative would signal renewed flattening and invalidate this lean.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_change_21d

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-on/risk-off flows would clarify whether the recent uptick reflects flight-to-quality unwind.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Actual pace of Fed Treasury purchases under the new open-ended language would show real duration absorption.

arbitrated.notes:
This looks like a crisis-and-response scenario: a massive labor market shock (unemployment 4.4%→14.8%) and severe disinflation (headline CPI down to 0.31% y/y) are being met with an aggressive, now open-ended Fed balance-sheet expansion. The core narrative is coherent — real shock, disinflation, policy response — which lets me lean fairly hard on the drivers with the clearest, freshest data and the least ambiguity: balance sheet (unambiguous, accelerating expansion, open-ended language removes cap) and labor tightness (a shock of this magnitude rarely reverses within a month, so further deterioration or at least no quick reversal is the base case). Inflation gets a real but tempered down call: headline is collapsing but core PCE is stable, so the story is a volatile-component shock rather than broad deflation — still, headline is what's being forecast, so down.

Financial conditions and curve slope are secondary derivatives of the same regime: massive liquidity injection plus front-end anchoring should ease NFCI and support gradual curve steepening, but both analysts' own conviction was moderate given noisy recent prints, so I keep those lower and directional only.

Term premium and inflation expectations are the weakest links — both are genuinely torn between recent upticks and still-negative multi-month trends, and both analysts flagged their own uncertainty and reversed a prior call under new data. I treat both as close to flat, with only a whisper of directional lean where the analyst leaned. I explicitly do not lean on term premium given its self-acknowledged blind spots (no visibility into financial conditions or actual purchase pace) and the two-print noise it's hanging its call on.

discounted.why: Self-reported blind spots (no financial_conditions/balance_sheet context) and pure two-print noise underpinning the call.
Analyst's own thesis reversed mid-report; reads as directionless chop, not a real signal.

arbitrated.falsifier: A CPI mom rebound above +0.20%, an unemployment print back toward 10-12%, or a Fed weekly balance-sheet print showing flat/declining assets would each unwind a leg of this read.

risks.text: Extreme one-month CPI and unemployment moves may partially mechanically reverse next print, flipping both calls.
Open-ended QE language may not translate into actual purchase pace as fast as balance-sheet momentum implies.
Term premium and inflation expectations calls rest on very recent, low-conviction, noisy ticks that could reverse either way.

why.inflation: Headline collapse is real and shock-like, but core PCE stability tempers magnitude; keep down but below analyst's own 0.45 given rebound risk.
why.labor_tightness: Shock of 10+pp in unemployment is sticky; base rate favors continued elevation or further rise next print.
why.balance_sheet: Open-ended purchase language plus accelerating asset growth is unambiguous; slight trim for last-print pace deceleration.
why.financial_conditions: Two consecutive declines after liquidity facilities announced, but still elevated level tempers conviction in continued easing.
why.curve_slope: Recent uptick and multi-horizon steepening trend support modest up lean, but flat 21d change caps conviction.
why.inflation_expectations: Analyst's own call flipped from down to flat on noisy chop; barely lean positive given 21d rebound, no strong basis.
why.term_premium: Tentative stabilization/uptick off multi-year lows, but analyst flagged missing context (FCI, purchase pace); minimal lean.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct and dramatically so: headline_yoy collapsed from 1.49 to 0.31 to 0.20 over the last two prints, with headline_mom swinging to -0.79% then a smaller -0.09%. This is clearly a demand/oil-price shock (explicitly named in policy language: "weaker demand and significantly lower oil prices are holding down consumer price inflation"), consistent with the falsifier I set not being met — mom did not rebound above 0.20%, it stayed negative, just less severely so (-0.09 vs -0.79). Core PCE also dropped sharply to 1.00 from ~1.5-1.7 range, confirming this isn't just energy/food noise but a broader disinflationary hit, likely demand destruction from the crisis referenced in policy language. Headline is now at its 12-month low with essentially zero cushion above 0. Momentum (3m annualized headline -5.21%) is severely negative, though the deceleration in the pace of monthly decline (-0.79 to -0.09) suggests the sharpest drop may be past. Given the level is already near zero and the mom decline is moderating, further material downside in yoy terms is possible but the pace of deterioration seems to be easing. I lean down but with reduced conviction given how much air is already out and the mom print's deceleration in decline.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print turning positive and above +0.10%, or stabilization in core_pce_yoy, would signal the disinflationary shock has bottomed.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_12m_low, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y breakeven series shows a choppy but essentially rangebound pattern over the last 10 readings (1.09→1.15), sitting mid-range (be_range_position 0.50) and well below the 12m high. My prior "flat" call was reasonably accurate at the very short end: be_change_21d is now +0.01pp, near zero, so no fresh directional break occurred at the 21-day horizon. However, the 63-day and 252-day changes remain solidly negative (-0.26pp, -0.58pp), and the policy language explicitly flags that market-based inflation compensation "has declined," confirming the medium-term downtrend is still the dominant signal even though the most recent print (1.15) ticked up modestly from 1.14. Breakevens remain -0.85pp below the 2% objective, a sizable gap that has not been closing. The latest uptick to 1.18 then pullback to 1.15 looks like noise within a range, not a re-anchoring higher — my falsifier condition (break above 1.17 with both 21d and 63d positive) was not met since 63d is still deeply negative. Given the mixed short-term stabilization against a persistent medium/long-term downdrift and explicit policy confirmation of decline, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down rather than a reversal, though the very recent stabilization tempers conviction.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 1.18 sustained with be_change_21d and be_change_63d both turning solidly positive would signal renewed re-anchoring upward.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.55) was correct in direction: unemployment rose again from the initial 14.80% spike... actually the newest print, 13.20%, is a decline from 14.80%, meaning last month's move was down, not up. My previous view anticipated further deterioration; instead the print pulled back. That said, the level remains catastrophically far from the ~4% natural rate (distance +9.2pp), and the 3/6/12-month changes are all near +9.6-9.7pp, reflecting the initial shock. The single month-over-month move (14.80→13.20) is a partial retracement, consistent with the sharpest labor-market shock beginning to unwind rather than continuing to worsen. However, one data point is not a trend, and given the extraordinary range width (11.3pp) and the fact the rate is still near the top of its 12-month range (position 0.86), the market is still overwhelmingly loose versus history — but the direction of travel just reversed downward. Absent further deterioration signals, the immediate next print more likely continues this improvement (down) or stabilizes (flat) rather than resuming the spike. I'll shift from up to a cautious down/flat lean, weighting the observed reversal but tempering conviction given the extreme volatility and uncertainty about whether 13.20 is a genuine turn or noise.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 14% would confirm renewed deterioration rather than recovery; a continued drift toward 10-12% would confirm the recovery thesis.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_high, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y sits at 0.65%, near the very bottom of its 12-month range (position 0.06, only 0.11pp above the 0.54% low). The prior call of flat was reasonably accurate: yields oscillated in a tight 0.64-0.73% band over the last ten readings with no sustained trend. The 21d change is essentially flat (+0.02pp), while 63d and 252d changes are sharply negative, reflecting the earlier large decline that has since stabilized into a narrow, low-level range. The policy language shift — dropping the explicit $500bn/$200bn purchase targets in favor of open-ended "amounts needed to support smooth market functioning" — is a subtle change but doesn't signal a shift toward less accommodation; if anything it preserves flexibility to keep suppressing long-end yields as needed. The 30s10s slope (0.76pp) is elevated but not sharply steepening relative to what would signal a decisive move. With yields pinned near cycle lows and no fresh catalyst for a breakout in either direction, the most defensible call remains flat, though the extremely low range position and stable purchase support give a very slight tilt toward the premium staying compressed rather than rising.
Would change its mind if: A break below 0.60% with negative 21d momentum would confirm further compression; a move above 0.80% with steepening 30s10s would signal rising premium.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print continues the decline that began after peaking at 0.31, now at -0.21 — the lowest level in the 13-observation window and comfortably below the zero average. The 4-week change is a sharp -0.36, and while the 13-week change is still positive (+0.23) reflecting the earlier spike, momentum has clearly reversed and accelerated to the downside over the last several readings (0.16→0.06→-0.04→-0.13→-0.21). My prior "down" call was correct: conditions have eased steadily, not tightened. Policy language reinforces this — massive asset purchase commitments (at least $500B Treasuries, $200B MBS), discount window and swap line actions, and explicit commitment to "full range of tools" to support credit flow. These are precisely the interventions designed to compress the NFCI, and the measurement path confirms they are working. There's no sign of stabilization yet; the series is still falling at a steep pace and sits well within the lower half of its 52-week range (range_position 0.45, but trending down). Absent a shock reversing course, the trajectory points to further easing into month-end, though the pace of decline may moderate as it approaches deeply negative territory relative to the 52-week low of -0.62.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above 0.0-0.1 or flattening near -0.21 for two consecutive readings would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.80
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.85) was correct: total assets rose again to 7,097,316 from 7,037,258, continuing an uninterrupted expansion visible across all 13 observations. The 4-week change (+441,387) and 13-week change (+2,938,679) both remain sharply positive, and assets sit at a fresh 52-week high (assets_below_52w_high = 0). The policy language shift—from a specific minimum purchase commitment ($500B Treasuries/$200B MBS plus reinvestment) to open-ended "purchase in the amounts needed to support smooth market functioning"—removes a floor but does not announce any tapering, cap, or reduction; it is language broadening the mandate for continued purchases, not curtailing them. There is no hint of runoff, caps, or balance-sheet normalization in this language. Given the still-strong recent pace of increase and no evidence of a slowdown in the actual weekly prints, the trajectory into month-end should continue upward, though the vaguer "as needed" language introduces a little more uncertainty about pace than the explicit dollar commitments did, so I trim conviction slightly from last time.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print showing assets flat or declining, or statement language announcing tapering/capping/halting of purchases.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (0.49) sits within a choppy 0.48-0.55 range over the last 10 observations, with no clear trend continuation. My prior "up" call at 0.35 conviction was only modestly right at best — the spread did tick up intermittently but ended flat-to-lower than several intervening readings, oscillating rather than steepening cleanly. Looking at the fuller picture: slope_change_21d is +0.06pp (mildly steepening), slope_change_63d +0.22pp and slope_change_252d +0.35pp both show steepening over longer windows, and slope_range_position at 0.74 confirms the curve sits in the upper part of its 12-month range (low -0.04, high +0.68), consistent with a broader steepening trend intact since the depths of inversion. However, the most recent few prints (0.53→0.49) show a pullback, and 21d momentum is barely positive. The 10s30s context at +0.76pp is well steeper than 2s10s, suggesting long-end curve steepness generally, but that's just context per mandate. Net: the multi-month trend favors steepening, but near-term momentum has stalled and the last observation retreated to the lower end of its recent local range. This is a weak lean rather than a clear signal.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 0.45pp with 21d change turning negative would confirm renewed flattening and invalidate this lean.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation lacks inflation_expectations — Market-based inflation compensation trend would clarify if disinflation is expected to persist or reverse.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Actual pace/composition of purchases vs. stated language would clarify duration demand pressure on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment would clarify flight-to-quality vs. risk-on pressure on long yields.

This is a crisis-disinflation regime: labor shock, energy/demand collapse, and aggressive Fed balance-sheet expansion dominate the picture, while term structure and expectations remain pinned near cycle extremes with little fresh signal.

Inflation: the disinflationary shock (headline yoy to 0.20%, core PCE to 1.00%) is real and confirmed by policy language, but the pace of monthly decline is decelerating (-0.79%→-0.09%), so I keep the "down" lean but tempered — most of the move is likely already priced into the level.

Inflation expectations: breakevens are rangebound short-term (21d flat) but the 63d/252d trend and policy language both confirm a still-declining medium-term drift, well below target. I lean down, slightly firmer than the analyst's "flat" given the corroborating policy language and inflation driver's disinflation call — expectations and realized inflation should move together here.

Labor tightness: the analyst flipped from up to a cautious down/flat on a single retracement print after a catastrophic spike; one data point against a 9pp deviation from natural rate is weak evidence. I moderate the down conviction — genuine improvement is plausible but far from confirmed.

Term premium and curve slope: both flat-ish with weak, opposing longer/shorter-term signals; no strong view beyond noting yields pinned at lows and curve steepening trend stalled.

Financial conditions: clear, accelerating easing trend (NFCI to -0.21, 4w change -0.36) corroborated by aggressive Fed tool deployment — high conviction down.

Balance sheet: uninterrupted expansion to fresh 52w highs, policy language broadens rather than caps purchases — high conviction up, trimmed slightly for the removed explicit floor.

Overall this is a Fed-in-crisis-response regime: balance sheet expanding forcefully, financial conditions easing, inflation and expectations soft, labor still historically slack despite a tentative one-month improvement.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was wrong for this latest print: headline_mom rebounded sharply to +0.48%, well above my stated trigger of +0.10%, pushing headline_yoy up from 0.20% to 0.72%. This is a clear bounce off the pandemic-shock trough (headline_yoy_12m_low of 0.20%), consistent with the collapse in oil/demand from a few months prior dropping out of the y/y base. Core_pce_yoy printed flat at 1.00% (unchanged from prior month), which is the stabilization signal I flagged — it stopped falling even though it hasn't recovered. The 3m-annualized measures are still negative (headline -1.59%, core -1.18%), reflecting the deep mom prints from two-three months back, but those are aging out of the window and the most recent single-month reading (+0.48%) is far above the 3m average (-0.13%), signaling a turn in momentum rather than continuation of decline. Given the outgoing_mom of only +0.23% is being replaced by +0.48%, the mom_gap is actually negative but that's a stale comparison — the newest print itself is the strongest in over a year. I weight this shift toward a bottoming/reacceleration pattern: base effects plus a real positive monthly print argue for headline drifting higher next release.
Would change its mind if: A renewed negative or near-zero headline_mom print, or core_pce_yoy resuming its decline below 1.00%, would reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, outgoing_mom

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The breakeven series has stabilized after its earlier climb: last four readings are 1.33, 1.32, 1.34, 1.34, essentially flat around 1.33-1.34% following the run-up from 1.26 to 1.38. My prior "flat" call was directionally reasonable for the most recent leg, though I underestimated how much the 63-day change (+0.47pp) still reflects a real upward shift that has since plateaued rather than reversed. The 21-day change (+0.16pp) is now mostly noise around a new, higher plateau rather than fresh momentum — the last three prints are within a 0.02pp band. Breakeven remains 0.66pp below the 2% objective and sits at 0.64 of its 12-month range, meaning it has recovered a large share of the prior decline but is not pushing to new highs. With no fresh policy language signaling a shift in the inflation-expectations component of the reaction function, and the short-term trend now flat rather than accelerating, I see the series as consolidating. There's no evidence of renewed upward momentum or of a rollover — just sideways drift at an elevated-but-still-below-target level. This supports a flat call, slightly firmer than before given the four-observation plateau confirms stabilization rather than continued ascent.
Would change its mind if: A break above 1.38 or below 1.30 sustained for multiple readings, with be_change_21d confirming the new direction, would signal resumed momentum.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (down/improving) was directionally correct: unemployment fell from 14.80 to 13.20 to 11.00 over the last two prints, a sharp two-month decline of 3.8pp from the peak, consistent with a fast post-shock labor market recovery rather than continued deterioration. However, the level remains extraordinarily far above any natural-rate anchor (distance_from_natural +7.0pp, sahm_gap +9.5pp), and the pace of decline, while strong, follows an unprecedented spike whose trajectory is highly uncertain month to month — these initial reopening-driven drops can be lumpy and are not a reliable guide to the size of the next move. Policy language confirms the context: acknowledgment of "sharp declines in economic activity and a surge in job losses" with the Fed's crisis-response tools engaged, meaning the committee itself is treating this as a labor market still working through acute damage, not one that has stabilized. Given two consecutive months of large improvement (13.2→11.0), the momentum favors continued sequential improvement in the near term, but the magnitude of further declines is highly uncertain. I'll maintain a lean toward continued improvement (down) but temper conviction given the extreme volatility and unprecedented range this series is moving through.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 12.5% or flat near 11% would signal stalled recovery; continued drift below 10% would confirm improvement thesis.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_high, unrate_distance_from_natural, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield sits at 0.66%, near the very bottom of its 12-month range (range position 0.08, only 12bp above the 12m low of 0.54%). Recent momentum is essentially flat to mildly compressing: 21d change is 0.00pp, 63d change is -0.04pp, and the longer 252d trend of -1.35pp shows a large prior decline that has largely stalled at low levels. Neither of my prior trigger conditions was clearly met — no confirmed break below 0.60% with negative momentum, nor a move above 0.80% with steepening. My previous flat call was reasonable and the yield has indeed stayed range-bound near 0.64-0.72%, ticking up slightly to 0.66 in the latest print. The policy language shift — from "purchase" to "increase its holdings... at least at the current pace" — signals an explicit commitment to expand and sustain Treasury/MBS purchases, which is a dovish, duration-suppressing signal for the long end, reinforcing downward or flat pressure on term premium rather than upward. The 30s10s slope at 0.75pp is elevated but not sharply rising per new data. Overall evidence still supports a flat-to-slightly-down call for month-end, with modestly more confidence than before given the explicit QE reinforcement language.
Would change its mind if: A move above 0.80% with steepening 30s10s would signal rising premium; a sustained break below 0.60% with negative 21d momentum confirms further compression.

Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

The 4-week change (-0.16) and 13-week change (-0.54) both confirm a persistent, undecelerating easing trend, and the index is now well below its 13-week average (-0.03) and sits at only 27% of its 52-week range, close to the 52-week low of -0.62. Policy language also turned explicitly positive, noting financial conditions "have improved" due to policy support, reinforcing the measurement trend rather than contradicting it. There is no sign yet of stabilization — each successive weekly print has been lower than the last for eight consecutive readings. Given the strength and consistency of this momentum, plus supportive policy language, I expect the easing trend to continue into month-end, though the pace of decline has been very gradual and consistent rather than accelerating, so further easing is more likely than a sharp move.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above -0.20 or flattening for two consecutive readings would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.

=== balance_sheet ===

My prior call of "up" with 0.80 conviction was wrong for this most recent print — the weekly data turned before I recognized it. Meanwhile the statement language actually strengthened commitment to purchases ("will increase its holdings... at least at the current pace"), which is inconsistent with the two-week decline and muddies the signal: language points to continued or accelerated QE, but the measured data shows outright shrinkage in the last two observations. Given the mandate to weight recent pace over level, and over statement language when they conflict, I lean toward continued softness or flat/down movement into month-end, but with real uncertainty given the hawkish-to-dovish language mismatch and only two down-weeks of data.
Would change its mind if: A new weekly print showing assets resuming clear increases toward/above the prior 7,168,936 high would reverse this call.

=== curve_slope ===

  The 2s10s spread has been choppy but essentially range-bound over the last 10 readings, currently at 0.50pp versus 0.55pp ten observations back. The most recent moves show 0.47→0.48→0.50, a modest bounce off the recent low, and slope_change_21d is only -0.02pp — not the confirmed break below 0.45 with negative 21d momentum that would have validated a flattening call. The 63d and 252d changes are both positive (+0.03 and +0.23pp), indicating the dominant trend over the past year has actually been steepening from a much flatter base (12m low of -0.04pp). Current level sits at 0.74 of the 12-month range, well above the low, suggesting the spread remains in the upper part of its range despite the recent pullback. Policy language shift toward "at least at the current pace" for purchases signals continued or expanded accommodation, which historically supports front-end anchoring and can allow long-end steepening pressure to persist, though this is a subtle signal. My prior flat call was reasonable given mixed short-term wiggles, and the evidence still doesn't support a strong directional call — the recent uptick (0.47→0.50) mildly favors stabilization or slight re-steepening rather than continued flattening.
Would change its mind if: A break below 0.45pp with 21d and 63d changes both turning clearly negative would confirm renewed flattening.

arbitrated.notes: This is a pandemic-recovery-era snapshot with a highly unusual constellation: an economy still deeply damaged (unemployment 11%, sahm_gap +9.5pp) but with financial conditions easing fast and QE explicitly reaffirmed, while inflation is bouncing off a base-effect trough. The inflation call is credible but should be read as a base-effect/technical bounce rather than a durable trend — headline_yoy jumping off a 0.20% trough and a single strong mom print is real signal, but 3m-annualized is still deeply negative and core_pce is merely flat, not rising. I lean modestly positive on inflation but temper it: this is a turn in momentum, not a sustained acceleration yet. Inflation expectations look genuinely becalmed — a four-print plateau after the prior run-up is decent evidence of stabilization, so flat is the right call with modest conviction. Labor tightness continues to loosen (unemployment falling), but the pace is decelerating and coming off an unprecedented spike, so I keep the "down" (loosening) direction but with humility about magnitude. Financial conditions is the highest-conviction call on the panel: eight consecutive weekly declines, accelerating easing, and supportive policy language — this is a clean, low-noise trend. Balance sheet is the most conflicted signal: two weeks of asset shrinkage against explicit statement language committing to sustained/expanded purchases. I side mildly with the data turn (down) but with low conviction given only two observations and the language mismatch — this could easily reverse. Term premium and curve slope are both genuinely flat/rangebound with no confirmed breaks; I keep both flat with low conviction, leaning very slightly toward term premium compression given the QE-reinforcing language and 10y sitting near its 12m low.

Flat momentum, no confirmed break; used only as a tiebreaker, not a driver of the overall read.
Choppy and rangebound with no confirmed directional break; minimal informational value this month.

arbitrated.falsifier: A renewed negative headline CPI print with core_pce resuming its decline, alongside NFCI flattening for two weeks, would unwind this read.

risks.text: Inflation reacceleration may be purely a base-effect artifact that fades once trough comparisons roll off.
Unemployment prints are unprecedented in size and lumpy; next move's magnitude is highly uncertain.
Statement language and actual purchase data disagree, risking a sharp reversal in either direction.
Crisis-era Fed language and tools make historical relationships between drivers less reliable than normal.

why.term_premium: 10y near 12m low with QE-reinforcing language nudges toward compression, but momentum is essentially flat.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.45) was correct: headline_mom printed +0.48 then +0.51 in the last two months, well above the outgoing 0.09 and the trailing 3m average of 0.30, pushing headline_cpi_yoy up from 0.20 to 0.72 to 1.00 — a clear reacceleration off the pandemic trough. Headline is climbing rapidly off a base-effect low (12m low was 0.20%), and the 3m annualized headline pace (+3.67%) is now running well above the current y/y print, meaning further mechanical y/y increases are likely as depressed prior-year comparisons roll off. However, core_pce_yoy continues to soften (1.70→1.53→1.00→1.00→0.97), and core_3m_annualized is negative (-0.26%), indicating underlying persistent inflation is not yet participating in the bounce — the recovery is concentrated in volatile/comparison-driven components (likely energy/base effects) rather than broad-based price pressure. Given the mandate to weight headline for near-term CPI calls but flag the core divergence: the momentum and base-effect arithmetic strongly favor another higher headline print next month, even though core weakness tempers confidence this is durable. I maintain an up call, slightly higher conviction given two consecutive strong mom prints confirm the turn.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print near zero or negative, or a stalling headline_cpi_yoy despite favorable base effects, would reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_cpi_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year breakeven has climbed steadily over the last 10 readings, from 1.46 to 1.55, with the most recent print making a new local high. My prior "flat" call was wrong: the falsifier condition I set (break above 1.38 sustained with confirming 21d change) was already stale relative to the level shown here, and the series has clearly continued its upward drift rather than stalling. The 21-day change (+0.18pp) and 63-day change (+0.49pp) both confirm positive, accelerating momentum, even though the 252-day change is still negative (-0.23pp), indicating this is a shorter-term reversal within a longer decline. Breakevens sit well within the upper portion of their 12-month range (range_position 0.81), only 0.25pp below the 12-month high and far above the 12-month low, suggesting the move up is not just noise. Still 0.45pp below the 2% objective, so there is room to keep normalizing upward without being extreme. Given the consistent step-up in the last several observations and the confirming short/medium-term derivatives, I expect breakevens to be modestly higher again by month end, though the still-negative 252d change tempers full conviction.
Would change its mind if: A reading below 1.50 sustained for two or more observations, with be_change_21d turning negative, would signal the uptrend has reversed.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate has fallen sharply for three straight months after the pandemic spike: 14.80 → 13.20 → 11.00 → 10.20. My prior call (down/0.40) was correct — the print continued its rapid decline, staying well below the 12.5% threshold I flagged and consistent with the "continued drift below 10%" improvement path I outlined. The 3-month change of -4.60pp is a strong, consistent signal of labor-market healing off the peak, even though the level remains extremely elevated (6.2pp above the natural rate, sahm_gap still near 8pp). The trend is the dominant signal here: three consecutive monthly declines of roughly 1.5-2pp each show the labor market re-tightening quickly from crisis lows, even if it's nowhere near normal. Momentum has been consistent and large in magnitude, and there's no sign of stalling in the last print (10.20, still down from 11.00). Barring a plateau or reversal, the most probable next print continues this improvement trajectory, albeit likely at a decelerating pace as the easiest reopening-driven rehiring is captured. I'll maintain a "down" call, keeping conviction moderate given the level is still far from equilibrium and the pace of improvement in a recovery like this can stall unpredictably.
Would change its mind if: A flat or rising print (back toward 11% or above) would signal the recovery has stalled; continued decline toward 8-9% would confirm the improvement thesis.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_3m_avg, unrate_12m_high, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y yield has continued to grind lower, printing 0.55% at the latest reading versus 0.58-0.62% over prior observations. This is now essentially at the 12-month low (dgs10_above_12m_low +0.01pp, range_position 0.01), confirming the break below 0.60% with negative momentum that I flagged last time as a trigger for a down call. The 21d change (-0.14pp) and 63d change (-0.09pp) are both negative and accelerating rather than stabilizing, and the 252d change (-1.51pp) shows the broader trend remains firmly compressive. My prior flat call at 0.25 conviction was too cautious given this was already near the range floor; the subsequent print confirms continuation of the downtrend rather than stabilization. The 30s10s slope (+0.65pp) is elevated but not signaling a steepening shock at the long end — it reflects front-end anchoring more than a long-end premium repricing. The ongoing Fed balance-sheet expansion (Treasury/MBS purchases) is a policy backdrop consistent with continued downward pressure on long-end premium via duration absorption, not a lift. Sitting at the very bottom of the 12-month range with no evidence of reversal, I now lean toward continued modest downside in the term premium proxy, though with the yield already near historic lows, room for further compression is arithmetically limited.
Would change its mind if: A move back above 0.62% with positive 21d momentum, or a steepening 30s10s beyond +0.80pp, would signal the compression has ended.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady decline, closing at -0.46, a new multi-observation low and below the prior 52w low reference of -0.62 shown historically (current value still within range). The 4-week change is -0.07 and the 13-week change is -0.61, both confirming continued easing momentum, though the pace of weekly declines has moderated somewhat (from roughly -0.02 to -0.03 per period in earlier readings to about -0.02 in the most recent prints). My prior call of "down" with 0.65 conviction was correct: the index kept falling and never rose back above -0.20, nor did it flatten for two consecutive readings. Conditions remain well below the zero average, sitting at -0.46, roughly 0.18 of the way up the 52-week range from the low, indicating financial conditions are historically loose and still loosening. Policy language reinforces this, now emphasizing "overall" financial conditions have improved in recent months, a slightly broadened and more confident characterization than before, consistent with continued easing. With no signs of stabilization or reversal in the recent prints, the trend of easing looks intact, though the rate of decline is decelerating slightly, warranting a modest reduction in conviction relative to last time.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above -0.30 or flattening for two consecutive readings would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_13w_avg, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last several weekly prints show total assets bouncing off a local trough (6,920,716) and rising for three consecutive weeks to 6,958,604, 6,964,755, then a small pullback to 6,949,032. My prior "down" call was directionally right over the medium term (assets are well off the 52w high, down -219,904, and 4w change is still negative at -60,008), but the most recent weeks show stabilization/modest recovery rather than continued clear runoff. The 13w change is actually positive (+293,103, +4.40%), reflecting the earlier sharp expansion phase before the recent plateau/slight pullback. The policy language still speaks only of increasing holdings "at least at the current pace," which is stale/inconsistent with the actual flat-to-mixed recent prints, so I weight the measurements over the language. Given the last three prints are essentially flat with tiny wiggles (6.958M, 6.965M, 6.949M) after a bigger decline earlier, I read this as a balance sheet that has stopped its sharp swings and is now oscillating near a plateau, not clearly trending down anymore. I do not have enough evidence of a fresh strong runoff or renewed QE to call a clear direction with confidence.
Would change its mind if: A clear multi-week break either above 7,000,000 (renewed expansion) or below 6,900,000 (renewed runoff) would resolve this plateau read.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has been rangebound near 0.44-0.47pp for the entire ten-observation window, with the last two prints at 0.44pp — a slight softening from the 0.46-0.47 cluster mid-series. My prior "flat" call holds up: the 21d change is -0.08pp (a modest flattening impulse) but the 63d change is exactly 0.00pp, showing no sustained trend, and the 252d change is still +0.23pp, meaning the broader 12-month trajectory has been steepening, not flattening. The spread sits at 0.66 of its 12-month range (position between the -0.04 low and 0.69 high), well above the midpoint, so the curve is not near its most inverted levels. The 10s30s slope at 0.65pp remains firmly positive, consistent with a normally-shaped long end and no acute long-end flattening pressure. The falsifier I set previously — a break below 0.45pp with both 21d and 63d clearly negative — is only partially met: we are at 0.44pp and 21d is negative, but 63d is flat at zero, not clearly negative. This is a modest, inconclusive wobble within a well-established range rather than a confirmed trend change. I'll maintain a flat call, slightly firmer given the small recent softening, but not enough to call directional flattening.
Would change its mind if: A clear break below 0.40pp with 21d and 63d changes both clearly negative would confirm renewed flattening trend.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s

Inflation expectations (10y breakeven) are grinding higher with confirming 21d/63d momentum and a new local high, though still below the 252d trend and below target, so a moderate up call is warranted, consistent with and reinforcing the headline inflation move. Labor tightness continues to improve rapidly (unemployment falling 3 straight months) but remains far from equilibrium — momentum is strong so I keep a down call on the tightness gap (i.e., labor market tightening), but temper conviction since pace should decelerate mechanically. Term premium: 10y yield is at/near 12-month lows with negative and accelerating short/medium momentum, and this is reinforced by financial conditions easing (NFCI at multi-observation lows) which is consistent with flight-to-safety/duration demand suppressing term premium — I lean into the down call with a bit more conviction than the analyst's own 0.35 given the corroborating easing-FC backdrop, though absolute room for further compression is limited. Financial conditions are clearly and consistently loosening, a well-supported down call. Balance sheet has plateaued after the earlier runoff-then-recovery — legitimately no strong directional view, flat is right. Curve slope is rangebound with conflicting short vs long-term signals (21d flattening vs 252d steepening) — genuinely no clear direction, flat with low conviction is appropriate. Overall the macro picture is one of easing financial conditions and compressing term premium alongside a headline inflation/expectations pickup — a classic reflation-off-a-trough dynamic with the real economy (labor) still healing but far from tight.

discounted.why: Plateau/mixed recent prints give no reliable directional signal for month-end.
Conflicting short vs long-term momentum leaves no confirmed trend to act on.

arbitrated.falsifier: A stalling headline CPI print with reversing breakevens, plus a rebound in NFCI above -0.30 or 10y yield back above 0.62%, would flip this reflation-with-easing-conditions read.

risks.text: Headline inflation pickup may prove transitory if core PCE keeps softening, undermining the inflation call.
Term premium analyst lacked balance sheet data; a policy shift on purchases could reverse the compression call.
Unemployment improvement pace likely slows mechanically, and a stall could quickly flip the labor tightness call.
Breakevens remain below their 252d trend; a reversal below 1.50 would undercut the inflation expectations call.

why.inflation: Headline reacceleration off base effects is real and mechanical, but core softness caps full conviction on durability.
why.inflation_expectations: Breakevens rising with confirming short/medium momentum to new local high, but long-term trend still negative.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment falling fast three straight months signals continued tightening, but level still far from equilibrium tempers conviction.
why.term_premium: 10y at 12-month lows with accelerating negative momentum, reinforced by easing financial conditions supporting duration demand.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI at multi-observation lows, consistent 4w/13w easing momentum, policy language corroborates continued loosening (down in FC index).
why.balance_sheet: Recent prints show plateau/mixed stabilization after earlier decline; no clear directional evidence either way.
why.curve_slope: 21d flattening offset by flat 63d and positive 252d steepening; rangebound with no confirmed trend.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.

The mom_gap_vs_outgoing (+0.30pp) shows the latest month's momentum exceeds the recent average, reinforcing acceleration rather than stabilization. Weighting core as the persistence signal, but headline's momentum is the more relevant read for the next print given depressed base effects continuing to roll off. With three consecutive strong m/m prints (0.48, 0.51, 0.37) following the trough, and no sign of deceleration in the data, another increase in headline y/y looks likely, though the deceleration from 0.51 to 0.37 m/m tempers conviction slightly versus last time.

Call: up, conviction 0.65

The 10-year breakeven has risen steadily across the last ten observations (1.68→1.80), with the most recent print a fresh 12-month high (be_range_position = 1.00, be_above_12m_low = +1.30pp). Momentum is intact and accelerating: 21-day change is +0.25pp, and the 63-day change of +0.62pp shows the move has been sustained, not a single-print blip. The 252-day change of +0.25pp confirms this is not just a short-term wiggle — expectations are higher than a year ago too. Breakevens remain 0.20pp below the 2% objective (be_distance_from_target = -0.20pp), so there is no sign of expectations becoming unanchored to the upside; rather, the move reads as a normalization/reflation toward target from previously depressed levels. I maintain and slightly strengthen the "up" call given the consecutive new highs and broadening confirmation across all three lookback windows.

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (down/improving) was correct: unemployment fell again, from 10.2% to 8.4%, continuing the sharp post-pandemic-spike recovery trajectory. The series shows a clear, rapid unwind from the 14.8% peak: 14.8 → 13.2 → 11.0 → 10.2 → 8.4, a decline of 4.8pp over three months and consistent monthly improvement with no stalling. Policy language corroborates this, noting activity and employment "have picked up somewhat in recent months," a clear upgrade from the prior "sharp declines" language. That said, the level remains 4.4pp above the estimated natural rate and 4.9pp above the 12-month low, so labor market conditions are still far from tight — this is a loosening-to-tightening reversal still in its early-to-middle innings, not a return to normal. The pace of improvement has been decelerating in magnitude terms (14.8→13.2 was -1.6pp, 10.2→8.4 was -1.8pp, so not clearly decelerating yet, but off the initial ~11-13% pace). Given the strong, consistent monthly declines and supportive policy language, I expect the unemployment rate to continue falling at the next report, though further from the trough the pace may moderate. I maintain a directional call of continued improvement (down) with moderate-high conviction given the consistency of the trend.
Would change its mind if: A flat or rising print (back toward 9.5-10% or above) would signal the recovery has stalled; continued decline toward 7-8% would confirm the improvement thesis.

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.35) was wrong: yields did not resume compressing but instead pushed higher, from 0.65 up to 0.72-0.74, with the 21d change now +0.17pp — well above the 0.62% threshold with positive momentum I flagged as a reversal trigger. The 10-year sits near the very low end of its 12-month range (position 0.14, only 0.20pp above the 12m low), but the recent trend within that range is clearly upward: 21d momentum is strongly positive even as 63d is only marginally positive, suggesting the move higher is recent and accelerating rather than fading. The 30s10s slope at +0.77pp is just shy of my 0.80pp steepening trigger but close to it, consistent with mild curve steepening at the long end, which typically accompanies rising term premium (issuance/supply concerns) rather than flight-to-quality. The Fed's continued asset purchases are a offsetting anchor holding levels low in absolute terms, but the recent price action dominates. Given the clear recent uptick, the near-trigger steepening, and the failure of the prior compression thesis, I now lean toward a continued modest rise in the 10-year yield into month-end, though the low absolute level and range position temper conviction.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.65% with negative 21d momentum, or 30s10s slope falling back under +0.60pp, would signal the recent rise has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, slope_30s10s, dgs10_range_position

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:

The 4-week change (-0.05) shows the pace has slowed somewhat versus the 13-week change (-0.30), but momentum remains clearly negative with no sign of flattening or reversal — each of the last several readings has made a fresh low. The index sits well below the zero average and near the 52-week low, with range_position of only 0.12, confirming conditions are looser than average and near cycle-loose extremes. My prior "down" call was correct: the print never rose back above -0.30 nor flattened, so the easing trend is intact. Policy language reinforces this, now stating conditions "have improved in recent months," a slightly stronger and more explicit acknowledgment of easing than the prior statement. Combined with continued asset purchases supporting credit flow, this supports further gradual easing over the coming month. The main tempering factor is that the 4-week pace of decline is smaller than earlier declines, suggesting the rate of easing may be moderating even as the direction remains down.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above -0.40 or two consecutive flat/rising readings would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
