Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation shows total assets at 6,990,418, up from 6,957,277 and 6,945,237 two and three readings back — a clear bounce off the recent trough of 6,920,716. The 4-week change is now +41,386M, positive and accelerating, reversing the prior downtrend. However, the 13-week change remains negative (-106,898M, -1.51%), and assets sit well below the 52-week high (-178,518M), so this is not a resumption of sustained QE — it looks more like a data plateau/wobble with a modest recent uptick, possibly technical (bill settlements, reserve balance swings) rather than a policy shift. The policy language shown is stale QE-era language about increasing holdings "at least at the current pace," which does not match the actual multi-month decline in assets and should be discounted as non-current guidance rather than an active signal. My prior flat call was reasonable given the plateau, and the latest print's small bounce doesn't yet confirm a durable reversal — three of the last four readings still cluster in the 6.94-6.96M range before this uptick. I'll lean slightly toward flat-to-up given the recent 4-week momentum, but conviction stays low given the mixed short vs medium-term signals.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed break above 7,000,000 sustained for 3+ weeks (renewed expansion) or a drop back below 6,950,000 (resumed runoff) would resolve this.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

Call: up, conviction 0.45

Report:

The spread sits at slope_range_position 0.85 — near the top of its 12-month range (low -0.03, high +0.69), meaning most of the room to steepen further has already been used. My prior 'flat' call was wrong: the data continued to steepen modestly rather than stall, as both dgs10 and dgs2 rose but the long end rose much more (+0.72 vs +0.14), pushing the curve further away from flat/inversion. Given it's already near the top of range, further steepening at the same pace is less likely, but momentum and the still-positive 21d/63d changes argue for continued mild steepening rather than reversal. 10s30s at +0.77pp confirms the long end is not inverting either.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.50pp with negative 21d and 63d changes would signal the steepening trend has reversed into flattening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s

arbitrated.notes: The economy is in a clear post-trough reflation/recovery phase across all fronts, but the various signals are mutually consistent rather than independent surprises, so I don't stack conviction additively. Inflation is reaccelerating in both headline (strong 3m annualized, positive mom gap) and core PCE, with no deceleration signal yet — I keep this up but temper slightly given the m/m deceleration from 0.51 to 0.37. Inflation expectations corroborate this from a market-based lens: breakevens at fresh 12m highs across all three lookback windows, a cleaner and more independent confirmation of the reflation story, so this gets my highest conviction. Labor tightness is loosening rapidly (unemployment still falling sharply toward the natural rate) — this is a real, high-conviction trend but is somewhat priced/expected at this stage of recovery, so I keep it firm but not maximal. Financial conditions continue to ease, consistent with the broader reflation/recovery backdrop and supportive of the inflation and breakeven moves — a corroborating rather than surprising signal. Term premium and curve slope both point to modest continued upward pressure on long yields/steepening, driven by recent momentum, but both analysts flag they are near range extremes (10y near 12m low in level but rising fast; slope near top of 12m range), which caps my conviction — the easy part of the move is likely done. Balance sheet is genuinely ambiguous — a small recent uptick against a negative 13-week trend and stale guidance — I have no confident directional view here and lean flat. Overall: a coherent, broad-based reflation narrative, with the strongest evidence in market-based inflation expectations and labor improvement, and the weakest/most crowded evidence in duration-sensitive drivers (term premium, curve) given their extended positioning.

discounted.why: Conflicting short vs medium-term signals and stale policy language leave no confident directional view.

arbitrated.falsifier: A flat/negative headline CPI print with falling breakevens, or unemployment stalling/reversing above 9.5%, would break the reflation narrative and flip several of these calls.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI rose again to 1.39% y/y from 1.28%, and core PCE rose to 1.39% from 1.21%. Both series have now recovered from their pandemic-trough lows (0.20% and 0.97% respectively) in a steady, monotonic climb over the last 3-4 prints. Momentum confirms this: headline_mom has been positive and firming (0.48, 0.51, 0.37, 0.26), with 3m annualized headline running at 4.68% and core at 3.26% - both well above the 12-month trailing y/y readings, meaning the y/y comparisons still have upward base-effect room to run even as sequential prints moderate slightly from their peak. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing is only +0.10pp, and the latest single-month print (0.26) is below the 3m average (0.38), suggesting the sharpest re-acceleration impulse may be past its peak, but not reversing. Core remains 0.61pp below the 2% target, so this is a recovery-from-very-low-levels story rather than overheating. Given the strong recent 3m annualized pace still exceeding the trailing y/y rate, and no sign of the mom prints turning negative or flat, I expect headline CPI y/y to rise further next month, albeit the pace of increase may begin decelerating slightly given the softer latest mom reading.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print near zero or negative, or headline_cpi_yoy stalling/reversing despite favorable base effects, would reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.65) was wrong on the near-term path: breakeven fell from 1.67 to a trough of 1.58 before recovering to 1.63. That is exactly the falsifier I set — sub-1.70 readings sustained across multiple observations with be_change_21d turning negative (-0.17pp). However, the most recent three prints (1.58, 1.61, 1.64, 1.63) show a clear bounce off the low, and be_change_63d (+0.26pp) and be_change_252d (+0.10pp) remain positive, indicating the medium-term trend is still upward even though the last month was a pullback. Breakeven sits well below the 12m high (1.80) but comfortably above the 12m low (0.50), with range_position at 0.87 showing it's still in the upper part of its 12-month range despite the recent dip. The level remains 0.37pp below the 2% target, and policy language has shifted to explicitly emphasize averaging inflation moderately above 2% for some time — a dovish/reflationary framework that should support inflation expectations recovering further. Given the recent stabilization and turn upward over the last 3 observations, plus supportive policy language, I lean toward continued modest recovery rather than renewed decline, but conviction is tempered by the recent volatility and my prior miscall.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.60 sustained for two+ observations, or be_change_21d turning sharply negative again, would signal the bounce has failed.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate continued its rapid post-pandemic-spike decline: 8.40% → 7.80%, a third consecutive strong monthly improvement following the COVID shock that took the rate from 3.5% to 14.8%. My prior call (down) was correct again this release. The 3-month change of -3.20pp shows the pace of improvement remains brisk, even as the 6- and 12-month changes still reflect the enormous initial spike. The level (7.80%) is still 3.8pp above the ~4% natural rate estimate, so the labor market remains loose in absolute terms, but the trajectory is unambiguously one of rapid tightening (falling unemployment) as the economy reopens and recovers. Policy language confirms this: activity and employment "have picked up somewhat in recent months" though "remain well below" pre-crisis levels — consistent with a recovering-but-still-slack labor market. Given the consistent, large monthly declines (10.2→8.4→7.8), the momentum strongly favors another decline next month, though the pace of improvement could plausibly slow as the low-hanging reopening gains are captured. I'll maintain a directional call of down with slightly higher conviction than last time given the confirmed trend, tempered by the fact that such sharp initial recovery rates often decelerate.
Would change its mind if: A flat or rising print, or a 3-month change turning positive, would signal the recovery has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_high, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call has not played out: the 10y sits at 0.69%, essentially unchanged from a month ago and still deeply pinned near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.12, only 0.17pp above the 12m low of 0.52%). The 21d change is slightly negative (-0.03pp) and 63d change is flat (0.00pp), so momentum is not building higher. The last print did tick up from 0.66 to 0.69, a modest bounce, but it's well within noise given the tight 0.66-0.70 band of the last ten readings. Policy language reaffirms continued Treasury/MBS purchases "at least at the current pace" to sustain accommodative financial conditions, which is a lasting suppressive force on long-end yields, and the new average-inflation-targeting framework signals patience before any removal of accommodation — both keep a lid on term premium expansion. The 30s10s slope at 0.77pp remains elevated, still consistent with some steepening pressure at the very long end, but the outright 10y level shows no sustained upward trajectory. Given the flat 63d change and continued ultra-low range position, I no longer see convincing evidence of a rising term premium; the recent uptick is too small and unconfirmed to extrapolate.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break above 0.75% with positive 21d/63d momentum and 30s10s slope rising further would confirm a genuine upward move; a drop back toward 0.65% confirms continued flatness.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last two readings (-0.50, -0.50) confirm the stall I flagged as my reversal trigger: after a long easing run from -0.38 to a low of -0.51, the index has now printed flat-to-slightly-up over the last four weeks (nfci_change_4w +0.01) and effectively plateaued for three consecutive observations. My prior "down" call was correct for most of the run but the momentum has clearly faded in the newest data. Conditions remain deeply accommodative in level terms (nfci_distance_from_zero -0.50, sitting near the 52w low of -0.62, range_position 0.13), so there is no tightening signal, but the easing impulse itself has run out of steam. Policy language still reinforces "accommodative financial conditions" and continued asset purchases, which supports conditions staying loose rather than reversing, but this is consistent with a plateau rather than further easing. Given the flat 4-week change and repeated -0.50/-0.51 prints, I don't see enough momentum either way to call another leg down confidently. This looks like a genuine flat/consolidation phase at very loose levels, not a new trend.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above -0.40 would signal renewed tightening; a fresh push below -0.55 would signal the easing trend has resumed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.70
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was wrong: total assets have clearly broken above 7,000,000 and sustained there, with the last four readings (7,010,637 / 6,990,418 / 7,017,492 / 7,010,614 / 7,064,475 / 7,093,161) trending firmly upward and the most recent print (7,093,161) at a fresh high in the series, just below the 52-week high (7,168,936). The 4-week change (+102,743) is large and positive, well above the 13-week change (+10,859), indicating the pace of expansion has accelerated sharply in the most recent month rather than being a one-off blip. The 13-week average (6,991,650) sits well below the current level, confirming the recent readings are pulling the series up, not just noise around a flat trend. Policy language corroborates this: both statement vintages commit to increasing holdings of Treasuries and MBS "at least at the current pace," explicit QE language with no mention of runoff or caps. This is unambiguous ongoing balance-sheet expansion. Given the sustained multi-week rise above my prior trigger level and consistent policy language, I now see clear evidence of active QE continuing into the next month-end, likely extending the recent trajectory further, possibly approaching the 52-week high.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below ~6,990,000 sustained for 2+ weeks, or a statement shift signaling tapering/caps on purchases, would overturn this.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_13w_avg, assets_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread's last print ticked up to 0.56pp from 0.55/0.53 in the prior couple of readings, matching the series' earlier high. Over 21 days the slope is essentially flat-to-slightly-down (-0.02pp), but the 63-day change is positive (+0.04pp) and the 252-day change shows a large steepening of +0.50pp, confirming the dominant trend over the past year has been steepening from deeply compressed levels. The spread sits at 0.80 of its 12-month range (near the high end, close to the 0.69pp ceiling), well above the 0.05pp low. This positioning suggests the steepening trend, while decelerating somewhat in the very short term, remains intact rather than reversing. My prior call for continued steepening was directionally correct — the last two prints rose back to a fresh local high (0.55→0.56) after a dip. The 21d softness is minor noise, not a reversal, since it hasn't broken below 0.50pp nor shown coordinated negative 21d/63d readings — my stated falsifier condition was not met. Given the spread is now near a 12-month high and 63d/252d momentum remain positive, I lean toward continued mild steepening, though the flattening in the very recent 21d window tempers conviction somewhat.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.50pp with negative 21d and 63d changes together would signal the steepening trend has reversed into flattening.

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Realized CPI trend would clarify whether breakeven bounce reflects genuine reflation or noise.
  inflation_expectations lacks financial_conditions — Easing or tightening conditions affect risk appetite for inflation-linked assets and breakeven demand.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/composition of Treasury purchases directly affects long-end supply-demand and term premium.
  term_premium lacks curve_slope — Fuller front-end context would clarify whether 10s2s steepening reflects long-end premium vs front-end anchoring.

arbitrated.notes: This is a recovery-from-COVID-trough macro picture, consistent across drivers rather than contradictory. Inflation (headline and core) is climbing steadily off pandemic lows with 3m-annualized rates above trailing y/y, so the y/y print should keep rising near-term even as the monthly pace decelerates slightly — a clear but moderating "up." Inflation expectations tell a softer, secondary version of the same story: breakevens dipped and are bouncing, medium-term momentum (63d/252d) still positive, but the analyst's own prior miss and the shallow bounce warrant only modest conviction. Labor tightening is unambiguous: unemployment is falling fast and consistently (10.2→8.4→7.8), so tightening (falling unemployment) continues, though the pace of decline should be expected to decelerate as reopening gains are captured — I read this as the labor_tightness metric rising (tightening) even though absolute slack remains. Term premium and financial conditions both look like stalled/plateaued series after strong prior trends — the data no longer supports extrapolating the old trend, and policy language (continued QE, average-inflation targeting) argues for continued suppression of term premium and continued loose-but-flat financial conditions. Balance sheet expansion is the most unambiguous call — accelerating QE with fresh highs and explicit policy commitment to continued purchases at current pace. Curve slope shows continued mild steepening, trend intact on 63d/252d despite noisy 21d, consistent with the balance-sheet-driven long end and gradual growth optimism steepening the front-belly relationship. Overall: inflation and growth normalization is progressing, Fed balance-sheet expansion is the dominant, most confidently forecastable driver, term premium and financial conditions are in a holding pattern rather than resuming their prior trends.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline_cpi_yoy ticked down to 1.23% from 1.39%, with headline_mom slowing sharply to 0.12% from 0.26% — my prior "up" call was wrong on this final print, as momentum decelerated rather than continued rising. That said, the broader trend since the trough (0.20% y/y) remains a recovery: headline is up 1.03pp from the 12m low, and 3m-annualized headline (3.08%) and core (3.27%) both still run above the recent y/y levels, implying some further upward pressure is still baked in via base effects even as monthly prints cool. Core PCE has been climbing more steadily (0.97→1.48 over last four prints), suggesting underlying persistence firmed even as headline momentum stalled. The policy language shift toward explicitly seeking inflation "moderately above 2%" for a sustained period signals the Committee views current readings as still-too-low and is not fighting a re-acceleration — consistent with least resistance being upward. Given the deceleration in the immediate mom print, I lower conviction but do not flip the sign: the 3m annualized pace still exceeds the trailing yoy, so the arithmetic favors another modest yoy increase next month even after this month's soft print.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print near zero/negative again, or headline_cpi_yoy falling further despite the 3m-annualized pace exceeding trailing yoy, would reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, yoy_above_12m_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last release: prior call was "up" at 0.35 conviction. The newest observations show breakeven_10y has actually softened slightly and flattened — 1.72→1.71→1.70→1.71→1.70 over the last five prints, having pulled back from the local peak of 1.76. That prior call is looking marginal at best: the very short-term trend has stalled/reversed modestly, even though the medium-term changes (63d +0.15, 252d +0.09, 21d +0.07) are still net positive. The series sits near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.92, only 0.10pp below the 12m high of 1.80) and is still 0.30pp below the 2% target, so there's no urgency from the target-gap side. The new policy language reinforces an explicit average-inflation-targeting framework aiming for inflation "moderately above 2 percent for some time" — a a dovish tilt that argues for expectations staying supported rather than collapsing, but it doesn't explain the last five flat-to-down prints. Given the tension between stalling recent prints and the standing average-inflation-targeting commitment plus elevated range position, I see this as closer to flat with a mild upward lean rather than a continuation of a clear uptrend. My previous falsifier condition wasn't fully triggered (no break below 1.60), but the sharp deceleration in 21d momentum is a caution flag.
Would change its mind if: A clear break below 1.65 sustained 2+ observations, or 21d change turning negative beyond -0.05pp, would confirm downside; a fresh push above 1.76 would confirm upside.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: unemployment fell again, from 7.80% to 6.90%, continuing the sharp post-pandemic-spike recovery. The trend remains firmly one of rapid labor market tightening (loosening in unemployment terms): 3m change -3.30pp, 6m change -7.90pp, and the rate has fallen every month since the 14.80% peak. However, the level is still 2.90pp above the ~4% natural rate, and the pace of monthly declines has been decelerating (8.40→7.80→6.90 is a ~0.9pp drop, similar to prior months, but off a much higher base earlier in the recovery — the huge early declines like 13.20→11.00 are unlikely to repeat as the rate normalizes). The 12m change is still positive (+3.30pp) reflecting the crisis scarring, but this lags the recent trajectory which is unambiguously improving. Absent evidence of stalling (flat or rising prints), the continued monthly declines and strong momentum support another decrease next month, though likely at a more moderate pace given diminishing returns as the rate approaches more normal levels. I maintain a directional call of continued improvement (down) but temper conviction slightly given the deceleration in the magnitude of monthly drops.
Would change its mind if: A flat or rising print, or a stalling 3-month change near zero, would signal the recovery has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y has pushed to 0.88%, the highest print in this series, extending a rising trend that began several months ago. My prior "flat" call was wrong: 21d (+0.19pp) and 63d (+0.33pp) momentum are both clearly positive and have strengthened since the last release, and the yield broke above the 0.75% level I flagged as the threshold for a genuine upward move. The 252d change is still deeply negative (-0.96pp), showing the level remains far below where it was a year ago, but that's a longer base effect, not the current signal. Range position of 0.25 shows the yield is still in the lower quartile of its 12-month range, so there is room to rise further before hitting resistance near the highs. The 30s10s slope at +0.77pp is steep, consistent with continued long-end cheapening, likely reflecting increased Treasury issuance and Fed asset-purchase language that supports market functioning rather than aggressively suppressing long yields. With momentum, slope, and the recent breakout all pointing the same direction, I'm updating to a clear "up" call for month-end, though I stay shy of very high conviction given the level is still low in absolute and historical-range terms.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below 0.80% with negative 21d momentum and flattening 30s10s slope would signal the upward move has stalled.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print is -0.50, right in the middle of the recent -0.51 to -0.49 band the index has occupied for the last several readings. My prior flat call holds up: neither the -0.40 tightening threshold nor the -0.55 easing threshold was breached. The 4-week change is essentially zero (-0.01) and the 13-week change is a modest -0.04, both consistent with a plateau rather than a trend. Conditions remain deeply accommodative in absolute terms (-0.50 vs. zero average, near the 52-week low of -0.62, range position of only 0.13), but the pace of further easing has clearly stalled after the earlier improvement. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed shifted wording to explicitly note it will act to "help foster accommodative financial conditions," an addition suggesting the central bank is actively working to keep conditions loose amid what could otherwise be a stabilization or mild reversal. This is supportive of continued looseness but doesn't signal fresh easing momentum. Absent a clear break in either direction, the most defensible call remains flat, with the index likely oscillating in the -0.49 to -0.51 zone into next month-end.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above -0.40 would signal renewed tightening; a fresh push below -0.55 would signal the easing trend has resumed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_13w_avg

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: assets rose from 7,151,426 to 7,146,306 net over the last two prints but the broader trend remains clearly expansionary — 4-week change +90,177M and 13-week change +197,274M, both positive, with the series sitting at/near the 52-week high (only ~31,000M below it). The most recent single observation ticked down slightly (7,177,265 → 7,146,306), but this is noise within an otherwise steady uptrend, not a reversal — it doesn't come close to the ~6,990,000 sustained-reversal threshold I set as my falsifier. Policy language remains unambiguously QE-supportive: the Fed reiterates it "will increase its holdings of Treasury securities and agency MBS at least at the current pace" — no mention of tapering, caps, or runoff. The wording change (dropping reference to "commercial mortgage-backed securities" and rephrasing "smooth market functioning" language) is stylistic, not a substantive shift toward tightening. With purchases continuing at pace and no announced reduction, I expect total assets to be higher again at next month-end, consistent with continued balance-sheet expansion. The one-observation dip is worth watching but not yet a trend break.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed multi-week reversal back toward ~6,990,000 or below, or explicit statement language announcing tapering/caps on purchases.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call for continued steepening was correct: slope_2s10s rose from 0.69 to 0.74pp over the latest observation, extending the uptrend. The spread has climbed steadily from a 12-month low of 0.12pp to the current 0.74pp, which is also the 12-month high (range position 1.00). All momentum measures point the same direction: 21d change +0.18pp, 63d +0.30pp, 252d +0.54pp — steepening is broad-based across horizons, not a short-term blip. The 10s30s slope at 0.77pp is also elevated, consistent with long-end steepening pressure rather than an isolated 2s10s move. Policy language shows a shift toward more explicit forward guidance tying rate liftoff to outcome-based thresholds (employment and inflation levels), plus continued asset purchases — language consistent with anchoring the front end while the long end responds to growth/inflation expectations, which tends to support further steepening. With the spread sitting at a fresh 12-month high and momentum consistently positive across all three lookback windows, the trend is intact. However, since the spread is already at the top of its range, some mean-reversion or pause risk exists, tempering conviction slightly below the previous call despite confirmation.
Would change its mind if: A reversal below 0.65pp with negative 21d change would signal the steepening trend has stalled or turned.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Realized CPI surprises often precede breakeven moves; would clarify if pullback is durable.
  inflation_expectations lacks term_premium — Distinguishing real yield vs breakeven-specific moves would clarify true expectation shift.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/composition of Fed Treasury purchases directly affects long-end supply/demand and term premium.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader financial conditions tightening/easing could confirm whether long-end move is durable.

arbitrated.notes: The macro picture is one of a Fed still in full accommodation mode against a real economy that is normalizing faster than nominal markets are repricing. Inflation itself is soft-but-still-drifting-up: the latest mom print decelerated sharply, undercutting the prior "up" call, but base-effect arithmetic (3m annualized > trailing yoy) and firming core PCE keep the lean modestly positive rather than flat. Inflation expectations, by contrast, have stalled — breakevens are flat-to-down over the last several prints despite dovish average-inflation-targeting language, so I read this as genuinely flat with only a marginal upward tilt from the policy backstop, not the "up" call the analyst inherited. Labor tightness continues to improve (unemployment falling) but decelerating in magnitude, which keeps the "down" call intact but not accelerating. The rates complex is the most internally consistent story here: term premium is breaking out to fresh highs with strengthening 21d/63d momentum, curve slope is steepening broadly across horizons and sits at a 12-month high, and balance sheet expansion continues at pace with no taper language — all three reinforce a coherent supply/duration story (continued QE + steepening + rising term premium) even though it looks like tension (why would term premium rise while the Fed buys duration?) — the resolution is that purchases are anchoring the front end while the long end cheapens on issuance/growth expectations, exactly as the curve_slope analyst argued. Financial conditions remain flat/accommodative, consistent with a market not yet stressed by the long-end move. I weight the rates cluster (term premium, curve slope, balance sheet) most heavily since they're mutually corroborating and freshly formed; inflation and labor get moderate weight; inflation expectations gets down-weighted to near-flat given the stalled breakeven prints contradicting the inherited "up" bias.

If unemployment declines stall further, the labor-tightening narrative supporting curve steepening could reverse quickly.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline y/y ticked down to 1.18 from 1.23, continuing the slight softening my prior "up" call anticipated wrongly — momentum did not reverse; headline mom actually rose to 0.23 from 0.12, a firming rather than a fresh weakening. arbitrated.notes: Reconciling the panel: inflation momentum (headline mom, core 3m annualized) is edging up but y/y prints are still soft, so I keep a modest up tilt on inflation but temper it since the analyst's own conviction was middling and the driver is somewhat entangled with the stronger inflation_expectations signal. Breakevens have cleanly broken to a new local high with accelerating 21d/252d changes and policy language tolerant of overshoot — this is the most convincing directional call on the panel, so I lean on it most heavily, tempered only slightly by proximity to the 12m ceiling. Labor tightness continues to improve (unemployment falling) but the pace is decelerating sharply, so I keep the "down" call (unemployment falling = tightness rising) but with moderate conviction given the flattening trend. Term premium (10y yield) shows stalling momentum at the low end of its range; the modest up-call is credible but weak — recent flat prints and Fed QE support argue against a breakout, so I keep only slight upward tilt. Financial conditions show renewed easing (NFCI falling) with confirming policy language and multi-week momentum — I trust this fairly strongly. Balance sheet expansion remains intact per 4w/13w trend and explicit QE-at-least-current-pace language; the one-week dip is noise, so I keep a solid up call, and this reinforces why term premium is capped (Fed absorption of duration muting term premium despite curve steepening pressure elsewhere). Curve slope is genuinely stalled in the last two prints despite a positive medium-term drift — I call this closer to flat with slight steepening bias, not a strong call either way. Overall this is a moderately consistent read: expectations rising, financial conditions loosening, balance sheet still growing, but term premium and curve slope lack conviction due to recent stalling, and labor tightening is decelerating.

Given the accelerating mom trend and rising headline momentum outpacing a still-soft core, I lean toward another higher headline print next month, though core's tepid 3m pace tempers conviction that this is a strong regime shift rather than a bounce.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous call for "up" was correct: headline y/y rose from 1.32 to 1.37 and core PCE y/y rose from 1.45 to 1.51, continuing the recovery from the pandemic trough. However, momentum is decelerating within that uptrend: headline_mom fell to 0.24% from 0.43% the prior month, and the 3m annualized headline pace (3.69%) is still running hotter than trailing y/y, but the month-over-month gap versus the outgoing print (mom_gap_vs_outgoing +0.25pp) shows deceleration, not acceleration, in the most recent single month. Core PCE's 3m annualized (1.86%) is now below its y/y level, suggesting core momentum has flattened rather than continuing to build. The y/y series has climbed steadily for four straight readings, which is a real trend, but the deceleration in monthly core and headline momentum (0.51→0.37→0.26→0.12→0.23→0.43→0.24) suggests the y/y trend may be near an inflection or at least slowing its ascent. Given base effects likely still supportive from the very low prints a year earlier, another modest y/y increase is more probable than a reversal, but the momentum slowdown tempers conviction versus last time.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print below zero or core_3m_annualized dropping further below core y/y would signal the uptrend has ended.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was validated: breakeven_10y moved from a flat ~2.08-2.10 range to 2.13 in the last two observations, printing at the 12-month high (be_range_position = 1.00). Momentum indicators confirm the uptrend across all horizons: +0.17pp over 21 days, +0.42pp over 63 days, and +0.48pp over 252 days — all positive and accelerating in the near term relative to the longer trend, suggesting the move is not decelerating. The series is now 0.13pp above the 2% target, meaning market-implied inflation expectations have pushed moderately above the Committee's longer-run objective. Given the stated average-inflation-targeting framework, this modest overshoot is broadly consistent with policy language calling for inflation "moderately above 2 percent for some time," which may keep breakevens anchored slightly above target rather than triggering a sharp reversal. With the series sitting at its 12-month high and short-term momentum still positive, I see continuation as more likely than reversal, but the string of flat prints just before the recent jump (2.08 three times) urges some caution about extrapolating two data points into a full trend. I maintain an up call, similar conviction, given confirmed continuation but with limited additional confirmation beyond the last two prints.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.08 sustained for 2+ observations, or be_change_21d turning negative, would signal the uptrend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 6.40% from two consecutive 6.70% readings, resuming the downtrend after a brief stall. My prior "down" call at 0.30 conviction was correct in direction and this new data reinforces it. The 3-month change (-0.50pp) and especially the 6-month change (-3.80pp) show a clear, if decelerating, improvement trend from the pandemic-era peak of 14.80%. The unemployment rate remains 2.40pp above the ~4% natural rate estimate, meaning there is still substantial room for further tightening (declines) in the unemployment rate before the labor market normalizes. The 12-month change (+2.80pp) is a stale artifact of the pandemic shock and shouldn't be read as a Sahm-rule recessionary signal here — the trailing 12-month low (3.50%) predates the crisis, and the recent trajectory is unambiguously downward from the crisis peak. Range position (0.26 of 0-1) confirms we're closer to the low end of the post-shock range, consistent with continued recovery. Given the resumed decline after two flat prints, I'm modestly more confident than before that the next print continues lower, though the pace of improvement has clearly slowed compared to the sharp early recovery months.
Would change its mind if: A print at or above 6.70% (flat/uptick) would signal the recovery has genuinely stalled, shifting my call to flat or up.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield sits at 1.11%, up from 1.07% last observation and near the top of its recent range within this 10-print window. The prior "flat" call was too cautious given the trend: 21d change (+0.17pp) and 63d change (+0.32pp) both show clear upward momentum, even though the 252d change remains negative (-0.54pp), reflecting a much lower starting base a year ago. Range position at 0.52 is mid-range, but the recent bounce off the 1.04-1.05 local low (three readings back) followed by a steady climb to 1.11 shows the level breaking above the threshold I previously set (0.96%) for reviving the up case, with rising short-term momentum intact. The 30s10s slope at 0.76pp is a decent positive continuation of long-end curve steepness, not flattening, which supports the up case for further term premium expansion rather than compression. The ongoing $80bn/month Treasury purchases (unchanged language) should be a dampening force on term premium, but the price action is overriding that in the recent prints. I am revising from flat to a modest up lean: the data show consistent upward drift in yields over the last two windows even if the very long lookback is still negative.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.05% with negative 21d momentum, or a flattening 30s10s slope, would reverse this up call toward flat/down.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, slope_30s10s, dgs10_range_position

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues to grind lower, printing new 52-week lows at -0.62, though the pace has decelerated sharply. The prior call (down, 0.60) was directionally correct: the index fell from -0.61 to -0.62 over the period, extending the easing trend. However, momentum is clearly flattening — nfci_change_4w is now essentially flat at -0.01, versus the steeper -0.12 over 13 weeks, indicating the easing trajectory is losing steam and conditions are stabilizing near the lows. The index sits at the bottom of its 52-week range (range_position ~0), at -0.62, far below the zero average, so conditions remain very loose in absolute terms. Policy language is unchanged, still describing accommodative conditions supported by asset purchases, with no hint of tapering — consistent with continued (if decelerating) ease rather than a reversal. Given the near-zero 4-week change, I lean toward continued mild easing or flat, but the deceleration in momentum means I can't have high conviction in further material declines. The most likely near-term path is a continuation of the shallow drift down or a flattening near current lows, absent any shift in policy language.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above -0.55 sustained for 2+ weeks, or explicit policy language signaling tapering/tightening intent, would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.72
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call is confirmed: fed_assets rose from 7,333,968 to 7,414,942 over the last two prints, and the 52-week high was just set at the latest observation (assets_below_52w_high = -10,016, essentially at the high). The 4-week change (+41,575) and 13-week change (+258,620, +3.62%) both remain solidly positive, consistent with active QE rather than runoff. The series shows some week-to-week noise (e.g., a dip from 7,404,039 to 7,363,351 mid-series), but the trend over the full 13 observations is clearly upward, and the most recent value is near the cycle high. Policy language is unchanged and explicitly commits to continued purchases of at least $80B/month Treasuries and $40B/month MBS until substantial further progress is made — there is no taper or cap language present. Given the combination of a still-rising 4-week and 13-week trend, a fresh 52-week high, and unchanged expansionary language, I maintain the expansion call for the coming month. Conviction is slightly tempered by the intra-period volatility (the dip and rebound pattern suggests the pace, while positive, is not perfectly smooth).
Would change its mind if: A confirmed multi-week decline in fed_assets back toward ~7,150,000 or below, or explicit statement language announcing tapering or capping purchases.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call missed the follow-through: slope_2s10s printed 1.00 at the latest observation, a fresh 12-month high (slope_range_position 0.99), up from a dip to 0.92 a few readings back. Momentum is clearly positive across horizons: +0.18pp over 21d, +0.37pp over 63d, and +0.80pp over 252d, all pointing the same direction. The spread sits 0.88pp above its 12-month low and only 0.01pp from its 12-month high, meaning the trend has essentially reached a new extreme rather than stalling or reversing. The recent dip to 0.92 looks like noise within an otherwise persistent steepening trend, not a reversal — the last print snapped back to the cycle high. The 10s30s slope at 0.76pp confirms the long end is also steep, consistent with broad-based steepening rather than a narrow 2s10s quirk. With the curve pressing against its range ceiling and momentum still positive at every horizon, the most likely near-term path is continued steepening or at least a hold near these highs, though a print at the very top of a 12-month range does raise some risk of consolidation. I lean toward further steepening but temper conviction given proximity to the range high.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below ~0.90pp with negative 21d change would signal the steepening trend has stalled at the range high.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/composition of Treasury purchases directly affects long-end supply-demand and term premium.
  term_premium lacks curve_slope — Front-end curve context would clarify whether long-end move reflects term premium or policy-path repricing.

arbitrated.notes: Panel paints a consistent reflation/steepening picture with decelerating momentum across the board — a classic mid-cycle recovery signature rather than an inflection. Inflation is still rising y/y but monthly momentum has clearly cooled (core PCE 3m annualized now below y/y), so I keep the up call but with reduced conviction versus the analyst's own framing. Inflation expectations are more convincing: breakevens at 12-month highs with positive momentum across all three horizons, and an average-inflation-targeting framework that tolerates overshoot — I lean on this more heavily. Labor tightness resumed its decline after a stall, with unemployment still 2.4pp above the natural rate estimate, supporting continued (if decelerating) tightening. Term premium and curve slope are two sides of the same steepening move: 10y yields and 2s10s both broke to fresh highs with positive momentum at all horizons, and the panel itself flagged that these two calls are related (30s10s corroborates both) — I take a coherent steepening view across both rather than double-counting independently. Financial conditions are easing but losing momentum, sitting at 52-week lows with a nearly flat 4-week change; I keep a mild down/easing lean but recognize this is closer to stabilization than a real trend continuation. Balance sheet expansion is the highest-conviction call — fresh 52-week high, unchanged QE language, no taper signal — and it mechanically supports the term premium/curve steepening thesis via long-end supply dynamics, reinforcing rather than contradicting those views. Overall: a reflationary, steepening regime, still intact but showing broad-based deceleration that argues for trimming conviction versus the analysts' own stated levels, especially on inflation and financial conditions where momentum indicators are flattest.

discounted.why: Momentum deceleration undercuts the y/y trend story; kept but at reduced weight.
Near-zero 4-week change suggests stabilization, not a trend I want to size heavily.

arbitrated.falsifier: A reversal in yields/breakevens back below recent range floors (10y<1.05%, breakeven<2.08, 2s10s<0.90) alongside a flat/negative CPI print would flip this to a stalling-recovery read.

risks.text: Multiple drivers (inflation, financial conditions) show decelerating momentum, raising risk of an unrecognized inflection point.
Term premium and curve slope calls are highly correlated; treating them as independent risks overstating the steepening thesis.
Any taper signal on balance sheet purchases would simultaneously undercut balance sheet, term premium, and curve slope calls.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 13 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose from 1.37 to 1.67 (+0.30pp), and the falsifier condition (negative mom print or decelerating core annualized) did not occur — headline_mom was a solid +0.34%, in line with its 3m average. Momentum remains clearly positive: headline_3m_annualized (4.15%) is running well above the latest y/y rate (1.67%), meaning further monthly prints at this pace will keep pulling the y/y figure up over the next several releases via base effects. Core PCE y/y also climbed to 1.70%, up 0.28pp over three months, and its 3m annualized pace (2.97%) is now above the 2% target, reinforcing that underlying inflation is genuinely firming rather than just reflecting energy/food noise (core_minus_headline is near zero, so the two measures agree). The 12m range position sits at the top of its range (1.00), confirming this is a clear uptrend rather than noise around a low base. Policy language softened slightly (dropped "in the near term") but still frames inflation as held down by weak demand and past oil declines — a backward-looking description that hasn't caught up to the recent acceleration. Given consistent mom prints all quarter and rising 3m annualized rates on both headline and core, I expect headline CPI y/y to rise again at the next release.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print near/below zero, or headline_3m_annualized falling back toward or below the current y/y rate, would signal the acceleration has stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last ten readings show breakeven_10y drifting sideways-to-slightly-down at the front (2.21→2.24→2.21→2.16→2.14→2.16→2.16→2.17→2.14→2.15) after an earlier climb. My prior call of "up" was based on the strong 63d/252d momentum (+0.41/+0.62 pp), and that longer-run trend evidence remains intact and hasn't reversed — the series never broke below the 2.08 falsifier level I set, and be_change_21d is still positive (+0.07 pp). So the previous call wasn't wrong, but the most recent observations show the level essentially flat and rangebound in the 2.14-2.17 band for the last several prints, well off the earlier highs of 2.21-2.24. The series is sitting near the top of its 12-month range (be_range_position 0.95) and 0.15pp above the 2% target, consistent with anchoring "moderately above 2%" per the Committee's stated framework, but the momentum that drove the earlier rise has clearly stalled — this looks like consolidation rather than continued ascent. Given the flattening in the last 5 observations despite a positive 21d change, I lean toward flat with a modest upward tilt reflecting the still-positive longer-term trend and proximity to range highs, rather than a fresh confident "up" call.
Would change its mind if: A break above 2.20 sustained for 2+ observations would revive the uptrend; a drop below 2.10 would signal renewed softening.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (down) was correct: unemployment fell from 6.40% to 6.20%, continuing the post-pandemic recovery trend. The trailing trend remains clearly improving: -0.50pp over 3m, -2.20pp over 6m, though still +2.70pp above the 12m low (reflecting the pandemic spike base) — that 12m-change figure is a legacy of the crash, not a current signal. The recent trajectory (7.80→6.90→6.70→6.70→6.40→6.20) shows steady, if decelerating, improvement with no reversal. However, the newly changed policy language is notable: it now explicitly states the pace of recovery "has moderated in recent months," with weakness concentrated in pandemic-sensitive sectors. This is a downgrade from continued brisk recovery to a slower, uneven one — consistent with the last print's deceleration (6.70→6.40→6.20 is still improvement, but the 6.70→6.70 flat month shows moderation already visible). Given the level is still 2.2pp above the ~4% natural rate, there is room for further improvement, but the moderating pace suggests smaller monthly declines ahead rather than a reversal. I lean toward continued improvement (down) but with reduced conviction given the explicit acknowledgment of moderation.
Would change its mind if: A print at or above 6.20% (flat/uptick) would confirm the moderation has stalled the improvement trend entirely.

The 10y sits at 0.90 of its 12-month range (near the top), well above the 12m low (0.92pp above), confirming the move up in long-end yields has been substantial and durable. The 30s10s slope at +0.73pp remains steep rather than flattening, which is inconsistent with a reversal signal and instead suggests continued upward pressure or at least no imminent retracement in the term premium. With both the measured pace and the stated reinvestment/purchase policy pointing the same direction, this is a clear, low-ambiguity QE expansion signal. Absent any statement change (taper announcement, purchase reduction) or a multi-week reversal in the weekly prints, the balance sheet should continue expanding into next month at a similar or slightly faster pace given the recent 4-week acceleration.

This is the first down-tick after a long, persistent climb (1.09→1.37 over the sample), so the multi-month steepening trend is intact in level terms: slope sits at 1.14pp above its 12-month low, at 0.94 of its 12-month range (essentially at the top), and 21d/63d/252d changes are all strongly positive. Given the curve is stretched near range highs after a large run, I lean toward a stall or slight give-back rather than a continuation of aggressive steepening, but the underlying trend remains up, not reversing into flattening territory.
Would change its mind if: A further drop below ~1.15pp with negative 21d change would confirm the steepening trend has genuinely reversed, not just paused.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.55) was correct: headline CPI y/y jumped from 1.67 to 2.67, a large +1.00pp move, confirming the acceleration rather than a stall. Momentum remains strong and building: headline_mom just printed 0.52%, the highest in the 13-month window, pushing headline_3m_annualized to 4.53%, well above the current y/y rate itself — meaning further y/y acceleration is baked in unless monthly prints suddenly decelerate sharply. Core PCE is also accelerating, now at 1.71% y/y (up from 0.97% at the recent trough), with core_3m_annualized at 3.44%, still comfortably above the 3m annualized pace needed to hold y/y flat. The gap between headline (2.67%) and core PCE (1.71%) reflects energy/base effects, but I weight core for persistence, and even core is clearly trending up, not just headline. With headline_mom_3m_avg at 0.37% versus outgoing_mom of -0.79% (the month rolling off), the math strongly favors another y/y increase next release: dropping a deeply negative month from the base while adding a strong new month mechanically lifts y/y further. Policy language changes are minor (tense/wording only) and don't alter the inflation signal. No evidence here suggests deceleration; base effects and current momentum both point the same direction.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print near/below zero, or a sharp drop in headline_3m_annualized toward or below the new 2.67% y/y rate, would signal deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was wrong: breakeven_10y has continued grinding higher, closing at 2.37, a fresh 12-month high (be_range_position = 1.00). The 21d and 63d changes are both positive and accelerating in magnitude relative to level (+0.17pp and +0.41pp), and the 252d change of +1.43pp shows this is a sustained, not transient, repricing of inflation expectations. The series shows only minor pullbacks within an otherwise steady uptrend (e.g., 2.33→2.29, 2.36→2.35), with each dip quickly reversed to a new high. Breakevens are now 0.37pp above the 2% objective, and policy language explicitly tolerates inflation running moderately above target for a time, which removes any signal that policymakers would push back against the current level. With the series sitting at its 12-month high and momentum still positive over multiple windows, the path of least resistance is further upside or at minimum consolidation at these elevated levels. There is no evidence in the breakeven data itself of stalling or reversal. I am raising conviction from the prior flat call to a modest up call, tempered by the fact that the last two prints (2.36→2.35→2.37) show some choppiness rather than clean acceleration.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 2.30 sustained for 2+ observations, or be_change_21d turning negative, would signal the uptrend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 6.10% continued the decline from 6.20%, so the prior "down" call was directionally correct despite the policy language flagging moderation. Looking at the trend: unemployment has fallen steadily from a pandemic peak of 14.80% to 6.10%, with the 3m change at -0.60pp and 6m change at -1.70pp — still clearly improving, though the pace of improvement has slowed (each recent monthly decline is only 0.1-0.2pp vs sharper drops earlier). The rate sits at the 12-month low (unrate_above_12m_low = 0.00), confirming no loosening currently, and the Sahm gap of just +0.13pp is far from triggering recession-signal territory. Policy language explicitly notes "the pace of the recovery... has moderated in recent months, with weakness concentrated in sectors most adversely affected by the pandemic" — this suggests deceleration in job gains, which could plausibly translate into a stalling unemployment rate rather than continued decline. Given the deceleration signal now embedded in the language change, combined with the market still being 2.10pp above the natural rate (room to keep improving), I lean toward continued modest improvement but with reduced confidence given the explicit moderation callout. The trend still favors further(if slower) decline rather than reversal.
Would change its mind if: A print at or above 6.20% would confirm the moderation has stalled the improvement trend entirely.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

  The 10Y sits at 1.74%, exactly at its 12-month high (range_position = 1.00), and the last print confirms the prior call: yields continued grinding higher, with 21d change +0.32pp and 63d +0.80pp still positive, and 252d +1.02pp showing a sustained year-long climb. The last two readings (1.73, 1.74) show momentum decelerating slightly versus the sharp move from 1.62→1.74 over the prior several observations, but there is no reversal signal — no pullback, no negative momentum. The 30s10s slope at +0.67pp remains firmly positive, not flattening toward zero, which was my stated reversal trigger; it hasn't fired. With yields pinned at the top of the 12-month range and ongoing Fed purchases still described as continuing (unchanged QE pace language), there's no fresh flight-to-quality bid evident, and the long end continues to absorb term premium pressure consistent with supply/duration concerns. This validates the previous up call. That said, sitting at the very top of a 12-month range with decelerating momentum warrants some caution about further acceleration — this looks more like a plateau-at-highs than a fresh breakout, so I temper conviction slightly rather than raise it.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward mid-range with negative 21d momentum, or 30s10s slope compressing toward zero, would flip this toward flat/down.

The 4-week change (-0.02) and 13-week change (-0.04) both confirm a gentle but persistent easing trend rather than a plateau. My prior "flat" call undersold this: conditions have not stabilized at -0.62, they have kept drifting looser, now printing a new cycle low. The move is small in absolute magnitude and the trend is not accelerating (change of -0.02 over 4 weeks is modest), but it is consistent and one-directional across the last five-plus readings, and policy language continues to affirm accommodation with no hint of tightening bias. Given the persistence of the drift and the absence of any offsetting language, I lean toward continued easing into month-end rather than a stall. Conviction is moderate rather than high because the magnitude of weekly moves is small (0.01 index per step) and could flatten out again, but the directional consistency over 13 weeks argues against calling this flat again.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back toward -0.62 or higher sustained for 2+ weeks, or policy language signaling withdrawal of accommodation.

The 4-week change (+129,511M) and 13-week change (+315,583M, +4.26%) both remain firmly positive, and the series is sitting at its 52-week high with zero drawdown from that high. The trajectory over the full 13-week window shows a steady climb from ~7.33T to ~7.72T, consistent with ongoing large-scale asset purchases rather than any plateau or reversal. Policy language remains explicit and unchanged: the Committee commits to increasing Treasury holdings by at least $80B/month and agency MBS by at least $40B/month until substantial further progress is made — this is textbook QE language with no hint of tapering, capping, or tapering discussion. There is no evidence of runoff, no announced caps being hit, and no decline in any window (4w/13w/52w all positive). Given the consistency between the stated reinvestment/purchase policy and the observed balance sheet growth, this is a high-confidence continuation call. Nothing here suggests deceleration in pace; the 4-week pace is actually only modestly below the stated $120B/month combined pace, consistent with continued full-speed purchases.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed multi-week decline in fed_assets, or explicit statement language announcing tapering, capping, or ending asset purchases.

  The spread series ends 1.55,1.58,1.54,1.48,1.48,1.49,1.53,1.59,1.57,1.58 — a dip mid-series followed by a recovery back to the high end. My prior "flat" call was too cautious: the last three prints (1.53→1.59→1.57→1.58) show the steepening trend reasserting after a brief pause, not reversing. slope_range_position is 0.99, meaning the curve sits essentially at its 12-month steep extreme, and slope_change_21d (+0.29pp) and slope_change_63d (+0.76pp) both confirm strong positive momentum over multiple windows. slope_10s30s (+0.67pp) corroborates a broadly steep long end, consistent with the 2s10s move rather than an outlier. The level (1.58pp) is nowhere near flat/inverted (slope_distance_from_flat +1.58pp), so there's no proximity-to-zero drag pulling it back. Policy language turned modestly more upbeat on activity/employment ("turned up recently"), which is consistent with markets pricing a firmer growth/inflation path further out the curve, supporting continued steepening rather than flattening. With the series oscillating narrowly near cycle highs but the net trend still up and momentum intact, I lean toward continued mild steepening rather than flat, though the narrow last-three-print range (1.57-1.59) tempers conviction.

Balance sheet growth is the highest-conviction, cleanest read — mechanical, policy-confirmed, unambiguous. Inflation expectations, term premium, and curve slope all tell the same secondary story — nominal yields and breakevens sitting at or near 12-month highs with decelerating but still-positive momentum. I read these three as correlated expressions of the same steepening/reflation trade rather than three independent confirmations, so I don't stack full conviction across all of them; if anything they cross-validate but also share the same risk (a stall at range highs). The explicit panel caveat that inflation_expectations wasn't shown realized CPI or term premium is worth noting but the correlation actually strengthens rather than weakens my prior, since realized inflation (from the separate inflation analyst) is independently accelerating too. Financial conditions continue to ease modestly, consistent with an accommodative backdrop supporting risk assets and duration extension, though the pace is small. Labor tightness continues to improve but at a visibly decelerating pace, with policy language flagging moderation — I keep this as a lower-conviction, smaller-magnitude call than the others. Overall: rising inflation and inflation expectations, rising term premium and curve steepness, continued balance sheet expansion, easier financial conditions, and modestly tightening labor market — a classic reflationary, still-accommodative regime.

arbitrated.notes: Inflation is the strongest, freshest signal on the panel: headline CPI y/y has accelerated three straight readings (2.67→4.13→4.92) with m/m re-accelerating above trend and core PCE also jumping, so the "transitory" framing looks premature. I keep this at high conviction but shade it slightly given the analyst's own hedge that Fed language plus a decelerating m/m gap could presage a peak. Inflation expectations have plateaued at cycle highs (2.40-2.42, 21d change flat) — momentum has stalled but the level stays elevated and there's no rollover, so I read this as flat with a mild upward skew consistent with a Committee that wants inflation running above target; not enough fresh momentum to call it rising. Labor tightness resumed its decline (unemployment 5.80% vs 6.10%, multi-month trend intact, 12m low, distance from natural rate still wide) — I take this as unemployment falling further, i.e., tightening continues. Term premium: near top of 12m range with strong 63d/252d uptrend but a softening last 21d — I lean up but with reduced conviction given the recent pullback. Financial conditions: NFCI easing trend has flattened for three straight weeks at -0.69; I still lean toward further mild easing but tempered heavily by the plateau — call it modestly down. Balance sheet: one weekly dip against an otherwise firmly expansionary multi-week/month trend and explicit ongoing QE guidance — I keep this up. Curve slope: 2s10s has tilted flatter over 21d even as longer trend is positive; near top of range limits further steepening — lean flat-to-slightly negative. Overall this describes a still-hot inflation backdrop with tightening labor and continued balance-sheet growth, against elevated but stalling inflation expectations and slightly softening term premium/curve momentum.

discounted.why: Momentum has stalled with no fresh catalyst; plateau gives weak basis for directional call.
Conflicting short vs long-term momentum with no decisive breakout; low-conviction signal only.

arbitrated.falsifier: A headline CPI m/m print materially below 0.5%, a second consecutive weekly fed_assets decline, or unemployment printing above 6.00% would flip this read toward disinflation/easing.

risks.text: Fed's own 'transitory' language could be validated if next CPI decelerates, undercutting the inflation call.
Breakeven plateau could roll over quickly given how close it sits to prior peaks.
Term premium's 21d softening could extend into a genuine reversal, invalidating the up call.
A second consecutive weekly asset decline would flip the balance sheet call despite guidance.

why.labor_tightness: Unemployment resumed decline to 12m low with consistent multi-month trend; expect further tightening (unrate down).
why.term_premium: 63d/252d uptrend intact and near top of range, but 21d softening tempers conviction versus prior call.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI easing trend has flattened three weeks running; lean continued mild easing but momentum has visibly slowed.
why.balance_sheet: Multi-week/month expansion trend and explicit ongoing $80bn/$40bn purchase guidance dominate single weekly dip.
