Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print shows dgs10 dropping to 1.83% from 1.97%, a notable one-observation decline after a period of grinding higher (63d +0.35pp, 252d +0.39pp). My prior "up" call was directionally right over the medium term but the most recent move contradicts it - this looks like a pullback within an uptrend rather than a reversal, since range_position remains elevated at 0.74 (well above the 1.19-2.05 range midpoint) and 30s10s/10s2s slopes remain positive and unchanged, not flattening or inverting as my falsifier specified. The Fed's continued taper of asset purchases (reducing to $20bn/mo Treasury purchases) is a structural reduction in official-sector demand for long bonds, which should keep upward pressure on term premium over the coming month even if there's short-term noise. The single-observation drop to 1.83 could reflect a flight-to-quality bid or temporary positioning, but without confirmation via low position or slope inversion, I don't yet see this as a trend change. I'm lowering conviction from the prior call given the recent softness, but the tapering backdrop and elevated range position still lean toward higher yields by month-end.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break below 1.75 with range_position falling toward 0.5 and 30s10s/10s2s flattening would confirm the uptrend has stalled.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was wrong: NFCI has moved clearly and consistently higher, from -0.56 to -0.47 over the last several weeks, breaking well above the -0.54 level I had flagged as the tightening trigger. Both the 4-week (+0.09) and 13-week (+0.10) changes are positive and of similar magnitude, indicating steady, persistent upward momentum rather than noise — this is not a one-off print. The index is now at its 52-week high (range_position = 1.00), meaning conditions have moved from the loose end of the recent range to the tight end, even though the level (-0.47) remains below the zero average, so conditions are still accommodative in absolute terms but tightening in trend. Policy language describing conditions as accommodative is unchanged and lagging the recent measurement move. Given the consistency of the climb across all 13 observations with no reversals, I expect this drift to continue into month-end, likely printing modestly higher again (toward -0.45 to -0.42), unless something abruptly reverses the trend. I am revising toward a tightening call with moderate conviction, reflecting the clear and sustained directional evidence despite the still-negative absolute level.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below -0.54 or a flattening of the 4w change toward zero would indicate the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_high

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was correct: fed_assets rose from 8911033 to 8928129 (+17,096 last obs), extending the run of higher weekly readings. The series is monotonically at a fresh 52-week high (assets_below_52w_high = 0), with 4-week change +67,644 and 13-week change +246,358, both still solidly positive. Policy language confirms this trajectory is now tapering rather than reversing: purchases have been cut again to at least $20B Treasuries and $10B MBS per month starting [date] down from $40B/$20B in [date] and from the still-larger pace before that. This is still net asset purchases — QE at a shrinking but positive pace — not runoff. The balance sheet should continue to grow into month-end, though the pace of increase should decelerate further given the smaller monthly purchase minimums now in effect. There is no language yet about halting purchases or beginning redemptions/runoff. Given the clear, consistent taper trajectory (not reversal) and continued positive weekly increments, I expect fed_assets to be modestly higher at next month-end, with lower conviction than pure QE periods since the pace is shrinking toward zero.
Would change its mind if: A statement announcing purchases reduced to zero/ended, or two consecutive weekly declines in fed_assets, would flip this to flat/down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.68
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call played out further: the last reading (0.39) is the lowest of the 10-observation window, sitting essentially at the 12-month low (0.38) with range position of just 0.01 — the curve has been flattening persistently. The 21d, 63d, and 252d change measures are all negative and accelerating in magnitude (-0.24, -0.59, -0.91), showing sustained, broad-based flattening momentum, not a one-off print. Distance from flat is only 0.39pp, meaning the curve is closing in on inversion territory. Policy language confirms the driver: forward guidance shifted from patient/tapering to signaling imminent liftoff ("expects it will soon be appropriate to raise the target range"), which typically pressures the front end (2yr) higher faster than the long end, compressing 2s10s. The 10s30s slope (0.34) is still positive but modest, consistent with long-end anchoring while front-end yields rise on hawkish repricing. There's no evidence of stabilization — the last two readings (0.42, 0.42, 0.39) show a slight uptick then renewed decline, not a basing pattern. My prior falsifier conditions (rebound above 0.70pp or 3+ readings of stabilization) were not met. I maintain a flattening call, with conviction slightly higher given the acceleration in longer-window change measures and hawkish policy shift reinforcing the front-end-led compression.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.55pp sustained over 2+ readings, or 3+ consecutive readings stabilizing near current level, would signal flattening has paused.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_12m_low, slope_range_position, slope_distance_from_flat, dgs2, dgs10

  curve_slope lacks inflation_expectations — Shifts in long-run inflation expectations directly affect the 10yr anchor and thus 2s10s compression pace.

arbitrated.notes: The overall picture is a late-cycle, hawkish-repricing regime: inflation is still accelerating on both headline and core measures, breakevens have broken to fresh highs, financial conditions are tightening off their loosest point, the Fed is still adding to the balance sheet (shrinking pace) even as it signals imminent liftoff, and the curve is flattening hard as front-end yields reprice for hikes while the long end anchors. These stories are mutually consistent and reinforcing rather than contradictory, which raises my confidence in each individually. Inflation and inflation expectations both show clean, multi-observation momentum with no sign of a turn — I keep both up, with inflation slightly higher conviction since it has more history and momentum confirmation (3m annualized > y/y, core PCE broadening) while breakevens are a fresher, one-meeting call. Curve flattening is the most mechanically well-supported call: acceleration across 21d/63d/252d windows, near 12-month lows, and directly consistent with the hawkish forward guidance that is also the causal story behind the inflation-expectations and financial-conditions moves. Financial conditions tightening (off very accommodative levels) is consistent with, and probably partly caused by, the same repricing. Balance sheet is a lower-conviction "up" — it's still growing but decelerating, more a taper trajectory than a genuine expansion signal, so I trim conviction. Term premium is the shakiest call: a single-observation drop in DGS10 cuts against the "up" thesis even though range position and taper logic still lean higher; I keep it up but with reduced conviction, treating it as the one driver where the "flat-to-down" case has real teeth. Labor tightness is basically a continuation call (flat, slight tightening bias) — I keep it modest since the print sits at a floor with limited room to move further without a fresh shock.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.75
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose from 7.94 to 8.57, and my falsifier condition (soft m/m plus core PCE flattening) did not occur — instead the outgoing m/m print jumped to 1.12%, well above the 3-month average of 0.80% and the highest in the series. Momentum is still building, not fading. Core PCE y/y continued climbing every single month in the series (1.71→5.60), with the most recent 3-month annualized core pace at 6.27%, still running hotter than the y/y rate itself, indicating acceleration rather than plateau. Headline 3m annualized at 10.09% is even more extreme, though I weight core more heavily for persistence — and core shows no sign of turning. The yoy_range_position at 1.00 confirms headline is at its cycle high with no evidence of deceleration. Policy language shifted materially this period, dropping accommodative framing and moving to "well above 2 percent" with anticipation of tightening — language that only shifts this decisively when officials perceive inflation as broadening and persistent, corroborating the data. With both core momentum and the latest monthly print accelerating, another higher headline print next month is the more likely outcome.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below ~0.3% combined with core PCE y/y flattening or declining would signal a peak and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, outgoing_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last three observations show breakeven_10y sliding from 2.95 to 2.86 to 2.86 to 2.84 — a clear reversal after peaking. My prior "up" call at 0.60 conviction was correct through the run-up to 2.95 but has been wrong-footed by the most recent turn: the last three prints are down or flat, not up. The 21d change is still nominally positive (+0.13pp) because it's measured from a lower base three weeks ago, but the trajectory within that window is negative — the series peaked mid-window and has fallen ~0.11pp off the high in the last three readings. be_range_position at 0.85 still shows expectations elevated near the 12-month high, and distance from the 2% target remains wide at +0.84pp, so there's no case for a sharp reversal to flat/down objective anchoring. But the immediate momentum has clearly stalled and turned negative over the last three observations, which is the more relevant signal for a one-month-ahead call. This looks like consolidation or mild pullback after an extended run, not a resumption of the uptrend. I'm shifting off "up" toward flat, acknowledging the recent stall while not calling a reversal given the level still sits high in its range.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 2.90 would revive the uptrend; a break below 2.80 would confirm a genuine reversal lower.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was too cautious: unemployment printed 3.70%, a fresh 12-month low, undershooting my own reaffirmation threshold of 3.90%. This is a continuation of an unambiguous tightening trend, not a plateau. The series shows steady decline from 6.10% down to 3.70%, with 3m change -0.20pp, 6m -1.00pp, and 12m -2.40pp — all still negative, confirming deceleration in the rate itself (i.e., a tightening labor market). Sahm-rule spirit checks are clean: unrate_above_12m_low is 0.00pp (we ARE the low), sahm_gap is a negligible +0.17pp, and range_position is 0.00 (bottom of the 12-month range). There is no evidence of a rise off a trough — the opposite is occurring. The level is now 0.30pp below the assumed 4% natural rate, historically a tight labor market by any conventional read. Policy language changes reinforce this: statements shifted from "continued to strengthen" toward "soon appropriate to raise" the policy rate, explicitly citing a "strong labor market" as justification — corroborating that labor conditions are viewed as tight, not loosening. Momentum, level, and language all point the same direction. I expect unemployment to hold near cycle lows or tick down slightly next month; a print of 3.6-3.8% would be consistent with this trend continuing.
Would change its mind if: A print at or above 3.90-4.00% (reversing the downtrend and moving off the 12-month low) would signal a genuine loosening shift.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.20
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.25) was directionally correct on the trend but the most recent print shows the 10y easing off its highs: from 2.48 down to 2.32 over the last three readings, after a sharp run-up (63d +0.80pp, 21d +0.46pp still positive but the last two prints are declining). Level sits near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.88, only 0.16pp off the 12m high of 2.48), which argues limited near-term upside room absent fresh catalysts. The 30s10s slope (+0.12) and 10s2s (+0.04) remain positive but modest, not signaling a fresh steepening impulse. Policy language now explicitly flags upcoming balance-sheet runoff ("expects to begin reducing its holdings... at a coming meeting"), a hawkish, long-end-supply-negative-for-price (yield-positive) signal that should still exert some upward pressure over the coming month, offsetting the recent pullback. Given the tug-of-war between a stretched high range position/recent pullback and forward guidance on runoff that hasn't yet been priced, I lean toward continued mild upward pressure but with reduced conviction versus last time, since the most recent two prints have already reversed some of the move.
Would change its mind if: A further drop below 2.20 with range_position falling toward 0.6 and continued flattening of 30s10s would confirm the uptrend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_12m_high, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has risen steadily for 13 straight weeks, from -0.55 to -0.38, with 4-week change +0.09 and 13-week change +0.16 — a clear, persistent tightening trend in relative terms. The index sits at the top of its 52-week range (range_position 1.00), meaning conditions are the tightest they've been in a year, even though the absolute level (-0.38) remains well below the zero average, so conditions are still accommodative in absolute terms. My prior "up" call at 0.55 conviction was correct: the trend continued and did not reverse below -0.54, nor did the 4w change flatten toward zero — it actually held steady. Momentum shows no sign of stalling; each of the last several prints has moved higher by similar increments, with no deceleration evident in the last 3 observations (-0.41, -0.39, -0.38). Policy language still describes conditions as accommodative, which is a lagging/backward-looking description and does not contradict the ongoing measured tightening trend. Given the consistency and lack of any reversal signal, I maintain a tightening call for month-end, though I temper conviction slightly since the pace of increase per period is modest and the series remains far from zero, limiting how much further near-term tightening is plausible before mean-reversion pressures build.
Would change its mind if: A reversal below -0.42 or a 4w change turning negative/near zero would indicate the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_13w_avg

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was correct: fed_assets rose again to 8,962,474 from 8,954,306, marking the 52-week high, with assets_change_4w (+34,345) and assets_change_13w (+171,979) both positive. The series shows steady week-over-week gains over the last 13 observations, consistent with ongoing net purchases. However, the policy language has now shifted decisively: the old language committing to at least $20B/month Treasury and $10B/month MBS purchases has been struck through (removed), replaced by explicit forward guidance that the Committee "expects to begin reducing its holdings... at a coming meeting." This is a clear signal that the expansion phase is ending and runoff is imminent, even though it hasn't shown up in the measured data yet — balance sheet levels are a lagging confirmation of a policy stance that has already pivoted. Given the mandate to weight recent pace of change but also treat statement language as the "why," I read this as still up for the immediate month-end (purchases likely continue through transition before runoff mechanics start), but with much lower conviction than before since the growth impulse is clearly terminating and could stall or reverse within the horizon.
Would change its mind if: A weekly decline in fed_assets, or statement confirming runoff caps have started, would flip this to down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_52w_high, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: the spread continued collapsing from 0.11 to 0.04pp over the last three readings, and has now essentially flattened at the zero-bound of its 12-month range (range position 0.00, distance from flat only 0.04pp). The last two readings are identical at 0.04, which could suggest stabilization, but this is far below my stated threshold for a pause (0.55pp) and the trend into this floor was steep (21d change -0.32, 63d -0.75). Policy language has shifted decisively hawkish: liftoff has begun (funds rate now 1/4-1/2%), with explicit guidance for "ongoing increases," and one dissent favoring a larger 50bp hike. This kind of front-loaded tightening cycle historically presses hardest on the 2-year, which should continue to compress or invert the 2s10s spread further as the front end reprices faster than the long end. The 10s30s at 0.12pp offers no offsetting steepening signal. Given the spread is already near zero and hawkish repricing is intensifying, I lean toward continued flattening/inversion risk rather than a bounce, though the two flat prints introduce some uncertainty about the pace.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 0.20pp sustained over 2+ readings, or clear deceleration in the pace of front-end hikes reducing hawkish repricing pressure.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_distance_from_flat, slope_10s30s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Actual pace/start date of runoff would clarify how much long-end supply pressure is imminent.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-off/flight-to-quality flows could explain and extend the recent yield pullback.

arbitrated.notes: Cross-checking the seven reports, the picture is a late-cycle, front-loaded tightening episode. Inflation momentum is unambiguous and accelerating on both headline (record m/m, cycle-high yoy range position) and core (3m annualized running hotter than yoy), corroborated by policy language dropping "accommodative" — this is the highest-conviction call on the panel and I keep it near full strength. Labor tightness is equally clean: unemployment at a fresh 12-month low, all horizons negative, Sahm-gap negligible, and hawkish language citing a "strong labor market" — I keep this strong. Inflation expectations, by contrast, show a stalling breakeven after a run-up; three consecutive soft/flat prints outweigh the stale 21d window, so I side with the analyst's shift to flat rather than treating this as still rising — a mild positive lean is enough to respect the still-elevated range position without fighting the recent turn. Financial conditions show a persistent, un-stalled 13-week tightening trend at the top of its range — I trust this fully. Balance sheet is a case where language leads data: purchases are still running (fresh 52-week high) but explicit runoff guidance is imminent, so I keep the "up" call but heavily discount conviction since the impulse is expiring within the horizon. Term premium is the most conflicted call — the 10y has pulled back two straight prints off a stretched high range position, and the analyst itself flagged missing cross-checks (balance-sheet runoff timing, risk-off flows) that could either support or undercut the call; I keep only a token positive lean, mostly on runoff-supply logic, given the fresh pullback. Curve slope is the cleanest complement to the inflation/labor picture — near-zero and pinned by front-loaded hiking language with two dissenting-hawkish signals — I keep it near the analyst's conviction, flattening/inversion continuing.

discounted.why: Data still rising but language signals expansion is ending within horizon; conflicting signals reduce trust.
Recent pullback conflicts with prior uptrend and analyst flagged missing cross-checks it wasn't given.

arbitrated.falsifier: A soft CPI m/m (<0.3%) with core PCE flattening, alongside unemployment rising to/above 3.9-4.0%, would reverse the tightening-cycle read entirely.

risks.text: Balance-sheet and term-premium calls hinge on guidance not yet reflected in hard data, risking a data/language disconnect.
If breakevens resume climbing rather than stalling, inflation expectations call understates upside risk.
Two identical flat 2s10s prints could presage stabilization rather than continued inversion.

why.inflation: Accelerating core and headline momentum, cycle-high range position, hawkish language shift all align; trust the analyst's high conviction.
why.inflation_expectations: Three consecutive stalling/declining breakeven prints outweigh stale 21d window; side with 'flat' lean, slight upward tilt for elevated level.
why.labor_tightness: Fresh 12-month low, all horizons negative, clean Sahm-gap, hawkish 'strong labor market' language corroborate continued tightening.
why.term_premium: Two-print pullback off stretched highs vs. pending runoff supply pressure; token positive lean given analyst's own flagged blind spots.
why.financial_conditions: Persistent 13-week uninterrupted tightening trend at top of 52-week range with no deceleration; trust fully.
why.balance_sheet: Data still shows growth to fresh 52-week high, but explicit runoff guidance imminent within horizon; heavily discount conviction.
why.curve_slope: Spread pinned near zero-bound of range, steep recent flattening trend, front-loaded hawkish hikes with dissent for larger move.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release's headline_mom fell to 0.31%, right at the threshold I flagged as a potential peak signal, and headline_yoy_change_3m and core_yoy_change_3m did decelerate slightly in pace (though still positive). The prior call (up, 0.75) was directionally correct as headline rose from 8.23 in the second-to-last reading... wait, actually the newest print (8.23) is LOWER than the prior (8.57), meaning headline_cpi_yoy already turned down last month, contradicting the up call. That print was likely driven by the low 0.31% m/m outgoing print. However, core PCE yoy is still climbing (5.59, barely off 5.60 high) and core_3m_annualized (5.48%) remains well above target, indicating underlying persistence despite the headline dip — likely energy-driven given the policy language citing "higher energy prices" and geopolitical (invasion) upward pressure. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing is only +0.04pp, so momentum isn't clearly breaking down; one soft month could easily reverse given cited new upward pressures on energy from the war shock. Given core is still rising and policy language explicitly flags fresh upward pressure from an energy/geopolitical shock, I expect headline to resume upward or at minimum not sustain the decline. But the recent single-month deceleration and range position near top-of-range warrants tempering conviction versus last time.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive headline m/m prints below 0.4% alongside core PCE yoy turning down would confirm a genuine peak and reversal.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (2.88) sits within recent choppy range after touching 2.97 two readings back and a recent low of 2.85. My prior "flat" call anticipated exactly this kind of noisy oscillation between roughly 2.85-2.97, and that's what materialized — the print did not confirm either a break above 2.90 sustained, nor a break below 2.80. The 21-day change is essentially flat (+0.02pp), while 63-day and 252-day changes are both solidly positive (+0.44, +0.47pp), showing the medium-term uptrend from lower levels is intact even though very recent momentum has stalled and chopped. Breakeven remains near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.82, only 0.14pp off the 12m high of 3.02) and is 0.88pp above the 2% target — a meaningfully elevated level. Given the series has been oscillating in a band (2.85-2.97) without a clean directional break, and the newest value (2.88) is mid-band, I don't see strong evidence for a fresh directional move over the next month. The medium-term uptrend argues against calling this "down," but the stalled short-term momentum and lack of a clear new high argues against calling "up" with confidence. I'll stay flat, slightly lower conviction than before since the range remains wide and choppy.
Would change its mind if: A sustained close above 2.95 would confirm renewed uptrend; a break below 2.80 would confirm reversal lower.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 3.70%, matching the prior month and sitting exactly at the 12-month low, so my prior "down" call did not materialize — the rate was flat rather than continuing to fall. The trend has clearly decelerated: 3m change is only -0.30pp and the last three readings (3.90, 3.70, 3.70) show the sharp declines of earlier months have stalled. The Sahm gap is a negligible +0.07pp, well below any recession-signal threshold, and the range position is at 0 (at the low), indicating no loosening impulse at all. Policy language reinforces this: "unemployment rate has declined substantially" and job gains "strong," with the labor market expected to "remain strong." There is no evidence of turning upward — no rise off the low, no acceleration in softening. At the same time, with unemployment already 0.3pp below my 4% natural-rate anchor and momentum flattening, further declines look limited; a plateau near current levels is the most probable outcome. I don't see strong evidence for either a fresh leg down or an upturn — the data argue for stability at a tight level.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.80-3.90% would indicate the flat plateau is breaking into a genuine upward/loosening turn.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield last printed 2.89, near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.98, only 0.04pp below the 12m high of 2.93). Momentum remains strongly upward: +0.54pp over 21d, +1.11pp over 63d, +1.26pp over 252d. My prior "up" call at 0.20 conviction was directionally correct and, if anything, understated the persistence of the move. The 30s10s slope has steepened modestly to +0.07pp, consistent with continued long-end pressure rather than curve flattening that would signal stalling. Policy language has shifted toward acknowledging balance-sheet runoff ("reducing its holdings of Treasury securities... at a coming meeting"), which historically adds term premium pressure at the long end via reduced official-sector demand. With yields sitting at/near 12-month highs, some mean-reversion risk exists, but nothing in the measurements (range position near 1.0, positive slope, strong multi-horizon momentum) supports calling a top yet. I lean toward continued upward pressure into month-end, though conviction is moderate given how stretched the level already is relative to the 12-month range, which raises reversal risk.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below the 12m high's proximity with range_position falling toward 0.6-0.7 and 30s10s flattening/inverting would signal the uptrend has stalled.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up/tightening, 0.55 conviction) was wrong: NFCI continued its multi-month rise but the pace has clearly decelerated and nearly stalled. Over 13 weeks the index rose 0.20 (steady easing-of-accommodation trend), but the most recent 4-week change is only +0.02, essentially flat, and the last several prints (-0.37,-0.37,-0.37,-0.36,-0.35) show the series flattening out at the top of its 52-week range (range_position=1.00, at the 52w high of -0.35). This matches my stated falsifier condition (4w change near zero), so I should revise down my conviction in continued tightening. The index remains deeply negative (-0.35, distance from zero -0.35), meaning conditions are still far looser than average, and policy language continues to describe conditions as accommodative with ongoing supportive securities purchases — consistent with continued loose conditions rather than a new tightening impulse. Given the flattening momentum and sitting at a 52-week high (i.e., least loose point in a year but still loose), the near-term signal is ambiguous: modest upward drift could continue marginally, or the index could stall/reverse slightly. I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-up call with reduced conviction, given the clear deceleration.
Would change its mind if: A renewed 4w change clearly above +0.03-0.05 would confirm tightening resumed; a drop back toward the 13w avg (-0.42) would confirm easing resumed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_high

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation shows fed_assets fell to 8,939,199 from 8,955,851 the prior week — a weekly decline of about -16,652 $M, the largest single-week drop in this series. This follows a peak at the 52w-high of 8,965,487, and assets are now -26,288 $M below that high. My prior "up" call was based on continued purchase language, and that was directionally right through most of the series (4w and 13w changes are still positive, +2057 and +78714 respectively), but the newest print and the statement language have both turned. The policy language has explicitly shifted: the "increase holdings by at least $20B/$10B per month" language is now struck, replaced by forward guidance that the Committee "expects to begin reducing its holdings... at a coming meeting." This is the language marking the end of QE and the transition toward runoff. Combined with the latest weekly decline, this looks like the inflection point. Note runoff hasn't formally started (no caps announced yet), so the pace of decline should be modest initially, but the direction of travel — both in the newest data point and in the stated intent — has flipped from expansion to contraction.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward the 52w-high, or statement reverting to reinvestment/purchase language, would flip this back to up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was directionally right through most of the sequence (0.32→0.18 over the middle stretch) but the last three prints show stabilization/modest bounce (0.23, 0.24, 0.22, 0.19), not a continued slide. The most recent reading, 0.19, sits essentially flat vs. the prior print and within the narrow 0.18-0.24 band of the last six observations. slope_change_21d is now positive (+0.15pp), a reversal from the deep negative 63d and 252d changes, suggesting the flattening impulse has decelerated even though the level remains historically compressed (range_position 0.15, only 0.24pp above the 12-month low). The Fed has now begun actual hiking (raised to 1/4-3/8, with a dissent favoring 50bp), confirming the front-end repricing that drove flattening is now largely delivered rather than purely anticipatory — often such curves stabilize or modestly re-steepen once the first hike is in the rearview and the path becomes data-dependent rather than a one-way hawkish surprise machine. However, the spread is still deeply compressed near cycle lows with no clear reversal signal strong enough to call outright steepening. I'll shift to flat, since the 21d trend is stabilizing but the level offers no room for confidence in a rebound.
Would change its mind if: A break below 0.15pp would confirm renewed flattening; a sustained move above 0.25pp for 2+ readings would confirm steepening resumption.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace and market reaction to actual runoff implementation would clarify long-end supply pressure.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-off/flight-to-quality flows could reverse long-end yields regardless of momentum.
  curve_slope lacks inflation_expectations — Would clarify if long-end anchoring is stabilizing or still driving front-end-led flattening

Headline CPI has ticked down off its peak but core PCE and 3m-annualized core remain elevated, and the analyst flags energy/geopolitical upside risk that could easily reverse the one soft print — I read inflation as leaning up but with less conviction than the analyst's own framing given the peak-adjacent range position. Inflation expectations are genuinely rangebound (2.85-2.97) with flat short-term momentum but a still-positive medium-term drift; I treat this as a real flat call, not a hidden directional lean. Labor market tightness has plateaued at cycle lows with no loosening signal (Sahm gap negligible, range at zero) — flat is well supported and I keep decent conviction there. Term premium is the strongest directional call: 10y yields near 12-month highs, strong multi-horizon momentum, steepening 30s10s, and now-flipped balance-sheet language all point the same direction, so I keep this up but temper slightly given stretched positioning and reversal risk (especially since term premium was explicitly not cross-checked against balance-sheet implementation pace or risk-off flows). Financial conditions have decelerated to flat after a multi-month tightening drift — take the analyst's flat call but recognize the underlying level is still very loose, so risk is skewed toward eventual reacceleration rather than easing. Balance sheet has genuinely inflected: peak reached, largest weekly drawdown in the series, and explicit statement language marking the end of purchases — I take this down with reasonable conviction. Curve slope is stabilizing/flat after the flattening impulse decelerated post-first-hike, though it remains historically compressed with no reversal margin, so I keep flat but low conviction, aware it lacks a read from inflation expectations that could clarify long-end anchoring.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call played out: headline CPI y/y rose from 8.23% to 8.54%, driven by a hot 0.95% m/m print. That confirms the near-term acceleration I flagged, though the trajectory is not clean — mom prints have been volatile (0.31% then 0.95%), so the falsifier condition (two consecutive sub-0.4% prints) was clearly not met. Momentum context: headline 3m annualized is running at 9.91%, well above the 12-month trend, and headline y/y is at range position 0.99 — essentially the cycle high. Core PCE y/y, my preferred read on persistence, ticked down slightly (5.60→5.59→5.35), a hint of deceleration in the 3-month change (-0.01pp) but still far above target (+3.35pp) and not a clean turn given only one month's pullback. Policy language reinforces the acceleration narrative: the statement now asserts (not hedges) that the invasion is "creating additional upward pressure on inflation," and adds "highly attentive to inflation risks" — language tightening, not softening. Given the outgoing 0.95% m/m print, headline y/y is very likely to rise again next month absent a sharp deceleration in monthly prints, even as core shows tentative early softening. I weight headline's own momentum here since the horizon is the headline print itself.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive headline m/m prints below 0.4% alongside core PCE yoy turning down would confirm a genuine peak and reversal.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last three prints (2.62, 2.63, 2.64) show a modest but consistent uptick after the sharp 21-day decline to the 2.55-2.56 trough. My prior "flat" call was reasonable given the choppy path, but the most recent three observations show a small, steady grind higher that deserves acknowledgment — not a reversal call, but a mild upward lean rather than pure flat. The 21-day change (-0.24pp) still reflects the earlier drop from 2.71-2.72, but the 63-day change is now essentially flat-to-positive (+0.02pp) and the 252-day change is clearly positive (+0.22pp), suggesting the longer-trend drift is up even as the short-term move was volatile. Breakeven sits mid-range (0.53 range position) between 12-month low (2.22) and high (3.02), and remains 0.64pp above the 2% target — a level that has now persisted with the recent inching-up rather than falling back toward the range low. With three consecutive higher closes and 63d/252d trends turning positive, I lean toward a modest continuation higher over the next month, though the magnitude of recent moves is small and could stall.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.58 would negate the recent uptick; a push through 2.72 (prior high) would confirm renewed upward momentum.
Leaned on: be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 3.60% came in below the prior plateau of 3.70%, so my previous "flat" call was slightly wrong in direction terms—unemployment actually ticked down rather than staying pinned. The market is at its 12-month low, sitting at the bottom of the 13-month range (range_position 0.00), and 0.40pp below the assumed natural rate of 4%. The 3m and 6m changes are both modestly negative (-0.30pp, -0.50pp), and the 12m change is a sizable -2.20pp decline, though that reflects the older, steeper part of the disinflationary-labor-tightening trend rather than recent dynamics. The Sahm-gap is trivial (+0.07pp), confirming no loosening signal at all. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed's shift from "strong" to "robust" job gains signals continued tightness, not loosening. There is no evidence of any turn upward — the trend, if anything, has been grinding tighter at the margin. Given the rate is already at cycle lows with no sign of momentum reversal, and the small recent moves are still negative (tightening), I lean toward continued flat-to-slightly-tightening conditions, but with limited room to fall further given proximity to structural lows, so I don't expect a sharp additional decline either.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.80% would signal a genuine loosening turn breaking the low-range plateau; a print at/below 3.50% would confirm continued tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call played out: 10Y ticked up to 2.85% from 2.74%, reversing a brief dip. Level sits high in its 12-month range (range_position 0.86, near the 3.12% high, 1.66pp above the low), and the 63d/252d changes (+1.13/+1.24pp) confirm a strong sustained uptrend, even though the 21d change is flat/slightly negative (-0.04pp), suggesting a pause rather than a reversal. The 30s10s slope remains positive at +0.22pp, not flattening/inverting as would be needed to signal stalling — my stated falsifier condition has not been met. Policy language now confirms actual commencement of balance-sheet runoff (moving from "expects to begin" to "decided to begin... on [date] "), which is a concrete supply-side tailwind for long-end yields as Treasury/MBS demand from the Fed recedes. This reinforces rather than undercuts the up case. The China lockdown/supply-chain language is more ambiguous for term premium (could cut either way between growth-scare flight-to-quality and inflation-persistence pressure), so I weight it lightly. Given confirmed runoff onset, still-elevated range position, and positive slope, I maintain an up view, with slightly higher conviction than before given the runoff is now actually starting rather than merely anticipated.
Would change its mind if: A break of range_position back toward 0.6-0.7 with 30s10s flattening/inverting and 21d change turning meaningfully negative would signal the uptrend has stalled.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s, dgs10_change_21d

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a clear, steady uptrend over the past 13 weeks, moving from -0.44 to -0.28 — a +0.19 move over 13 weeks and +0.07 over the most recent 4 weeks. This is consistent, monotonic tightening with no reversals in the last several prints (each newest observation is higher than or equal to the prior one). The index sits at its 52-week high and at range_position 1.00, meaning conditions are the tightest they've been in the observed window, though still notably below the zero average (distance_from_zero -0.28), so conditions remain looser than historical norm in absolute terms. My prior "flat" call at 0.30 conviction was wrong — the 4w change of +0.07 was already above my own stated threshold of +0.03-0.05 for confirming tightening resumed, and I should have caught that. The momentum here is unambiguous and persistent, not noisy: every single one of the last 9 readings has ticked up or held flat, no down-ticks. That is a strong, low-ambiguity trend. Absent any new information reversing it, the most probable near-term path is continuation of this gradual tightening drift toward zero.
Would change its mind if: A 4w change turning negative or a drop back toward the 13w avg (-0.36) would signal the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print fell sharply to 8,914,281 from 8,945,898, a drop of ~31,617 in one observation and confirming the down call from last time. The 4-week change is now -24,918 and 13-week change -13,848, both negative, with the series clearly rolling off its 52w high (now -51,206 below it). The recent path was choppy through the middle of the series (oscillating in the 8.94-8.96mm range) but the last two prints show a decisive downward break, consistent with runoff finally biting. Critically, the policy language has flipped from merely "expects to begin reducing holdings" to "decided to begin reducing its holdings... on [date] as described in the Plans for Reducing the Size of the Balance Sheet" — i.e., QT has been formally launched with an announced caps-based runoff plan. This is a strong confirmation that the balance sheet is now in active, mechanical runoff mode rather than passive drift. Given the announced monthly caps framework, the pace of decline should persist or even accelerate toward the caps in the coming month. I raise conviction that this is a genuine regime shift into QT rather than noise.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward/above the 52w-high, or reversion to reinvestment/purchase language, would flip this to up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has risen to +0.32pp, the highest print in the series and above the +0.25pp threshold I set previously for confirming steepening resumption, with the last three readings (0.27, 0.29, 0.27, 0.32) holding above 0.25 for four consecutive observations. My prior "flat" call was too cautious in hindsight — the 21-day change is now +0.13pp, a clear positive momentum signal, even though the 63-day (-0.09pp) and 252-day (-1.15pp) changes still show the spread well off its highs and in a longer flattening trend. The spread sits at only 0.24 of its 12-month range (low -0.05, high +1.47), so this remains a historically compressed/inverted-adjacent curve, but the short-term direction is unambiguously steepening. Policy language shows a shift from a 25bp hike with a dissent favoring 50bp to a larger 75bp-equivalent step (3/4 to 1%), consistent with front-end rates catching up and would typically pressure the curve flatter — yet the realized spread has widened, suggesting long-end yields are also moving up or the market is pricing terminal rate proximity. Given the confirmed break above my threshold, I'm shifting to a modest steepening call for month-end, though conviction is tempered by the still-negative 63d/252d trend.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.25pp for 2+ readings, or resumption of the 63d/252d flattening trend, would reverse this call.

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Actual pace/size of runoff caps would clarify how much added long-end supply pressure to expect.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-asset stress could indicate flight-to-quality dampening the long end despite runoff.
  curve_slope lacks financial_conditions — Tightening financial conditions could presage renewed flattening pressure on the curve.
  curve_slope lacks term_premium — Rising term premium would help explain and validate the long-end-led steepening.

Inflation: headline momentum is unambiguous (hot 0.95% m/m, 3m annualized near 10%, y/y at cycle-high range position) even as core PCE shows a tentative one-month wobble down — I weight the headline momentum and hawkish policy language over the single-month core softening, since the horizon is the headline print itself. Inflation expectations are drifting up but on thin, recent evidence (three small upticks after a larger prior decline); I keep this as a lean, not a call. Labor tightness has no credible loosening signal — Sahm-gap trivial, unrate at cycle lows, language shift to "robust" — so tightness persists, but with little room to fall further, hence a mild negative (tighter=lower unemployment) rather than large. Term premium and balance sheet reinforce each other well: QT has moved from "expects" to "decided," fed assets are breaking down decisively, and 10Y is elevated with positive 63d/252d trends — the missing cross-link (balance sheet feeding term premium) actually strengthens conviction here since both drivers independently point the same way. Financial conditions show the cleanest, most monotonic signal in the panel — nine straight non-negative NFCI ticks — so I take that at high conviction. Curve slope is the most conflicted: 2s10s steepening on short-term momentum but against a longer 63d/252d flattening trend and a policy backdrop (front-end hikes accelerating) that would typically flatten; I temper this call given the missing term-premium/financial-conditions cross-checks the analyst itself flagged as absent.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose again to 8.98% from 8.23/8.54, and the m/m print accelerated sharply to 1.26%, the highest in the series, well above the 3-month average of 0.84%. Headline 3m annualized is running at 10.52%, confirming momentum is still building, not fading — my falsifier condition (two sub-0.4% m/m prints) was not met; instead the opposite occurred. Core PCE y/y, the better read on persistence, has ticked down for two consecutive readings (5.60→5.59→5.35→5.18), a modest but real deceleration of 0.42pp over 3 months, suggesting underlying pipeline pressure may be cresting even as headline is dominated by energy/food shocks referenced explicitly in the policy language (the invasion's upward pressure on inflation). Given headline is still accelerating on m/m momentum and remains far above target, while core shows only tentative softening, I weight the still-rising headline trajectory more heavily for the one-month-ahead call, since energy pass-through and the strong recent print suggest another firm reading is likely. But the core rollover tempers conviction that this is a sustained re-acceleration rather than a volatile energy-driven bump.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print back below 0.4% combined with continued core PCE y/y decline would confirm a genuine peak and reversal.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: breakeven_10y has fallen sharply since, from 2.55 to 2.36 to 2.33, breaching my own stated invalidation level (below 2.58) decisively. The trend is clearly down: -0.31pp over 21 days and -0.53pp over 63 days, both accelerating into the most recent print. The series is now sitting near its 12-month low (only 0.11pp above it) and in the bottom 14% of its 12-month range (be_range_position 0.14). The 252-day change is flat, meaning this is a fresh move rather than a continuation of a longer downtrend — but the recent two-month deterioration is the dominant signal and shows no sign of stabilizing in the last two prints (2.36 → 2.33). Distance from the 2% target remains positive at +0.33pp, so expectations are still anchored above objective, but the gap has compressed materially from where it started this window. With no offsetting evidence of stabilization and momentum clearly to the downside, I expect breakeven_10y to be lower or flat-to-lower at month end. I'll set moderate-high conviction on 'down' given the consistency and recency of the decline, tempered by the fact that a 10-observation series this compressed could see a small bounce.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.48 (prior level) would signal stabilization; a further break below 2.30 would confirm accelerating downward momentum.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_above_12m_low, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 3.60%, matching the prior month and still at the 12-month low—my flat call from last time was correct. The unemployment rate has been essentially pinned in a 3.6-3.7% band for the last four readings after a long decline from 5.90% a year earlier. The 3m change (-0.10pp) and 6m change (-0.30pp) are both small and negative, showing the labor market is still very slightly tightening rather than loosening, but the pace of improvement has clearly flattened compared to the earlier plunge. Sahm-rule gap is essentially zero (+0.03pp), meaning there's no recession-signal loosening underway. Distance from the natural rate remains meaningfully negative (-0.40pp), consistent with a still-tight labor market. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed's shift from "strong" to "robust" job gains signals continued confidence in labor market strength, not any incipient loosening. With the rate glued at its 12-month low for two consecutive prints and no acceleration in either direction, the most likely outcome next month is another flat or marginal print. I don't see evidence for a meaningful move up or down.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.80% would signal a genuine loosening turn breaking the low-range plateau; a print at/below 3.50% would confirm continued tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_3m_avg, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.50) was directionally right over the medium term (63d +0.63pp, 252d +1.49pp show a strong secular rise), but the most recent print (2.98%) is a sharp drop from the prior 3.10%, and the last few observations show topping/stalling behavior (3.20, 3.20, 3.10, 2.98) after peaking near 3.31. The 21d change is still positive (+0.13pp) but that masks a clear recent reversal within the window - the trend cooled markedly into the latest print. Range position remains high at 0.78, so the level is still elevated versus the 12-month range, but has pulled back from the top. The 30s10s slope is modestly positive (+0.16pp) and not flattening/inverting, which is one leg of my stated falsifier not yet met, but the 21d change turning "meaningfully negative" and the recent single-print decline of -0.12 is a warning sign. Balance-sheet runoff continues (language shifted from "will begin" to "will continue" reducing holdings), which is a steady upward force on term premium, but the immediate price action suggests some flight-to-quality or demand absorbing that pressure recently. Given the mixed signal - strong medium-term uptrend but a sharp late reversal - I lower conviction and lean toward continued softness or consolidation rather than a resumption of the sharp climb.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 3.15-3.20 with 21d change turning solidly positive and range_position climbing back above 0.85 would revive the uptrend view.
Leaned on: dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.68
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a clean, monotonic climb from -0.37 to -0.19 over 13 weeks, with the pace actually accelerating recently (4w change +0.09 vs 13w change +0.19, meaning roughly half the total move happened in the last third of the window). The index just posted its 52-week high and sits at range_position 1.00, meaning conditions are the tightest they've been in the observed year, though still notably below the zero average (distance_from_zero -0.19), so we're still in loose territory in absolute terms — but the trajectory is unambiguously toward tightening. My prior call was for continued tightening and it was correct: the trend did not stall or reverse: there was no negative 4w change, no reversion to the 13w average. If anything the trend firmed. With no policy language contradicting this and no sign of deceleration in the most recent observations (-0.23, -0.21, -0.19 continuing the steady ~0.02/week climb), the momentum argument for continuation is strong. Absent a catalyst for reversal, I expect the index to continue rising modestly toward month end, extending the multi-month tightening trend, though it will likely remain negative in absolute terms.
Would change its mind if: A flattening or reversal in the last few weekly prints, or 4w change turning negative/near zero, would signal the tightening trend has stalled.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_high, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last four readings show a clear rebound: 8914281 → 8915050 → 8918254 → 8932420 → 8934346, with assets_change_4w now +20065 $M positive, reversing the prior month's decline. My previous "down" call was wrong for this most recent stretch — the series troughed and has since risen for four consecutive observations, nearly erasing the 13-week decline (assets_change_13w is only -28128, and pct_change_13w is a modest -0.31%). Policy language is unchanged in substance: QT continues per the same balance-sheet reduction plan, so there's no stated reinvestment/purchase pivot. This means the recent uptick is likely technical/operational (settlement timing, temporary reserve-management operations, or auction-related noise) rather than a policy shift, since the language explicitly confirms continued runoff at the announced pace. Given the mandate to weight recent pace over level, the 4-week rise is notable, but it sits within a longer runoff regime with an active cap still in force. I lean toward flat/modest uncertainty rather than confidently calling further increases, since the stated policy still points to continued balance-sheet reduction going forward, even though realized data just ticked up. I'll call this flat-to-up with low-moderate conviction, acknowledging the recent reversal while not fully trusting it against explicit continued-runoff language.
Would change its mind if: A further 2-3 weeks of rising fed_assets alongside any softening/pause language on the reduction plan would confirm a genuine turn to expansion.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.45) was wrong: the last two prints fell to 0.04 and 0.06pp, well below the 0.25pp threshold I set, and the 21-day change is -0.26pp, confirming renewed flattening rather than steepening. The spread sits near the bottom of its 12-month range (position 0.08, only 0.11pp above the 12m low of -0.05pp), and is only 0.06pp from outright inversion. While the 63d change is marginally positive (+0.02pp), that's noise against a much larger 252d flattening move of -1.18pp, and the most recent two readings show the flattening trend resuming after a brief pause. Policy language shows continued rate hikes (funds rate raised again, with a dissent favoring an even larger 50bp hike), consistent with front-end-driven flattening pressure as the Fed tightens aggressively. This aligns with the curve being pinned near historic lows in slope. Given the curve is already very close to flat/inverted and hiking momentum continues, I expect the slope to stay compressed or drift lower over the next month, not steepen. I'm reversing my prior lean.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move back above ~0.20-0.25pp for 2+ consecutive readings, with 21d change turning clearly positive, would indicate re-steepening.

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation lacks inflation_expectations — Near-term consumer/market inflation expectations would clarify if the acceleration is seen as persistent or transitory.
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Whether realized CPI trend corroborates or contradicts the breakeven decline.
  inflation_expectations lacks financial_conditions — Tightening conditions could explain and reinforce falling inflation expectations.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/size of actual runoff flows would clarify how much upward pressure is being applied now.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-off/flight-to-quality signals would explain the sharp recent yield drop.

arbitrated.notes: The picture is one of aggressive, ongoing Fed tightening colliding with early but incomplete signs of disinflation and market-side cracks. Headline CPI momentum is still hot (10.5% 3m annualized, fresh series-high m/m print) and I respect that for the one-month CPI call, but core PCE has now decelerated two straight readings and breakevens have broken down sharply and decisively through the analyst's own invalidation level — a real, fresh move, not noise. That divergence between still-hot headline and falling market-implied expectations plus rolling-over core is the central tension. I read it as: near-term headline prints stay firm (energy/food pass-through), but the forward-looking/expectations and underlying-trend indicators are turning down, so I keep inflation up but shade conviction down slightly versus the analyst's own confidence, and take inflation_expectations down with high conviction since the break was decisive and unmet by any contradictory signal shown here. Labor is genuinely stuck — four prints pinned near a 12-month low with sahm-gap near zero — flat is the right call and easy to trust. Financial conditions are the cleanest, most monotonic series shown (accelerating tightening, fresh 52-week high) and I lean hard on it. Term premium is the most conflicted: strong secular uptrend but a sharp, multi-print reversal at the margin; I keep this near flat, shaded slightly down given the recent price action and unaddressed flight-to-quality/risk-off explanation. Balance sheet shows a real but likely technical 4-week bounce against continued explicit QT language — I keep this flat, low conviction. Curve slope is flattening decisively, consistent with continued front-end hiking pressure and near-inversion positioning — I trust this fairly strongly. Overall: tightening financial conditions + flattening curve + falling breakevens is an internally consistent tightening-cycle-late-stage picture, even as headline inflation prints still run hot for one more month.

Conflicting medium-term uptrend vs sharp recent reversal, analyst itself only holds 0.30 conviction.

arbitrated.falsifier: A headline CPI m/m print back below 0.4% paired with breakevens stabilizing/rebounding above 2.48 and NFCI flattening would flip this toward a disinflation-without-further-tightening read.

risks.text: No analyst connected inflation expectations decline to financial conditions tightening, so causal read is inferred, not evidenced.
If core PCE deceleration is the true signal, headline inflation call may be overstated for next print.
Term premium reversal could reflect risk-off flows rather than genuine trend change, misreading its persistence.
Technical 4-week asset rebound could extend and contradict flat call if operational factors persist.

why.financial_conditions: Cleanest, most monotonic series, accelerating into fresh 52-week high with no deceleration signal; high trust.
why.balance_sheet: Real 4-week bounce but explicit continued-QT language argues it's technical; keep near flat, low conviction.
why.curve_slope: Renewed flattening near 12-month lows/near-inversion, consistent with continued aggressive front-end hikes; trust the reversal-of-prior-call.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The last print was a clear downside surprise: headline m/m fell to -0.01% from 1.26%, well below my stated falsifier threshold (<0.4%), and headline y/y ticked down to 8.46% from 8.98% — the first meaningful decline in the series after a long climb. This partially validates the "peak" thesis I flagged last time, though my prior call (up, 0.55) was wrong on direction for this release. Core PCE y/y also ticked up slightly to 5.32% from 5.18%, but the 3m trend (core_yoy_change_3m -0.28pp) and core_3m_annualized (5.28%) suggest core momentum is decelerating even if the single-month reading is noisy. Headline 3m annualized (9.10%) is still hot, reflecting the volatile prior months, but the freshest monthly data point (mom -0.01%) is the most informative signal of current momentum and it's benign. Energy/food swings likely drove the headline drop given the gap between headline and core mom trends. Weighing the sharp deceleration in the latest month against still-elevated y/y levels and a core print that ticked up, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for next month: the disinflationary impulse from this release is real but core stickiness and one soft month don't yet confirm a durable trend reversal.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in headline m/m above 0.6% combined with core PCE y/y resuming its climb would signal the deceleration was a one-month fluke, not a trend.
Leaned on: headline_mom, outgoing_mom, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, core_3m_annualized, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (down, 0.55) was wrong: breakeven_10y has risen sharply over the last four readings from 2.36 to 2.53, a +0.17pp move over 21 days that clearly exceeds my stated falsifier threshold (rebound above 2.48). This confirms stabilization and reversal rather than continued decline. The 63-day change is still negative (-0.44pp), reflecting the earlier drawdown, but the recent trend is unambiguously upward and accelerating into the latest print, which sits at a 12-month range position of 0.35 — off the lows but not yet stretched. Distance from the 2% target is now +0.53pp, the widest of the recent series, indicating expectations are drifting further above objective rather than converging toward it. The monotonic run of increases over the last four observations (2.36→2.36→2.42→2.48→2.53) without any pullback is a strong momentum signal. Absent a specific catalyst in the policy language (which is boilerplate/unchanged here), I read this as a genuine trend shift driven by market pricing rather than noise. Given the size and persistence of the recent move, I expect continued upward drift into month-end, though some deceleration is likely given the pace already banked.
Would change its mind if: A pullback below 2.45 in the next print would signal the rally is stalling; a drop below 2.36 would negate the reversal thesis entirely.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print, 3.50%, matches the 12-month low, confirming my prior "flat" call was directionally fine - unemployment did not loosen. The rate is now at the very bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.00), sitting a full 0.50pp below the ~4% natural-rate anchor, with unrate_change_3m at -0.20pp and unrate_change_6m at -0.50pp - still trending down, not sideways. Sahm-gap is a negligible +0.07pp, so no recession-signal loosening is present. Policy language shifted from "declined substantially" to "remained low," which describes a market that has stopped falling further but is not deteriorating - consistent with a plateau at a tight level rather than a turn. Given the market is already at the cycle low with no evidence of rising, the most defensible call for the next print is flat-to-slightly-down: extremely tight labor markets at cyclical lows tend to stabilize before turning, and there's no measurement here showing upward pressure. I lean flat rather than down because three-month momentum, while negative, is modest and the level cannot fall indefinitely. Conviction is moderate given the absence of any loosening signal in the data.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would signal a genuine loosening turn; a print at/below 3.40% would confirm continued tightening beyond the current plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y has continued to fall since my last call: latest print 2.67 vs prior readings near 2.78-2.81, with dgs10_change_21d at -0.43pp and dgs10_change_63d at -0.18pp both negative — my prior "flat" call understated the downward momentum that was already building. The yield sits at range_position 0.64, mid-to-upper range but declining from a recent local peak (3.04), well off the 12m high of 3.49. The 10s30s slope remains positive at +0.33pp, showing no long-end curve inversion signal that would suggest independent long-end stress, but the recent price action is a clear pullback in yield. Balance-sheet runoff language is unchanged (continuing QT), which is a structural upward pressure on term premium, but it has not been enough to offset the recent decline, suggesting other flows (flight-to-quality or duration demand) are dominating short-term. Given the consistent negative 21d and 63d changes and the fresh low print of 2.67, I now lean toward continued softness or at least no reversal by month-end. I don't see strong evidence of a snapback given no move above prior key level and no positive 21d change yet.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 2.85-2.90 with 21d change turning positive and range_position climbing back above 0.75 would reverse this softening view.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up/tightening, 0.68) has been contradicted by the last three prints. After a long steady climb from -0.34 toward -0.16 (the 52w high), NFCI reversed over the last three weekly observations: -0.16 -> -0.17 -> -0.18 -> -0.20. The 4w change is now -0.03 (easing), reversing the prior tightening momentum, even though the 13w change is still +0.15 (net tightening over the quarter) and the index sits near the top of its 52w range (range_position 0.93). This is exactly the stall/reversal condition I said would change my mind. Level remains well below the zero average (-0.20), so conditions are still historically loose in absolute terms, but the momentum has clearly flipped from tightening to easing over the most recent weeks. Given only three data points confirm the reversal, I can't be highly confident it's a durable turn rather than noise, but the consistency of three consecutive declines plus the negative 4w change is enough to lean toward continued easing into month end rather than a resumption of the prior uptrend.
Would change its mind if: A resumption of rising weekly prints or 4w change turning positive again would indicate the tightening trend has reasserted itself.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (8,890,004) continues the modest downtrend after a brief mid-series bounce. Over the last 13 weeks assets are down ~49bn (-0.55%), and the 4-week change is -23.5bn, confirming renewed net runoff rather than the pause I flagged last time. The series shows some week-to-week noise (a rise from 8,913,553 to 8,932,420 mid-window) but the last four readings (8,913,553 → 8,891,851 → 8,895,867 → 8,899,213 → 8,890,004) show the level oscillating in a narrow low band with the newest print the lowest of the last 13. Assets remain ~75bn below the 52w high, and cumulative 52w change is still strongly positive (+668bn) — a legacy of pre-QT expansion, not relevant to the forward path. Policy language is unchanged, reaffirming continued balance-sheet reduction per the standing plans, with no hint of tapering or pausing runoff. My prior flat call was too cautious; the weight of evidence (negative 4w and 13w changes, unchanged runoff language) supports continued gradual QT. Pace remains modest (roughly consistent with the monthly cap), not accelerating, so conviction is moderate rather than high.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-week rise in fed_assets alongside explicit language pausing or slowing the reduction plan would flip this to expansion.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread remains deeply inverted at -0.22pp, sitting exactly at its 12-month low (slope_range_position 0.00) and near the bottom of a wide 1.51pp range versus a 12-month high of +1.29pp. My prior "down" call was correct: the last observation (-0.22) matches the most negative print in the series, confirming continued flattening/inversion rather than the stabilization the -0.17 reading briefly suggested two prints ago. Momentum metrics corroborate this: slope_change_21d (-0.26pp), slope_change_63d (-0.44pp), and slope_change_252d (-1.27pp) are all negative and accelerating in magnitude at shorter horizons, indicating the flattening trend is still active, not decelerating. The policy language shows the Committee continuing to raise the funds rate target (now to 2-1/4 to 2-1/2 percent) with "ongoing increases... appropriate," which is consistent with front-end pressure that has been driving this inversion. There's no language suggesting a pause or dovish pivot. Given the spread is pinned at its 12-month low with negative short-term momentum and no policy signal of deceleration in front-end hikes, I expect the inversion to persist or deepen modestly into month-end rather than reverse.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move back above ~-0.10 to 0.00pp for 2+ consecutive readings, with 21d change turning positive, would indicate re-steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_12m_low, slope_range_position

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation lacks inflation_expectations — Survey-based expectations would clarify if the deceleration is seen as durable or transitory by consumers/markets.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/size of ongoing Treasury runoff would clarify supply pressure on long end going forward.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-off/flight-to-quality flows would explain and forecast further yield declines.

arbitrated.notes: The panel gives a fairly coherent macro picture: front-end policy pressure remains dominant even as growth/inflation signals show early softening. Inflation itself is mixed — a sharp one-month deceleration in headline CPI plus decelerating core momentum argues for a modest downside lean, but the analyst's own conviction is muted (one soft print, core y/y actually ticked up) so I keep this modest. Inflation expectations, by contrast, show a clean, monotonic four-print run higher in breakevens with no pullback — this is the freshest and most unambiguous signal on the panel, so I weight it fully; it also creates some tension with the inflation call (falling realized inflation vs rising priced-in expectations), which I read as market expectations lagging the realized deceleration rather than the driver being wrong. Labor tightness is genuinely at a plateau — cyclical lows with decelerating (but still negative) 3m/6m momentum and no Sahm-rule loosening; I read this as flat, not a directional call. Term premium (10y yield) is falling on clear 21d/63d momentum and a fresh local low, but the analyst flags a structural offsetting force (ongoing QT) it wasn't shown the balance-sheet driver's own conclusion for — I moderate conviction slightly given that omission. Financial conditions have only a three-print reversal from a 52w tightening high; I treat this as a real but unconfirmed easing turn, not yet a strong call. Balance sheet shows confirmed renewed runoff (negative 4w/13w, unchanged language) — a clean, decisive call. Curve slope is pinned at a 12-month low with accelerating negative momentum and hawkish, unchanged policy language — the most confident directional call on the panel alongside inflation expectations.
