user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was directionally right but weak: headline CPI y/y fell only from 8.98 to 8.22 (still within the same elevated range), and the last print actually ticked up slightly (8.46→8.22 is a drop, but preceding step 8.98→8.46 was already the bulk of the move, and most recently mom went from -0.01% to +0.06%, a modest reacceleration in month-on-month terms). Core PCE y/y also ticked up last observation (5.32→5.11 is actually down, but 5.18→5.32 preceding was up) — the series is bouncing rather than trending cleanly. The 3-month annualized rates (headline 5.32%, core 4.75%) sit well below the y/y levels, confirming genuine disinflationary momentum from the peak, and yoy_range_position at 0.79 shows we're still closer to the highs than the lows, so there's room to keep falling as base effects from last year's high prints roll off. Policy language shift (adding "food" to energy price drivers) suggests broadening price pressure concern, a mild hawkish nuance, but not decisive for near-term print direction. Given base effects dominate next month's y/y print and the trend, however uneven, is toward deceleration, I lean down again, though with the mom_gap flattening I temper conviction versus last time.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.6% combined with core PCE y/y resuming a clear upward climb would indicate reacceleration, not continued disinflation.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.60) was wrong: the last print fell to 2.48 from 2.55, extending a decline that began after the 2.62 peak. The series has now dropped in four of the last five readings (2.62→2.60→2.57→2.58→2.55→2.48), and the 63-day change is -0.17pp, a meaningful reversal from the prior uptrend. The 21-day change is flat at 0.00pp only because it spans the peak-to-trough round trip, masking the recent acceleration lower in the newest print. Breakeven remains 0.48pp above the 2% target and range_position of 0.27 shows it's now in the lower third of its 12-month range (2.28-3.02), well off the highs. While my prior falsifier threshold (below 2.45) wasn't quite breached, the newest 2.48 print is close to it and the trend is clearly down, not up. I misread the momentum last time — the pullback that started as a "stall" has become a sustained decline. Given the consistent downward drift over the last three observations and the break below the recent 2.55-2.58 consolidation range, I now favor continued softening in breakeven inflation expectations, though I'm not at high conviction since we haven't broken the harder 2.36 floor.
Would change its mind if: A bounce back above 2.55 in the next print would suggest the decline was noise; a break below 2.36 would confirm a deeper downtrend.
Leaned on: be_change_63d, be_change_21d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked up to 3.60% from 3.50%, right in line with the "flat" call I made last time — the move stayed well inside my stated 3.40-3.70% no-signal band, so that view held up. Looking at the fuller series: unemployment is still just 0.10pp above the 12-month low, the 3m change is exactly 0.00pp, and the Sahm gap is a negligible +0.07pp, far below the ~0.5pp threshold that would flag a genuine turn. The rate remains 0.40pp below my assumed natural rate of ~4%, and the 12-month change of -1.50pp reflects the earlier disinflationary-tightening period, not new momentum. Policy language is essentially unchanged in substance — the added "nonetheless" is a rhetorical hedge, not a data-driven signal, and the underlying description (robust job gains, low unemployment) is identical. One month's uptick from 3.50 to 3.60 is well within normal noise for this series, which has oscillated in a narrow 3.50-3.70 band for the last six readings. I see no rapid-rise, off-low pattern that would justify calling a loosening trend. The market looks like it's plateaued at a tight level with no clear directional momentum either way.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would signal a genuine loosening turn; a print at/below 3.40% would confirm continued tightening beyond the current plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.45) was wrong: dgs10 has risen further, from 3.11 to 3.15 at the most recent print, extending a clear uptrend. The 21d change is +0.40pp and 63d is +0.21pp, both positive, and the level sits at 0.85 of its 12-month range - near the top, well above the 12m low by 1.87pp. That is the exact rebound/reversal condition I set previously (above ~2.90, 21d positive, range_position >0.75), so the softening thesis is falsified. The 30s10s slope is +0.12pp, modestly positive, consistent with continued long-end pressure rather than curve flattening from a rally. Balance sheet runoff continues per unchanged language, which structurally supports upward pressure on long yields. Short-term (10-day) wiggle from 3.04 to 3.15 shows some choppiness but the trend across 21d/63d windows is unambiguously higher. Given the level is already near the 12-month high with strong upward momentum and continued QT, I now see the term premium as rising, not falling. I hold moderate-high conviction given the consistency across horizons (21d, 63d) and the range position, though the very short 10-day series shows some pullback-then-rebound noise.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below ~3.00 with 21d change turning negative and range_position falling under 0.70 would reverse this rising-premium view.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest prints show NFCI resuming its decline after the mid-series bounce: from -0.17 the index fell steadily to -0.24 over the last six readings, with the last two prints flat at -0.24. The 4-week change is -0.04 (easing), reversing the tightening impulse that had pushed the 13-week change to +0.04. My prior "down" call was correct for this stretch — conditions eased rather than the tightening trend reasserting itself. The index remains well below zero (-0.24), meaning conditions are looser than historical average, and sits near the top of its 52-week range in easing terms (range_position 0.85, close to the 52w high of -0.16 in absolute terms but still historically loose). Momentum is modestly negative (easing) but has flattened over the last two observations, suggesting the easing pace is decelerating rather than accelerating. Given the flattening at -0.24 for two consecutive weeks, I lean toward continued mild easing or stabilization near this level rather than a sharp move either way. The signal is not strong enough for high conviction, but the recent 4w trend and lack of any tightening catalyst support a modest continuation of easing or flat conditions.
Would change its mind if: A resumption of rising weekly prints or 4w change turning clearly positive would indicate tightening has reasserted itself.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: fed_assets fell again this release (8849762 → 8851436, essentially flat over the last two prints but down from the 8879138/8874620 range before that), continuing the broader downtrend. The 4-week change is -38,568M and the 13-week change is -62,845M, both negative, confirming ongoing runoff. The 52-week change remains positive (+518,693M) but that reflects the earlier QE/reinvestment period and is far less relevant than the recent trend per mandate. Assets sit 114,051M below the 52-week high, and the 13-week average (8,895,808.77) is above the latest print, showing the recent observations are consistently below trend — consistent with steady balance-sheet contraction. The policy language is unchanged, explicitly reaffirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings per the announced caps, with no hint of tapering or pausing runoff. The latest single-week uptick (8849762→8851436) is tiny and well within normal noise around auction/settlement timing, not a reversal. Absent any language change signaling a slowdown, the trajectory should continue lower into next month-end.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-week rise in fed_assets alongside explicit language pausing or slowing the reduction plan would flip this to expansion.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down/flattening) played out over the medium term: slope_change_63d is -0.58pp and slope_change_252d is -1.39pp, confirming the multi-month flattening trend, and the curve remains deeply inverted at -0.30pp, sitting near the bottom of its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.10, only +0.18pp above the 12-month low of -0.48pp). However, the most recent short-term signal has turned: slope_change_21d is now +0.01pp, essentially flat, and the last two prints (-0.35, -0.30) show a small bounce off the recent trough rather than continued deterioration. This is not the sustained re-steepening move above -0.10/0.00pp I set as my falsifier, so I don't think a trend reversal is confirmed yet. The continued Fed tightening language (rate hikes "anticipated to be appropriate," moving to 2.25-2.5%) keeps front-end pressure alive, which argues against a strong steepening move at month end. On balance, the flattening trend has stalled but not reversed — the near-term change is negligible and the level remains deeply inverted near range lows. I'll shift from a confident "down" to a much weaker lean, since the 21d momentum no longer supports further flattening, but nothing signals genuine steepening either.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move back above ~-0.10 to 0.00pp for 2+ consecutive readings, with 21d change turning clearly positive, would indicate re-steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_above_12m_low

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Realized CPI trend would clarify whether breakeven decline reflects falling actual inflation pressure.
  inflation_expectations lacks term_premium — Would help separate real yield moves from genuine inflation expectation repricing in breakeven decline.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel points to a moderate, uneven disinflation backdrop meeting rising term premium and continued QT, with easing financial conditions and a plateaued labor market. Inflation itself is bouncing rather than trending cleanly — base effects argue for a lower y/y print but m/m reaccelerated slightly, so I keep a modest down lean rather than a strong one. Inflation expectations show a clearer break lower (four of five recent prints down, 63d change -0.17pp, range position in the lower third), and this dovetails with the inflation analyst's base-effect logic even though the two weren't cross-informed — that consistency raises my confidence in the expectations call specifically. Labor tightness has no directional signal; the print is squarely mid-band with negligible 3m change and Sahm gap, so flat stands. Term premium is the most unambiguous call on the panel: 21d and 63d momentum, range position near highs, and continued balance-sheet runoff all point the same way, so I take that up-call at good conviction. Financial conditions show easing but the pace is decelerating (two flat prints at -0.24), so I take a light down lean, not a strong one. Balance sheet contraction is confirmed by consistent 4w/13w declines and unchanged runoff language — a clean down call. Curve slope is the weakest read: multi-month flattening has stalled, 21d change is flat, and the level is pinned near 12-month lows with no re-steepening confirmed, so I keep a very light flat/near-zero view rather than sticking with the prior down call.

Overall this is a picture of a Fed still tightening via balance sheet and holding front-end pressure, while long-end term premium rises on QT and supply concerns, inflation gently decelerating but not cleanly, inflation expectations rolling over, financial conditions loosening modestly, and labor market genuinely flat. No single driver dominates; the read is a continuation of recent trends with softened conviction on the noisier series (inflation, curve slope).

discounted.why: No basis for a directional call; data squarely mid-band, kept flat rather than discounted, but no conviction lean applied.
Signal too noisy/stalled to support any but near-zero conviction; largely set aside as inconclusive.

arbitrated.falsifier: A hot CPI m/m print (>0.6%) with breakevens bouncing back above 2.55 and dgs10 falling below 3.00 would flip this entire read toward reacceleration/tightening reversal.

risks.text: Inflation series is genuinely bouncing, not trending; next print could easily surprise either direction.
Inflation expectations analyst lacked realized CPI and term premium context, so breakeven decline could partly reflect real yield moves, not inflation views.
Curve slope flat call has very low conviction; a real re-steepening or renewed flattening could emerge without warning.
Fed language shifts (e.g., pausing QT or hiking pace) could abruptly move balance sheet and term premium calls.

why.inflation: Base effects still favor lower y/y, but m/m reaccelerated slightly and core PCE is bouncing — tempered down lean vs analyst's 0.35.
why.inflation_expectations: Consistent multi-print decline, negative 63d change, low range position — credible reversal, kept near analyst's conviction.
why.labor_tightness: Print squarely within noise band, negligible 3m change and Sahm gap — no directional view, matches analyst's flat call.
why.term_premium: Clean multi-horizon uptrend (21d/63d positive), near 12m-high range position, QT reinforcing — kept analyst's conviction.
why.financial_conditions: Easing trend intact but decelerating (two flat prints) — softened analyst's 0.30 down call slightly.
why.balance_sheet: Consistent 4w/13w declines, unchanged runoff language, minor uptick is noise — kept analyst's down call near full conviction.
why.curve_slope: Flattening has stalled (21d ~flat) but no re-steepening confirmed; downgraded analyst's residual down lean toward neutral.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The previous call (down, 0.35) was roughly right in direction: headline y/y ticked down slightly from 8.22 to 8.19, continuing the drift off the 8.98 peak. But the composition is less benign than a simple headline read suggests. The outgoing m/m print of 0.42% is well below the near-term 0.96% pace, but the 3m annualized headline (1.90%) is now far beneath the 12m trend, driven by volatile components — this looks like an energy/base-effect air-pocket rather than durable disinflation, especially since core PCE y/y actually rose to 5.36%, matching its high for the period, with core_yoy_change_3m at +0.18pp and core_3m_annualized still running hot at 5.65%. The core-headline gap (-2.83pp) is unusually wide, meaning underlying persistence (core) is not falling in line with headline. Policy language reinforces this: it explicitly broadened supply-shock references (adding "food" to energy) and reiterated "elevated" and "additional upward pressure," signaling the Committee itself sees the disinflation as fragile/incomplete. Given headline is still near the top of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.71) and core momentum is reaccelerating, I do not have confidence that next month's headline print continues its recent modest cooling. The mom_gap and single soft month look like noise against persistent core strength.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.3% paired with core PCE y/y falling back below 5.2% would confirm renewed, broad-based disinflation.
Leaned on: headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, core_minus_headline, yoy_range_position, outgoing_mom

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year breakeven has continued to slide, printing 2.19 then 2.15 in the two most recent observations after a run in the 2.32-2.41 range. My prior call (down, 0.55) was correct and the falsifier condition for confirming a deeper downtrend (break below 2.36) has been met decisively - the series is now at its 12-month low, with be_above_12m_low at 0.00pp and be_range_position at 0.00, meaning breakeven sits at the bottom of its trailing range. The 21-day change of -0.33pp is the sharpest of the three windows (63d -0.19, 252d -0.23), indicating the pace of decline has accelerated recently rather than stabilizing. Distance from the 2% target has compressed to +0.15pp, the smallest cushion in this dataset, suggesting market-implied inflation expectations are approaching the Committee's objective from above. With no bounce back above 2.55 and instead a fresh low, the evidence supports continuation of the downtrend into month-end. The rate of decline in the last few prints is the strongest signal here - two consecutive sub-2.20 readings after a period of relative stability around 2.35-2.40 suggests a genuine regime shift rather than noise, though the move could decelerate given how close breakevens now sit to the 2% anchor.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 2.33 (prior local level) in the next print would suggest the recent drop was a transient dislocation rather than a trend.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_above_12m_low, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print came in at 3.50%, right at the 12-month low, confirming my prior "flat" call was correct — the print stayed within the plateau I described and did not breach 3.70%. The unemployment rate has been essentially pinned between 3.5-3.7% for the last seven readings after the earlier decline from 4.7%. Short-term momentum (3m change -0.10pp, 6m change -0.20pp) is marginal and consistent with noise around a stable floor rather than a fresh tightening or loosening impulse. The Sahm-rule style gap (current vs 3m avg vs 12m low) is a negligible +0.03pp, far below any threshold that would signal an inflection. Policy language continues to describe job gains as robust and unemployment as low, with only a minor rhetorical addition ("nonetheless") that doesn't change the substantive characterization. Level-wise, the rate sits 0.5pp below my 4% natural-rate anchor, indicating a still-tight labor market, but there's no directional signal of further tightening or the start of loosening. Absent a clear break either above 3.70% (loosening) or below 3.40% (further tightening), the most defensible call remains flat.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a genuine loosening turn; a print at/below 3.30% would signal renewed tightening beyond this plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_3m_avg, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call is validated: yield rose from 3.72 to 3.83 in the latest print, extending a sharp climb (21d +0.68pp, 63d +0.95pp, 252d +2.29pp). The 10-year now sits at 0.95 on its 12-month range (near the top of the 1.35–3.97 band), confirming persistent upward pressure rather than mean-reversion. The 10s30s slope is only slightly negative (-0.04pp), indicating the long end itself is not inverting sharply — the move is broad-based across the long end rather than a curvature quirk. Continued balance-sheet runoff (ongoing Treasury/MBS redemption caps per policy language) keeps a structural upward bias on term premium via increased net supply absorption by the private sector. There's no sign of flight-to-quality bidding yields down; quite the opposite, yields are grinding to fresh highs within the observed window. The pace of increase (last two prints: 3.72→3.76→3.83) is a bit more moderate than the prior 3.88→3.97 spike, suggesting deceleration but not reversal. Given proximity to the 12-month high and lack of any measurement suggesting a pullback, I maintain a rising-premium view, though I temper conviction slightly since the very top of the range historically invites some consolidation.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward 3.50 with 21d change turning negative and range_position falling under 0.70 would reverse this rising-premium view.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down/easing continuation) was wrong: NFCI has risen for the last several weeks, moving from -0.24 up to -0.12, with the 4-week change now +0.10, a clear reversal from the flattening/easing trend seen before. The index sits at its 52-week high and at range_position 1.00, meaning conditions are the tightest they've been in the past year relative to their own range, even though the level itself (-0.12) remains modestly below the zero average. Momentum is the key signal here: six consecutive up-prints in the most recent stretch (-0.22→-0.20→-0.18→-0.15→-0.12) show a persistent tightening impulse, not noise. The 13-week change is only +0.05, smaller than the 4-week change, confirming the tightening is a recent, accelerating phenomenon rather than a stale trend. With conditions still below zero in absolute terms, there's no danger of extreme tightness, but the direction is unambiguous: financial conditions are tightening relative to recent history. I now expect the index to continue drifting higher toward zero over the next month, given the sustained momentum and no offsetting information suggesting a reversal.
Would change its mind if: A stall or reversal in weekly prints, or 4w change turning negative, would indicate the tightening momentum has faded.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest observation (8,795,567) extends the decline seen since the recent local peak (8,899,213), confirming the previous "down" call was correct. Total assets fell in the most recent week by roughly 21,235 vs the prior print, and the 4-week change is -30,526M while the 13-week change is -117,986M, both negative and consistent with ongoing runoff. The pace is modest but steady — roughly $8-30B per 4 weeks, in line with a balance sheet still working through its announced caps rather than accelerating or reversing. The 52-week change remains positive (+347,586M) reflecting the longer look-back still capturing a period before runoff dominated, but that is a stale signal for a one-month-ahead call; the recent trend is what matters per mandate. Policy language is unchanged and reaffirms continued reduction in Treasury and MBS holdings with no mention of tapering the pace or pausing, so there is no basis to expect a reversal. Given the consistent week-over-week decline and unchanged reinvestment language, I expect fed_assets to be modestly lower at next month-end, continuing the current runoff pace.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-week rise in fed_assets alongside explicit language pausing or slowing the reduction plan would flip this to expansion.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread remains deeply inverted at -0.39pp, near the bottom of its 12-month range (position 0.07, only 0.12pp above the 12m low of -0.51). My prior "flat" call was roughly correct in direction terms: the last four readings (-0.39, -0.33, -0.35, -0.40, -0.39) have oscillated in a narrow band without a clear trend, though the 21d change of -0.09pp shows a mild residual flattening bias and the 63d/252d changes (-0.43, -1.62) confirm the broader multi-month flattening/inversion trend has not reversed. The Fed's continued rate hikes (funds rate raised again, "ongoing increases... appropriate" language retained) keep pushing the front end (dgs2 +4.22%) against a more anchored long end (dgs10 +3.83%), sustaining inversion pressure. There's no evidence of steepening impulse — no re-acceleration in 10yr yields relative to 2yr, and 10s30s is flat-to-slightly-negative, suggesting the long end isn't independently steepening either. Given the sideways chop within a tight recent range and no signal of imminent reversal, I expect the spread to stay roughly flat near current inverted levels into month-end, with a slight lean toward continued mild flattening given the persistent hiking language and negative 21d momentum.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move back above ~-0.10 to 0.00pp for 2+ consecutive readings, with 21d change turning clearly positive, would indicate re-steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, dgs2, dgs10, slope_10s30s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Whether realized CPI surprises are driving the breakeven decline would clarify if it's demand-driven or technical.
  inflation_expectations lacks financial_conditions — A liquidity or risk-off shock could distort TIPS pricing independent of true inflation views.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Updated pace/size of Treasury runoff would clarify supply pressure on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader tightening/easing context would confirm whether long-end rise is idiosyncratic or systemic.

arbitrated.notes: Cross-checking the seven reads, a coherent picture emerges of a still-tight, still-tightening macro backdrop even as headline inflation and breakevens soften. Inflation: headline CPI cooling is likely a base-effect/energy air-pocket, not durable disinflation — core PCE reaccelerating to a cycle high and policy language broadening supply-shock references both argue against taking the soft headline print at face value. I keep a mild "flat-to-up" lean rather than down. Inflation expectations are the cleanest, freshest signal: breakevens made a fresh 12-month low with accelerating downside momentum across all three windows, and no financial-conditions shock was flagged as distorting TIPS pricing, so I trust this as a genuine expectations decline, tempered slightly by proximity to the 2% anchor which could slow the pace. Labor tightness is a well-supported flat call — unemployment pinned in a narrow plateau, Sahm-gap negligible — no reason to override. Term premium and financial conditions corroborate each other even without being explicitly linked: rising NFCI (tightening) and rising 10y yields near range highs both point the same direction, consistent with balance-sheet runoff continuing to add net supply. I keep both "up" calls, with term premium tempered for proximity to its 12-month high. Balance sheet is a clean, low-noise runoff continuation — high conviction down. Curve slope stays flat/mild-flattening: deeply inverted, oscillating in a tight band with no steepening impulse, consistent with front-end hikes against an anchored long end. Overall: cooling headline inflation and falling breakevens sit alongside tightening financial conditions, rising term premium, and steady balance-sheet runoff — a market pricing softer expected inflation while realized conditions stay tight and long yields grind higher.

discounted.why: Single soft headline print looks like noise against reaccelerating core; low conviction retained on opposite sign.

arbitrated.falsifier: A CPI m/m print below 0.3% with core PCE back under 5.2%, plus breakevens rebounding above 2.33, would flip the disinflation read; a stall in NFCI/yield momentum would flip tightening view.

risks.text: Falling breakevens alongside reaccelerating core PCE and rising term premium/NFCI could reflect diverging growth vs inflation narratives, not a single regime.
Term premium and financial conditions both sit near 12-month range extremes, raising odds of mean-reversion/consolidation next month.
Panel lacks cross-driver data (e.g., CPI surprises vs breakevens, balance-sheet pace vs term premium) that could materially change these reads.

why.inflation_expectations: Fresh 12-month low, accelerating 21d decline across all windows; cleanest, freshest signal on the panel.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment pinned in narrow plateau, negligible momentum and Sahm-gap; well-supported flat call, no override.
why.term_premium: 10y grinding to fresh highs, near top of 12m range; tempered slightly for proximity to range top inviting consolidation.
why.financial_conditions: Six consecutive up-prints, accelerating 4w change, at 52w high on range_position; momentum signal is clear.
why.balance_sheet: Steady weekly/4w/13w declines, unchanged reinvestment language, low-noise continuation of runoff.
why.curve_slope: Deep inversion oscillating in tight band; mild residual flattening bias from 21d momentum, no steepening evidence.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release's headline_mom came in at 0.56%, above the 0.3% disinflation threshold I set, and core PCE y/y ticked up to 5.61% from 5.36% — the opposite of confirmation. My prior "flat" call was too cautious; the data since have leaned mildly toward re-acceleration in underlying pressure even as headline y/y continues to decelerate off high base effects (7.76% vs a 12-month high of 8.98%). The core_yoy_change_3m is now +0.29pp and core_3m_annualized (5.03%) sits well above the headline 3m annualized (4.26%), meaning underlying momentum is running hotter than the top-line number suggests. Headline mom_3m_avg (0.35%) is modestly above the prior month's own pace, and the latest 0.56% print reversed two months of very soft (~0%) readings. Base effects still favor a lower headline y/y print next month if energy/food stay contained, but the core reacceleration signals genuine persistence risk that could show up as a firmer-than-expected headline mom. Given headline y/y has been declining for three straight months on base effects while core is re-accelerating, I lean toward the headline print being roughly flat-to-modestly-up next month rather than continuing its recent downtrend, since the disinflation confirmation I was waiting for did not materialize.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.2% combined with core PCE y/y resuming its decline below 5.1% would confirm disinflation resuming.
Leaned on: headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, core_3m_annualized, headline_3m_annualized, headline_mom_3m_avg

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was wrong: breakeven_10y has since risen and now stabilized around 2.51-2.52, well above the 2.33 threshold I flagged as a reversal signal. The series shows a sharp climb from 2.41 to a peak of 2.59, a pullback to 2.44-2.45, and now a renewed push back to 2.51-2.52 for the last two prints. The 21-day change (+0.33pp) is strongly positive, though the 63-day change is flat (+0.01pp), indicating the net move over the medium term has been a round-trip rather than sustained acceleration. However, the most recent two observations show renewed upward momentum after the mid-period dip, ending at the second-highest reading in the window. Breakeven sits 0.52pp above the 2% target and in the upper-middle of its 12-month range (0.43 position), not extreme. Given the recent uptick in the last two prints following a consolidation, and the fact that inflation expectations have decisively broken above the level that would have confirmed my prior downside thesis, I now lean toward continued firmness or modest further upside rather than renewed decline. Conviction is moderate given the choppy 63-day flat trend suggests this could also just be range-bound oscillation.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.45 in the next print would confirm range-bound oscillation rather than renewed upward momentum.

arbitrated.notes: Cross-checking the seven single-driver reads, the picture is one of tightening/hawkish drift across most channels, with labor the lone stable pillar. Inflation: headline momentum re-accelerated (0.56% mom) and core PCE y/y ticked up despite falling headline y/y on base effects — genuine but moderate persistence risk, not yet confirmed disinflation reversal. Inflation expectations corroborate this modestly: breakevens broke above the analyst's own reversal threshold and show renewed upward momentum in the last two prints, though the 63d change is flat, so this is a tentative re-anchoring higher rather than a runaway move — it lends some credibility to the inflation call being more than noise. Labor is genuinely flat — no Sahm signal, tight range, no directional information to add either way. Term premium, financial conditions, and balance sheet all point the same direction: balance-sheet runoff is unambiguous and undecelerating (highest conviction read on the panel), financial conditions are tightening off their loosest point in a year, and 10y yields sit near cycle highs with positive medium-term momentum — these three reinforce each other via the missing-link connections the panel itself flagged (balance sheet runoff as supply pressure on term premium, tightening financial conditions consistent with higher long-end risk premium). Curve slope is flattening again after a failed steepening attempt, consistent with a hawkish policy stance still being priced, though this partly reflects front-end pressure rather than duration risk per se. Overall this reads as an environment of continued policy tightening transmission: runoff continuing, financial conditions incrementally tighter, term premium elevated and stable-to-rising, inflation showing early re-acceleration signs with expectations modestly confirming, labor a stable non-mover, and the curve continuing its flattening bias.

discounted.why: Report itself describes pure noise around a stable plateau; no basis for directional conviction.

arbitrated.falsifier: Headline CPI mom below 0.2% with core PCE y/y resuming decline, breakevens back under 2.45, and NFCI/10y reversing lower would flip this to a disinflationary/easing read.

risks.text: Three of seven calls were formed this meeting with no track record, adding noise to the cross-driver reinforcement story.
Headline inflation y/y decline from base effects could still dominate next print despite core reacceleration.
Both breakevens and 2s10s show recent round-trip patterns; current readings could just be mid-oscillation, not a new trend.
Financial conditions and term premium moves are small in absolute magnitude; direction is right but conviction is thin.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  My previous "up" call at 0.35 conviction was wrong: headline y/y fell from 7.76 to 7.12, a sizable drop, continuing the decline from the 8.98 peak. I should have weighted the falling base-effect trajectory more heavily. Looking at the current data, headline y/y has now declined for several consecutive months and sits at the 12-month low, with headline_yoy_change_3m at -1.10pp — clear deceleration. The latest m/m print of 0.26% is below the 3m average of 0.42% and well below the outgoing 0.69%, suggesting continued softening momentum rather than reacceleration. However, core PCE tells a more mixed story: it ticked up to 5.46 from 5.11 two months prior, and core_yoy_change_3m is +0.35pp, indicating underlying persistence has not broken down despite headline's favorable base effects (energy/food likely driving the divergence, given core_minus_headline of -1.66pp is unusual — core running above headline). Given headline is the metric being asked about, and both the y/y trend and the low recent m/m print point the same direction, I lean toward continued headline deceleration next month, though the core stickiness tempers my confidence that this becomes a durable trend break.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.6% or a re-acceleration in core PCE y/y above 5.6% would signal the disinflation trend is stalling or reversing.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

Report:
  The previous "up" call was wrong: breakeven_10y has not sustained any upward momentum, and in fact the falsifier condition (drop and range-bound oscillation) has effectively played out. The latest print (2.34) is a bounce off two prints at 2.25-2.26, but the broader trend is negative: be_change_21d (-0.17pp), be_change_63d (-0.24pp), and be_change_252d (-0.21pp) all point down over multiple windows, showing sustained cooling in market-implied inflation expectations rather than noise. The series has oscillated in a fairly narrow band (2.25-2.34 in the last ten prints, 12-month range 2.15-3.02) with be_range_position at just 0.22 - solidly in the lower quartile of the annual range. That said, the last single print did tick up 0.09pp from the prior observation, which tempers a purely bearish read. Distance from the 2% target remains positive at +0.34pp, so expectations are still anchored above objective, but the trajectory over the past year and past quarter is clearly one of receding breakevens, not renewed inflation concern. Given the multi-horizon negative changes dominate a single-point uptick, I lean toward continued softening or at best sideways consolidation near current lower-range levels rather than a resumption of the up move I previously expected.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above 2.40 over multiple consecutive prints would signal renewed upward momentum rather than range-bound consolidation.

The rate sits 0.4pp below the 4% natural-rate anchor, indicating a still-tight labor market by level. The 12m change of -0.50pp reflects the earlier decline from 4.0-4.1% into this plateau, but that move is now stale relative to the last several flat months. The Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is a negligible +0.07pp, well below any threshold that would signal an accelerating loosening trend. Range position of 0.20 shows the rate sits near the low end of its 12-month range, consistent with tightness rather than loosening. Policy language reinforces this: job gains described as robust and unemployment as low, with no acknowledgment of labor market deterioration. With no directional momentum in either direction over three or six months, and the level still comfortably below the natural rate, the most defensible call is continued flatness. I see no evidence pointing to an imminent uptick or further decline.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a genuine loosening turn; a print at/below 3.30% would signal renewed tightening beyond this plateau.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel paints a picture of a disinflationary macro backdrop but with several nuances. Headline CPI momentum is clearly decelerating (falling y/y, soft recent m/m) even though core PCE has ticked up, so I give inflation a moderate down lean, tempered by the core stickiness the analyst flagged. Inflation expectations show multi-horizon negative changes (21d/63d/252d all down) dominating a single recent uptick, consistent with genuine but modest cooling rather than a durable break — I read this as leaning down/flat, favoring a soft downward lean rather than pure flat given the dominance of longer-window declines. Labor tightness shows no directional momentum over 3-6 months and sits near cycle lows in range position — a clean flat call with decent conviction given multiple corroborating metrics (Sahm gap negligible, distance from natural rate stable). Term premium is genuinely mixed: near-term easing (21d down) versus still-elevated 63d/252d and range position, plus structural balance-sheet-runoff pressure — I keep this close to flat but tilt slightly down given the recency of the pullback dominates the still-high but stale longer-window comparisons. Financial conditions show a clean three-observation easing streak with negative 4w change overriding the stale 13w tightening trend — I lean down here with moderate conviction. Balance sheet runoff is the cleanest, highest-conviction call: steady, accelerating, policy-confirmed decline with no reversal signal — clear down. Curve slope shows a deeply inverted level with a tentative two-print bounce; the trend dominates the noise, so I lean toward continued flat-to-mild-flattening rather than a reversal, keeping conviction low given the bounce's ambiguity.

Cross-checking the gaps the panel flagged: inflation's move is somewhat corroborated by softening expectations (both drivers point the same direction, mildly reinforcing durability of headline deceleration). Term premium's mixed signal is only partially explained by the missing balance-sheet linkage, which if anything should support the "still elevated" side, so I discount the near-term dip slightly.
arbitrated.falsifier: Headline CPI m/m above 0.6%, core PCE y/y above 5.6%, breakevens sustaining above 2.40, or fed_assets rising for multiple weeks would flip this toward a less disinflationary, more balance-sheet-expansionary read.

risks.text: Core PCE re-acceleration could undercut the headline disinflation narrative and stall further declines.
Term premium's recent dip could be noise; a rebound above 3.80 would reassert the prior up trend.
Policy language still framing conditions as tight could presage a re-tightening in financial conditions or curve re-steepening.
Several calls (curve slope bounce, breakeven uptick) rest on very recent single-print moves that may not persist.

why.inflation: Headline y/y and m/m clearly decelerating; core PCE stickiness tempers conviction from analyst's own 0.45 read.
why.inflation_expectations: Multi-horizon breakeven declines dominate single-print bounce; softer than analyst's 'flat' framing given consistent negative windows.
why.labor_tightness: No 3m/6m momentum, negligible Sahm gap, stable range position — genuine flat call, matches analyst's high-conviction flat read.
why.term_premium: Near-term pullback (21d) vs stale elevated 63d/252d; slight down tilt given recency but structural runoff caps the downside.
why.financial_conditions: Three-print easing streak and negative 4w change override stale 13w tightening; moderate down lean per analyst's own call.
why.balance_sheet: Consistent multi-week runoff, accelerating pace, unchanged policy language — highest-conviction clean down call.
why.curve_slope: Deep inversion trend dominates tentative two-print bounce; slight down/flat lean, low conviction given ambiguous recent uptick.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct and the newest data confirm continued disinflation. Headline CPI y/y fell sharply to 6.40% from 7.12%, a full percentage point drop, and the outgoing m/m print was just 0.02%, the softest in the 13-month window. The 3-month annualized headline pace is now 3.39%, well below the y/y rate, signaling the trend is decelerating, not just base effects. My falsifier (m/m above 0.6% or core PCE y/y above 5.6%) was not triggered — m/m came in near zero and core PCE y/y sits at 5.21%, below its recent peak of 5.61%. Core PCE remains sticky in the 5.1-5.6% band with no clear downtrend, still 3.2pp above target, so persistence risk remains — but I weight headline more here since the question is specifically about headline CPI y/y, and its momentum (3m annualized, falling mom_gap) points firmly downward. The softened policy language ("contributing to" vs "creating additional") also suggests officials see inflation pressure easing modestly. With headline already down five consecutive months from its peak and the freshest print near flat, I expect the next release to continue lower, though the sticky core keeps me from very high conviction.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.5% or core PCE y/y jumping back above 5.6% would signal disinflation stalling.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "flat" call holds up reasonably well: breakeven_10y has moved from 2.27 to 2.30 over the last few prints, staying within the recent range and well short of the 2.40 threshold I set as a falsifier for renewed upward momentum. Looking at the full series, the last three readings (2.26, 2.28, 2.27, 2.30) show a mild grind higher after a dip to 2.19-2.20 mid-series, but the 21d and 63d changes are both slightly negative (-0.04, -0.03 pp), indicating the very recent trend is flat-to-softening even though the latest single print ticked up. The 252d change of -0.20 pp confirms the broader trajectory has been gently disinflationary in market pricing over the past year, and the range_position of 0.19 (near the low end of the 12-month band) suggests expectations remain closer to the low than the high. Distance from the 2% target is +0.30 pp, a modest but not alarming premium. With no consecutive multi-print break above 2.40, and short-term derived changes negative despite the latest tick, I don't see enough evidence for a strong directional shift. This looks like continued range-bound consolidation rather than a new trend, so I maintain a flat call but with slightly higher conviction given the negative 21d/63d changes reinforcing the range-bound read.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above 2.40 or below 2.10 over multiple consecutive prints would signal a genuine breakout from the current range.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target, breakeven_10y

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.60
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print (3.50%) matches the 12-month low and confirms the previous "flat" call was correct: the unemployment rate has been oscillating in a narrow 3.50-3.60% band for the last several months with no directional break. The 3m change is exactly 0.00pp, the 6m change is a negligible -0.10pp, and the Sahm-rule gap is a trivial +0.07pp, far below the ~0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine turn. The rate sits 0.50pp below the assumed natural rate of 4%, indicating a still-tight labor market, and the 12-month trend of -0.40pp reflects earlier tightening now fully plateaued rather than any fresh loosening impulse. Policy language reinforces this picture, describing job gains as robust and the unemployment rate as remaining low, consistent with a stable, tight labor market rather than one deteriorating. There is no evidence of acceleration in either direction — the range position sits at the very bottom of its 12-month band (0.0), meaning the market is if anything at its tightest point recently, not loosening. Absent a break above 3.70% or below 3.30%, I see no basis for a directional call beyond continuation of the current plateau.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a genuine loosening turn; a print at/below 3.30% would signal renewed tightening beyond this plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call at low conviction was too cautious: yields have continued climbing, with the 10Y now at 3.88%, up from levels near 3.83-3.88 and confirming the rebound-above-3.80 condition I flagged as reaffirming an up call. The 21d (+0.20pp) and 63d (+0.16pp) changes are both positive, and the 252d change is a large +2.39pp, showing sustained upward momentum over the past year. Range position sits at 0.86, near the top of the 12-month range, indicating the yield is pressing toward cycle highs rather than mean-reverting. The 10s30s slope remains modestly positive (+0.09pp), consistent with continued steepening pressure at the long end rather than curve inversion pulling long yields down. Ongoing balance-sheet runoff (continued Treasury/MBS reduction) is a structural tailwind for term premium, reducing price-insensitive demand and requiring the market to absorb more duration — consistent with the upward drift observed. There is no flight-to-quality signal evident in the data. Given the confirmed break above 3.80, elevated range position, and structural runoff backdrop, I am revising toward a clear up call for month-end, correcting my previous underweighting of the recent uptrend.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 3.75 with range_position falling under 0.70 and negative 21d change would signal the up move has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady grind lower: -0.10 → -0.20 over 13 weeks, with the latest print (-0.20) making a fresh low for the window. The 4-week change (-0.02) and 13-week change (-0.08) are both negative, confirming continued easing momentum rather than stalling. My prior "down" call was correct — the falsifier (print back above -0.14 or 4w change turning positive) did not trigger; instead conditions kept loosening. The index sits at -0.20, well below the zero average, and at 0.77 range position (77% of the way from the 52-week low toward the high, i.e., closer to the loose end historically for this window), reinforcing that financial conditions remain accommodative and are still loosening at the margin, not just historically loose. There is no sign of deceleration in the last few prints (-0.18, -0.18, -0.19, -0.19, -0.20) — the pace is modest but consistently negative. Absent any policy language shift signaling renewed tightening intent, the trend favors continued mild easing into month end. I maintain a "down" call, with slightly higher conviction than before given the consistent confirmation of the trend and lack of any reversal signal.
Would change its mind if: A print back above -0.17 or 4w change turning positive/flat would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The balance sheet continues to run off. Total assets fell each of the last several weeks, from 8,564,411 to 8,551,169 ($M), a 4-week decline of -33,407 and a 13-week decline of -244,398 (-2.78%). The 52-week change (-206,291) confirms this is a sustained trend, not noise, and assets are now -414,318 below the 52-week high. The policy language remains unchanged, explicitly confirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency MBS/debt holdings per the announced runoff plan — no language of pausing or slowing. My prior call (down, 0.75) was correct: assets declined further as expected. There is no evidence of any deceleration in pace — the 4-week pace (~-33.4k) is roughly consistent with recent monthly runoff caps, and the 13-week trend is steady and monotonic downward with only two minor upticks in the 13-observation series. With unchanged policy language reaffirming continued runoff, the base case for month-end is a further decline in total assets of similar magnitude to recent weeks. I maintain a confident down call, though conviction is not raised further since the pace itself hasn't been accelerating.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-week rise in fed_assets alongside explicit language pausing or slowing the reduction plan would flip this to expansion.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_change_52w, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print pulled back to -0.53pp from -0.43pp two readings prior, breaking a string that had touched -0.43pp. Over the last 10 observations the spread moved from -0.69 to -0.53, a net steepening of ~0.16pp, but the most recent two readings (-0.51, -0.53) show a modest re-flattening after the local high. The 21d change is still positive (+0.17pp), consistent with steepening over the past month, but the 63d change is negative (-0.18pp) and the 252d change is sharply negative (-1.28pp), showing the dominant trend over the past year has been flattening/inversion. The spread sits at 0.18 on the 12-month range (near the inverted end), still -0.53pp from flat, well within the 12m band of -0.84 to +0.89. My prior "flat" call was reasonably accurate — the spread has oscillated in a narrow -0.43 to -0.57 band for the last six readings, not sustaining a clear breakout above -0.55 as my falsifier required, though it briefly poked above that. The oscillation without a decisive breakout suggests continued near-term consolidation rather than a strong directional move. The 25bp hike continuing the tightening cycle keeps front-end anchored, limiting steepening room. I lean toward flat with a slight downward tilt given the two most recent prints reversing off the local high.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move below -0.60pp for 2+ consecutive readings with 21d change turning clearly negative would confirm renewed flattening; a clean break above -0.45pp would confirm steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_distance_from_flat

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/schedule of runoff would confirm whether long-end supply pressure is intensifying or stabilizing.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader tightening or easing in credit markets could corroborate or offset term premium direction.

arbitrated.notes: Headline disinflation continues but core PCE stickiness caps conviction — inflation call is down but not extreme. Inflation expectations remain range-bound with a very slight softening bias in short-window changes despite the latest uptick; treat as effectively flat. Labor market is plateaued at cycle-tight levels with no directional break, so flat with decent conviction. Term premium is the most notable shift: 10Y yields have broken to fresh range highs, momentum is positive across 21d/63d/252d windows, and balance-sheet runoff is a structural tailwind for higher term premium — this is corroborated by the balance_sheet analyst's own confident down call on the Fed's asset base, which mechanically reduces price-insensitive demand for duration and supports the term-premium analyst's up call even though that analyst wasn't shown the balance-sheet data directly. Financial conditions are easing further (NFCI grinding to fresh lows), which is a bit in tension with rising term premium, but the two series can coexist — easier credit conditions and higher risk-free duration compensation are not mutually exclusive, and the financial-conditions read is itself steady and unaccelerating. Curve slope is noisy: near-term steepening tension against a still-dominant one-year flattening trend, with the two most recent prints reversing lower — I read this as flat with a mild flattening tilt rather than a clean directional call. Balance sheet itself is the highest-conviction call on the panel: monotonic, policy-confirmed runoff with no deceleration. Overall the reconciled picture is disinflation progressing (headline) but sticky at the core, a tight-but-stable labor market, easier financial conditions, continued balance-sheet drawdown, and — tying those together — rising term premium as duration supply pressure builds against a still-anchored front end.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was directionally correct but the falsifier condition partly triggered: headline m/m came in at 0.53%, just above my 0.5% stated threshold, even as headline y/y continued falling (7.12→6.40→6.33) and core PCE y/y ticked down to 4.97 from 5.21. So disinflation in y/y terms is intact but momentum in the most recent month re-accelerated versus the prior two prints (0.02, 0.26). The 3m annualized rates (headline 3.28%, core 4.08%) are still well above the trailing y/y average deceleration pace, and core running hotter than headline on a 3-month annualized basis is a caution sign for persistence. Headline y/y sits at the 12-month low, a good sign, but the single-month uptick in MoM warns the pace of decline is decelerating, not reversing. Weighting core PCE for persistence: it's still falling, just slowly, and remains ~3pp above target. Net: I expect headline y/y to continue falling next release but by a smaller increment, given base effects still favorable but momentum less supportive. Policy language also softened only marginally, still describing inflation as elevated with upside risks from the war.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.6% or core PCE y/y rising back above 5.2% would signal disinflation has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last two prints show breakeven_10y falling from 2.33 to 2.29 to 2.24, a clear reversal after the series had risen from 2.12 up to a peak of 2.33. My prior "flat" call is now looking off: the 21d change (-0.03) had understated the momentum building beneath it, and the 63d change (-0.21) confirms a real downtrend has emerged, not just noise. The most recent two-observation drop (2.29→2.24, -0.05 in one step) is the sharpest single-period decline in the series and pushes the level back toward the lower third of the 12-month range (range_position 0.13, only 0.12pp above the 12m low of 2.12). Breakevens remain 0.24pp above the 2% objective, so there's no sign of expectations becoming unanchored to the downside, but the trajectory is now decisively negative over the past several prints. Given the acceleration in the last two observations rather than a stabilization, I read this as a genuine directional move rather than range-bound chop. I do not have visibility into what is driving this (real yields, term premium, or growth scares) since those are outside my mandate, but the pattern in the breakeven series itself is now clearly downward-sloping and accelerating, warranting a directional call rather than flat.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.29 sustained over two or more prints would signal this was a temporary dip, not a breakdown.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.65
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate sits at 3.50%, matching the 12-month low, with a range of only 3.50–3.90% over the past year. The last print was flat versus the prior month (3.50 → 3.50), and the 3-month change is a mild -0.10pp, while the 6-month change is exactly 0.00pp. The Sahm-rule gap is a negligible +0.03pp, far below the ~0.50pp threshold that would signal a genuine turn. My prior "flat" call was correct: the print came in at 3.50%, well within the plateau and not above my 3.70% loosening trigger. There is no evidence of acceleration in either direction — the series has oscillated in a tight 3.50-3.60% band for the last seven readings, essentially noise around a floor. Distance from the ~4% natural rate remains -0.50pp, indicating the labor market is still historically tight, not loosening. Policy language reiterates "robust" job gains and a "low" unemployment rate, consistent with continued tightness rather than any turn. With no momentum in either direction and the rate pinned at its cycle low, the most defensible call remains flat, and I hold conviction fairly high given the very low volatility and absence of any directional signal in the recent data.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a genuine loosening turn; a print at/below 3.30% would signal renewed tightening beyond this plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.55) was wrong to the extent it anticipated continued rising yields: the 10y has actually drifted down over the past month, with dgs10_change_21d at -0.31pp and dgs10_change_63d at -0.44pp, and the level (3.52) sits below recent local highs (3.55). This matches the falsifier condition I set previously in spirit (negative 21d change), though the level itself (3.52) is still elevated versus the 12m low and range_position (0.71) remains in the upper third of the 12-month range, so this isn't a reversal to low-yield territory — just a stall/pullback from a large prior run-up (dgs10_change_252d +1.67pp). The last few prints (3.46, 3.46, 3.49, 3.52, 3.55, 3.52) show choppy, range-bound movement rather than a clean trend either way. Slope_30s10s is modestly positive (+0.13pp), not signaling strong long-end steepening pressure from term premium building further. Given continued balance-sheet runoff language (a structural upward pressure on term premium) but recent price action showing consolidation/mild pullback rather than acceleration, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for month-end, with low-to-moderate conviction given the choppiness.
Would change its mind if: A break above 3.60 with range_position rising above 0.75 and positive 21d change would signal renewed upward term-premium pressure.

Policy language is unchanged, reaffirming continued balance-sheet reduction per the Plans for Reducing the Size of the Federal Reserve's Balance Sheet, with no hint of pausing or tapering the runoff. There is no evidence of caps being lowered or reinvestment resuming. Given the consistent week-over-week declines across the last 13 observations with no inflection or deceleration visible in the trend, the most probable outcome at the next month-end is a continuation of runoff at a similar pace, likely another decline in the $60-90B range. This is a high-conviction continuation call, not a reversal, since both the measurement trend and the policy text point the same direction with no contradicting signal.

Call: down, conviction 0.25

  The 2s10s spread has oscillated in a narrow, deeply inverted band around -0.65 to -0.70pp over the last 10 readings, ending at -0.69pp — essentially the same level as several readings ago. My prior "flat" call was reasonably accurate over the short window: the series shows no clean sustained break in either direction; it wiggles within a 0.05pp range near the bottom. However, the longer-horizon change measures (63d -0.35pp, 252d -1.41pp) show flattening pressure has dominated over the past year, and the curve sits at only 0.10 of its 12-month range (near the 12m low of -0.84pp), confirming deep inversion. The 21d change of -0.18pp is a modest renewed flattening impulse, but the last two prints (-0.70, -0.69) don't show acceleration — they're consistent with continued chop near cycle lows rather than a fresh leg down. Continued Fed hikes (funds rate raised again to 4.25-4.5%) keep front-end anchored high, which is a flattening force on the margin, but the curve has already priced substantial inversion. Absent a clear break below -0.75/-0.80 or above -0.55, I lean toward continued rangebound/slightly flattening bias given the persistent hiking cycle, but with low conviction since recent prints show stabilization.
Would change its mind if: A break below -0.80pp sustained for 2+ readings would confirm renewed flattening; a move above -0.55pp would confirm steepening.

arbitrated.notes: Cross-checking the seven drivers, a coherent macro story emerges: disinflation continues but is losing momentum, while financial conditions are easing even as the Fed mechanically drains its balance sheet — an interesting divergence worth flagging rather than reconciling away. Inflation (down, moderate conviction) is corroborated by falling breakevens, which independently confirms disinflation expectations are becoming entrenched rather than just a base-effect artifact; I lean on both together for a firmer "down" read on realized and expected inflation than either alone. Labor tightness is genuinely flat — very low volatility, no directional evidence, high conviction in the "no move" call itself. Term premium and curve slope are the two weakest, choppiest signals: 10y yields have stalled/pulled back from a big run-up, and 2s10s is rangebound near cycle lows. Both analysts admit low-to-moderate conviction and both would benefit from the balance-sheet read they didn't get — QT continuing at an undiminished pace is a structural upward force on term premium that argues against extrapolating the recent dip, so I fade the term-premium "down" bias toward flat/neutral rather than accept it. Financial conditions easing (down/looser) sits in tension with continued QT (down, i.e., shrinking assets) — normally these move together, but NFCI here is dominated by non-balance-sheet channels (credit spreads, equity vol, risk pricing) that have decoupled from Fed balance-sheet mechanics for now. I keep both calls but flag the tension as a risk. Overall: disinflation intact but decelerating, expectations turning down, labor still tight, financial conditions easing despite ongoing QT, term premium and curve directionally soft but low-conviction.
