user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 13 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: headline CPI y/y fell from 6.40 to 5.96, continuing a clear disinflationary trend from the 8.98 peak. My stated falsifier (m/m above 0.6% or core PCE back above 5.2%) did not trigger — the latest headline m/m was 0.34% and core PCE y/y sits at 4.94%, its lowest in the series. Momentum signals are consistent: headline_yoy_change_3m is -1.17pp, core_yoy_change_3m is -0.52pp, and headline_3m_annualized (3.61%) is now noticeably below the outgoing m/m pace, though core_3m_annualized (4.55%) remains stickier and above target, reflecting persistent core services/shelter pressure. I weight core PCE for persistence, and it too is grinding lower, just more slowly than headline. The policy language shift — from "elevated" to "eased somewhat but remains elevated" — corroborates the disinflation narrative without declaring victory. Base effects (the prior year's high comparisons rolling off) plus a modest recent m/m of 0.34% suggest headline y/y should keep drifting down next month, absent a shock. Distance from target remains large (core ~2.94pp above 2%), so this is deceleration, not disinflation danger — the direction is still down but the pace of decline may slow given the still-firm 3m annualized core figure.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.5% or core PCE y/y rising back above 5.0% would signal disinflation has stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My previous call (down, 0.55) was wrong: breakeven_10y has since risen and the latest print (2.43) is the highest in the 10-observation series, with be_change_21d and be_change_63d both firmly positive (+0.10, +0.11pp) even as be_change_252d remains negative. That longer-term decline is now stale relative to the recent trend reversal—my stated falsifier (sustained rebound above 2.29 for 2+ prints) has been met and then some, with the series pushing to new highs rather than just recovering. The level sits 0.34 into its 12-month range (34th percentile), so not extreme, but distance from the 2% target has widened to +0.43pp, above the recent average. Policy language dropped the explicit "public health" reference from the list of factors the Committee monitors, a subtle simplification that doesn't change the inflation-expectations framing itself but suggests reduced emphasis on pandemic-era disinflationary/deflationary risk factors, consistent with modestly firming expectations. Taken together, the short-term momentum is clearly up, breakeven is at a local high, and there's no offsetting signal in the recent data. I'm reversing to an up call, though conviction is moderate given the series remains mid-range historically and one month is a short window for continuation.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.38 sustained over two or more prints would signal this uptick was noise, not a new trend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.65
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate remains in a tight, narrow band (3.5-3.7% over the trailing year), and the latest print of 3.60% sits right in the middle of that range. My prior "flat" call was correct: the 3m and 6m changes are both +0.00pp, confirming no directional momentum. The 12m change of -0.30pp actually shows a slight tightening bias over the past year, not loosening. The Sahm-rule gap is a negligible +0.03pp, far below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine turn, and the range position of 0.50 shows the current reading is exactly mid-range between the 12m low (3.50) and high (3.70) — not a breakout in either direction. Distance from the natural rate (-0.40pp) confirms the labor market remains historically tight, well below 4%. Policy language changes (removal of "public health" from the assessment list) don't alter my read on this driver; the substantive language ("job gains robust," "unemployment rate remained low") is unchanged. With no clear momentum signal and the rate oscillating within a 0.2pp band for over a year, I see no basis for calling a directional move. The market looks stable at a tight level, consistent with continued flat readings ahead absent a new shock.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a genuine loosening turn; a print at/below 3.30% would signal renewed tightening beyond this plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield last printed 3.92, essentially flat over the newest two readings but up sharply on trend: +0.40pp over 21 days, +0.24pp over 63 days, and a massive +1.93pp over the past year. The yield sits at 0.87 of its 12-month range (near the top), well above the 3.60/0.75 threshold I had flagged previously as the trigger for renewed upward pressure — that condition has now been met and my prior "flat" call looks too cautious in hindsight, though the last two prints (3.92, 3.92) show the immediate momentum stalling just below the recent 3.95 local high. The 30s10s slope is near flat (+0.01pp), suggesting no strong long-end-specific steepening pressure beyond what's already priced, but continued balance-sheet runoff (reaffirmed in policy language) keeps a structural upward bias on term premium via supply. Given the yield is pinned near cycle highs with recent momentum positive but decelerating, I lean toward a modest further drift up or stabilization at elevated levels rather than a reversal. I don't see evidence of flight-to-quality or reversal signals here.
Would change its mind if: A drop in range_position below 0.75 with negative 21d change would indicate the upward pressure has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call was "down" (further easing) with conviction 0.60. The newest print is -0.29, up from -0.31 the prior period — a reversal, not continuation of easing. My stated falsifier ("print back above -0.24") was not hit, but the 4w change has clearly stalled: nfci_change_4w is -0.01 (essentially flat), and the last three readings (-0.31, -0.31, -0.29) show the multi-month easing trend leveling off and ticking back up. Over 13 weeks conditions did loosen substantially (-0.12), and the index remains well below zero (-0.29), so conditions are still looser than average — but the momentum that drove the prior call is gone. The policy language shift from "pace" to "extent" of future increases is a subtle change but doesn't by itself signal a financial-conditions shift. Given the flattening/slight reversal in the last two prints and near-zero 4-week change, I no longer see a confident easing trajectory into month-end. The most likely outcome is conditions holding roughly steady to marginally tighter, not continued easing. I'm revising to a modest "up" lean, reflecting the small uptick and stalled momentum, but with limited conviction given the noise in a single-period reversal.
Would change its mind if: A fresh print below -0.31 (new 52w-adjacent low) or 4w change turning clearly negative again would revive the easing call.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.80
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series continues its steady decline: total assets fell from 8,382,190 in the latest observation, down from 8,584,576 thirteen weeks prior. The 4-week change is -88,367$M, the 13-week change is -239,200$M (-2.77%), and the 52-week change is -545,939$M — all consistently negative, confirming ongoing runoff (QT) with no deceleration evident in the pace; if anything the most recent 4-week pace (~-88k) is roughly in line with or slightly faster than the 13-week average pace (~-61k/4wk), so no slowing is visible. Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-583,297$M), consistent with sustained balance-sheet reduction. My prior call (down, 0.78) was correct — the data confirm continued runoff exactly as expected.
  
  The policy language changed only cosmetically: the explicit reference to the "Plans for Reducing the Size of the Federal Reserve's Balance Sheet... issued in May" was replaced with a generic "as described in its previously announced plans." This is not a substantive change — it still affirms continued reduction, just with less specific phrasing. There is no language suggesting a pause, slowdown, or cap change. Given the unambiguous measurement trend and unchanged substantive policy stance (continued runoff), I maintain a confident down call for month-end.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-week rise in fed_assets alongside explicit language pausing, slowing, or ending the reduction plan would flip this to expansion.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (down/flattening, 0.25 conviction) was correct: the spread fell from -0.83 to -0.89, a fresh 12-month low, confirming the break below -0.80 for multiple readings that I flagged as the flattening-confirmation threshold. The trend is unambiguous in direction: -0.22pp over 21 days, -0.15pp over 63 days, and -1.30pp over the past year, with the series sitting at slope_range_position 0.00 (the very bottom of its 12-month range) and slope_distance_from_flat at -0.89pp, the most inverted level in the sample. The 10s30s slope near flat (+0.01pp) suggests the flattening pressure is concentrated in the front-to-belly relationship rather than the long end, consistent with continued front-end-led inversion. Policy language shows the Committee still raising rates (now 4.5-4.75%) and explicitly retains the tightening bias ("ongoing increases... will be appropriate"), reinforcing upward pressure on the front end that has been driving this inversion. There is no sign of deceleration in the flattening move within this data; the pace of decline (21d vs 63d) actually accelerated slightly. This supports continuation of the same directional call, with conviction modestly raised given the confirmed break and accelerating short-term momentum.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.80pp sustained for 2+ readings, or a sharp deceleration in the 21d change, would signal stabilizing/reversing flattening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_distance_from_flat, slope_10s30s

arbitrated.notes: The panel paints a coherent tightening-but-decelerating macro backdrop. Inflation continues to fall (headline clearly, core more slowly), but breakevens have turned up on real momentum (21d/63d positive, new series high), so realized disinflation and inflation expectations are diverging — a classic late-cycle wedge where markets price sticky core/services risk even as headline cools. I weight the inflation call moderately: direction still down but conviction trimmed slightly since core-3m-annualized is stubborn and the CPI analyst itself flags deceleration in the pace of decline. Inflation expectations get a real but capped-conviction "up" since it's a fresh reversal after being wrong last cycle and sits only mid-range historically. Labor tightness is the cleanest read on the panel — a full year of range-bound unemployment, negligible Sahm gap, no momentum — flat is the only defensible call. Term premium and curve slope are two views of the same front-end-tightening dynamic: term premium is drifting up (yields near cycle highs, QT ongoing) while curve slope confirms flattening/inversion (accelerating, at range bottom), both consistent with a Fed still hiking and running off the balance sheet. Balance sheet is the highest-conviction call on the panel — unambiguous, accelerating runoff with no policy language suggesting a pause. Financial conditions get low conviction "up" (tightening) — the reversal is only two prints old and could easily be noise, but it's consistent with the rest of the tightening mosaic (higher term premium, flatter curve, hawkish language). Overall the reconciled read: policy-driven tightening (rates, balance sheet, curve) continues unambiguously, inflation is still cooling but expectations are pushing back against the disinflation narrative, and labor is inert.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)

Momentum indicators now matter most for the next print: outgoing m/m was just 0.12%, well below the 0.33% 3-month average m/m pace, and headline 3m annualized (4.06%) is now tracking close to core 3m annualized (4.86%), suggesting the very low base effects that drove the big y/y drop are becoming less extreme going forward. Given the low outgoing m/m print (0.12%) and continued but decelerating downtrend, I expect one more modest headline y/y decline, though the pace of improvement should slow markedly since the easy base-effect comparisons are largely exhausted and core remains sticky. Sahm-rule gap is trivially positive (+0.03pp), far below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine turn. Range position at 0.0 (sitting at the low) confirms the labor market is at its tightest point in the observed window, not loosening. Policy language changes (dropping the public-health reference) are administrative and don't touch the substantive employment characterization, which remains "job gains have been robust... unemployment rate has remained low" — unchanged. With the rate 0.5pp below the 4% natural-rate anchor and no directional momentum in either direction over 3/6/12 months, I see no evidence-based case for a directional shift next month. Given the mixed signals — negative short-term momentum vs. positive longer-term trend and modestly positive slope — I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-down call for month-end, with modest conviction given the conflicting signals.

The 4-week change is now +0.11 (strongly positive) and even the 13-week change has flipped positive (+0.05), confirming the tightening move has broadened beyond just near-term noise. Range position at 0.79 shows the index sitting in the upper part of its 52-week range, well off the -0.37 low. My prior "up" call was correct: conditions have continued tightening steadily, not reversed. The pace of increase per observation has been fairly consistent (roughly +0.02 to +0.03 per period), suggesting momentum is intact rather than decelerating sharply. Policy language reinforces this, explicitly flagging that recent developments are likely to tighten credit conditions further and weigh on activity — consistent with continued upward pressure on the index. There is no sign of the 4w change turning negative or a fresh low, which was my stated falsifier. Given the consistency of the trend across multiple windows (4w, 13w, range position all pointing the same way) and confirming policy language, I maintain a tightening call, though the index remains below the zero average, so this is tightening from a still-loose base.
Would change its mind if: A print reversing below -0.20 (retracing the recent gains) or 4w change turning negative would signal the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.

Call: flat, conviction 0.35

Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last three observations show a sharp jump in total assets: from ~8.342T to 8.639T to 8.734T to 8.706T — a rise of roughly +366B over 4 weeks, reversing the prior steady runoff pattern (which had ground from ~8.51T down to ~8.34T over the preceding ten weeks). This is a clear inflection: my prior "down" call was correct through the runoff phase but the most recent prints show the level has snapped back sharply, now sitting only ~260B below the 52w high, with 13w change flipping positive (+1.81%). The policy language is unchanged, still describing continued reduction of Treasury/agency holdings under previously announced plans — so this is not an announced QE program. That mismatch (rising assets vs. runoff-only language) suggests the jump is likely a technical/liquidity event (e.g., repo operations, bill settlement effects, or balance sheet mechanics unrelated to a policy shift) rather than a deliberate reversal of QT. However, since my mandate is to weigh the recent pace of change most heavily, and the last three prints are unambiguous large increases, I have to respect the measurement even though the "why" doesn't support sustained expansion. The latest print (8705942) did tick down from the peak (8733787), suggesting the spike may already be fading.
Would change its mind if: A continued decline back toward the pre-spike ~8.34T level over coming weeks, or explicit language confirming this was a temporary technical add, would confirm reversion to runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

Report:

However, the 21d change is now +0.23pp — a notable stabilization/steepening impulse versus the 63d (-0.07) and 252d (-0.69) trends, both negative. The series itself is choppy: -0.45→-0.58→-0.48→-0.38→-0.38→-0.41→-0.47→-0.51→-0.55→-0.58, oscillating in a band roughly -0.38 to -0.58 with no clean trend, ending back at the cycle-recent low. The spread sits at -0.58, only 0.49pp above the 12m low (-1.07) and well below the 12m high (+0.44), giving a range position of 0.32 — still in the lower third, i.e. meaningfully inverted with more room below than above. Fed language continues to signal further hikes ("additional policy firming may be appropriate"), which historically presses front-end yields up faster than the long end, a flattening/inverting force. Given the latest print re-tests the recent low despite the positive 21d change, I read this as still biased toward flattening, but the mixed short-term signal tempers conviction.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above -0.45pp for 2+ consecutive readings with 21d change staying positive would indicate genuine steepening reversal.

arbitrated.notes: The panel points to a still-disinflating but decelerating inflation backdrop, a labor market that remains firmly tight with no loosening signal, and financial conditions that are tightening steadily and credibly. These three are the highest-conviction, best-evidenced calls and I largely defer to them. Inflation expectations look to have stalled just below recent highs rather than resuming an uptrend — the analyst's own reversal from "up" to "flat" given negative 21d momentum is a sensible read, though the medium-term picture is genuinely mixed (63d flat, 252d still down), so I keep conviction modest and lean flat rather than fully embracing a directional call. Term premium and curve slope are the weakest reads: both analysts flagged their own prior calls as wrong and are now hedging with conflicting short vs. long-term momentum signals, and both explicitly note they're missing cross-driver context (balance sheet pace, financial conditions flows) that would sharpen the picture. I give term premium a mild downward lean given the clear 21d/63d negative momentum and range position below the analyst's own threshold, but keep conviction low given the acknowledged uncertainty. Curve slope gets a similarly tempered flattening lean — the print re-tested cycle lows even as short-term momentum firmed, and hawkish Fed language still argues for front-end pressure, but the choppiness undercuts confidence. Balance sheet is the most confusing signal: a sharp technical-looking asset jump that contradicts stated runoff policy, with the analyst's own narrative suggesting the spike is fading. I treat this as a genuine "no clean view" driver and stay near flat, tilted only slightly by the analyst's own hedging toward continued unwind.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release confirmed the "down" call: headline CPI y/y fell to 4.95% (essentially flat vs 4.92% prior, but down sharply from the high-8s a year earlier), and core PCE y/y ticked further down to 4.78%, a new low in the series and continuing its slow grind lower. Neither of my stated reversal triggers fired: headline m/m came in at 0.34%, below the 0.5% threshold, and core PCE stayed under 5.0%. So the disinflation trend, while decelerating in pace, remains intact. That said, momentum is not uniformly friendly: headline_mom_3m_avg (0.27%) implies a 3-month annualized headline pace of 3.27%, still above the 2% target, and the two most recent monthly prints (0.12%, 0.34%) show some bounce after a very soft 0.02% reading three months back — the disinflation is decelerating in speed, not reversing. Core remains the more important read given headline's flatness and past energy swings; core's slow but steady decline (5.61%→4.78% over the window) is the dominant signal. With headline y/y sitting at a 12-month low and core also easing, but base effects from year-ago prints likely to be less favorable going forward, I expect next month's headline y/y to be roughly flat to slightly up, not a continuation of sharp declines.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.5% or core PCE y/y rising back above 5.0% would signal disinflation has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print fell sharply to 2.18 from 2.27, a -0.09pp single-observation drop that breaches the 2.20 level I flagged as the trigger for a renewed downtrend. This follows a series that had been drifting sideways-to-down (2.28→2.28→2.26→2.28→2.28→2.25→2.25→2.27→2.18), so my prior "flat" call was reasonable given the range-bound behavior through most of the window but is now invalidated by this latest breakdown. The 21-day change (-0.16pp) and 63-day change (-0.11pp) both confirm the drop is not just a one-day noise blip but a real shift lower, and the 252-day change (-0.67pp) shows this is part of a larger, sustained disinflationary repricing in market expectations. The breakeven is now sitting near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.09, only 0.08pp above the 12m low of 2.10), suggesting further downside is plausible if the trend continues, though proximity to the 12m low could also invite some stabilization or mean-reversion. Distance from the 2% target is still +0.18pp, so expectations remain anchored above target, giving some room to fall further without dramatic dislocation. Given the clear break below my downtrend threshold and multi-horizon confirmation, I now lean toward continued softening.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.25-2.27 sustained over the next print would suggest the 2.18 reading was noise rather than trend continuation.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print came in at 3.40%, a new 12-month low, down from 3.50% and squarely within the tight range this cycle. My prior "flat" call held up: the print stayed within the plateau band and did not breach 3.70%. If anything the market tightened further rather than loosening. The unrate_change_3m (-0.10pp), 6m (-0.20pp) and 12m (-0.30pp) all point modestly downward, and the sahm_gap is a negligible +0.10pp, well below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine turn. Distance from natural rate is -0.60pp, meaning the labor market is tighter than the 4% longer-run anchor, not looser. Policy language reinforces this: job gains have "picked up" and are "running at a robust pace," with unemployment "remained low" — language that got more emphatic on strength, not less. There is a forward-looking caveat about tighter credit conditions weighing on hiring going forward, but that is anticipatory, not yet visible in the data. Given the level is at a 12-month low and momentum is neutral-to-tightening, I lean toward continued flat-to-slightly-tighter conditions next month, with modest conviction since a print this low leaves limited room to fall further and asymmetric risk of a bounce.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.60% would suggest the low has passed and loosening is beginning; a print at/below 3.20% would signal further tightening beyond this plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield sits at 3.44, near the middle of its 12-month range (range_position 0.51) and down slightly over both the 21d and 63d windows (-0.11pp each), while still up sharply over the past year (+0.67pp). The last print (3.44) is a pullback from the prior local high (3.53), and the series has been choppy without a clear trend within the last two weeks. My prior flat call was reasonably accurate — yields oscillated in a narrow band and did not break the 3.57 threshold I set as a trigger for an upward revision. The 30s10s slope remains modestly positive (+0.23pp), consistent with steady but unremarkable term-premium compensation at the long end; no steepening acceleration is evident. Ongoing balance-sheet runoff continues to be a background upward pressure on term premium, but there's no fresh signal of accelerating issuance concerns or flight-to-quality flows in this data. With the yield roughly mid-range and recent momentum mildly negative, I see no strong case for a directional move by month-end. The evidence continues to support a flat call, perhaps with a very slight bias toward down given the recent 21d/63d softness, but not enough to override the mid-range positioning and stable slope.
Would change its mind if: A move back above 3.57% with range_position climbing over 0.75 and positive 21d change would revive the upward term-premium case; a break below 3.40 with steepening would confirm downward.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call ("up"/tightening) has been overtaken by the data. After rising steadily from -0.30 toward -0.15 over the middle of the series, NFCI has now ticked back down for three consecutive prints: -0.15, -0.17, -0.18. The 4-week change is -0.02, i.e. mildly negative, which is exactly the reversal condition I flagged as a falsifier. The longer 13-week change (+0.11) still shows conditions net looser than three months ago in absolute tightening terms (recall: rising NFCI = tightening, so +0.11 over 13w actually means conditions tightened over that window from a very loose base), but the recent momentum has clearly stalled and inflected downward. The index remains well below zero (-0.18), meaning conditions are still looser than historical average, and range position (0.66) shows we're in the tighter half of the past year's range but pulling back from the highs. Policy language noting "recent developments" likely tightening credit conditions suggests some exogenous stress, but the actual index has not confirmed further tightening—it's easing over the last month. Given the 3-print pullback and negative 4w change, I now lean toward continued easing (down) rather than further tightening, reversing my prior stance.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above -0.15 or 4w change turning positive again would signal the tightening trend has resumed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.62
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last four readings show a clean, steady decline: 8632384 → 8614797 → 8593263 → 8562768, a run-off of about -69.6B over the last 4 observations and -143174 ($M) over the 4-week derived window. This confirms the reversal I flagged as a condition last time: the mid-series spike (peaking near 8733787, the 52w high) has been unwinding steadily for several consecutive readings, not just one blip. The 13w change is still slightly positive (+92211) only because the spike is inside that window, but the trend within the window is now unambiguously downward and monotonic in the last four prints. The policy language confirms this is consistent with continued runoff: the Committee statement explicitly reaffirms continued reduction of Treasury and agency MBS/debt holdings per previously announced plans — no language of reinvestment expansion. Given the pace (~-20 to -35B per weekly-equivalent step) and the confirmed multi-period consecutive decline, I now think my prior "flat" call was too cautious — this looks like renewed/continued QT runoff. Absent any new liquidity-facility or emergency-lending shock, the trajectory should continue lower into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back above ~8.6T over the next few readings, or new language suspending/pausing runoff, would overturn this call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print shows slope_2s10s falling back to -0.60pp after a brief pop to -0.46/-0.47, reversing the steepening seen in the middle of this window. My prior "down" call is now validated: the move above -0.45 did not sustain for 2+ readings and the 21d change has turned negative again (-0.05pp), confirming the reversal I flagged as the falsifier condition did not materialize. The 63d change is still positive (+0.10pp) but that's stale relative to the newest print showing renewed flattening. The curve remains deeply inverted at -0.60pp, sitting only 0.31 of the way up its 12-month range (low -1.07, high +0.44), i.e., closer to its most inverted levels than to flat. The 252d change of -0.83pp shows the dominant trend over the past year has been flattening/deepening inversion. Policy language shows continued rate hikes (funds rate raised twice in the shown sequence, from 4.50-4.75% to 4.75-5.00%) with guidance for "additional policy firming," which via front-end pressure (dgs2 4.04% vs dgs10 3.44%) is consistent with continued inversion pressure rather than steepening. Given the snap-back to -0.60 and hawkish continuation language, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for month end, not a resumption of steepening.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above -0.45pp for 2+ consecutive readings with 21d change staying positive would indicate genuine steepening reversal.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high, dgs2, dgs10

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===

  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment shifts could signal flight-to-quality demand for long bonds.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous flat call was too cautious: headline y/y actually fell sharply, from 4.95 to 4.13 (-0.82pp in one month), extending a clear disinflationary trend (3m annualized headline at just 2.52%, and the yoy series is sitting at its 12-month low with yoy_range_position of 0.00). The latest print (0.16% m/m) was well below both the outgoing print (1.26%) and the 3m average (0.21%), so my falsifier condition (m/m above 0.5%) did not trigger — disinflation continued, it did not stall. Core PCE is stickier: yoy sits at 4.79%, barely down from 4.86-4.94% a few months back, and 3m annualized core is running hot at 4.25%, well above target and above the recent yoy pace, suggesting core momentum is not decelerating as fast as headline. Given base effects (a very high year-ago comparison of 8.98% two years back rolling off) and continued soft monthly headline prints, I expect headline yoy to fall again next release, though the size of the base-effect tailwind is diminishing as the high-inflation comparison base is now mostly cycled through. Core's stickiness argues against a large further headline drop, but the immediate direction for headline yoy is still down.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.5%, or core PCE y/y rising back above 5.0%, would signal disinflation stalling/reversing.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: breakeven_10y fell further to 2.18 from 2.22, extending the decline rather than rebounding above the 2.25-2.27 range that would have signaled noise. The trend is now well-established across horizons: -0.05pp over 21d, -0.30pp over 63d, and -0.44pp over 252d, showing consistent, accelerating downward momentum in market-implied inflation expectations. The series sits near its 12-month low (only 0.08pp above the 2.10 floor) and in just the 12th percentile of its 12-month range, indicating this is not a minor fluctuation but a sustained repricing lower. While the breakeven remains 0.18pp above the Committee's 2% longer-run objective, the trajectory suggests convergence toward or even below target-implied levels if the trend continues. With no rebound signal materializing and the most recent print (2.18) being the lowest in the series, momentum favors continued softening. I'll maintain a down call, with slightly higher conviction given the confirmation of trend continuation and proximity to the 12-month low, though the magnitude of further decline may moderate given how compressed levels already are relative to the range floor.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.22-2.25 sustained over the next print would suggest 2.18 was a trough rather than continued decline.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.50
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 3.60% sits at the top of the trailing 12-month range (3.40–3.60%), matching prior peaks seen earlier in the series rather than breaking above them. My prior falsifier threshold (a print above 3.60%) was not breached, so the "flat" call from last period was correct: the market has oscillated in a narrow 0.2pp band for over a year with 3m/6m/12m changes all at 0.00pp. The Sahm-gap of 0.10pp is trivial and well below the 0.50pp threshold that would signal recessionary loosening. Policy language continues to describe job gains as "robust" and unemployment as "remained low," reinforcing that this uptick to 3.60% is noise within a tight-labor-market plateau rather than a trend break. Distance from the natural rate remains solidly negative (-0.40pp), meaning the market is still tighter than long-run normal. Given the flat multi-horizon changes and unchanged qualitative language, I see no basis for a directional call — the print has simply returned to the upper edge of its established range, not exceeded it. I maintain flat, with slightly lower conviction than before given the print sits exactly at the range ceiling, raising modest odds of a genuine break next month.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.60% would suggest the low has passed and loosening is beginning; a print at/below 3.20% would signal further tightening beyond this plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield has pulled back over the last few observations, closing at 3.64% after peaking near 3.83%. My prior "flat" call held reasonably well: the 21d change is only +0.05pp, essentially flat, though the level did briefly spike above my 3.57% trigger before retreating. Range position at 0.63 shows the yield still sits in the upper half of its 12-month range (2.60-4.25%), but the recent 3-observation decline (3.83→3.80→3.69→3.64) suggests some cooling in long-end pressure after the run-up. The 63d change of -0.44pp is notably negative, indicating the longer-run trend within the quarter has actually been one of falling yields, even though the 252d change of +0.89pp shows a much higher level than a year ago. The 30s10s slope at +0.21pp is modestly positive but not steepening dramatically, and continued balance-sheet runoff remains a persistent mild upward force on term premium that hasn't disappeared. On net, the recent turn lower in yields plus flat 21d momentum argues against calling this "up" with conviction, but the range position still in the upper two-thirds and ongoing QT keep me from calling a confident "down" either. This looks like consolidation after a peak, with modest downward drift in the immediate data.
Would change its mind if: A renewed move above 3.80% with range_position back over 0.75 and positive 21d/63d change would revive the upward case; a break below 3.50% would confirm the downward drift.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (-0.21) continued the easing-then-mild-retightening pattern: after bottoming near -0.15 mid-series, NFCI has ticked down (tighter) for four consecutive readings (-0.17→-0.18→-0.19→-0.20→-0.20→-0.21), giving a 4w change of -0.03 (further easing... wait, more negative = looser). Actually the last four prints show the index moving more negative, i.e., financial conditions loosening further, not tightening. My prior call was "down" (looser) and this materialized: the index fell from -0.17 to -0.21, a continuation of easing. The 13w change (+0.08) reflects the earlier bottoming-out rebound from -0.15, but the most recent 4-5 observations show renewed softening in conditions, pushing the index back toward its 52w low (-0.31) and away from the recent high (-0.10). Range position at 0.47 shows we're mid-range, but the momentum (4w change -0.03) points to continued loosening. Policy language shift removed explicit reference to "recent developments" causing tighter credit, softening the tightening narrative, consistent with easing financial conditions. This supports a continuation of the easing trend at a modest pace.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above -0.17 or 4w change turning positive would signal tightening has resumed and reverse this easing call.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.68
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last several readings confirm continued balance-sheet runoff. Total assets fell from 8,503,994 to 8,436,255 over the most recent observations, with assets_change_4w at -126,513$M — a clear acceleration of decline versus prior weeks. Assets are now -498,091$M below the 52-week high (8,934,346), and the 52w change is -478,026$M, consistent with steady QT. The 13w change shows a small positive (+54,065$M, +0.64%) only because it spans an earlier bump in the series (a jump from ~8.34T to ~8.64T-8.73T well before the recent declines); the trend since that bump has been consistently downward for seven straight readings. Policy language is unchanged, reaffirming continued runoff of Treasury and MBS holdings with no signal of pause or reinvestment. My prior call of "down" was correct — the rebound-above-8.6T scenario that would have overturned it did not materialize; instead assets kept falling to fresh cycle lows. With the 4-week pace of decline actually steeper than before and no policy language change, the most likely path over the next month is continued gradual decline, consistent with the announced runoff caps.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back above ~8.55-8.6T over the next few readings, or new language pausing/suspending runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call played out: the spread fell from -0.55 to -0.76pp over the ten readings, deepening inversion. However, the most recent three prints (-0.67, -0.74, -0.76) show the pace of flattening decelerating — the 21d change is -0.21pp (still negative, confirming near-term flattening pressure) but the 63d change is now +0.05pp, meaning the flattening has essentially reversed over the medium window. The curve sits at -0.76pp, near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.22, only 0.31pp above the 12m low of -1.07), which is itself a stretched, historically inverted level. Policy language shifted from "additional firming may be appropriate" to softer language about "extent to which additional policy firming may be appropriate," dropping the more forward-leaning tightening bias — consistent with a Fed nearing the end of hikes, which in past cycles has coincided with curve stabilization or modest re-steepening as front-end expectations cap out. Given the mixed signal — recent 21d still flattening but 63d flat-to-slightly-steepening, and rate hikes appearing near a pause — I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-down call rather than continued sharp flattening. Conviction is modest given the conflicting short vs medium-term signals.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive readings above -0.65pp with positive 21d change would confirm a genuine steepening reversal; renewed move below -0.85pp would confirm continued flattening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Confirm whether QT pace or Treasury issuance plans are accelerating, which would pressure long end higher.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Flight-to-quality flows could explain recent yield pullback independent of term premium dynamics.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: headline y/y fell sharply from 4.13% to 3.07%, a decline consistent with the disinflation trend, and my falsifier conditions (m/m above 0.5% or core PCE back above 5.0%) were not triggered — outgoing m/m was actually negative-to-flat (-0.01%) before the latest 0.22% print. However, the picture for the next release is more mixed than the y/y number suggests. The 3-month annualized headline pace (2.94%) is running near target, but this reflects a very favorable base a few months back; the most recent m/m prints (0.34, 0.16, 0.22) are firmer than the near-zero prints from earlier in the window that are about to roll off the y/y calculation. Core PCE has been essentially flat for three months (4.86→4.78→4.79→4.73), still well above the 2% target (distance +2.73pp) and decelerating only marginally — core_yoy_change_3m is just -0.12pp, indicating stalling progress on the persistent-inflation measure. Because a low base month is exiting the 12-month window and recent m/m readings are modestly above the very low prints being replaced, headline y/y is more likely to tick up or hold flat than continue its steep decline. I weight core PCE's stalling here as a caution against extrapolating further headline improvement.

In hindsight the falsifier I set (core PCE below 4.5%) did trigger (4.38%), which would have argued disinflation continuing, but headline itself moved up on base effects, showing the two can diverge over a single month. Looking at momentum now: headline m/m has been 0.16, 0.22, 0.20 over the last three prints — modestly above the prior trough (outgoing_mom 0.06) and above the 3m average (0.19), so sequential momentum is stabilizing rather than falling further. Headline 3m annualized (2.35%) is still below the current y/y rate, implying some further disinflationary pull, but the last two m/m prints (0.22, 0.20) are firmer than earlier in the year, so the pace of deceleration is clearly slowing. Core PCE y/y continues to grind down slowly (4.73→4.38), consistent with gradual, not stalled, disinflation, but the gap between core and headline (core_minus_headline +1.10pp) and firming headline momentum both point toward headline y/y likely ticking up again next print due to base effects (last year's comparison base was falling then). Net: modest upward pressure on headline y/y is more likely than a fresh leg down.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15% alongside a renewed drop in core PCE y/y below 4.2% would signal disinflation resuming rather than stalling/reversing.

arbitrated.notes: The panel presents a fairly coherent picture with a few genuine tensions. Inflation: headline CPI y/y has ticked up on base effects even as core PCE continues to grind lower and 3m annualized headline sits below y/y — the analyst's own evidence is mixed but leans toward a near-term upward print given firming m/m momentum; I take a modest up lean, tempered by the fact that the underlying core trend still disinflating. Inflation expectations have plateaued in a tight band after their earlier reversal — no more upward or downward information content, so flat is right. Labor tightness continues to oscillate in a narrow, well-established range with zero 12m drift; flat is well supported and I see no reason to override. Term premium has the cleanest trend: persistent, multi-horizon (21d/63d/252d) increases in 10y yields, near top of 12-month range, plus a structural tailwind from ongoing balance-sheet runoff — I have high conviction here. Financial conditions show a clean, monotonic, multi-horizon easing trend (NFCI making fresh lows) with no evidence of turning — down is well-supported, though this creates a tension with the term-premium call (rising long rates vs. easing financial conditions) that I read as: the easing is driven by non-rate channels (credit spreads, equity vol) while duration/term premium is a separate supply-driven phenomenon, so both can be true simultaneously. Balance sheet is the highest-conviction call: uninterrupted, consistent runoff across all horizons with unchanged policy language — clearly down. Curve slope is the weakest and most conflicted signal — recent stabilization/mild steepening against a dominant multi-month flattening trend — I keep this near flat with low conviction, slightly tilted toward steepening given the freshest data and the term-premium overlap (long end rising and 10s30s flat suggests any steepening is a long-end story, consistent with the 2s10s uptick).

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release headline CPI y/y rose from 3.29 to 3.72 (+0.43pp), driven by a firm 0.48% m/m print — well above the 3m average of 0.30% and above the falsifier threshold I set. My prior "up" call was correct: disinflation had stalled and reversed, not resumed. The trend since the trough (3.07%) is now two consecutive increases, and the m/m readings (0.16, 0.22, 0.20, 0.48) show acceleration in the most recent month specifically. Core PCE y/y continues to grind down (4.38→4.30), and core 3m annualized (2.80%) is much cooler than headline's recent pop, suggesting the headline jump may partly reflect volatile components (energy/food) rather than broad-based re-acceleration. Still, headline is the metric being asked about, and its momentum is clearly upward now: outgoing m/m of 0.48% versus a 3m average of 0.30% implies base effects will push y/y higher again next month unless the comparable month a year ago was unusually high. Given the sharp jump and the base effect arithmetic (a weak base print rolling out is likely, given the sequence), I lean toward another increase in headline y/y, though core's continued deceleration tempers full conviction.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.20% combined with core PCE y/y falling below 4.2% would indicate the reversal was a one-off, not renewed acceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, outgoing_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been overtaken by events: the falsifier I set was a break below 2.30, and breakevens have now printed 2.29, 2.26, 2.24 — a clean, accelerating decline through that level. The last four readings (2.30, 2.29, 2.26, 2.24) show consistent downward momentum, not noise. be_change_21d is -0.12pp and the most recent three prints alone account for most of that move, suggesting the decline is fresh and ongoing rather than stalling. The 63d change (+0.08) is stale, reflecting the earlier plateau near 2.32-2.35 before the recent breakdown; the 252d change (-0.34) confirms the broader trend has been down over the year, and the recent action looks like a resumption of that trend after a mid-period pause. be_range_position at 0.29 shows breakevens sitting in the lower third of the 12-month range, and falling further. Distance from the 2% target is still +0.24pp, giving room for further compression before expectations would look anchored at target. With no sign of stabilization in the last three prints, I expect the move to continue or at least hold, not revert, over the next month.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above 2.30 over the next few readings would signal the recent decline was noise rather than a new trend.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 3.70%, a new 12-month high, up from 3.50% the prior month and 0.10pp above the previous 3-month/6-month/12-month range. This confirms the loosening scenario I flagged as my falsifier last time — my prior "flat" call was too cautious given the data was already sitting at the top of its range. The Sahm-rule-style gap (current rate minus 12m low) is now 0.30pp, still below the classic 0.50pp recession-signal threshold, but the move is not noise: it's a fresh cycle high following a sequence that had been rangebound 3.40-3.60% for a year. The level itself (3.70%) remains close to estimates of the natural rate (near 4%), so this is not an alarming absolute level, but the direction and the fact that this is the highest print in the window matters more than the level alone per my mandate. Policy language still describes the labor market as robust with low unemployment, which is backward-looking and doesn't capture this print. One month's uptick could be noise, so I won't go to high conviction, but the balance of evidence points to a modest loosening trend continuing rather than reversing sharply.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.50% would suggest this was noise; a print above 3.80% would confirm accelerating loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_high, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y has drifted down over the most recent readings (4.34 → 4.09), giving back some of the run-up that justified my prior "up" call. The 21-day change is now essentially flat (+0.01pp), showing the recent uptrend has stalled, even though 63d and 252d changes remain solidly positive (+0.48pp, +0.97pp), reflecting the larger multi-month rise. Range position at 0.80 still shows yields sitting in the upper portion of the 12-month range, well above the 12m low, which argues against a genuine reversal in term premium — this looks more like a modest pullback within an elevated range than a new downtrend. The 30s10s slope is a modest +0.11pp, not signaling aggressive long-end cheapening pressure but not flagging flight-to-quality either. Continued balance-sheet runoff remains a background force keeping term premium biased upward structurally, even as short-term momentum has cooled. My prior "up" call was too confident given that the last several prints show yields easing, not extending. Given the flat 21d change and the pullback from the 12m high, I lean toward a smaller, more moderate upward bias rather than a strong one — the level remains elevated in-range, but momentum has faded enough that a continued grind higher is less assured than last time.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 3.70% with range_position under 0.60 and negative 21d/63d change would revive the downward case; a fresh push to new 12m highs would confirm renewed upward pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady, monotonic decline, now at -0.32, a fresh 52-week low, from -0.21 thirteen weeks ago. Momentum is intact and even accelerating slightly: the 4-week change (-0.06) is close to the average pace of the 13-week change (-0.11 over ~13 weeks), showing no deceleration in the easing trend. Range position is at 0.00, meaning current level sits at the very bottom of its 52-week range, confirming this is the loosest financial conditions have been in a year. Distance from zero (-0.32) shows conditions are well below the historical average, i.e., looser than normal, and getting looser each week without a single uptick in the last 13 prints. My prior call (down, 0.40) was correct — the print has moved further below -0.20 and the 4w change remains negative, so the falsifier condition was not triggered. Given the unbroken monotonic run of declines across all 13 observations with no sign of stabilization or reversal, I am increasing conviction that this trend continues into month end. There is no policy language suggesting any shift; the cited language is generic and unchanged, offering no new signal either way.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above -0.25 or 4w change turning positive/flat would signal the easing trend is stalling or reversing.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.78
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: total assets fell further, from 8,145,727 to 8,139,066 over the latest reading, continuing an unbroken decline across all 13 observations. The 4-week change (-104,278M), 13-week change (-297,189M), and 52-week change (-712,370M) all confirm steady, ongoing runoff with no deceleration evident in the most recent print. Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-693,693M) and below the 13-week average, consistent with mechanical QT proceeding at (or near) the announced monthly caps. The policy language is unchanged, explicitly reaffirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings per previously announced plans — no hint of tapering the runoff pace or pausing balance-sheet reduction. Given the consistency of the trend (13 consecutive readings without reversal) and the reaffirmed reinvestment/runoff language, the highest-probability outcome for month-end is another decline of roughly similar magnitude to the recent 4-week pace, i.e., continued QT. There is no evidence in front of me of any slowing, pause, or reversal signal — the falsifier condition (rebound above ~8.5T or paused-runoff language) has not been met.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward/above ~8.3-8.5T, or new statement language pausing or slowing the runoff pace.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread remains deeply inverted (-0.76pp last print), essentially unchanged from the prior reading (-0.78 → -0.76) and near the midpoint of its 12-month range (range_position 0.35). The 21-day change is a modest +0.04pp (mild steepening pressure), but the 63-day change is -0.04pp and the 252-day change is -0.46pp, showing no durable trend either way — the last ten readings have oscillated in a narrow -0.63 to -0.78 band with no clear breakout in either direction. My prior flat call was correct: neither the -1.00pp flattening threshold nor the -0.85pp steepening threshold was breached, and the spread stayed range-bound. The Fed's rate hike (to 5.25-5.50%) keeps front-end anchored high, and with dgs2 (4.85%) still well above dgs10 (4.09%), inversion persists but isn't deepening. The 10s30s slope at +0.11pp shows the long end itself isn't inverted, suggesting the inversion is concentrated in the front-to-belly relationship tied to policy rate expectations, not a broad flattening impulse. Absent a clear catalyst or range breakout, I expect the spread to stay near current levels into month-end, with modest chop within the recent band.
Would change its mind if: A close below -1.00pp would confirm renewed flattening; a sustained push above -0.60pp with positive 21d/63d change would confirm steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===

  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk appetite/flight-to-quality signals would clarify whether the recent pullback is safe-haven driven.

arbitrated.notes: Cross-checking the panel, a coherent picture emerges: financial conditions are easing decisively (NFCI at 52-week lows, monotonic 13-print decline) even as the Fed continues balance-sheet runoff at an unabated pace (13 straight weekly declines, no deceleration, language reaffirming the plan). That combination is notable — QT continuing mechanically while broad financial conditions loosen anyway, likely reflecting risk-asset strength and narrower credit spreads outweighing the liquidity drain. Inflation data show a genuine split: headline CPI just posted a hot m/m print breaking a stall in disinflation, but core PCE keeps grinding lower, and — critically — the inflation analyst was never shown breakeven data. Breakevens are actually falling hard and accelerating (10y breakeven through 2.30 to 2.24, clear momentum, distinct from a stale 63d readings). That divergence matters: market-implied inflation compensation is not corroborating the headline CPI pop, and if anything argues the acceleration is being read by markets as noise/transitory. I weight the expectations decline as the more forward-looking, market-based signal and temper the inflation call accordingly — still leaning up given the m/m base-effect math, but with reduced confidence versus the analyst's own conviction. Labor is loosening modestly (new 12-month high in unemployment, still below Sahm threshold) — consistent with, not contradicting, easier financial conditions. Term premium's own momentum has stalled (flat 21d change) even though it sits high in-range; missing balance-sheet and financial-conditions context cuts both ways — QT argues for continued upward pressure, but the easing NFCI argues for compression — so I keep this muted. Curve slope has no catalyst and stays range-bound.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y ticked down slightly (3.72→3.69) despite a firm 0.39% m/m print, and core PCE y/y fell to 3.83 from 4.30 — a sizable step down that breaches the falsifier I set (core PCE below 4.2%) even though the m/m print was above the 0.20% threshold I specified. My prior "up" call was wrong: the bounce in headline m/m (0.48, 0.39 in the last two prints) did not translate into renewed y/y acceleration; instead headline y/y is flat-to-down from its recent local low and core continues to decelerate meaningfully (core_yoy_change_3m -0.90pp). The 3-month annualized core rate of 2.05% is right at target, a much better signal than the noisier headline print, and I weight it heavily given the mandate's PCE framing. Headline is being held up by base effects and volatile components while the underlying trend (core) is cooling. Headline's own 3m annualized (4.36%) is still elevated versus core, reflecting energy/food swings, but the y/y series shows headline near its 12-month low with no clear acceleration signal. On balance, momentum favors continued disinflation or flatness in headline y/y at the next print, not acceleration.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.45% combined with core PCE y/y rising back above 4.0% would signal renewed acceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.55) was wrong: breakeven_10y has since printed 2.34, 2.39, 2.35 — the exact reversal back above 2.30 I flagged as my falsifier. The last 10 readings show a choppy but modestly rising pattern (2.36→2.35 net, but with the 21d change +0.09pp and 63d change +0.13pp both positive), while the 252d change is nearly flat (+0.02pp), indicating the recent upward drift is a short-term move within a longer stable range rather than a durable regime shift. The series sits at 0.51 of its 12-month range (2.10-2.59), squarely in the middle — no extreme positioning either way. Distance from the 2% target remains a notable +0.35pp, keeping breakevens elevated versus objective, which caps how much further downside conviction is warranted since the level itself doesn't look like it's collapsing toward target. Given the latest print (2.35) is a pullback from the recent local high (2.39), and the short-term momentum is positive but noisy with no clear trend acceleration, I read this as most consistent with a flat-to-slightly-up near-term path. I don't have enough evidence of a decisive breakout to call a strong directional move.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move outside the recent 2.33-2.39 band, especially a break above 2.45 or below 2.30, would confirm a new trend rather than range-bound noise.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 3.70%, matching the prior month rather than falling back to 3.50% or accelerating above 3.80%. That validates the prior "up" call: the unemployment rate is now at its 12-month high, sitting at the top of the observed range (range_position 1.00), and up 0.20pp over both 6 and 12 months, with a 0.30pp rise off the trailing 12-month low. The Sahm-gap of 0.23pp is approaching but not yet at the classic 0.50pp recession-signal threshold, so this looks like a gradual loosening rather than a sharp deterioration. Still, two consecutive prints at the cycle high, after a period of noisy oscillation between 3.40-3.60%, is a real directional shift rather than noise. The level itself (3.70%) remains only modestly above the ~4% natural rate, so this is not yet a tight-to-loose regime change, but the trend is clearly no longer flat. Absent a pullback, momentum favors continued mild upward drift or at least stabilization at this higher plateau. I lean toward another flat-to-up print, with some chance of a technical retracement given two months already flat at 3.70%.

arbitrated.notes: Cross-checking the seven reads, the picture is a labor market gradually loosening while inflation continues to cool at the margin, even as term premium and yields grind higher on continued balance-sheet runoff and a modest financial-conditions re-tightening. Inflation: core PCE decisively broke its own falsifier to the downside (3m annualized near target at 2.05%), while headline y/y is flat near 12-month lows — I read this as flat-to-mild-disinflationary rather than truly neutral. Inflation expectations are genuinely rangebound; the analyst's own prior call reversed and the current read is noisy chop within a stable 12-month band, so I keep this near zero, tilted only slightly positive given recent 21d/63d drift. Labor tightness: two consecutive prints at the unemployment cycle high with unemployment at range top and rising 6/12m changes is a credible gradual loosening signal — up in unemployment rate, i.e., labor loosening. Term premium and curve slope both show durable, multi-horizon confirmed moves (10y at 12m highs, 2s10s steepening for months) even though both note deceleration in the last couple of prints — I keep both up but shade back slightly for the stalling. Financial conditions: only a two-print reversal against a much larger easing trend — genuine but tentative, so modest up/tightening lean, not a strong call. Balance sheet: unambiguous, high-conviction, multi-quarter accelerating runoff with unchanged hawkish-by-omission policy language — this is the highest-confidence call on the panel and it mechanically supports the term-premium/curve-steepening logic (duration supply/demand channel), reinforcing my treatment of those two as directionally aligned and self-consistent with balance-sheet runoff.

Sahm-gap approaching 0.50pp threshold; a sharper labor deterioration would be a regime change, not gradual loosening.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was essentially correct: headline CPI y/y moved from 3.69 to 3.25, a decline, and my stated flip trigger (mom above 0.45% plus core PCE back above 4.0%) did not fire — instead the newest headline m/m print was just 0.14%, well below the 3m average of 0.34%, and core PCE y/y fell further to 3.69. So if anything the disinflation trend firmed rather than stalled. Looking ahead one month: headline y/y sits near the bottom of its 12m range (yoy_range_position 0.04, only 0.18pp above the 12m low), and the 3m annualized pace for headline (4.12%) is still above target while core's is much closer (2.26%), consistent with core leading the deceleration. The most recent monthly print (0.14%) is soft versus recent averages, which mechanically pulls next month's y/y down further unless there's a base-effect reversal, but the low prior-year comparison base could make the y/y comparison less favorable next print. Core continues to converge toward target and is the better persistence signal; headline's y/y change over 3m is roughly flat (-0.04pp) masking the softer core trend. Net: modest continued easing bias, not a strong acceleration signal.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.40% combined with core PCE y/y stalling or rising would signal renewed acceleration and flip this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, yoy_range_position, outgoing_mom

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last ten readings show breakeven_10y oscillating narrowly between 2.40 and 2.49, with the most recent print at 2.42 — essentially the same as the prior observation (2.44) and within the range that has held for the whole window. My prior "flat" call anticipated exactly this kind of noise-bound behavior, and the falsifier I set (a sustained break above 2.45 or below 2.30) has not been triggered; the series has touched 2.49 briefly but settled back to 2.42, not a sustained breakout. The medium-term signals are mixed: be_change_21d (+0.07) and be_change_63d (+0.05) suggest mild upward drift, but be_change_252d (-0.03) shows the year-long trend is essentially flat to slightly down. Range position at 0.74 (near the upper part of the 12-month band) suggests some upward pressure but not a decisive break to new highs. Distance from target remains modest at +0.42 pp, well within historical norms and not indicative of de-anchoring. Given the choppy, sideways pattern over the last three prints (2.44, 2.42, 2.42), I see no strong evidence of a new trend forming in either direction. I maintain a flat call, with conviction slightly lower than before given the mild positive short-term drift is not confirmed by longer-term data.
Would change its mind if: A sustained close above 2.49 or below 2.35 over multiple observations would confirm a genuine trend break rather than range-bound noise.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print jumped to 3.90% from 3.70%, confirming the loosening trend I flagged last time. This is the highest level in the 13-month window, up 0.30pp over 12 months, 0.40pp over 3 months, and 0.50pp over 6 months — all consistent, non-reverting acceleration rather than noise. The Sahm-rule-style gap (current vs 12m low of 3.40%) is now 0.50pp, close to historical recession-signal thresholds, and my prior falsifier condition (a print above 3.80%) has been met, which per my own framework should raise conviction sharply rather than soften it. The level itself (3.90%) is now essentially at the ~4% natural rate estimate, so this is no longer just "low readings drifting up" — it's a labor market that has moved from tight to roughly balanced-to-loosening in under a year. Two consecutive months of increase (3.70 to 3.70 to 3.90) with widening range position (now at 1.00, i.e., sitting at the 12-month high) argues for continued upward momentum in unemployment rather than mean-reversion. I'm calling for another increase or at least stabilization at this elevated level, with meaningful odds of further deterioration given the pattern of accelerating, not decelerating, monthly changes.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.70% would signal reversion; deceleration in the pace of increase would lower conviction on continued loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_high, unrate_distance_from_natural

Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has stabilized in a tight 4.83-4.98% band over the last 10 readings, currently at 4.88, essentially flat over the most recent several observations after an earlier climb. My prior "up" call was directionally correct in trend terms (63d/252d changes remain strongly positive at +0.83pp/+0.92pp) and the yield sits near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.94, just below the 4.98 high). However, the most recent short-term move (21d +0.29pp) has decelerated and the last few prints (4.86, 4.84, 4.88, 4.88) show sideways consolidation rather than fresh highs. The 30s10s slope remains modestly positive (+0.16pp), consistent with continued term-premium pressure from duration supply/balance-sheet runoff language, which is unchanged. Given the level is high in its range but momentum has flattened, I lean toward continued modest upward pressure rather than reversal, but with less conviction than before since new highs haven't been decisively made. A close to 4.98 or above would confirm continuation; a drop back toward the low-4.80s with negative momentum would signal the rally in yields has stalled or reversed.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 4.70% with range_position under 0.60 and negative 21d change would reverse this; a push to fresh highs above 4.98% would strengthen it.

The 4-week change is now +0.04, confirming the tightening tilt I flagged last time, though the 13-week change is still slightly negative (-0.02), reflecting the earlier easing phase before the trough. Momentum is clearly positive and consistent (six straight upticks with no reversal), and the level remains well below zero (-0.28), so conditions are still looser than average in absolute terms, but the direction of travel is unambiguously toward tightening. My prior falsifier (a new 52w low or negative 4w change) did not occur — instead the index kept rising, so the prior "up" call was validated. Range position at 0.24 (of 0-1) shows we're still closer to the loose end of the 52-week range, but the steady grind upward from the low is the dominant signal for the next month. Absent any reversal signal in the last three prints, I expect this grind to continue modestly rather than snap back.

The 13-week change (-335,514M, -4.07%) and 52-week change (-815,260M) confirm a persistent, roughly linear runoff with no deceleration visible in the last several observations — each successive reading is lower than the last, and the pace of decline (roughly 90-100k per 4 weeks) has been remarkably steady. Assets remain ~826,000M below the 52-week high, reinforcing the runoff narrative. The policy language is unchanged, explicitly reaffirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency MBS holdings per previously announced plans — no hint of tapering, pausing, or ending the runoff. My prior call of "down" at 0.80 conviction was correct: the balance sheet continued to shrink exactly as expected, with no rebound toward 8.3T and no dovish shift in language. Given the consistency of the monthly cap-driven runoff and the absence of any statement change, I expect the same trajectory next month: total assets should decline by roughly 80,000-100,000M from the current 7,907,830M level, landing somewhere near 7,810,000-7,830,000M at month-end.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward/above ~8.3T, or new statement language pausing, slowing, or ending the runoff pace.

However, the last observation ticked down from -0.15 to -0.19, a modest 4bp re-flattening move within an otherwise steepening trend. Looking at the full run, the series has been broadly steepening over medium horizons (63d change +0.68pp, 252d change +0.15pp) even though the most recent few prints (-0.13, -0.16, -0.15, -0.15, -0.19) show stalling/consolidation rather than continued acceleration. The curve remains inverted (-0.19pp) but is near its least-inverted level of the past year, having moved up substantially from the -1.08 low. The FOMC language shift (from "raise" to "maintain" the funds rate, and description of activity from "moderate" to "solid" pace) suggests a pause/hold posture, which historically supports gradual continued steepening as front-end pressure eases, though the immediate print doesn't yet confirm fresh momentum. Given the mixed short-term signal (recent tick down) against the medium-term uptrend and high range position, I maintain a steepening lean but with reduced conviction given the stall.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below -0.35pp or a sustained negative 21d change over the next month would signal renewed flattening and invalidate this steepening call.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)

Report:
  My prior "down" call was directionally correct: headline CPI y/y ticked from 3.25% to 3.13% before the latest print at 3.32%. Looking at the most recent print now in hand, headline actually rose from 3.13% to 3.32% (+0.19 pp), while the last m/m reading was 0.19%, unremarkable and below the 0.40% threshold I flagged as a reversal trigger. Core PCE y/y continues its steady grind lower, 3.47% to 3.29%, a fourth consecutive month of deceleration, and core 3m annualized (2.31%) sits close to target even as core_yoy_change_3m is -0.54pp, still clearly disinflationary. The headline uptick is likely energy/base-effect noise given core_minus_headline is near zero and mom_gap_vs_outgoing is negative, showing recent monthly prints are cooler than the outgoing one. Policy language modestly acknowledges progress ("eased over the past year") without declaring victory, consistent with continued but slow disinflation. Given headline just rose while core still fell, the two are diverging this month, and I weight core PCE more heavily for persistence, per mandate. Net: momentum is disinflationary but headline's own path shows an uptick, so my confidence in "down" for the very next headline print is reduced versus last time.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or core PCE y/y stalling/rising would confirm reacceleration and flip this call to up.
