The last two prints (2.15, 2.16) show a slight stabilization/tick-up, so momentum may be decelerating, but the level is still near the low end of its 12-month range (range_position 0.14, only 0.06pp above the 12m low of 2.10). Distance from the 2% target remains positive at +0.16pp, meaning expectations are still anchored above target, but the trend direction is unambiguously down over multiple horizons. Given the deceleration in the most recent two observations, I don't think a continued sharp decline is guaranteed, but the weight of evidence favors a modest further move down or at best stabilization at a lower level rather than a reversal back up. I'm revising from flat to a lean toward down, while acknowledging the tentative flattening in the last two prints tempers conviction.

Inflation: headline CPI just popped (+0.19pp) even as core PCE continues a fourth straight month of deceleration; the analyst itself flags divergence and trims conviction — I read this as still disinflationary underlying but with a live upside risk in the headline print, so a mild "flat-to-down" lean rather than a confident down call. Labor tightness: unemployment retraced this month (3.90→3.80) but trend since the 12m low is unmistakably higher, Sahm-gap at threshold — I read this as a genuine but not-yet-confirmed loosening, tempered by the retracement. Term premium: 10Y yields down over 21d/63d but 252d flat and last three prints show a small bounce; explicitly missing balance-sheet (structural upward pressure) and financial-conditions (loosening, which cuts the other way) context — I keep this mild and hedge it against the balance-sheet and financial-conditions reads, which argue for offsetting forces on term premium. Financial conditions: NFCI six straight declines to a fresh 52w low, steady pace, high conviction, no reversal signal — this is the cleanest, most persuasive report. Balance sheet: runoff continuing steadily, 4w change actually larger than typical, no tapering language — very high conviction, mechanical process. Curve slope: steepening trend intact across all horizons, near 12m highs, tempered slightly by mean-reversion risk near range extremes. Overall: strongest, cleanest signals are balance-sheet (down) and financial-conditions (down), with curve-slope (up) and labor (up) as secondary confirmed trends; inflation and term-premium calls are the shakiest given self-reported divergence/deceleration.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y ticked up to 3.09% from 3.32%, actually a decline, not a rise — my prior "flat" call undersold the still-present disinflation, though direction was at least not wrong (I said flat, not up, and it did not accelerate). The latest print (0.31% m/m) sits right at my stated trigger threshold (0.35%) but just below it, so no reacceleration signal fired. Looking at the fuller picture: headline y/y has been oscillating in a narrow 3.07–3.72% band for nearly a year with no clear trend either way (yoy_range_position 0.01 confirms we're at the low of that range). Core PCE y/y continues to grind lower steadily (4.97%→3.11%), a sustained and unbroken deceleration, and core 3m annualized (1.79%) is now comfortably below the 12-month trend, signaling continued disinflationary momentum in the underlying/persistent component. Headline 3m annualized (2.63%) is also below the y/y print, consistent with headline drifting down toward core. Policy language shift ("inflation has eased over the past year") corroborates the disinflation narrative from the Committee's own read. With core decisively decelerating and headline's momentum readings (3m annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing) both pointing down, I lean toward a lower headline print next month, though the flat y/y range keeps conviction moderate.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above ~0.35-0.40% or core PCE y/y flattening/rising would signal reacceleration and flip this call to up.
Leaned on: core_pce_yoy, headline_cpi_yoy, core_3m_annualized, headline_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.40) referenced thresholds around 2.13-2.19 that don't match the current data scale (2.26-2.34), suggesting that view was calibrated to a different level or was simply off-base; the actual series has stayed in a tight 2.26-2.34 band with no sustained directional break. Latest prints: 2.30, 2.30, 2.26, 2.26 — a slight further softening from the 2.29-2.34 opening levels, consistent with the 63-day change of -0.18pp, but the 21-day change is +0.10pp, indicating the very recent trend has stabilized or ticked up slightly before the last two flat readings at 2.26. Net-net the series looks to be consolidating near the lower end of its 12-month range (range_position 0.38, only 0.16pp above the 12m low of 2.10), but not accelerating lower. Distance from the 2% target remains modest at +0.26pp, well within historical norms, and not screaming for a big directional resolution. Given the flattening in the last two observations after a decline, and mixed short vs medium-term changes, I read this as basically stable-to-softening, but not a strong directional signal for the next month.
Would change its mind if: A break above 2.34 or below 2.20 sustained over multiple observations would confirm a clear directional move rather than range-bound consolidation.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print (3.70%) actually pulled back from the prior 3.80% and 3.90% peak, partially validating my "up" call at the previous release but the follow-through wasn't confirmed further — it reversed. Looking at the full series: 3m change is now -0.20pp (down from a recent peak), while 6m and 12m changes remain modestly positive at +0.20pp each. The rate sits 0.30pp above the 12-month low and at 0.60 of the 12-month range (3.40-3.90), still leaning toward the upper half of the range but not making new highs. Sahm-rule gap is a modest +0.33pp — well below the ~0.50 threshold that signals recession-onset dynamics, and the 3-month trend has actually reversed lower rather than accelerating. This is not a confirmed loosening trend; it looks more like noisy oscillation around the 3.60-3.90 band with a recent tick down. Given the reversal in the 3-month change and lack of consecutive prints above 3.90%, my prior "up" call looks premature this cycle. The market is still historically tight (level below the 4% natural rate proxy), and the most recent move is a mild tightening, not further loosening.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.90% or two consecutive increases would confirm renewed loosening; a return to 3.60% or below would confirm stability/tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield has drifted down to 3.99 from recent highs, with the last three prints (4.08, 4.06, 3.99) showing a clear downward tilt in the most recent data. That said, the 21d change is still positive (+0.11pp), meaning the very recent trend (last month) has actually been mildly upward before the sharpest drop at the tail; the 63d change is strongly negative (-0.89pp), while the 252d change is positive (+0.44pp), indicating a longer-term uptrend that has partially reversed over the past quarter. The yield sits at 0.41 of its 12-month range - roughly the middle - which is not extreme in either direction. My prior "down" call was directionally consistent with the 63d move but the most recent single-month window (21d) actually ticked up, so it was only partially validated; the sharp final print (3.99) came right at the end and pulls the level down again. The 30s10s slope remains modestly positive, consistent with normal term-premium compensation, not signaling a sharp shift. Ongoing balance-sheet runoff is a steady upward pressure on the long end, but it hasn't stopped the recent slide. Given the choppy, non-extreme range position and mixed short vs. medium-term signals, I lean modestly toward continued softness but without strong conviction.
Would change its mind if: A push back above 4.15 with positive 21d change would revive the up case; a sustained break below 3.85 would confirm continued down move.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady grind lower: -0.28 down to -0.39 over the 13-week window, with 4-week change -0.03 and 13-week change -0.10, both negative, and the index sitting at a fresh 52-week low with range_position at 0.00. There is no sign of the reversal I flagged as a falsifier last time (no break back above -0.30, no consecutive up-prints); every single observation in the most recent stretch is lower than or equal to the one before it. My prior "down" call was correct and the trend has not stalled. The policy language, while noting "tighter financial and credit conditions," is boilerplate forward-looking commentary about the effect of past cumulative tightening on activity — it does not describe current NFCI dynamics, which the hard data show continuing to ease (looser than average, and getting looser). Momentum is consistent, monotonic, and the distance from zero is expanding further into negative (looser than average) territory. Absent any measurement showing deceleration or an uptick, the most reasonable one-month-ahead call is a continuation of the same easing trend, though the pace of decline (-0.01/week recently) is modest so I temper conviction slightly rather than treat this as an extreme move.
Would change its mind if: A print breaking back above -0.34 (13w avg) or two consecutive weekly increases would signal this easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.68
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: the last observation (7,677,230) is only marginally above the preceding one (7,673,741) but sits well below the level two readings ago (7,686,710) and far below the 13-week and 4-week starting points, confirming continued runoff. The 4-week change is -35,551M and 13-week change is -230,600M, both negative, consistent with the ongoing QT pace. The 52-week change of -793,327M and the $1.06T gap below the 52-week high underscore that this is a sustained, multi-quarter drawdown, not noise. The latest tick up from 7,673,741 to 7,677,230 is small and within the noise of weekly operational factors (settlement timing, etc.) — it does not represent a pause; it's dwarfed by the trend. Statement language remains unchanged, explicitly continuing the runoff of Treasury and MBS holdings with no mention of slowing or halting. There is no evidence of tapering the cap or ending QT. Given the consistent monthly cap-driven decline and unchanged language, I expect total assets to be lower again at next month-end, though the magnitude of decline may be modest given the recent deceleration in the weekly pace compared to earlier in the 13-week window.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward/above ~7.9-8.0T, or new statement language pausing, slowing, or ending the runoff pace.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has been in a broad steepening trend over the past year (252d change +0.42pp, off the 12m low of -1.08pp) and sits near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.84), consistent with my prior "up" call. However, the most recent readings show the spread has slipped back to -0.28 from a recent high near -0.14, and the 63d change is now -0.13pp — a clear reversal within the broader trend. The 21d change is still marginally positive (+0.07pp), but the last two prints (-0.30, -0.28) show the curve re-flattening after touching -0.14 a few observations back. My prior falsifier (drop below -0.42 or sustained negative 21d) hasn't technically triggered, so the long-term steepening thesis isn't invalidated, but the near-term momentum has clearly turned against it. The FOMC language shift (from "growth has slowed" to "solid pace," dropping tightening-bias language toward a cut threshold) suggests the market may be pricing a slightly more hawkish near-term hold, which could keep the front end pinned and cap further steepening. Given the recent pullback and mixed short-term signal, I'm lowering conviction on further steepening this month and leaning toward flat-to-slightly-down rather than continued up.
Would change its mind if: A move back below -0.35pp or two more consecutive prints deepening the negative slope would confirm renewed flattening; a break above -0.10pp would restore the steepening call.

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/changes in runoff or QT tapering would clarify upward pressure on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment shifts could signal flight-to-quality effects on long yields.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 14 days ago.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.40) was wrong: headline CPI y/y actually ticked up from 3.09 to 3.16, and the m/m print came in at 0.41%, above my own stated flip threshold of 0.35-0.40%. That is exactly the reacceleration signal I flagged. The 3-month annualized headline rate (3.71%) now sits above the latest y/y (3.16%), and headline_mom_3m_avg (0.30%) plus the most recent 0.41% print show momentum building after a soft patch (0.14-0.19% in the prior three months). Core PCE y/y did tick up slightly too (3.11→3.16), interrupting its multi-month deceleration trend, though core_yoy_change_3m is still -0.32pp reflecting the broader downtrend over the quarter. Headline sits at the bottom of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.05), so the recent uptick is a change in trajectory, not yet a return to prior highs. Given two consecutive months of accelerating m/m prints and a rise in both headline and core y/y readings, I read this as tentative reacceleration rather than noise, though one month of core PCE ticking up could just be residual seasonality. I'll weight the momentum signal (3m annualized, m/m trend) over the still-favorable y/y distance from last year's peak.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print back below ~0.20% or core PCE y/y resuming its decline would indicate this was noise, not reacceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom_3m_avg, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y breakeven series has been range-bound: 2.30-2.34 over the last 10 readings, ending at 2.33 after touching 2.34 mid-series. My prior "flat" call held — no sustained break occurred; the series oscillated within a ~4bp band and the latest print (2.33) is essentially mid-range, not a new extreme. The 21d (+0.07) and 63d (+0.11) changes show mild upward drift, but the 252d change is -0.10, indicating the longer trend has actually been down, consistent with a mature consolidation rather than a fresh breakout. Range position at 0.55 (within a 12m band of 2.10-2.52) confirms the series is roughly mid-range, not stretched toward either boundary. Distance from the 2% target is +0.33pp — modestly elevated but not extreme, and not visibly widening. With no confirmed break above 2.34 sustained across multiple observations (my stated falsifier), and the longer-run trend actually negative, I don't see convincing evidence of a new directional move. arbitrated.notes: The panel points to a labor market that is quietly loosening while balance-sheet runoff and financial conditions continue to ease — two of the highest-conviction, most mechanically-grounded calls. Inflation shows tentative reacceleration in momentum terms (m/m and 3m-annualized above the y/y print, core PCE ticking up), but inflation expectations are flat/range-bound, which weakens the case that this is a durable regime shift rather than a one- or two-month wobble; if expectations were also breaking out I'd give the inflation call more weight. Term premium and curve slope are genuinely mixed: 10y yields are consolidating mid-upper range with no clean breakout, and 2s10s shows a modest 21d flattening pullback within an intact longer-run steepening trend — I read this as noise-dominated rather than a real regime call, though the flattening lean has some short-term momentum behind it. The cross-checks the panel flagged matter: term premium not seeing balance-sheet or financial-conditions inputs is a real gap, because continued QT (down) and continued NFCI easing (down) point in different directions for long-end pressure — the former argues for higher term premium via supply, the latter for lower via risk-on flows. Since financial conditions is unbroken/monotonic and higher conviction than the balance-sheet-driven supply argument, I'd lean the net long-end pressure story toward "no strong directional case," reinforcing my flat lean on term premium rather than pushing it hard either way. Labor tightness and balance sheet are my highest-conviction reads because they're both confirmed multi-window trends with no contradicting signal. Inflation gets a moderate, not high, conviction because the y/y story is still muted even as momentum measures flash reacceleration.

=== inflation ===

Headline 3m annualized is running 4.75%, well above the y/y rate, meaning the trend is deteriorating, not just noisy. My prior "up" call was correct and the falsifier condition (m/m back below 0.20%) did not occur — instead momentum built further. Core PCE is the counterpoint: it continues to grind lower (3.06% vs 3.16% prior, and down 0.23pp over 3 months), suggesting underlying persistence is still easing even as headline has been pushed up, likely by energy/food volatility given the core-minus-headline gap has swung to -0.43pp from near parity. I weight core as the better persistence signal, but for the specific headline_cpi_yoy metric being forecast, the momentum evidence (accelerating m/m prints, rising 3m annualized) is a direct read on where headline is headed next month. With outgoing momentum this strong and no sign of deceleration in the m/m series, headline is more likely to tick up again or hold near this elevated pace than to reverse sharply.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print back below ~0.25% alongside continued core PCE decline would signal the recent acceleration is fading, not building.

arbitrated.notes: Headline CPI momentum is genuinely accelerating (m/m and 3m-annualized both running above y/y), even though core PCE keeps drifting down — for the specific headline metric being forecast, momentum evidence dominates, so I keep an "up" lean but temper conviction given the core/headline divergence signals this could be a transitory energy/food-driven push. Inflation expectations are convincingly rangebound (three straight 2.32 prints, near-zero 21d/252d change) — flat is well supported and I take it close to full conviction. Labor tightness is more ambiguous: unemployment plateaued at the cycle high rather than extending its climb, so the "up" thesis is only partially intact — I shade the conviction down from the analyst's own number to reflect the stalled momentum. Term premium is a genuine coin-flip: short-term drift down, longer trend up, low conviction from the analyst himself, compounded by his own admission he lacks balance-sheet and financial-conditions context that would resolve the ambiguity — I keep this at flat with low conviction. Financial conditions look like an easing trend that has stalled right at the floor (range_position 0.01, flattening 4w change) — flat is the right call, not a continuation of "down." Balance sheet is the cleanest signal on the panel: multi-quarter, accelerating QT with unambiguous confirming policy language — high conviction down. Curve slope is noisy/whipsawing with weak, low-conviction flattening lean; I keep it soft. Given term premium's own analyst flagged missing balance-sheet input, and balance sheet shows accelerating runoff, there's a modest case duration supply pressure nudges term premium away from pure flat, but I don't have enough to override the analyst's own read, so I leave it flat with slightly asymmetric downside risk noted only qualitatively.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y ticked down to 3.36% from 3.49%, and the m/m print fell to 0.22%, back below the 0.25% threshold I flagged as my falsifier. Core PCE also eased slightly to 3.12% from 3.06%... actually it rose marginally (3.06→3.12), but is still well within the flat range of the last three prints (3.16, 3.06, 3.12) — essentially stalled, not accelerating. My prior "up" call was wrong: the acceleration I saw building in the prior 3-month annualized pace did not carry through into the newest headline print, which decelerated on both a y/y and m/m basis. Headline 3m annualized (4.36%) and core 3m annualized (4.78%) remain uncomfortably above target-consistent pace, showing underlying momentum has not resolved down to 2%, but the single newest data point moved the wrong way for my "up" thesis. Given headline y/y sits mid-range (yoy_range_position 0.27) and core PCE has been flat for three consecutive prints, the most defensible call now is that inflation is stalled just above 3%, with a slight downward wobble in the latest print. I don't have enough evidence of sustained deceleration to call "down" with confidence, but I no longer see a clear "up" signal either.
Would change its mind if: A clear headline m/m print above 0.35% with core PCE resuming its downtrend would resolve current ambiguity toward one direction.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call referenced a 2.28-2.34 band that has since been decisively broken to the upside — current readings (2.39-2.43) sit well above that range, so that call was wrong in retrospect; the breakeven has been in a slow, steady uptrend. Over the latest 10 observations, breakeven_10y rose from 2.39 to a peak of 2.43 before settling at 2.41, essentially flat over the most recent few prints after the earlier climb. The 21-day change (+0.06pp) and 63-day change (+0.15pp) both show upward momentum, and the 252-day change (+0.14pp) confirms this isn't just noise but a sustained multi-month drift higher. Range position at 0.77 shows the series is trading in the upper quartile of its 12-month range, well above the 12-month low (+0.27pp above it) and closer to the high. However, the last two prints (2.41, 2.41) suggest the ascent has paused or plateaued rather than continuing to accelerate. Distance from the 2% target is +0.41pp, a moderate but not extreme premium. Given the recent flattening after a clear uptrend, I lean toward continued mild upward drift or stabilization near current levels, but without strong conviction of further acceleration given the plateau in the last two readings.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 2.38 sustained across multiple observations, or a fresh push above 2.43, would clarify whether the plateau resolves down or up.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The last three prints have been flat at 3.90%, following a rise from the 3.50% trailing low. My prior call for "up" did not materialize in the most recent data — the rate held steady rather than continuing to climb, so that call was too aggressive given the plateau. The 3m change is now +0.20pp (driven by the earlier move to 3.90, not fresh deterioration), while 6m change is exactly 0.00pp, indicating the level has stabilized over the last two quarters. Sahm gap sits at 0.40pp, below the 0.50 threshold I flagged as confirmation of accelerating loosening, and the level itself (3.90%) is still just under the 4.0% watch-line and only 0.10pp above a 4% natural-rate estimate — not a alarming distance. Policy language reinforces this: it now says job gains "have remained strong" (upgraded from "moderated"), and unemployment "has remained low" — consistent with stabilization rather than deterioration. Three consecutive flat prints at the cycle high is a plateau, not a fresh acceleration. Absent a new confirmed break above 3.90-4.0%, I read this as flat with a mild upward lean given the elevated range position, but the momentum signal has clearly stalled.
Would change its mind if: A print at or below 3.70% would signal renewed slack easing; a move to/above 4.0% or sahm_gap above 0.50 would confirm renewed loosening acceleration.
Leaned on: unrate_change_6m, unrate_3m_avg, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "flat" call has been overtaken by events: the 10y has since risen well above the 4.10-4.35 band I set as my break-point, and now sits at 4.69, near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.82, only 0.29pp below the 12m high of 4.98). Momentum is consistently positive across all horizons: +0.36pp over 21d, +0.63pp over 63d, and +1.16pp over the year, showing a persistent uptrend rather than mean-reverting chop. The last few prints (4.65, 4.70, 4.67, 4.63, 4.69) show some wobble but no reversal — yield is oscillating in the high 4.6s after a clear push higher, and the latest print is the second-highest of the series. The 30s10s slope is modestly positive (+0.10pp), consistent with continued term-premium pressure at the long end rather than curve flattening from long-end demand. Ongoing balance-sheet runoff (continued Treasury/MBS reduction) remains a structural headwind adding duration supply to the market, consistent with an elevated and still-firm term premium. I was wrong to call this flat last time; the data since has clearly resolved upward. Absent a sharp risk-off/flight-to-quality shock, the path of least resistance for month-end is sideways-to-slightly-higher, holding near cycle highs.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 4.55 with sustained downside follow-through would signal the uptrend has reversed; a push above 4.75 confirms continued upward pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a clear trough around -0.44/-0.43 mid-sample followed by a modest but persistent uptick in the most recent readings: -0.43, -0.42, -0.42, -0.41. That's three consecutive weekly increases (or flat-to-up) off the low, which is exactly the reversal signal I said I'd watch for. The 4-week change is now +0.02 (tightening) versus the 13-week change of -0.02 (still net easing over the longer window), indicating momentum has shifted even though the level remains well below the zero average (-0.41 distance from zero) and near the bottom of its 52-week range (range_position 0.09). Conditions are still loose in absolute terms, but the recent trend is toward less loose — i.e., a mild tightening impulse at the margin. Given the previous flat call was reasonable at the time but is now contradicted by the emerging multi-week uptick, I'm shifting toward a modest 'up' call for month-end. Conviction is limited because the move is small in magnitude (only 0.02-0.03 index points) and the series has shown similar tiny wiggles before reverting, but the consistency of the recent uptick is enough to lean away from flat.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below -0.43 or resumption of the prior downtrend for two consecutive weeks would negate this tightening lean.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_13w_avg, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The balance sheet continues its steady runoff. Total assets fell from 7,405,506 to 7,402,434 ($M) in the latest observation, extending an uninterrupted decline visible across the full 13-observation window. The 4-week change (-82,305) and 13-week change (-274,796) both confirm ongoing contraction at a pace consistent with recent months, and the 52-week change (-1,160,334, -13.7% from high) shows the cumulative scale of QT. Assets remain -1,101,560 below the 52-week high with no sign of stabilization or reversal. The prior call (down, 0.78) was correct: assets kept falling as predicted, and the pace did not decelerate meaningfully. Policy language is unchanged, reaffirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings "as described in its previously announced plans" — no hint of tapering the runoff or pausing. Given the consistency of the trend, the unchanged reinvestment language, and the absence of any rebound signal, I expect assets to be modestly lower again at next month-end, roughly in line with the recent ~$60-80B monthly pace. The latest 4-week change is somewhat smaller than the prior 13-week average pace, but not enough to suggest an inflection — still comfortably within normal QT cadence.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward/above ~7.7T, or new statement language pausing, slowing, or ending the runoff pace.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has re-flattened over the last two prints, moving from -0.26 to -0.29 to -0.34 to -0.35pp, reversing the steepening move that had taken it as high as -0.24pp a few observations back. My prior "down" call at 0.25 conviction is now validated by this latest data: the curve did flatten further, exactly the scenario I flagged. The 21-day change is barely positive (+0.04pp) but that masks the fact that the most recent two prints are the most inverted in the series, essentially retesting the -0.34/-0.35 lows seen at the start of the window. The 63-day change is negative (-0.05pp), consistent with a flattening bias over the medium term, while the 252-day change is still positive, showing the broader multi-month steepening trend from deep inversion remains intact but has stalled recently. Range position at 0.77 shows the curve is still well above its 12-month low, but the immediate momentum is toward re-flattening, not away from it. With no new policy language shift evident, I lean toward continued modest flattening or stabilization near current inverted levels rather than renewed steepening.
Would change its mind if: A print steepening back above -0.29pp for two consecutive periods would signal the flattening lean is wrong.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation lacks labor_tightness — Wage growth trends would clarify whether services/core inflation persistence is fading.
  inflation lacks inflation_expectations — Survey-based expectations would show if the stall is anchoring or drifting.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader financial conditions tightening/easing would clarify whether long-end pressure is intensifying or stalling.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/size of ongoing runoff would quantify how much duration supply pressure remains ahead.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (flat, 0.35 conviction) was reasonably vindicated: headline CPI y/y ticked from 3.36 to 3.24, a modest decline rather than the acceleration some of the choppy m/m prints had hinted at. The latest m/m print of 0.05% is notably soft — well below the 3m average of 0.24% and the outgoing 0.22% — which argues for continued deceleration or at least stability near current levels. Headline 3m annualized (2.87%) sits below the y/y rate (3.24%), consistent with disinflationary momentum building. Core PCE also ticked down to 3.01% y/y, its lowest in the window, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.15pp confirming a gentle downtrend. However, core PCE remains 1pp above target and headline has been rangebound (3.07-3.72%) for over a year with no clear breakout either direction — yoy_range_position of 0.27 suggests we're near the low end of that range, a modestly favorable sign. The policy language shift is notable: new language flags "lack of further progress" on inflation, a hawkish addition suggesting policymakers see stalling disinflation risk, which tempers my read of the soft m/m print. Weighing the soft recent momentum against policymakers' own caution about stalled progress, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for next month's headline print, but without strong conviction given the mixed signals between data momentum and policy framing.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% combined with core PCE reversing upward would indicate reacceleration, overturning this flat-to-down lean.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, outgoing_mom

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call has not played out well: since that call, breakeven_10y drifted from 2.36 down to 2.35, and the short-term trend (be_change_21d = -0.03pp) is now negative, though the medium (63d = +0.03pp) and long (252d = +0.13pp) windows remain modestly positive. The series is essentially range-bound in the last few prints (2.32-2.36), sitting at 0.60 of its 12-month range — elevated but not near the highs. There's no fresh breakout above the recent 2.36 ceiling, and the latest two prints (2.36, 2.35) suggest stalling rather than continued ascent. Distance from the 2% target remains meaningfully positive (+0.35pp), so inflation expectations are still anchored above objective, but the near-term momentum has flattened rather than extended. Given the small pullback in the last observation and negative 21-day momentum offsetting the longer-term uptrend, I read this as a plateau with a slight downward tilt in the very near term, not a continuation of the prior uptrend. I don't see enough evidence for a confident directional call either way over the next month — this looks like consolidation just below the recent local high.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move below 2.32 across multiple observations, or a fresh push above 2.38, would clarify the plateau's resolution.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The last three prints are flat at 3.90%, so the immediate 3-month trend shows no further deterioration—my prior "flat" call was directionally correct in that unrate did not fall back below 3.70, but it also did not accelerate upward as the alternative scenario in my falsifier described. The 12-month trend, however, remains unambiguously one of gradual loosening: unrate has risen from a 3.50 low to 3.90, a 0.30pp 12-month increase and 0.40pp Sahm gap, sitting at the top of its 12-month range (range_position 1.00). This is off natural-rate territory only slightly (-0.10pp), but the trajectory over the past year is worth taking seriously even though the most recent three readings have plateaued. Plateauing at an elevated (relative to the 12-month low) level is consistent with a market that has already loosened and is now stabilizing at the new, softer level rather than tightening back up. Absent a fresh reversal below 3.70, I do not see strong evidence for renewed tightening (down), but the flatness in the last 3 months argues against calling further loosening (up) with high conviction either. I'll lean flat, modestly acknowledging the plateau, while flagging that the underlying 12-month drift argues for a slight tilt toward continued softening rather than a rebound to tighter conditions.
Would change its mind if: A print at or below 3.70% would indicate renewed tightening; a move to/above 4.10% or sahm_gap above 0.50 would confirm renewed loosening acceleration.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_3m_avg

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.55) was partially right: yields pushed to 4.61 mid-window before pulling back to 4.51, so the last two prints show clear fading rather than confirmation above 4.75. My stated falsifier (drop below 4.55 with follow-through) has now triggered. That said, the broader picture is not a reversal: 63d change is still +0.32pp, 252d change is +0.82pp, and the level sits at 0.66 of its 12-month range — comfortably in the upper half, well above the 12m low. The 21d change is only -0.12pp, a modest cooling rather than a rout. Slope_30s10s at +0.14pp is only mildly positive, not signaling aggressive long-end selling pressure. The policy change to slow balance-sheet runoff (cutting the Treasury redemption cap from $60B to $25B) is a mechanical reduction in net Treasury supply pressure from the Fed's own book, which is a modest tailwind for lower long-end yields (less runoff-driven selling). Combining a recent pullback with reduced QT pressure, I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-down outlook for month-end, but conviction is limited since the longer trend (63d, 252d) still points upward and could resume.
Would change its mind if: A push back above 4.61 with follow-through would revive the uptrend view; a drop below 4.40 would confirm a firmer downside reversal.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has been essentially flat-to-marginally-tightening over the past 13 weeks, moving from -0.43 to -0.41. The most recent six readings are pinned at -0.41, showing no momentum in either direction over the last month (nfci_change_4w = +0.00). The longer 13-week change is a negligible +0.01, confirming this is a plateau rather than a trend. Conditions remain well below the zero average (distance_from_zero = -0.41) and near the loose end of the 52-week range (range_position = 0.12), meaning financial conditions are still historically easy despite the tiny uptick from the cycle low.
  
  My prior call of "up" with 0.40 conviction was based on a modest drift off the 52-week low, but the last several weeks show that drift has stalled completely — the index is flatlined at -0.41 with zero week-over-week movement. There's no evidence of renewed tightening momentum, and no evidence of resumed easing either. The series is simply sitting still. Given the flat 4-week change and flat recent prints, I no longer see support for a directional call; the earlier tightening lean should be downgraded to a flat call given the plateau.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above -0.41 sustained for two+ weeks (tightening) or below -0.43 (renewed easing) would revive a directional call.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:

This is a slower but still clearly negative trajectory — QT continues, just at a reduced monthly rate. The statement change is exactly the kind of "slowing" language I flagged as a potential reason to soften conviction, and it has materialized. Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-1,105,006) with no sign of reversal or pause. Given the mechanical, pre-announced nature of the caps, next month's print should again be lower, but the reduced cap size means the magnitude of decline should shrink versus the prior few months. I'm maintaining a down call but trimming conviction to reflect that the pace of decline — the thing I'm asked to weight most — is now decelerating, even as direction stays negative.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff entirely.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (-0.38) actually ticked up from the prior reading (-0.37→-0.38 is a slight dip, but looking at the full recent path: -0.47, -0.40, -0.35, -0.37, -0.38, the spread has bounced off the recent low of -0.47 and is now oscillating narrowly in the -0.35 to -0.38 band). My prior "down" call was wrong in trend terms — the 21d and 63d changes are modestly negative (-0.05, -0.03pp) but the 252d change is strongly positive (+0.39pp), and the curve sits at 0.74 of its 12-month range (well above the low), consistent with a steepening trend over the past year that has stalled/consolidated recently rather than reversed. The last two prints (-0.37, -0.38) are a marginal flattening but far short of my stated falsifier threshold, and also far from re-testing the -0.47 low. Slowing balance-sheet runoff (redemption cap cut) is a mild technical support for longer-end demand/duration scarcity dynamics, which could lean modestly toward further flattening at the margin, but the effect is subtle. Net: recent action looks like sideways consolidation after a steepening move, not a fresh flattening trend. I'll call this flat with low-modest conviction, acknowledging my prior down call was premature.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive prints below -0.44 (re-testing the 12m low) would confirm renewed flattening; two consecutive prints above -0.33 would confirm resumed steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The previous flat call is superseded by clear disinflationary evidence in the newest print. Headline CPI y/y fell to 2.97% from 3.24%, the lowest in the 12-month window, with the month-over-month print actually negative (-0.04%), the weakest reading in the series and well below the 3-month average (0.07%) and outgoing pace (0.20%). Headline 3m-annualized is just 0.90%, a sharp deceleration signal even allowing for energy/food noise. Core PCE, the better persistence gauge, also fell to 2.77% y/y from 3.01%, continuing a steady downtrend from 4.73% thirteen months ago, and core 3m-annualized (2.81%) is now closer to target than the y/y figure, confirming the disinflation is not just base effects. Policy language upgraded from "lack of further progress" to "modest further progress" toward 2%, corroborating the data turn. Both headline and core are now decelerating in tandem, with core distance from target down to 0.77pp. My prior flat/mixed lean was wrong given today's negative headline print and confirmed core cooling — the momentum is now clearly downward. Given a single negative m/m print can be noisy, I won't go to extreme conviction, but the weight of evidence (core trend, language shift, weak 3m annualized) supports continued softening into the next release.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print rebounding above 0.3% alongside core PCE ticking back up would signal reacceleration and overturn this down call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last ten breakeven readings show a modest but clear uptrend at the margin: 2.17→2.28 over the window, with the most recent three prints (2.26, 2.26, 2.28) making new local highs after a brief mid-series dip. The 21d and 63d changes are still slightly negative (-0.08, -0.04 pp), reflecting the earlier dip, but the 252d change is positive (+0.08 pp) and the very latest tick (2.28) is the highest in the sample, sitting at 0.40 of the 12-month range and only 0.21 pp below the 12m high. My prior "flat" call anchored on a 2.32-2.38 band was miscalibrated to a series that never actually traded there — the actual range (2.14-2.49) and recent action show renewed upward drift after a shallow pullback, not a stable plateau. Breakevens remain 0.28 pp above the 2% objective, and the short-term momentum (last three consecutive higher/equal prints) suggests continuation rather than reversion. This isn't a large move, and the 21d/63d metrics still carry a slight negative sign, so conviction is limited, but the newest data point breaking to a fresh high is the most relevant signal for a one-month-ahead call. I'm shifting from flat to a modest "up" lean.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.22 over multiple observations, or resumption of the 21d/63d negative trend, would negate this and revert to flat/down.
Leaned on: be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print jumped to 4.10%, up from 3.90% the prior three months and off the 12-month low of 3.50% by 0.60pp — a meaningful acceleration versus the flat 3.90% plateau that preceded it. My prior "flat" call was wrong: the data broke to a new cycle high rather than holding steady. The 3m change (+0.20pp) and 12m change (+0.50pp) both show clear upward momentum, and the Sahm-style gap (0.47pp) is now just shy of the 0.50 threshold I flagged as confirming loosening acceleration — effectively at the edge of that signal. The level itself (4.10%) is now 0.10pp above my natural-rate anchor of ~4.0%, so this is no longer just "low and stable" but a market that has both risen in level and accelerated in pace. Policy language softened only slightly ("have moved toward better balance over the past year" vs. "are moving into better balance"), which is consistent with acknowledging labor cooling has been underway, not a one-off print. Given the range position is at 1.00 (the high of the 12-month window), the momentum and level both point the same direction. I now lean toward continued loosening (higher unemployment) next month, though not with extreme confidence since a single print can partially reverse.
Would change its mind if: A pullback to 3.90% or below, or sahm_gap receding under 0.30, would indicate the rise was noise, not renewed loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y has drifted up over the last few prints (4.20→4.36), with the last observation the highest in the window. My prior "flat" call held since the level stayed within the 4.40 threshold I set, so it wasn't falsified, though the recent tick up to 4.36 shows some modest firming pressure building rather than pure sideways action. The 21d change is still negative (-0.25pp), but 63d and 252d changes are positive (+0.16, +0.59), suggesting the medium/long trend is mildly up even as the very short-term move has been a pullback. Range position at 0.51 is right in the middle of the 12-month band, offering no strong directional signal on its own. The 30s10s slope is a modest +0.15pp, not steep, suggesting no acute long-end cheapening pressure beyond what's already priced. The key new policy input — slowing the Treasury runoff cap from $60B to $25B — reduces the pace of net Treasury supply being added back to the market via balance-sheet reduction, which is a mild negative for term premium (less technical/supply pressure on the long end going forward). This somewhat offsets the recent modest uptick. On balance I lean slightly toward flat-to-modestly-up, since the last three prints show acceleration (4.23→4.32→4.29→4.36) even though slower QT should cap upside supply pressure over time.
Would change its mind if: A push back above 4.61 with follow-through would confirm renewed uptrend; a drop below 4.15 would confirm downside reversal.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has drifted gently upward from -0.43 to -0.39 over the 13-week window, with the most recent two prints both at -0.39 versus -0.41 in the prior several weeks. That confirms the modest tightening drift I flagged as a trigger last time: conditions have now sustained a break above -0.41 for more than two weeks, satisfying my own falsifier for reviving a directional call. The 4-week change (+0.02) and 13-week change (+0.05) are both positive and consistent in sign, showing gradual, low-magnitude tightening rather than a reversal. Still, the index remains deep in easy territory at -0.39, well below the zero average and near the bottom of its 52-week range (range_position 0.24), so this is a small easing-of-easing move, not a regime shift. With no policy language addressing this driver, I have only the mechanical measurements to go on. Given the consistent, if slight, upward momentum across both short and medium windows, I lean toward continued mild tightening (higher nfci) into month-end, but conviction stays modest given the small magnitude and flat last-two-week reading.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to -0.41 or below, or two straight weeks of decline, would signal the drift has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.65) was correct: total assets fell again, from 7,252,542 to 7,231,163 (-21,379 over the latest week), continuing an unbroken downtrend. The 4-week change (-53,156), 13-week change (-253,576, -3.39%), and 52-week change (-1,109,751) all confirm steady, ongoing runoff with no signs of stalling. Assets remain far below the 52-week high (-1,067,149), consistent with sustained QT. The policy language confirms this trajectory continues, but at a reduced pace: the Treasury redemption cap was cut from $60B/month to $25B/month starting in [date] while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B (with excess reinvested into Treasuries). This is a deceleration of runoff, not a pause or reversal — the Committee explicitly states it "will continue reducing its holdings." Given the announced cap cut, I expect the pace of decline to slow from the ~$50-60B/4wk pace seen recently toward something closer to $25-35B/4wk going forward, but the direction remains firmly down. No indication of reinvestment exceeding runoff or a balance sheet floor being reached yet.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff entirely.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_change_52w, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has steepened steadily over the last several prints, moving from -0.47 to -0.35, a clear break above the prior consolidation range around -0.45/-0.46. This confirms the steepening resumption threshold I set previously (two consecutive prints above -0.33 was the bar; we're not quite there yet at -0.35, but the trajectory and slope_change_63d of +0.04 alongside slope_change_252d of +0.62 both point firmly in the steepening direction). My prior "flat" call was too cautious — the data was already showing a steepening bias building, and it has now extended further. Range position is 0.77, near the top of the 12-month range, indicating the curve has moved well off its most inverted levels toward flat (still -0.35pp inverted, per slope_distance_from_flat). The 10s30s slope at +0.15 (mildly positive) suggests the long end isn't driving inversion, consistent with front-end-led steepening dynamics. The QT taper announcement (reducing redemption caps) reduces the pace of balance-sheet runoff, which structurally supports somewhat more accommodative conditions at the margin and doesn't argue against continued steepening. With three consecutive up-moves in the last three prints and no reversal signal, I lean toward continued steepening into month-end, though the spread remains meaningfully inverted overall.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below -0.42 for two consecutive prints would signal the steepening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s, slope_distance_from_flat

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation lacks inflation_expectations — Would clarify whether the disinflation is expected to be sustained or a one-month blip.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace and composition of QT taper effects on net Treasury supply/demand technicals for the long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment/flight-to-quality flows that could pull long yields down independent of supply.

arbitrated.notes: The macro picture that emerges is a labor market loosening faster than inflation is cooling, against a backdrop of gently tightening financial conditions, a curve that is steepening (bear-steepening flavor given rising term premium and up-drifting breakevens), and a balance sheet still shrinking but at a reduced pace. Inflation's down call is well-supported by both headline and core decelerating in tandem with a negative m/m print, so I keep it but temper slightly given the single-print risk the analyst itself flagged. Inflation expectations ticking to fresh highs cuts against the disinflation narrative somewhat — breakevens rising while realized inflation falls is a mild divergence worth flagging, but the move is small and 21d/63d changes are still negative, so I keep conviction modest. Labor tightness (unemployment rate) rising toward/through the Sahm-adjacent threshold is a clean, high-conviction signal and consistent with an economy decelerating — I keep this near full conviction. Term premium's up-lean is data-confirmed at the margin (63d/252d positive) but partially offset by slower QT reducing net supply pressure; I temper it modestly, especially since it wasn't shown the balance-sheet or financial-conditions context that argues for a lower long-end path. Financial conditions tightening slightly is coherent with a curve steepening on the front end and a Fed still shrinking its balance sheet, so I keep that as a mild up-lean. Balance sheet decline continuing but decelerating is unambiguous from the data — I keep that call intact. Curve steepening is corroborated by cross-checks (10s30s, range position, momentum) and fits the broader story of front-end anchoring against a cooling labor market — I keep this call with reasonably high conviction, tempered by the not-yet-confirmed two-print threshold.

discounted.why: Directionally noted but move is small and short-term windows still negative; limited standalone weight.
Missing balance-sheet and financial-conditions cross-context flagged by the analyst itself; treated as lower-conviction.

arbitrated.falsifier: A rebound in headline CPI m/m above 0.3% with rising core PCE, alongside unemployment reverting to 3.90% or below, would overturn the cooling-labor/cooling-inflation read.

risks.text: Both inflation and labor calls hinge on recent single prints that could partially reverse next release.
Term premium and curve calls didn't see balance-sheet/financial-conditions interplay, risking an inconsistent long-end read.
Rising breakevens against falling realized inflation could presage a reacceleration the inflation analyst hasn't priced.

why.inflation: Solid headline+core deceleration and language shift support the down call; tempered for single-print noise risk the analyst itself flagged.
why.inflation_expectations: Fresh high on latest print, but 21d/63d still negative and move is small; kept as mild lean only.
why.labor_tightness: Clean break to cycle high, consistent 3m/12m momentum, near Sahm threshold — kept at analyst's conviction.
why.term_premium: 63d/252d trends up but slower QT and unseen easier financial-conditions context argue for capping upside; tempered down.
why.financial_conditions: Small but consistent tightening drift across 4w/13w windows, coherent with steepening curve and ongoing QT.
why.balance_sheet: Unbroken runoff confirmed across all windows; direction unambiguous even as pace decelerates via cap cut.
why.curve_slope: Steepening corroborated by multiple metrics and momentum, tempered slightly since two-print confirmation threshold not yet met.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "down" call was correct: headline CPI y/y fell from 3.24 to 2.97 to 2.94, a new 12-month low, and the last m/m print I now see (0.17%) did not breach my 0.3% reacceleration threshold, so the down call stands validated. Looking ahead one month, momentum still favors deceleration: headline_3m_annualized is just 0.70%, well below the trailing y/y rate, and core_3m_annualized at 2.21% is also below core's y/y pace, showing the recent trend is disinflationary even though the single latest m/m tick (0.17) rebounded off the prior month's -0.04. Core PCE y/y continues to grind lower (2.77 → 2.75), extending a steady multi-month decline, and is now only 0.75pp from target. Policy language upgraded to "modest further progress," corroborating the data-side improvement. The main risk to the down call is the mom_gap_vs_outgoing showing the newest print already firmer than the 3m average, hinting the deceleration pace may be flattening rather than continuing at the same speed. Given base effects (the y/y comparison next month drops against last year's print) and only mild m/m firming, I lean toward another modest downtick or flat print rather than a reversal, but with less conviction than last time given the mom uptick.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% combined with core PCE y/y ticking back up would signal reacceleration and overturn this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, headline_yoy_12m_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My previous call (up, 0.35) was wrong: the trend I flagged as vulnerable to reversal has instead continued. The 10-year breakeven has drifted from 2.31 to 2.24 over the ten observations shown, a steady, low-volatility decline rather than the stabilization/reversal I had leaned toward. All three change windows are negative and monotonic in magnitude (21d -0.08, 63d -0.17, 252d -0.14), indicating the decline is a persistent multi-month feature, not just short-term noise. The series sits at 2.24, close to the 12m low (2.14) and in the bottom third of its 12-month range (range_position 0.29), reinforcing that downward momentum is intact rather than exhausted. Breakevens remain above the 2% target (+0.24pp), so there's no sign of disinflation expectations overshooting into deflation territory, but the direction of travel is clearly down. The policy language is unchanged boilerplate and offers no new signal to offset the price action. Given the consistency of the decline across all horizons and the lack of any stabilizing tick in the most recent observations, I now expect the breakeven to continue drifting lower or at best flatten slightly by month end, not resume the uptrend I previously anticipated.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above 2.28 sustained over multiple observations, or a positive 21d change, would negate this and revert to flat/up.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.62
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: unemployment rose from 4.10% to 4.20%, extending the climb off the 12-month low of 3.70%. The trend is now unambiguous — six of the last seven prints have been at or above 3.90%, with the last two months accelerating to 4.10% and 4.20%. The 3m change (+0.30pp) and 6m change (+0.50pp) both show the pace of loosening picking up rather than stabilizing, and the Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) has widened to +0.50pp, with sahm_gap itself at 0.37 — above the 0.30 threshold I set as my falsifier, so the "noise" case is rejected. Level is now 0.20pp above the natural-rate anchor of ~4%, i.e., through it. Policy language still describes the labor market as strong, but that language is backward-looking and hasn't caught up to the recent prints. Given the consistent monotonic rise over the last three readings (3.90→4.10→4.20) with no offsetting pullback, I see this as a genuine, moderately strong tightening-reversal (loosening) signal, not just level-drift. I'll maintain an up call, with somewhat higher conviction than before given the confirmation of trend and breach of my own falsifier threshold.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.00% next month, or sahm_gap falling back under 0.30, would suggest the recent rise is stalling rather than continuing.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.30) was wrong: the 10-year has continued to fall, breaching my own downside trigger of 4.15 decisively, now at 4.09. The trend is unambiguous over the last month: -0.39pp over 21d and -0.60pp over 63d, with the last five prints showing a steady grind lower (4.27→4.20→4.17→4.15→4.09). The 252d change is still positive (+0.13pp), so the longer-run trend is flat-to-up, but the recent momentum has clearly reversed. The yield now sits at just 0.30pp above its 12-month low and only in the 25th percentile of its 12-month range (range_position 0.25), indicating the long end is trading toward the low end of its recent band, not the high end. The 30s10s slope is modestly positive (+0.26pp) and not showing acute steepening pressure that would signal fresh term-premium build-up from issuance concerns. Balance-sheet runoff language is unchanged (no new signal), so I don't see a fresh upward catalyst there. Given the clear, multi-week downward momentum and the position near range lows, I now expect the term premium to continue drifting down or stabilize near current lows into month-end, reversing my prior up call.
Would change its mind if: A push back above ~4.20 with follow-through would suggest the downtrend has stalled; a drop below 4.00 would confirm continued decline.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has moved steadily from -0.41 to -0.36 over the last 13 weeks, a gradual but persistent tightening drift. The most recent print (-0.36) confirms the prior call was correct: conditions continued to tighten rather than stall or reverse. The 4-week change (+0.02) and 13-week change (+0.05) both point the same direction, and momentum has not shown the two-week decline that would have falsified the view. That said, the index remains well below zero (-0.36, distance_from_zero -0.36), meaning conditions are still much looser than historical average — this is a mild normalization off very loose levels, not a shift to tight conditions. The range_position of 0.46 shows we're roughly mid-range of the 52-week band, not at an extreme. The pace of increase is slow and incremental (roughly +0.01/week average), so while the direction is consistently up, the magnitude is modest. Absent any new policy commentary on this driver, I extend the same tightening drift forward one month, expecting nfci to tick higher again, but I temper conviction slightly given the small absolute magnitude of weekly moves.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward -0.39 or below, or two consecutive weekly declines, would signal the tightening drift has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (7,205,455) continues the decline from the prior read (7,208,247), confirming the previous "down" call was correct. Total assets have fallen in nearly every one of the last 13 observations, with the 4-week change at -25,708M and 13-week change at -196,979M, both consistent with steady, ongoing runoff rather than any deceleration or pause. The pace over the last 4 weeks (~-25.7B) is roughly in line with or slightly below the trailing 13-week average pace (~-15.2B/week), so there's no clear reacceleration or deceleration signal — this looks like continued mechanical runoff under the standing caps. The statement language is unchanged, reaffirming that the Committee continues reducing Treasury and MBS holdings, with no hint of tapering the pace or halting runoff. Assets remain over $1 trillion below the 52-week high and continue to grind lower. Absent any change in reinvestment policy language, the trajectory into next month-end should continue in the same direction and at a similar pace. My falsifier condition (a flat/higher print or pause language) was not met — the print was lower and language unchanged — so I maintain the down call with similar conviction.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff entirely.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" (steepening) call has partially stalled but not reversed. Last release the spread was -0.20; the most recent print shows -0.20 again, following -0.19 before that — essentially flat over the last two readings after a run of steepening from -0.26 to -0.09 earlier in the window. The 21d and 63d changes remain positive (+0.09, +0.15), and the 252d change is strongly positive (+0.71), confirming the multi-month steepening trend is intact and the curve sits near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.87, only 0.09pp below the 12m high). However, the last three prints (-0.16, -0.19, -0.20) show a mild reversal/stall within that broader uptrend — steepening momentum has clearly slowed and ticked slightly backward from the recent peak of -0.09. This isn't a break of my prior falsifier (-0.42), so the broader steepening thesis isn't invalidated, but the immediate trend is directionless to slightly negative. Given the plateau at -0.19/-0.20 for two straight prints, near-term risk is a continued pause or minor further flattening before resuming the broader steepening trend. I'll moderate conviction and lean toward flat given the stall, while acknowledging the dominant multi-month direction remains up.
Would change its mind if: A print back below -0.30 would confirm renewed flattening; a fresh push above -0.09 would confirm resumed steepening trend.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high

Term premium has reversed hard to the downside over the last month, consistent with falling long yields, but this sits awkwardly against a still-positive 252d trend and a curve (2s10s) that has stalled near cycle steepening highs rather than confirming a fresh dovish repricing. I read the rates complex as: growth/labor data (unemployment up, Sahm gap widening) is the dominant, highest-conviction signal, and it is doing double duty — it explains both the term premium's recent grind lower (growth-scare rather than inflation-scare rallies) and gives inflation additional room to decelerate via base effects and softer demand. Inflation expectations are also drifting down in a low-noise, multi-horizon-consistent way, reinforcing the disinflation narrative rather than fighting it. Financial conditions tightening is real but incremental and off a very loose base, so I don't weight it heavily against the labor and rates signals. Balance sheet runoff continues mechanically and adds a modest structural tightening backdrop, but its magnitude is small relative to the moves in labor and term premium, and its interaction with term premium (a channel the panel itself flagged as untested) makes me only lightly credit it as an independent driver of yields right now. Curve slope I read as genuinely flat/paused near-term despite the longer trend, since two consecutive prints have not extended the steepening.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: headline CPI y/y fell to 2.61% from 2.94%, a large drop, and the falsifier condition (mom>0.35% plus core PCE reaccelerating) did not trigger — outgoing m/m was only 0.16%, well below 0.35%, and core PCE y/y actually ticked up slightly to 2.81% from 2.75% but that's a minor wobble, not reacceleration. Momentum readings are now quite soft: headline 3m annualized is just 1.14%, core 3m annualized 2.00% — both below the trailing y/y rates, implying further disinflation is baked into the pipeline unless m/m prints reaccelerate sharply. The last two monthly prints (-0.04, 0.17) are subdued versus the 3m-avg of 0.09%, consistent with continued easing. Policy language also softened only cosmetically ("elevated" to "somewhat elevated", "modest" to "some further progress") — consistent with continued gradual disinflation, not a reversal. Given the sharp two-tenths deceleration in headline is unlikely to fully repeat, but the underlying momentum is still soft, I expect headline CPI y/y to be roughly flat-to-slightly-down next month, not sharply higher. The small uptick in core PCE y/y is worth flagging as a minor caution but doesn't overturn the overall soft-momentum picture.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% combined with core PCE y/y rising further (e.g., above 2.9%) would signal reacceleration and overturn this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 observations show breakeven_10y grinding higher over the recent window: 2.07→2.07→2.07→2.10→2.13→2.15→2.15→2.15→2.16→2.15. That is a clear uptrend from the start of the series, contradicting my prior "down" call. The 21d and 63d/252d changes are still negative because they're measured from higher starting points earlier in the lookback, but the most recent trajectory (last 6-7 prints) is flat-to-slightly-up, having stabilized around 2.15-2.16 after climbing from 2.07. There's no sign of renewed decline — the series troughed and has been rising then plateauing near the top of its recent range (range_position 0.24 suggests it's still historically low in the 12m band, but the trend from the trough is upward). Given the previous fall from the 12m high (2.49) already happened, and now the series has stabilized just above its 12m low with a modest upward tilt, I read this as a shift from "down" to "flat," with a slight upward bias given the last few prints held steady after the climb rather than resuming the decline. Conviction is modest given the small absolute moves (a few bps) and mixed signals between short and longer lookback windows.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward 2.07-2.10 sustained over multiple observations, or renewed negative 21d change, would confirm the down call instead.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was correct: unemployment printed at 4.20%, matching prior month rather than falling back, and the Sahm gap widened to 0.47pp from a 3m/6m/12m rise of 0.30/0.30/0.50pp. The rate is now sitting at its 12-month high with range_position at 1.00, meaning no observation in the trailing year exceeds the current level. However, the pace of increase has stalled at the margin — the last two prints are both 4.20%, a flat sequential reading rather than a fresh acceleration. Policy language shifted from "remained low" to "moved up but remains low," and now emphasizes moderating job gains, confirming the loosening trend is real and being acknowledged, not a one-off. But since the two most recent prints are identical, the immediate one-month trajectory looks more like a pause at an elevated plateau than continued rapid ascent. Distance from natural rate (~4%) is only 0.20pp, still modest in level terms. Given the flattening of the most recent two months alongside a still-rising trend on 3/6/12m windows, I lean toward a further mild uptick or flat print next month, but with reduced conviction versus last time since the immediate momentum has decelerated.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.00%, or sahm_gap falling under 0.30, would signal the loosening trend has reversed rather than plateaued.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield has drifted up in the most recent observations, from 3.79 to 3.91, and last release's "down" call did not materialize further—yield sits near the low end of its 12-month range (range_position 0.11, only 0.13pp above the 12m low) but has ticked up over the last several prints. The 21d change is still slightly negative (-0.08pp) reflecting the prior decline, but the last five readings (3.81, 3.82, 3.83, 3.84, 3.87, 3.91) show a clear short-term uptick, suggesting the multi-month downtrend (63d -0.60pp, 252d -0.21pp) has stalled and modestly reversed. The 30s10s slope remains moderately positive (+0.29pp), consistent with continued balance-sheet runoff pressure at the long end, and the Fed's ongoing balance-sheet reduction language is unchanged, which structurally leans toward higher long-end supply pressure. Given the yield is still very low in its range, a full reversal isn't clear, but the recent momentum has shifted from down to modestly up. My prior "down" call was wrong for the most recent leg—yield rose rather than fell. I now lean toward a mild upward drift continuing into month-end, given the recent trend reversal and steady runoff pressure, though conviction is limited by how compressed the range still is.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below ~3.80 with follow-through would confirm the downtrend resumed; a push toward 4.00+ would confirm the reversal higher.

My previous "up" call is now falsified: the mild tightening drift from the 52w low (-0.44) toward -0.36 has stalled and reversed slightly. The 4-week change is -0.02 (easing), while the 13-week change is still +0.03 (net tightening over the longer window), so momentum has clearly shifted even though the level remains well below zero (distance_from_zero -0.38), meaning conditions are still historically loose/easy. Given the two-week pullback and negative short-term momentum, I now lean toward continued easing or at least a stall rather than further tightening, though the magnitude is small and could just be noise around a flat trend.

  The latest print continues the runoff trend I called last time: total assets fell to 7,123,238 from 7,139,952 (a ~$15.7B one-period drop), consistent with the 4-week change of -$55.15B and 13-week change of -$161.08B (-2.21%).
