There is no sign of deceleration in the pace — the last several prints show steady, consistent declines rather than any flattening. Statement language is unchanged, explicitly confirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency MBS holdings, with no mention of tapering the cap or pausing runoff. My prior down call was correct and the evidence supporting it is unchanged in kind and consistent in magnitude. Given the steady monthly cap-driven runoff pace persists and the language shows no shift toward pausing, I expect month-end total assets to be lower again, likely by a similar magnitude to the recent 4-week pace (roughly $40-60B). Conviction remains fairly high given the consistency of both the measurement trend and the unchanged policy language, though not extreme since caps could in principle bind less if reserves scarcity concerns emerge.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff entirely.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has completed its move from deeply inverted to essentially flat/slightly positive, printing 0.00, 0.01, 0.00, 0.00 over the last four readings after a steady climb from -0.20. My prior "flat" call was too cautious in retrospect — the trend continued to steepen right into my stated trigger (above -0.09), confirming resumed steepening rather than stalling. Now that the curve has reached and stabilized at effectively flat (slope_distance_from_flat = 0.00), the pace of steepening has clearly decelerated: the last four prints are essentially unchanged at 0.00, versus the strong momentum in slope_change_63d (+0.38) and slope_change_252d (+0.75). The spread sits at the top of its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.99), which mechanically limits further near-term upside — there's no headroom above without breaking into new high territory. The 10s30s slope (+0.29) shows the long end still modestly upward-sloping, consistent with a normalizing curve rather than one about to re-invert. Policy language remains static (rate hold, data-dependent), offering no new catalyst. Given the flattening of the flattening — i.e., deceleration right at a range extreme — I lean toward continued stabilization near zero with a slight bias to marginal further steepening given persistent momentum, but conviction is modest given the plateau.
Would change its mind if: A print back below -0.10 would confirm renewed flattening; a sustained push above +0.10 would confirm resumed steepening beyond the current plateau.

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/size of ongoing Treasury runoff would clarify supply pressure driving the recent uptick.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Would clarify if broader risk appetite is pushing yields via flight-to-quality reversal.

arbitrated.notes: Inflation momentum remains soft (3m annualized well below y/y, m/m prints subdued), so headline CPI likely drifts flat-to-down next month, though the pace of decline should moderate versus the recent sharp drop. Inflation expectations have stabilized after climbing off their lows — the multi-month decline appears over, but the recent plateau near 2.15-2.16 argues for flat rather than a fresh leg up; I temper the analyst's slight upward tilt given small absolute moves and mixed lookback windows. Labor tightness continues to loosen on a trend basis (unemployment at 12m high, Sahm gap widening), but the two most recent identical prints signal the pace has stalled — I keep the "up" (looser labor market / higher unemployment) call but shrink conviction given the plateau. Term premium: yields have turned up over the last several prints after a long downtrend, and this dovetails with balance-sheet runoff continuing unabated, which structurally pressures long-end supply/term premium higher — the missing balance_sheet context the term_premium analyst wanted is actually supportive of their reversal call, so I keep some conviction there. Financial conditions have eased over the last two prints, reversing the prior modest tightening drift; conditions remain historically loose. Balance sheet runoff is the most unambiguous, high-conviction call — steady, consistent decline with no change in policy language, so assets keep falling. Curve slope has hit a range extreme (flat, top of 12m range) and decelerated — flat is the right call with no strong directional bias either way given mechanical ceiling effects.

discounted.why: Small absolute moves and conflicting short/long lookback windows make the signal weak.
At a mechanical range extreme; directional signal is muted regardless of momentum.

arbitrated.falsifier: A headline CPI m/m print above 0.35% with core PCE reaccelerating, combined with 10y yields breaking back below 3.80 and NFCI rebounding above -0.36, would flip this read.

risks.text: A reacceleration in core inflation could simultaneously push term premium and CPI calls in the same unexpected direction.
Labor loosening could stall entirely if the flat unemployment prints persist, undermining the up-call conviction.
Unexpected Treasury issuance changes or debt ceiling dynamics could override balance-sheet-driven term premium logic.
A dovish policy shift pausing balance sheet runoff would invalidate both the balance_sheet and term_premium calls.

why.inflation_expectations: Trend has stabilized after climbing; plateau argues for flat, not a fresh uptrend.
why.labor_tightness: 12m-high unemployment and widening Sahm gap support looser labor, but two flat prints reduce momentum conviction.
why.term_premium: Recent 5-print uptick plus ongoing balance-sheet runoff supply pressure supports mild reversal higher in yields.
why.financial_conditions: Two consecutive declines reverse the prior tightening drift; conditions ease modestly.
why.balance_sheet: Consistent, unchanged-pace runoff with no policy shift; high-conviction continued decline in assets.
why.curve_slope: Curve sits at top of 12m range with decelerating steepening; flat is the base case, slight upward tilt only.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Headline CPI y/y fell to 2.43% from 2.61%, extending a clear downtrend from the 3.1-3.5% range seen in prior months, and the previous "down" call was correct. However, the underlying momentum is now more mixed than the headline trend suggests. The latest headline m/m was 0.21%, up from -0.04% and 0.17% in the prior two months, and headline 3m annualized (2.17%) is now below core 3m annualized (2.46%), indicating the recent disinflation has been aided by softer non-core components. Core PCE y/y has ticked up three months in a row (2.75 → 2.81 → 2.87), a +0.10pp change over 3 months, suggesting core persistence is not fully resolved even as headline y/y continues to benefit from base effects (headline was 3.24-3.36 a year ago, a high comparison base still rolling off). Policy language shows only incremental easing ("modest" → "some" further progress), consistent with deceleration continuing but decelerating in pace. Given base effects are largely exhausted (headline now at 12-month low) and core momentum has firmed, I expect headline y/y to be roughly flat to marginally lower next month, not a continuation of the sharp decline seen recently.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or core PCE y/y rising further above 2.9% would signal reacceleration; a headline m/m below 0.1% would confirm continued deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_mom, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation (2.18) sits at the top of the recent range and confirms the modest uptick flagged by be_change_21d (+0.02pp). Over the past ~10 readings, breakeven has oscillated in a tight 2.12-2.19 band, with the most recent print near the high end (be_range_position 0.34 relative to the 12-month range, but near the top of the short-window range shown). My prior "flat" call was reasonable: the series did not break down toward 2.07-2.10 as the falsifier specified, nor did it show renewed negative 21d momentum — instead it edged up slightly. The medium-term declines (63d -0.14, 252d -0.16) still dominate the longer-run picture, keeping the driver below its 12-month high and only modestly above its 12-month low, consistent with a broader cooling trend that has stalled recently. But the very last two prints (2.15→2.18) show a small pop, and the 21d change is positive, suggesting near-term stabilization or slight firming rather than continued decay. With breakeven still +0.18pp above the 2% objective, there's no urgency for the Committee's language to shift, and no strong directional catalyst evident in the data alone. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up for the next month, given the recent uptick outweighs longer-term softness at this short horizon, but the signal is not strong enough to call a clear reversal.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 2.13 sustained over multiple observations, or renewed negative 21d change, would confirm a down move instead of flat/up.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked down to 4.10% from 4.20%, a small pullback rather than continued deterioration. My prior "up" call was directionally too aggressive for this release: the last observation actually fell 0.1pp. That said, the broader trend since the 12-month low of 3.70% is still one of net loosening — unrate_change_12m is +0.40pp, sahm_gap is +0.47pp (above the 0.30 threshold I set as a reversal signal), and unrate_range_position sits at 0.80, meaning we're near the top of the past year's range. The 3m change is flat (0.00pp) and the 3m average (4.17%) is still elevated versus the 12m low, suggesting the level has plateaued at a higher plateau rather than reversing back toward 3.70-3.90%. Policy language also shifted to acknowledge "job gains have moderated" and unemployment "moved up," confirming the committee sees the same drift. Given the single-month decline, I don't think the loosening trend is over, but the immediate one-month momentum is ambiguous — a plateau with one down-tick is not strong evidence of continued monthly increases. I'll moderate conviction and lean toward flat-to-slightly-up rather than confidently up.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.90%, or sahm_gap falling under 0.30, would confirm reversal; a fresh rise above 4.20% would confirm renewed loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_3m_avg

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (3.81) is up from 3.75 and near the top of the recent short run, but the 10Y remains deep in the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.13, only 0.18pp above the 12m low of 3.63). The 21d change is essentially flat (-0.06pp) while 63d and 252d changes are sharply negative (-0.67, -0.80pp), meaning the dominant trend over the past quarter and year has been falling yields/premium, with only a modest recent uptick. My prior "up" call at 0.35 conviction was wrong in direction over the near term — the series continued to hover/drift rather than confirming a reversal toward 4.00+. The last two prints (3.75, 3.81) show some bounce, and the 10s30s slope steepened modestly to 0.33pp, consistent with some long-end cheapening pressure (continued balance-sheet runoff language reaffirmed, unchanged in substance). But this is a small bounce within a still-depressed range, not a confirmed trend reversal. Given the mixed signal — recent uptick but still low in range and longer-term trend down — I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-up call with low conviction, acknowledging the prior miss.
Would change its mind if: A clean break below 3.75 with follow-through confirms downtrend resumed; a sustained push through 3.90-4.00 confirms reversal higher.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (continued easing/down) is confirmed by the latest data. NFCI has fallen steadily over the last several readings, from -0.36 down to -0.42, with the most recent three observations (-0.40, -0.41, -0.42) showing accelerating momentum rather than the flattening I had flagged as a risk. The 4-week change (-0.04) and 13-week change (-0.03) both point in the same direction, and the index is now sitting near its 52-week low (-0.44), well below both its 13-week average (-0.38) and its long-run zero average. Range position at 0.11 (out of 0-1) confirms conditions are near the loose end of the recent 52-week band. There is no offsetting policy language to suggest a turn. Given the consistency and slight acceleration of the decline over the past three readings, I have more confidence than last time that this is a genuine easing trend rather than noise. I do not see evidence of stabilization or reversal, so I extend the down call with somewhat higher conviction than previously.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above -0.38 (the 13w average) over 2-3 readings would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.72
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print continues the runoff trend that my prior "down" call anticipated: fed_assets fell to 7,080,059 from 7,109,137, a fresh cycle low and well below the 52w high (-875,723). The 4-week change (-43,179) is actually larger in magnitude than the prior 4-week pace implied, and the 13-week change (-151,104, -2.09%) confirms a steady, uninterrupted decline rather than deceleration. There is no sign of a flat or higher monthly print, so the condition that would have made me reconsider did not occur — the prior call was correct and the evidence still supports it. Statement language continues to affirm ongoing balance-sheet reduction ("will continue reducing its holdings of Treasury securities and agency debt and agency MBS"), with only a minor wording trim (removal of "In addition,") that does not signal any change in substance or pace — no mention of tapering the cap, pausing, or ending runoff. With both the quantitative trend and the qualitative language pointing the same direction, this remains a clear, low-ambiguity QT signal. I expect total assets to be lower again at next month-end, continuing the established monthly decline of roughly $30-45B.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff, or announcement of slowing the monthly caps.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was too cautious: the spread had actually pushed decisively above the 0.10 threshold I set, and this release confirms further steepening momentum has been present, even as the last two prints (0.20, 0.15) show a modest pullback from the 0.26 high. Looking at the full run, the trend over 21/63/252 days is unambiguously steepening (+0.15, +0.44, +0.64 pp respectively), and the spread sits at 0.86 of its 12-month range — near the top, well above the -0.53 low. The recent dip from 0.26 to 0.15 is a retracement within a steepening channel, not a reversal, especially with the 50bp cut just delivered easing front-end yields more than the long end typically responds, which tends to support further steepening as cuts continue. The 10s30s slope at +0.33 pp corroborates a broadly un-inverted, normalizing curve further out, consistent with continued 2s10s steepening pressure. However, two consecutive lower prints (0.20 then 0.15) warrant some caution against calling for further sharp steepening this month — I'll lean toward continued gradual steepening but with moderate conviction given the recent softening in momentum.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.05 pp would signal renewed flattening; a fresh push above 0.30 would confirm resumed strong steepening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/schedule of QT runoff would clarify supply pressure on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment would clarify flight-to-quality flows affecting 10Y.
  curve_slope lacks financial_conditions — Would clarify whether easing conditions are driving front-end down faster, supporting steepening.
  curve_slope lacks term_premium — Rising term premium would explain and reinforce long-end-led steepening distinct from Fed-cut effects.

arbitrated.notes: Across the seven drivers, the panel is mostly low-conviction and mostly "flat," with two clear exceptions: balance sheet (QT continuing, unambiguous) and financial conditions (easing, fairly clean). Inflation itself: headline y/y disinflation is decelerating and core PCE has firmed three months running, so I read this as flat with a slight upward skew in near-term risk rather than continued sharp decline — the analyst's own data (core momentum firming, base effects exhausted) tilts risk toward stabilization/modest uptick even though the call is nominally flat. Inflation expectations: genuinely flat, a small recent uptick within a tight range, nothing directional. Labor tightness: one-month down-tick within a 12-month uptrend that has plateaued — the medium-term drift is still toward loosening (higher unemployment), so I keep a mild positive lean on the tightness measure (i.e., unemployment drifting up) rather than pure flat, though conviction is low given the single-month reversal. Term premium: mixed, small bounce off multi-year lows but longer trend still down; too noisy to take a real stance, essentially flat. Financial conditions: consistent multi-reading acceleration lower (looser) with no offsetting language — this is the most credible directional call besides balance sheet. Balance sheet: clean, monotonic runoff, strong statement-language confirmation — highest conviction call on the panel. Curve slope: steepening trend intact but two consecutive softer prints introduce real doubt about pace; I keep a modest positive lean but temper conviction given the retracement and the acknowledged blind spot (no term-premium or financial-conditions cross-check). Missing cross-links (term premium/curve slope not seeing each other or financial conditions) leave some risk that a shared driver (long-end supply, risk sentiment) is not being priced correctly across those three.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline m/m printed 0.29%, above the 0.1% deceleration threshold I flagged but just under my 0.35% reacceleration trigger. Headline y/y actually rose to 2.58% from 2.43% — the first uptick in this series after six months of decline from 3.49% to 2.43%. My prior "flat" call undersold this: the trend broke upward, not sideways. The 3m annualized headline (2.66%) and core (2.60%) rates sit modestly above the 2% target but below the recent m/m run-rate, and the mom_gap_vs_outgoing (+0.07pp) plus the 0.29% latest print (above the 0.22% 3m average) both point to mild upward momentum building into the next print. Core PCE y/y has also ticked up two months running (2.81→2.87→2.84), though it remains near its 12m low. I weight core PCE for persistence, but the recent CPI momentum — outgoing_mom rising through 0.16, 0.21, 0.29 — is the more timely signal for the next CPI release specifically. This isn't a strong reacceleration case, but the direction of travel over the last two prints is unambiguously up rather than flat, and I was wrong last time to call it flat. Given genuine but modest momentum, I lean up with moderate conviction rather than high.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15% or a drop in headline y/y back toward 2.4% would indicate the uptick was noise, not a turn.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, outgoing_mom

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (2.33) sits just below the recent local peak of 2.34 and above the prior flat band of 2.29-2.31 that persisted for six of the last ten readings. === inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55

  The prior call (up, 0.45) was correct: headline y/y rose again to 2.72% from 2.58%, and the m/m print (0.28%) stayed above the 0.15% threshold I set, confirming the uptick is not noise. Momentum now looks persistent rather than a one-off: headline_mom_3m_avg (0.26%) exceeds outgoing_mom, and headline_3m_annualized (3.18%) sits above the latest y/y reading, implying further upward drift in the y/y series over the next print. Core PCE is also creeping up (2.99% vs 2.84% prior, core_yoy_change_3m +0.18pp), reinforcing that this is a genuine firming in underlying inflation, not just energy/food noise — the two measures are moving together. Core sits 0.99pp above target and its 3m annualized pace (3.03%) is running above the y/y level too, so the direction of travel is consistent across both headline and core. Policy language softened slightly (dropped "further" progress, dropped explicit confidence language), consistent with a data-driven pause in disinflation. Given three consecutive months of firming m/m prints and rising y/y in both series, I expect the next headline CPI y/y to tick up again, likely into the 2.75-2.85% range, absent a sharp reversal in monthly prints.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15%, or headline y/y falling back below 2.6%, would signal the recent firming has stalled.

arbitrated.notes: Inflation is the clearest, most persistent signal: three consecutive months of firming headline and core prints, momentum measures (3m annualized, mom_gap) all pointing the same direction, and policy language corroborating a data-driven pause in disinflation. I give this high conviction. Inflation expectations have reversed from the prior up-call to a modest down/flat bias — four consecutive declines and a negative 21d change — but longer lookbacks still show net gains, so I treat this as a mild, lower-conviction fade rather than a trend reversal. Labor tightness continues to drift up: repeated touches of the 12-month high, accelerating 6m/12m change, and materially shifted policy language describing a "generally eased" labor market — a genuine, moderate-conviction loosening trend. Financial conditions are grinding to fresh 52-week lows with no single up-tick in 13 readings — high-conviction continued easing. Balance sheet runoff is the most mechanical and confidently down driver: consistent multi-week declines, unchanged statement language, no signs of pause — very high conviction. Term premium and curve slope are the more fragile calls: term premium reversed from up to down based on the last few prints, but the medium-term (63d) signal is still positive and the analyst itself flags missing balance-sheet/financial-conditions context that could explain or contradict the yield decline — given financial conditions are easing (risk-on, arguing yields down via less term premium) and balance-sheet runoff is a standing upward pressure, the down call is plausible but only moderate conviction. Curve slope down is consistent with the term premium read (front-end sticky post smaller cut, long end soft) — moderate conviction, correlated with term premium's fragility.

discounted.why: Recent reversal noted but longer-horizon momentum still positive, limiting conviction in the down call.

arbitrated.falsifier: A rebound in DGS10 above 4.40 alongside a stall in NFCI easing or a pause in QT runoff would flip the term premium/curve-slope down calls.

risks.text: Term premium analyst explicitly lacks balance-sheet and financial-conditions context, undermining confidence in the yield-down call.
Term premium and curve slope calls both flipped from 'up' last meeting, showing sensitivity to short window noise.
Firming inflation alongside easing financial conditions is an unusual combination that could reverse either read.

why.inflation_expectations: Reversal confirmed but longer lookbacks still net positive; treat as mild fade, not a trend break.
why.labor_tightness: Repeated new highs plus materially shifted policy language support moderate continued loosening.
why.term_premium: Recent downtrend real but 63d signal still positive and analyst flags missing cross-driver context; discount conviction.
why.financial_conditions: Monotonic 13-week easing to fresh 52w low with no reversal signs; high conviction continuation.
why.balance_sheet: Mechanical, cap-driven runoff with unchanged language and consistent weekly declines; near-certain continuation down.
why.curve_slope: Sharp 21d flattening at trigger level, corroborated by term premium logic (sticky front end); moderate conviction.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.50
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call played out: headline y/y rose from 2.72 to 2.87 (+0.15pp), with the outgoing m/m at 0.34%, above my 0.15% falsification threshold, so the firming trend continued rather than stalled. Momentum remains intact: headline_mom has trended up over the last four prints (0.16, 0.21, 0.29, 0.34), and headline_3m_annualized (3.70%) sits well above both the trailing y/y (2.87%) and above core_3m_annualized (2.74%), indicating headline pressure is still building, likely energy/food-driven given the gap versus core. Core PCE is comparatively stable, ticking down slightly from 2.99 to 2.98 y/y, and core_yoy_change_3m is only +0.11pp — core momentum is flat-to-mild, not accelerating. The divergence matters: I weight core as the persistence signal, which suggests underlying inflation is stabilizing just under 3%, not re-accelerating sharply. But for the headline_cpi_yoy call specifically, the near-term momentum (rising mom prints, high 3m annualized) points toward another firm print. Policy language softened only slightly ("further progress" → "progress"), consistent with a plateau rather than reversal. Given strong recent mom trajectory and elevated 3m annualized headline pace, I lean toward another modest increase in headline y/y at the next release, though not with high confidence given core's relative calm.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15%, or a pullback in headline_3m_annualized toward core's pace, would signal the recent firming has stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call has been invalidated: breakeven_10y has moved back above 2.30 and held there for multiple recent observations (2.32, 2.35, 2.35, 2.34, 2.31, 2.34), and the 21d change is now clearly positive (+0.08pp), which was my own stated falsifier. I was wrong to lean down last time. The series is choppy within a fairly narrow band but the trend over 63d and 252d is a consistent +0.19pp rise, and range_position of 0.78 shows breakevens sitting in the upper part of their 12-month range (2.02-2.43), well above the 12m low. Distance from the 2% target has widened to +0.34pp, indicating expectations remain moderately above objective and have not been correcting toward it. The latest print (2.34) matches the recent local high (2.35) closely, suggesting some stabilization at an elevated level rather than a fresh breakout, but no evidence of reversal downward. Given the multi-observation confirmation above 2.30 and positive short/medium-term momentum, I now shift to a modest up call, tempered by the fact that the series has been oscillating rather than trending cleanly, and month-to-month moves are small in absolute terms.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.30 sustained over two or more observations, or 21d change turning negative, would reverse this up call.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print (4.10%) came in below the prior 4.20% reading, consistent with my stated reversal trigger (a move back toward/at 4.00-4.10% range). My previous "up" call at 0.45 conviction was too confident given the data was already plateauing — the last four readings (4.10, 4.20, 4.10, 4.20, 4.10) show the unemployment rate oscillating in a narrow 4.10-4.20% band rather than trending decisively higher. The 3m and 6m changes are both flat (+0.00pp), and the sahm_gap at 0.43 has not accelerated further — it's roughly stable, not surging, which argues against an accelerating-loosening narrative. The 12m change (+0.30pp) confirms the market has loosened somewhat from a very tight base, but the level (4.10%) sits only modestly above the ~4% natural rate estimate. Policy language now describes conditions as having "generally eased" — an acknowledgment of past drift, not necessarily forward acceleration. Given the plateau pattern and lack of fresh confirmation of continued rise, I see this as essentially flat with a slight residual upward lean, not a confident continuation of loosening.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 4.20% or sahm_gap rising past 0.55 would confirm renewed loosening; a print at/below 4.00% would confirm tightening reversal.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_3m_avg, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.45) was wrong: yields did not confirm a downtrend and instead pushed higher, breaching my own 4.40 upside trigger decisively. The level series shows a persistent climb from 4.40 to 4.58, with the 21d, 63d and 252d changes all strongly positive (+0.40, +0.83, +0.79 pp), indicating sustained upward pressure across multiple horizons, not just noise. The yield sits at 0.89 on the 12-month range (near the top of its 12-month band, just 0.12pp off the high and 0.95pp above the low), which is a clear sign of elevated term premium rather than a mean-reverting dip. The 10s30s slope remains positive at 0.20pp, consistent with ongoing long-end steepening pressure rather than flattening/flight-to-quality. Continued balance-sheet runoff (unchanged policy language) keeps a structural bid against duration, adding supply pressure to the long end. Given the strength and consistency of the recent move, and the position near cycle highs, the path of least resistance is still higher yields into month-end, though after such a sharp run some consolidation or pause is plausible, tempering conviction from an outright strong call.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 4.40 with the 10s30s slope flattening or inverting would signal the upward pressure has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its slow, grinding easing trend: the latest print of -0.48 sits at the 52-week low, with nfci_change_4w at -0.01 and nfci_change_13w at -0.06, both negative, confirming persistent (if decelerating) easing momentum. The index is well below zero (-0.48 distance from zero), indicating financial conditions remain looser than historical average, and the trend has not reversed or stalled — the falsifier I set previously (a sustained reading above -0.43) has not been met; in fact the series has moved further away from that threshold, not toward it. My prior "down" call was correct and the evidence still supports continuation, though the pace of decline has slowed markedly (last four readings: -0.47, -0.47, -0.48, -0.48 — essentially flattening at the margin). This tension between continued negative sign on both change measures and the visibly decelerating slope tempers conviction somewhat. Range position at 0.00 (sitting at the 52w low with no cushion) also suggests limited room to ease further without breaking new ground, but the momentum signal itself still points down rather than flat. No policy language is offered to explain or contextualize the move, so this is a pure momentum read.
Would change its mind if: A reading back above -0.43 (13w avg) sustained over 2-3 releases, or a flat/positive 4w change, would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.68
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: the latest print (6,885,963) extended the decline from 6,889,332, continuing an uninterrupted string of weekly drops. The 4-week change (-19,177M) shows the pace has decelerated markedly versus the 13-week change (-194,096M, implying a faster average weekly pace over the quarter), suggesting runoff continues but at a slower clip recently than earlier in the window — consistent with reserves still being drained but perhaps approaching a more measured cadence. Total assets remain near multi-period lows, well below the 52-week high (-800,747M), and there is no sign of stabilization: every one of the last several weekly prints has been lower than the one before. Statement language is unchanged, explicitly confirming continued balance-sheet reduction with no mention of pausing or tapering caps. With no evidence of a plateau, flat print, or dovish shift in language, the trajectory clearly points to continued runoff into month-end. The deceleration in the 4-week pace is worth noting but not yet large enough to call a flattening — it's still firmly negative. I maintain a down call, slightly softened from before given the visible deceleration in weekly pace.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff, or announcement of slowing the monthly caps.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down/flattening) was wrong: the spread has continued to rise, hitting a new 12-month high at +0.33pp, with slope_range_position at 1.00 — the top of its range. Momentum is positive across all windows: +0.28pp over 21 days, +0.13pp over 63 days, +0.74pp over 252 days. The last few prints (0.29, 0.30, 0.28, 0.31, 0.31, 0.33) show a steady grind higher with only minor pauses, not reversal. The curve remains only mildly positive (33bp), still far from historically steep levels, but the trend is clearly one of continued steepening from deep inversion a year ago. The Fed's further 25bp cut to the front end, with one dissent preferring to hold, is consistent with continued easing pressure on the short end that supports further steepening if cuts continue while long yields stay anchored or drift up. With the spread sitting at a fresh high and no evidence of stalling in the last two prints, I expect the steepening trend to persist into month-end, absent a sharp reversal. Conviction is moderate given the spread is still a small absolute number and could plateau near this range high.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below +0.25pp over the next few readings would signal the steepening trend has stalled or reversed near this range high.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high

arbitrated.notes: The panel paints a consistent macro picture: modest reflationary pressure at the margin, still-easy but stabilizing financial conditions, continued balance-sheet runoff, and a bond market pricing higher term premium with a steepening curve. On inflation, headline momentum (rising m/m prints, elevated 3m annualized vs core) argues for another firm headline y/y print, even though core is flat-to-calm — I read this as a modest up lean, not a re-acceleration story. Inflation expectations have genuinely reversed from the prior down call: breakevens have held above 2.30 across multiple prints with positive 21d/63d/252d momentum, so a modest up lean is warranted, tempered by the choppy, range-bound nature of the series. Labor tightness is the cleanest "no view" case — unemployment is oscillating in a narrow band with flat 3m/6m changes and a stable Sahm gap; I keep only a whisper of upward (loosening) lean given the 12m drift, but treat this as essentially flat. Term premium and curve slope are the strongest, most confirmed calls this cycle: 10y yields broke decisively above the analyst's own downside trigger, sit near the top of the 12-month range, and are corroborated independently by a curve that has steepened to a fresh 12-month high — the two datasets are self-consistent even though neither analyst saw the other's data, which raises my confidence rather than lowering it. Financial conditions continue to ease (NFCI at 52w low) but the pace is visibly decelerating, so I keep the down call but shrink conviction versus the analyst's own framing. Balance-sheet runoff continues uninterrupted, though also decelerating in the 4-week window; I keep a strong down call since there is zero sign of a pause and language is unchanged.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is confirmed by the newest data. Headline CPI y/y rose again to 2.99% from 2.87%, its fourth consecutive monthly increase off the 2.43% trough, and the outgoing print's m/m gain was 0.43%, the strongest in the 13-month window and well above my 0.15% threshold for stalling. Headline 3m annualized momentum sits at 4.29%, more than double the 2% target and running hot relative to core's 2.39% annualized pace — a divergence that flags energy/food (or other volatile components) as the current driver of headline strength rather than broad-based persistence. Core PCE y/y, the better persistence gauge, is essentially flat (2.99% vs 2.98%, 2.99% three months prior), suggesting underlying inflation is stable, not accelerating. Still, headline is what this call is scored on, and three straight months of accelerating m/m prints (0.28, 0.34, 0.43) with no sign of deceleration argues for continued upward pressure into the next release. I weight the momentum in headline_mom over the core stability for this specific horizon call, since the trend in m/m prints has been consistently positive and accelerating. Policy language remains unchanged, unhelpful for timing.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15%, or headline_3m_annualized falling back toward core's ~2.4% pace, would signal the firming has stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is holding up reasonably well. The last observation (2.42) sits at the high end of the recent range, matching the prior peak. The series has been choppy (2.41→2.38→2.40→2.42→2.43→2.40→2.42→2.40→2.38→2.42) but the 21d, 63d, and 252d changes are all positive (+0.08, +0.12, +0.12 pp), confirming a mild upward drift rather than reversal. The falsifier I set previously (sustained break below 2.30, or negative 21d change) was not triggered — 21d change remains clearly positive and the series never approached 2.30. Range position at 0.93 (near the 12m high of 2.45) reinforces that breakevens are firming, not falling. Distance from the 2% target (+0.42 pp) is elevated and roughly stable, suggesting the market has priced in a persistent modest overshoot rather than an accelerating one. Given the oscillation within a narrow band (2.38-2.43) over the last several prints, I don't see strong momentum for a big move higher, but the drift and position near range highs favor continued mild upside over flat. Conviction remains moderate given the choppiness and lack of decisive breakout.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 2.35 sustained over two or more observations, or 21d/63d changes both turning negative, would reverse this view.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 4.00% from 4.10%, exactly at the level I flagged as confirming a tightening reversal. This is the lowest reading in three months and pulls the rate back toward the 12-month low of 3.90%. The 3m and 6m changes are now both negative (-0.10pp and -0.20pp respectively), reversing the drift higher that had persisted for several months. The 12m change is still positive (+0.30pp) showing the market is somewhat looser than a year ago, and sahm_gap at 0.20pp is well below any recession-signaling threshold. Range position at 0.33 shows the rate sitting in the lower third of its 12-month range. My prior flat call was reasonably positioned given the choppy 4.10-4.20 plateau, but this print breaks that plateau to the downside, suggesting a modest re-tightening rather than continued loosening. Absent a clear driver of the improvement in the narrative provided, I'll treat this as a genuine but modest turn rather than noise, given it's a decisive move off the recent range. One data point on the downside after several months of gradual increases doesn't yet establish a strong trend, so I lean toward flat-to-down with modest conviction, leaning down given the magnitude and directionality of the move.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back to 4.10%+ would suggest this was noise; a further drop toward 3.90% would confirm sustained re-tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10yr has been essentially flat to modestly higher over the last month (21d change ~0.00pp), but sits well up over 63d (+0.30pp) and 252d (+0.50pp), and remains high in its 12-month range (0.82 range position, only 0.95pp above the 12m low, near the 4.79 high). My prior "up" call at 0.55 conviction played out roughly correctly in trend terms — yields have held near recent highs (4.52-4.65 band) rather than reversing, though the most recent print (4.58) is not a fresh high and the last 10 readings show choppy sideways action with no clean breakout. The 10s30s slope at +0.25pp is positive but not steepening dramatically, consistent with continued but not accelerating term premium pressure. Balance-sheet runoff continues per policy language, a structural tailwind for higher long-end yields/term premium. Given the flat 21-day change and lack of a new high, I'll moderate conviction from the prior call — the uptrend has stalled somewhat but hasn't reversed. No signal of flight-to-quality or a drop toward the low end of the range. I lean toward continued modest upward pressure into month-end, given elevated range position and ongoing supply/runoff dynamics, but the flat near-term momentum tempers confidence.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 13 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline_cpi_yoy fell to 2.80 from 2.99, and the newest headline_mom printed 0.23%, below the outgoing 0.44% and below the 3m average of 0.33%. My prior "up" call at 0.55 conviction was wrong — the firming trend I flagged did not continue; the latest month's deceleration in m/m pace is exactly the kind of stall I said would change my mind. Core PCE also dropped to 2.78 from 2.99, and core_yoy_change_3m is now -0.21pp, confirming the disinflationary tilt. However, headline_3m_annualized still sits at 4.04%, well above core's 2.46%, driven presumably by volatile components (energy/food) — this divergence matters: I weight core PCE more for persistence, and core is now decelerating with a full 0.21pp drop over three months. Headline yoy is still mid-range (0.35 position) but the single-month drop from 2.99 to 2.80 is sizable. Given the softer m/m print, the drop in both headline and core yoy, and the softened Fed language (dropping "made progress... remains somewhat elevated" to just "remains somewhat elevated," arguably a slightly more hawkish edge but not indicating a re-acceleration), I now lean toward continued softening or flat, not a resumption of upward momentum. The high headline_3m_annualized keeps me from high conviction on "down" given possible base effects reversing.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print back above 0.35-0.40%, or headline_3m_annualized staying near 4% while core also reaccelerates, would reverse this toward up.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call (0.40 conviction) has not played out well. Since then breakeven_10y drifted down from 2.45 to a low of 2.36 before ticking back up slightly to 2.37, 2.38. The 21d change is now -0.02pp, i.e., mildly negative, though not the sustained sub-2.35 break or dual-negative 21d/63d signal I set as a falsifier — 63d is still +0.10pp and 252d +0.07pp, so the longer trend remains modestly up. The latest two prints (2.37, 2.38) suggest stabilization/slight bounce off the recent low rather than continued decline. Breakeven remains well above the 12m low (+0.36pp) and sits at 0.82 of its 12-month range, still close to the highs, and 0.38pp above the 2% target — elevated in level terms even after the recent pullback. Net picture: short-term momentum has faded and reversed slightly negative, but medium/long-term trend and level both still point to inflation expectations running above target. This is a mixed signal — the sharp fall from 2.45 to 2.36 was real, but the last two ticks up suggest a bottoming rather than a fresh leg down. I'll call this close to flat, with a slight upward lean given the higher-frequency stabilization and elevated level, but conviction is low given the choppy recent path.
Would change its mind if: A fresh break below 2.35 sustained over two+ observations, or renewed decline in both 21d and 63d changes, would confirm a genuine downtrend.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:
  Last release invalidated my prior "down" call: unemployment rose to 4.20% from 4.00%, which is exactly the rebound-to-4.10%+ scenario I flagged as disconfirming. The series now shows a choppy but rangebound pattern between 3.90-4.20% over the trailing year, with the rate now sitting at the top of that 12-month range (range_position=1.00). The 3m and 6m changes are flat at 0.00pp, meaning the latest print is a jump within a sideways band rather than a clear new trend — the 4.20% has been touched twice before in this window without follow-through. Sahm gap (+0.20pp) is well below the 0.50 trigger threshold, and the 12m change of +0.30pp is modest, not a rapid deterioration. Distance from natural rate is only 0.20pp, so the level itself is not alarming. Given the whipsaw pattern (4.00→4.20 the last two prints, after 4.10→4.00 before that), I read this as noisy oscillation around a slightly-above-natural plateau rather than a decisive loosening trend. I'll lean toward flat/slightly-up next print, since mean-reversion within the range has been the recent behavior, but the edge-of-range position keeps some upside risk alive.
Would change its mind if: A print back down toward 3.90-4.00% would confirm range-bound noise; a push to 4.30%+ would confirm genuine trend loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_high, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has fallen from 4.47 to 4.24 over the last ten readings, with the 21-day change at -0.31pp — a meaningful decline. My prior "up" call at 0.35 conviction was wrong: the specific falsifier I set (drop below 4.40 with slope not steepening) has now been met, since yield sits at 4.24 and slope_30s10s (+0.27pp) has not steepened aggressively. The yield now sits at the midpoint of its 12-month range (range_position 0.53), down from the higher end, showing the recent move is a genuine reversal rather than noise at the top of the range. The 63-day and 252-day changes are near flat (-0.06, -0.04), meaning this is a fresh, recent move rather than a sustained multi-month trend — the decline is concentrated in the last month. Balance-sheet runoff continues per unchanged policy language, which is a modest structural upward force on term premium, but it's being outweighed for now by the recent price action. Given the sharp and recent nature of the decline, I now lean toward continued softness or at least a pause rather than a rebound, though the lack of trend confirmation over 63/252 days keeps me from high conviction. I'll shift to a modest "down" lean, correcting my prior miscall.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 4.40 with 10s30s slope steepening further would signal the decline has reversed and upward pressure resumed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

Call: up, conviction 0.30

Report:
  The prior call (continued easing/down) was wrong: NFCI bottomed at -0.51 and has ticked up two consecutive readings to -0.49, giving a +0.01 4-week change. That is exactly the flat/positive 4w change I flagged as a reversal signal, though it's still well short of the -0.43 threshold for a sustained reversal. Conditions remain very loose in absolute terms (distance from zero -0.49, near the 52w low, range position just 0.09) but the momentum has clearly stalled and modestly reversed off the low. The 13-week trend (-0.02) still shows net easing over the quarter, but the most recent two prints are the freshest signal and point to a small tightening impulse. With no policy commentary to corroborate either the recent uptick or the prior easing trend, I read this as a stabilization with a slight tightening lean rather than continued easing. Given the tiny magnitude (0.02 index points off the low) and lack of confirming language, conviction should be modest.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to -0.51 or new 52w low would confirm easing resumed; a rise above -0.43 sustained would confirm tightening reversal.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position, nfci

Call: down, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: total assets fell again, from 6,782,332 to 6,766,101 ($M), continuing an uninterrupted decline. The 4-week change (-52,085) is actually steeper than the trailing 13-week average pace (-139,039/13w ≈ -10,700/wk vs -52,085/4w ≈ -13,000/wk), suggesting runoff has not slowed and may have modestly accelerated recently. The 13-week percent change (-2.01%) confirms a steady, non-trivial monthly drain rather than a plateau. Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-775,868), with no sign of reversal in any of the last several prints — every one of the last 13 observations is lower than several observations prior, with no up-week in the most recent stretch. Policy language is unchanged: the Committee continues to reduce Treasury, agency debt, and agency MBS holdings, with no mention of slowing or pausing caps. Given the consistent, uninterrupted weekly declines and unchanged reinvestment/runoff language, month-end total assets should be lower again, continuing at a similar or slightly faster pace than the trailing 13-week average. There is no evidence in these measurements of any deceleration, let alone a pause.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff, or announcement of slowing the monthly caps.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The slope series ended at 0.25pp after dipping to 0.20-0.22 the prior two prints, so the latest move is a bounce, not a new high. Last release I called flat (0.35 conviction), citing 0.30/0.40 as break levels; neither was hit — the series has oscillated in a tight 0.20-0.27 band for the last several readings, so that call held up reasonably well. Looking longer, slope_change_252d (+0.66pp) shows the curve has steepened substantially over the past year, and slope_range_position at 0.82 confirms we're sitting near the top of the 12-month range (-0.47 to +0.41). But short-term momentum has faded: slope_change_21d is -0.09pp (mild flattening) while slope_change_63d is +0.16pp (still net steepening over the quarter). The FOMC held rates steady this meeting after cutting previously — a pause in the easing cycle removes a prior source of front-end anchoring/steepening pressure, and with the front end roughly stable (dgs2 near 4%), the 2s10s spread looks more likely to consolidate than to break out further. The 10s30s at +0.27pp is consistent with a normal, mildly upward-sloping long end, not signaling fresh curve distortion. Net: the range-bound, choppy pattern near cycle highs with fading 21d momentum argues for continued consolidation rather than a decisive move either way.
Would change its mind if: A close below +0.18pp would confirm flattening reversal; a sustained push above +0.32pp would confirm renewed steepening breakout.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_12m_low, slope_10s30s

arbitrated.falsifier: Headline CPI m/m back above 0.35-0.40% with core reaccelerating, plus a rebound in DGS10 above 4.40% with steepening slope, would flip inflation and term premium views.

risks.text: Headline inflation's hot 3m-annualized pace could reassert once base effects fade, reversing the disinflation read.
Term premium analyst lacked financial conditions and balance sheet context, so the yield decline's driver (flight-to-quality vs. structural) is unclear.
Several views (expectations, labor, financial conditions) are low-conviction and could all flip on the next single print.

why.inflation: Core and headline yoy both decelerating, but hot headline 3m-annualized and base-effect risk cap conviction.
why.inflation_expectations: Breakevens stabilizing/ticking up after a sharp fall; level still elevated and 63/252d trend still positive.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment at top of 12m range but whipsaw pattern; slight loosening lean, not a trend break.
why.term_premium: Real, recent yield decline (21d) not yet confirmed by 63d/252d trend; modest down lean.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI has bottomed and ticked up twice; small tightening impulse though magnitude trivial.
why.balance_sheet: Uninterrupted, possibly accelerating runoff with unchanged policy language; cleanest signal on the panel.
why.curve_slope: Range-bound near highs with fading 21d momentum post-pause; no directional edge.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print vindicates the prior "down" call: headline CPI y/y fell sharply to 2.38% from 2.80%, hitting a fresh 12-month low, and the m/m print was just 0.03%, the softest in the 13-month window. Headline 3m annualized has cooled to 2.78%, and the m/m gap vs the recent outgoing pace is negligible. This is a clean deceleration signal on headline.
  
  However, core PCE tells a different story: it jumped to 2.97% y/y from 2.78%, essentially back to its recent range high, and core_3m_annualized is running hot at 3.87% — well above the 2% target and above headline's own pace. Core-minus-headline has widened to +0.59pp, the largest gap in the series, suggesting the headline drop may be flattered by volatile components (energy/food) rather than reflecting broad-based disinflation. Since core is the better persistence signal, this divergence tempers confidence that headline's next print will keep falling.
  
  Given headline's very low outgoing momentum (0.03% m/m) and a soft 3m annualized pace, base effects and current momentum both point to headline staying low or ticking down slightly next month, but the hot core reading raises risk of a mean-reversion higher in headline if food/energy normalize. I lean toward continued softness but with less conviction than before given the core divergence.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print back above 0.30-0.35%, or core_pce_yoy continuing to climb alongside a headline rebound, would reverse this toward up.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_minus_headline, outgoing_mom

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was reasonably close but slightly underweighted continued mild upward drift: breakeven_10y has now printed 2.38, near the top of its 12-month range (be_range_position 0.82), with 63d and 252d changes still positive (+0.04pp, +0.06pp) even as the most recent 21d change is flat (0.00pp). The series has been grinding sideways-to-up in a narrow band (2.30-2.39 over the window), with the last two prints (2.37, 2.38) holding above the 2.35 level that would have signaled a downtrend break — that condition was not met. Breakevens remain 0.38pp above the 2% objective and close to the 12-month high (2.46%), suggesting inflation expectations are well-anchored but tilted modestly above target with no sign of receding. The flattening in the very short-term (21d) change tempers conviction that this is an accelerating uptrend, but there's no evidence of reversal either. Given the plateau near cycle highs and absence of any downside catalyst in the data shown, I lean toward continued stability with a slight upward bias rather than a reversal.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 2.35 sustained two+ observations, or renewed negative 21d and 63d changes together, would confirm a genuine downtrend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target, be_12m_high

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 4.20%, matching the prior month rather than rising further or falling back — consistent with my prior "flat" call, which is confirmed by this data. Over the trailing 13 months the rate has drifted up from 3.90% to a plateau around 4.10-4.20%, with unrate_range_position at 1.00 (sitting at the 12-month high) and distance from natural rate of only +0.20pp. However, the 3m and 6m changes are both a modest +0.10pp, and the series has been oscillating within a narrow 4.00-4.20% band for the last five readings rather than trending. The Sahm-gap of 0.23pp is well below the 0.50 threshold that would signal a genuine turning point. Policy language has also shifted constructively, now describing the rate as "stabilized at a low level" with "solid" conditions, replacing prior language about "easing" — this supports a read of plateau, not renewed loosening. Given the rate is holding at its recent ceiling without a fresh breakout, and policy language corroborates stabilization, I lean toward flat with a slight tilt toward up risk given the level is at the top of its range and one more soft print could confirm a mild uptrend. Conviction remains modest given the noisy back-and-forth pattern.
Would change its mind if: A print back down toward 3.90-4.00% would confirm range-bound noise; a push to 4.30%+ would confirm genuine trend loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield closed the latest window at 4.23%, down from the prior print (4.27) and near the middle of its 12-month range (position 0.52). My prior "down" call was directionally correct: the series fell from a mid-4.30s peak to 4.23, with dgs10_change_63d showing a sizable -0.39pp move, though the 21-day change is essentially flat (-0.01pp), suggesting the decline has stabilized rather than continuing to accelerate. The 10s30s slope (0.36pp) remains modestly positive but not steepening aggressively, and 10s2s slope is similar, showing no sharp curve dynamics pointing to renewed long-end selling. On the policy side, the Fed's decision to slow balance-sheet runoff (cutting the Treasury redemption cap from $25B to $5B) reduces net Treasury supply pressure from the Fed's own portfolio, which is a mild disinflationary-for-term-premium/supportive-for-price factor — it should ease upward pressure on long yields rather than add to it. Waller's dissent (preferring to continue the faster runoff pace) is a hawkish-on-supply counterpoint but he was outvoted. Net: the tapering of QT reduces one source of upward term-premium pressure, while the yield sits mid-range with flat near-term momentum. This favors continued mild softness or stabilization rather than a fresh selloff.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 4.40 with 10s30s slope steepening further would signal the decline has reversed and upward pressure resumed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has moved consistently off its trough over the last several readings: from -0.51 to -0.42, a clear monotonic climb over the last six observations. The 4-week change (+0.07) and 13-week change (+0.05) both confirm this upward momentum, with the pace of increase accelerating slightly in the most recent weeks. The index remains solidly negative (-0.42), meaning conditions are still looser than the historical average, but the trend is unambiguously toward tightening from an unusually loose starting point. Range position at 0.55 shows the index is now past the midpoint of its 52-week range, having moved well off the 52w low of -0.51. My prior call of 'up' was validated by this data — the rise above -0.43 threshold I specified as a tightening-reversal confirmation has now been met and sustained across the last three prints. There is no policy commentary this period to explain the move, but the measurement itself is clean and consistent: six straight increases with no reversal. I'll maintain the up call, and given the confirmation of my prior threshold plus the consistency of the trend, I'm raising conviction modestly from 0.30.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward -0.48 or lower, or two consecutive prints reversing the recent climb, would signal easing has resumed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.75) was correct in direction: total assets fell again, from 6,755,982 to 6,740,253 (-15,729 over the latest month), continuing the run-off trend (4w change -25,848; 13w change -145,710; 13w pct -2.12%). However, the policy language shows a material deceleration signal that I flagged as a mind-changer: beginning in the next period, the Treasury redemption cap drops from $25B to $5B/month, a five-fold slowdown in the Treasury runoff pace (agency MBS cap unchanged at $35B). This doesn't reverse direction — assets should still decline — but it should sharply reduce the monthly pace of decline going forward, since Treasury holdings were likely running near or above the old $25B cap while the new $5B cap binds much tighter. Waller's dissent (preferring to continue the faster pace) confirms this is a genuine policy change, not just language reiteration. So for month-end: I expect continued runoff, but at a much slower pace than the trailing 4-13w average suggests, given the new cap takes effect and reserves drainage decelerates materially.
Would change its mind if: A monthly print flat/higher, or further cap cuts/pause announcement, or evidence the $5B cap isn't binding as expected.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (flat, 0.35) was tested and effectively falsified on the upside first — spread pushed to +0.41 (12m high) before pulling back to +0.34 in the last two prints. Level: still meaningfully positive (+0.34pp), off the 12-month low by 0.81pp, and sitting at 0.92 of its 12-month range (near the top). Momentum: 21d change +0.09pp and 63d +0.03pp are still positive but decelerating, and the last two observations (0.38, 0.34) show a modest pullback from the 0.41 peak. The 252d change of +0.66pp confirms the dominant multi-month trend has been steepening, but the immediate trend has stalled/reversed slightly. The slowdown in balance-sheet runoff (redemption cap cut from $25B to $5B) is a technical factor that reduces net Treasury supply pressure at the front/belly, which if anything supports modest further steepening or stability rather than flattening, though its effect is diffuse. Given the pullback from the highs but still-elevated range position and slowing-but-still-positive short-term momentum, I lean toward the spread holding roughly steady to slightly higher next month, but confidence is limited by the recent two-print pullback.
Would change its mind if: A close below +0.28pp would confirm the pullback into flattening; a fresh push above +0.41pp would confirm renewed steepening breakout.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  curve_slope lacks term_premium — Rising term premium would explain and extend long-end steepening beyond technical balance-sheet effects.

Cross-checking the seven drivers, a coherent story emerges: policy easing on the balance-sheet side (QT taper, Treasury cap $25B→$5B) is a common thread touching term premium, balance sheet, and curve slope, while financial conditions and inflation expectations point a different direction than the balance-sheet/term-premium calls, and labor and headline inflation are genuinely range-bound/noisy.

Balance sheet: direction is unambiguous (still shrinking) but pace decelerates sharply — down call stands but I trim conviction slightly versus the analyst's 0.55 since the magnitude of decline next print will be much smaller, and "down" is measuring the level trend, not a clean re-acceleration.

Term premium: the analyst's down call is plausible given QT taper reduces net supply pressure and 63d momentum was negative, but 21d is flat and the level sits mid-range — I read this as a mild lean lower, not a confident call, and note the panel flagged that curve_slope was never given term_premium context, meaning the two might be more tightly linked (both driven by the same supply story) than treated independently. I discount magnitude accordingly.

Curve slope: recent pullback from highs plus decelerating momentum argues for flat-to-mild-steepening bias, consistent with reduced supply pressure; I keep it close to flat with a small positive tilt since the multi-month steepening trend is intact even if stalled.

Financial conditions: six consecutive monotonic tightening prints with accelerating pace is the cleanest, least noisy signal on the panel — I have highest conviction here.

Inflation: headline momentum is soft but core is hot and diverging — genuine tension, so I keep the down call but heavily discounted.

Inflation expectations: breakevens plateaued near cycle highs with flat 21d change — modest upside lean, not a strong trend.

Labor tightness: genuinely range-bound, Sahm gap well below threshold — true flat, no directional lean.

Headline/core divergence is a real conflict, not resolvable from data shown; kept only a weak lean.

Financial conditions tightening may lag or fail to translate into curve/term-premium moves given easing balance-sheet policy offsetting it.
