user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release's headline print came in at 2.33% y/y with a soft 0.16% m/m, down from 2.38% prior - so the "down" call from last time was directionally correct, headline yoy fell to a 12-month low. However, the momentum signal is murkier than the yoy trend suggests: the outgoing m/m (0.05) that will drop out of the next 3m window was very low, while the 3-month average m/m (0.14) and especially core_3m_annualized (+3.49%) point to underlying pressure running hotter than the yoy figure implies. Core PCE yoy actually ticked up to 2.67 from a recent low but has been oscillating in a 2.67-2.99 band with no clear trend - not decelerating convincingly. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing (+0.09pp) means next month's yoy math faces a slightly higher comparison base being replaced by a firmer run-rate, which is a mild upward risk to headline yoy unless the incoming m/m print is unusually soft again. Given headline yoy sits at a 12-month low but 3m annualized core momentum is elevated and core yoy ticked up last print, I don't see strong conviction for continued deceleration. This looks more like a pause/flat than a clear down move, with some upside risk given the base effect and core momentum.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.30% or core_pce_yoy resuming a clear climb above 3.0% would confirm reacceleration; a sub-0.10% print would confirm continued deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, outgoing_mom, headline_yoy_12m_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.30) was wrong: since that reading, breakeven_10y has drifted down from the 2.29-2.30 peak to 2.23, and has now printed flat at 2.23 for two consecutive observations after three straight declines. The falsifier I set - renewed negative 21d and 63d changes together - has been met (-0.15pp and -0.17pp respectively), and 252d change is also negative (-0.19pp), confirming this is not just short-term noise but a multi-horizon downtrend. The series peaked at 2.30 and has retraced meaningfully, with range_position at 0.48 (mid-range) versus a 12m high of 2.46 - suggesting the earlier reflation impulse has faded. That said, the last two prints are identical (2.23, 2.23), suggesting the decline may be stabilizing rather than accelerating further. Distance from the 2% target remains a modest +0.23pp, not extreme, and well within the 12-month range, so this isn't a runaway move either way. Given the deceleration in the drop (last two obs flat) but persistent negative momentum across all three horizons, I lean toward continued softness or flatness rather than a reversal back up. I'll call this down but with modest conviction, acknowledging the stabilization signal at the most recent print.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 2.27 sustained two+ observations, or 21d change turning positive, would signal the downtrend has reversed.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 4.20% for the third straight month, matching the 12-month high and sitting at the top of the 3.90-4.20% range (range position 1.00). My prior "flat" call held: the rate did not fall back toward 3.90-4.00%, but it also did not break out above 4.20% into a clear new trend. The 3m change (+0.20pp) and 12m change (+0.30pp) show a gradual drift higher, and the Sahm-style gap (0.30pp above the 12m low) is a real but sub-threshold signal — not yet the rapid-rise pattern that would flag a sharp loosening. Policy language still describes the labor market as "stabilized at a low level" and "solid," though the shift to "risks roughly in balance" (from a prior tilt) suggests officials see somewhat less one-sided strength than before. Three consecutive prints at the cycle high, with no reversion, is a mild tightening-off-the-lows signal rather than pure noise. I lean toward continued stickiness at this elevated level or a marginal further uptick, but the string of unchanged readings at 4.20% argues against a confident directional call for the next single print.
Would change its mind if: A print back down to 4.00% or below would confirm range-bound noise; a push to 4.30%+ would confirm genuine loosening trend.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: the 10Y has continued to decline over the last several observations, falling from 4.19 to 4.17 most recently, with 21d/63d/252d changes all negative (-0.06, -0.38, -0.53 pp). The yield now sits at 0.47 of its 12-month range, comfortably off the highs (4.79) and well above the lows (3.63), but the recent trend is clearly downward and decelerating in magnitude (the 21d change is much smaller than the 63d/252d, suggesting the pace of decline is slowing rather than reversing). The 10s30s slope at 0.49pp is modest and doesn't show the kind of aggressive steepening that would signal fresh long-end selling pressure. The policy shift — slowing the redemption cap from $25bn to $5bn — reduces the pace of balance-sheet runoff, which is a modest supportive factor for the long end (less net supply pressure from the Fed side), reinforcing rather than reversing the recent decline. Waller's dissent preferring faster runoff is a minor hawkish counterpoint but was outvoted. Overall the evidence supports continued mild downward pressure on the 10Y, though the deceleration in the pace of decline and lack of range extremes tempers conviction.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 4.30 with 10s30s slope steepening further would signal the decline has reversed and upward pressure resumed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up/tightening, 0.45 conviction) was directionally correct: NFCI has risen steadily from -0.50 to -0.38 over the 13-week window, a clear tightening trend, though the last two prints (-0.38, -0.38) show the climb stalling. The 4-week change (+0.04) is smaller than the 13-week change (+0.12), consistent with deceleration but still positive momentum, not reversal. Range position at 0.83 shows conditions are now near the top of their 52-week range, still historically very loose (distance from zero -0.38, well below the long-run average of zero) but tightening relative to its own recent history. The flattening in the last two observations is notable — my stated falsifier condition (two consecutive prints reversing the climb) has not been met since the index did not fall back, it merely plateaued. Given no new policy commentary to explain either continuation or pause, I read this as tightening momentum losing some steam but not yet reversing. I lean toward continued mild tightening or flat, with reduced conviction versus last time given the visible stall in the two most recent prints.
Would change its mind if: A print back below -0.40 or two consecutive declines would indicate easing has resumed rather than a pause in tightening.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (6,726,930) was essentially flat vs the prior observation (6,727,113), continuing a pattern of the last three readings basically flatlining (~6,727,416 / 6,727,113 / 6,726,930) after a steady multi-month decline. My prior "down" call was directionally correct over the 13-week window (assets_change_13w -104,830; -1.53%), but the pace has clearly decelerated sharply in the most recent weeks — the 4-week change is now only -13,323, much smaller than earlier run-rates implied by the 13w/52w figures. This deceleration is fully consistent with the policy change: the Treasury redemption cap was cut from $25B to $5B starting in the new period, while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B (though actual paydowns are typically below cap). With the Treasury cap now much smaller, the mechanical drain on assets should slow to a crawl, and the last three data points already show this — near-zero net change. QT continues in name (language unchanged: "will continue reducing holdings"), but the pace is now very slow, and reinvestment dynamics could even produce a flat or marginally positive month given agency MBS paydown volatility. I lean toward continued mild runoff but at a much reduced pace, bordering on flat.
Would change its mind if: A monthly print showing a clear renewed decline (>$10-15B) would confirm continued meaningful runoff; a rise would confirm flat/reversal.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread's latest print is +0.57pp, up from +0.54 previously and near the top of its 12-month range (range position 0.91, having touched +0.67 high). My prior "flat" call was wrong in direction — the spread continued to push higher off the +0.54-0.56 consolidation, confirming renewed steepening rather than a pullback into flattening. The 21d and 63d changes are identical at +0.23pp, suggesting the steepening move largely happened in the last quarter and has since been holding near highs rather than accelerating further, while the 252d change of +0.83pp shows the broader steepening trend over the past year remains intact and dominant. The Fed's announced slowing of balance-sheet runoff (redemption cap cut from $25bn to $5bn) reduces pressure on Treasury supply dynamics at the margin, which is a technical factor that historically leans toward less flattening pressure on the long end. With the spread hovering just below its 12-month high and momentum still positive, the path of least resistance is toward continued modest steepening or holding near these elevated levels, not reversal. I'll shift to a modest steepening lean rather than flat, though the plateau in short-term change (21d=63d) tempers conviction that a strong new breakout is underway.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below +0.50pp would signal the steepening stalled; a fresh close above +0.65pp would confirm a strong breakout continuation.
Leaned on: slope_range_position, slope_change_252d, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_12m_high

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print (headline_mom 0.10%, headline_yoy 2.38%) landed inside my prior "flat" band—actually at the low end, below the 0.10% threshold I said would confirm continued deceleration (it hit exactly 0.10%, borderline). My prior flat call was roughly right in that no reacceleration materialized; if anything the data leans slightly softer. Headline yoy sits at 2.38%, matching the prior month and near the 12-month low (2.33%), with yoy_range_position at just 0.08—confirming we're hugging the bottom of the recent range. Headline 3m annualized is just 1.18%, well below the 12-month trend, suggesting recent momentum is soft. Core PCE has also cooled to 2.61%, its lowest in the window, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.17pp, a modest but real deceleration. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing (+0.14pp) shows the newest month ticked up slightly from the prior (-0.04%), but the 3-month average of 0.10% is still tame. Policy language now flags "risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen"—a two-sided risk statement, not a hawkish inflation warning—while still calling inflation "somewhat elevated." Overall, the mild softening in both core and headline, plus the low 3m annualized headline pace, tilts me toward a flat-to-slightly-down call for next month, not a strong signal either way given the small bounce in the latest mom reading.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.30% or core_pce_yoy climbing back above 2.9% would confirm reacceleration; a sub-0.05% print would confirm deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was wrong: breakeven_10y has stabilized and ticked back up, closing the last observation at 2.34 vs the 10-obs starting point of 2.34 — essentially flat over the window, but with the most recent print rising from 2.32 to 2.34. The 21d change is now +0.11pp, clearly positive, which is exactly the reversal condition I set as my falsifier. The 63d change is still mildly negative (-0.04pp) and 252d is flat (+0.02pp), so this isn't a strong renewed uptrend, but the short-term momentum has clearly turned. Range position at 0.73 (within a 2.02-2.46 12m range) shows breakevens sitting well above the lows and closer to the upper half of the range, not near a floor. Distance from the 2% target remains material at +0.34pp, meaning the market continues to price inflation moderately above objective, with no sign of anchoring lower. Given the short-term reversal in the 21d metric and the uptick in the last two prints, I now lean toward a modest upward drift or at least a stabilization above recent lows, rather than continued decline. Conviction is limited given the choppy, narrow range and conflicting medium-term signals.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.32 sustained two+ observations, or 21d change turning negative again, would reassert the downtrend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.50
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.30%, a new 12-month high, up from 4.20% previously and the low of 4.00%. My prior falsifier explicitly noted that a push to 4.30%+ would confirm genuine loosening, and that condition has now been met. The unrate_range_position sits at the top of its 12-month range (1.00), unrate_distance_from_natural is now 0.30pp above the ~4.00% natural rate estimate, and the 12-month change (+0.40pp) is approaching Sahm-rule territory (sahm_gap 0.23pp, though not yet at the 0.50 trigger). Policy language has also shifted meaningfully: the Fed now explicitly flags that risks of higher unemployment have risen, a notable change from prior boilerplate about a "stabilized" low unemployment rate. This combination — a fresh cycle-high print, a widening gap from the recent low, and a hawkish-on-labor shift in Fed language — supports a genuine, if still moderate, loosening trend rather than noise. I was wrong to sit flat last time; the data has now broken out of the tight 4.00-4.20% band that justified that call. I lean toward further softening (up in unemployment rate) over the next report, though conviction is moderate since one print can still reverse and the level remains historically low.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back to 4.10% or below would suggest this was a one-off print rather than a genuine loosening trend.
Leaned on: unrate_change_12m, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, sahm_gap, unrate_above_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "down" call was wrong: dgs10 has stayed elevated and recently ticked toward the upper part of its range rather than falling. The last print (4.41) is down slightly from the 4.58 peak a few observations back, but the 21d (+0.24pp) and 63d (+0.17pp) changes are still positive, and range_position at 0.67 shows the yield sitting well above the middle of its 12-month band. The 252d change is only slightly negative (-0.05pp), so the broader trend is essentially flat-to-up, not down. Slope_30s10s (+0.51pp) and slope_10s2s (+0.52pp) show a moderately steep curve, consistent with term premium being supported rather than compressed. The policy language is notably hawkish for the long end: the Fed is slowing the taper of its balance-sheet runoff cap from $25bn to $5bn, meaning it will let fewer securities roll off — this is actually a dovish balance-sheet signal that reduces net Treasury supply pressure from the Fed's own portfolio, which could cap upward pressure somewhat. But the more dominant signal is the recent stabilization near the upper half of the range after a run-up, suggesting near-term consolidation rather than a strong renewed decline. I lean toward yields staying flat to modestly higher into month-end, not resuming a decline.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break below 4.30 with range_position falling under 0.4 and slope flattening would confirm renewed downward pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series bottomed near -0.45/-0.44 mid-sample, rose to -0.38 (tightening pause reversal), but has since reversed again: -0.38, -0.39, -0.40, -0.42, -0.44, -0.45 — four consecutive declines and a fresh print back below -0.40. My prior "up" call was wrong: the mid-series uptick proved temporary, and conditions have resumed easing, now sitting at -0.45, matching the recent low and near the 52w low of -0.51. The 4-week change is -0.06 (easing), even though the 13-week change is still slightly positive (+0.04), reflecting the fact that the early tightening phase is now being unwound. Range position of 0.36 confirms the index is in the looser half of its 52-week range. Momentum clearly favors continued easing into month-end: four straight declines is a strong, unambiguous signal, not a pause. Distance from zero (-0.45) shows conditions remain looser than average, and the recent trajectory suggests this gap is widening again rather than closing. I see no offsetting information suggesting a turn back toward tightening.
Would change its mind if: A print back above -0.40 or two consecutive increases would signal tightening has resumed rather than easing continuing.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (6,673,244) continues the runoff trend: assets fell ~$15.5B from the prior observation and are down $36B over 4 weeks, $92.9B over 13 weeks, and $611B over the past year. This confirms my prior "down" call was correct — the >$10-15B decline threshold I set was met. However, the policy language shows a material regime change: effective the coming month, the Treasury redemption cap drops from $25B to $5B (an 80% reduction in the Treasury runoff pace), while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B. This slows the maximum pace of balance-sheet runoff substantially going forward, even though the trend remains QT (not reversal to QE). Waller's dissent (preferring the faster prior pace) confirms the cap cut is real and binding, not just optionality. Given the sharply reduced cap, the month-ahead decline should be much smaller than the trailing 4-week pace suggests — plausibly in the single-digit-to-low-teens billions range rather than continuing at the prior ~$15-36B/4wk pace. I still expect further decline (direction down) but with a much slower pace, and conviction is moderate given the transition and possible operational lags before the new cap fully takes hold.
Would change its mind if: A monthly print showing assets flat or rising would signal the new $5B cap plus reinvestment flows have halted runoff; a decline >$15-20B would suggest the cap cut isn't yet binding.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print ticked up to +0.52pp from a flat +0.51pp plateau over the prior three readings, following a dip from the +0.58pp peak. My prior "up" call at 0.40 conviction is roughly validated in direction but the move has been essentially sideways for four straight observations (0.51/0.51/0.51/0.52) rather than a clean continuation of steepening — the 21d change is actually slightly negative (-0.05pp), even though 63d (+0.27pp) and 252d (+0.99pp) confirm the broader steepening trend is intact. The spread sits high in its 12-month range (0.87 of 0-1), well off the -0.47pp low, so most of the steepening move has already happened and is now consolidating near range highs rather than breaking out. Neither of my stated triggers fired: no drop below +0.50pp, no close above +0.65pp. The 10s30s slope at +0.51pp shows the long end is not inverting further, consistent with a stable-to-mild-steepening backdrop rather than acceleration. With the curve stalling just under its 12-month high and no fresh catalyst in the policy language specific to duration supply/demand, I lean toward continued modest steepening bias but with reduced conviction given the stall.
Would change its mind if: A close below +0.50pp would signal the stall is breaking down; a fresh close above +0.65pp would confirm renewed steepening breakout.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Clarify net effect of slower runoff pace on net Treasury supply/demand dynamics at the long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment could signal flight-to-quality flows affecting long-end demand.
  curve_slope lacks term_premium — Rising term premium would explain and reinforce whether long-end steepening has further room to run.
  curve_slope lacks balance_sheet — QT taper pace changes duration supply dynamics that directly affect 2s10s slope.

arbitrated.notes: Inflation is genuinely quiet: headline and core both near cycle lows, 3m annualized soft, and Fed language has become two-sided rather than hawkish on prices — a mild "flat, slight easing bias" read. Inflation expectations show a short-term wobble higher (21d reversal) but medium/long-term measures (63d, 252d) are flat-to-slightly-down and breakevens remain range-bound rather than trending — I read this as noise around a flat level, not a genuine turn. Balance sheet continues to shrink but the sharp cut in the Treasury redemption cap (25bn to 5bn) means the pace of decline should slow materially even though direction stays down — this is a supply-relevant regime change that term_premium and curve_slope weren't given, so I temper their independent "flat/up" calls slightly, since a slower QT taper is a source of reduced term-premium and steepening pressure that neither analyst incorporated. Term premium's own analyst leans flat-to-up given elevated 21d/63d yield momentum and steep curve, but given the offsetting dovish balance-sheet signal they themselves flagged, I shade this down toward closer-to-flat. Curve slope's steepening is stalling near range highs with negative 21d momentum; combined with slower net supply reduction, I keep the up-bias but reduce conviction further than the analyst's own number.

Given the curve is still well off its lows and has not broken any range boundary, but the most recent tick is down and momentum has clearly faded, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down rather than continued steepening. arbitrated.notes:
Seven independent reads, none showing high conviction, but a coherent macro picture emerges. Inflation: y/y jumped mostly on base effects (3m annualized momentum flat at 2.08%, mom_gap zero), core PCE choppy — mild upward lean but not confirmed reacceleration. Inflation expectations have stalled just under 2.30 after a peak, with negative 21d/63d momentum offsetting a positive 252d trend — genuinely flat, prior "up" call not validated. Labor market is range-bound noise (4.00-4.30% band for a year, Sahm gap well below threshold) — no directional signal, despite a mild policy-language caution on unemployment risk. Term premium (10y yields) is drifting lower with real 21d momentum, though not yet a confirmed break; balance-sheet runoff is ongoing but apparently not the dominant driver of the yield decline, so this looks like duration demand/softer growth expectations pulling yields down. Financial conditions are easing steadily and monotonically, near 52-week loosest levels, high-conviction call. Balance sheet continues its programmatic runoff, unchanged policy language, straightforward continuation. Curve slope has stalled after a strong steepening run and ticked down last print — leaning toward flat-to-mild-flattening rather than fresh steepening, though not a breakdown yet.

Put together: financial conditions easing while term premium/yields fall and QT continues creates a slightly unusual mix — the easing in NFCI is likely driven by equity/credit-spread tightening rather than yields, since yields falling would show up as easing too but balance-sheet drain argues against pure liquidity-driven easing. Inflation and labor readings are both essentially inconclusive/range-bound, so I keep those muted. The most confident calls are financial conditions down and balance sheet down (mechanical, trend-confirmed); term premium down with moderate conviction; curve slope mildly negative on stalled momentum; inflation and inflation expectations get small positive/flat tilts; labor gets no view given true two-way chop.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline_cpi_yoy rose to 2.74% from 2.68%, and headline_mom printed 0.23%, within my stated "flat-ish" range (not above 0.30 nor below 0.10), so the prior "up" call at 0.35 conviction was directionally correct but the underlying momentum was moderate rather than a clear reacceleration signal. Core PCE also ticked up to 2.81% from 2.78%, though it remains below several recent readings (2.97, 2.99) earlier in the window, suggesting momentum is not building forcefully in either direction.
  
  Looking at the full picture: headline_yoy_change_3m is +0.42pp, showing recent upward drift from the 2.33% low, and yoy_range_position at 0.63 confirms we're in the upper half of the 12-month range. However, headline_3m_annualized (2.35%) is actually below the current yoy print (2.74%), which points to deceleration in the trend rather than acceleration. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing of only +0.04pp is negligible. Core_3m_annualized (2.75%) also sits below core_pce_yoy (2.81%), reinforcing that the trailing 3-month pace is cooler than the year-over-year figure — a sign the yoy uptick partly reflects base effects rather than fresh momentum. Policy language shift acknowledging inflation risk without characterizing it as accelerating supports a modest, cautious read. Given the mixed signal — yoy drifting up but 3m annualized pointing down — I lean flat with slight upward bias given the two consecutive yoy increases.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.30% would confirm reacceleration; a sub-0.10% print or renewed drop in core_pce_yoy below 2.6% would confirm deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release delivered headline m/m of 0.35%, above my stated 0.30% reacceleration threshold, and headline yoy rose to 2.94% from 2.68%, near the top of the 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.92). My prior "flat" call was wrong on the momentum signal — this is a fresh, clear acceleration print, not noise. Core PCE also ticked up to 2.86% from 2.81%, though core's 3m annualized (2.99%) is more muted than headline's (3.37%), and core_yoy_change_3m (+0.25pp) is smaller than headline's (+0.56pp). This divergence suggests some of the recent headline push is coming from food/energy volatility rather than pure underlying persistence, but core is still not decelerating — it's drifting sideways-to-up near 2.8-2.9%, well above target (distance +0.86pp). Given headline sits at a 12-month high with mom_gap_vs_outgoing positive and the 3-month annualized pace clearly outrunning the 12-month trend, the momentum evidence favors another firm or higher print next month rather than a reversal. I weight the headline momentum surprise heavily here since it directly missed my own threshold last time, while treating core's smaller rise as corroborating (not driving) the call.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print back below ~0.15% or core_pce_yoy dropping below 2.7% would signal the acceleration stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, yoy_range_position, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My previous "down" call at 0.40 conviction was wrong: the series printed 2.41 twice near the end but before that touched 2.43, and the broader trend since has held near multi-month highs rather than reversing. Looking at the last 10 prints, breakeven_10y rose from 2.38 to a peak of 2.43 before settling to 2.41-2.41, essentially flat over the most recent two readings. The 21d change (+0.02) and 63d change (+0.07) are both still positive, and the 252d change (+0.26) confirms a sustained upward drift over the past year. The series sits at be_range_position 0.89 — near the top of its 12-month range (2.02-2.46) — and is +0.41pp above the 2% target, a meaningful and persistent overshoot in market-implied inflation expectations. The last two observations flattening at 2.41 could be early stabilization, but with the level still elevated and no genuine reversal (no break below prior support, no multi-observation decline), I don't see convincing evidence of a down-trend. Instead this looks like a plateau near cycle highs after a longer uptrend — flat-to-slightly-up is more consistent with the data than continued decline. I'm revising away from "down" given the previous call's failure to materialize.
Would change its mind if: A clear multi-observation break below 2.38 (prior range) or renewed negative 21d/63d change would confirm a genuine downturn rather than plateau.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate sits at 4.30%, matching the prior print and tying the 12-month high, after a choppy range of 4.00-4.30% over the past year. The last observation shows no further increase (4.30 to 4.30), so my prior "flat" call was directionally correct in that no new high was made and there was no two-consecutive-increase confirmation. The 3-month change is exactly 0.00pp, and the series has been oscillating rather than trending — 4.10/4.30/4.10/4.30 in the last four readings looks like noise around a slowly rising centerline rather than a clean breakout. That said, the level is now sitting at the top of its 12-month range (range_position = 1.00), 0.3pp above both the 12-month low and the natural-rate anchor near 4%, and the 6m/12m changes are modestly positive. This is consistent with a labor market that has stopped improving and is drifting sideways-to-slightly-looser, but has not shown the rapid acceleration that would signal a Sahm-rule-style regime shift. Policy language is unchanged and still characterizes conditions as "solid," offering no independent confirmation of deterioration. I lean toward flat with a slight tilt toward up given the range position sitting at its ceiling, but conviction remains low given the choppiness.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.30% (new 12m high) or two consecutive month-over-month increases would confirm a genuine loosening trend rather than range-bound noise.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_high, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield has drifted down to 4.23 from 4.34 ten observations ago, with 21d change -0.14pp and 63d change -0.18pp both negative, confirming a recent downward drift rather than the modest upward lean I called last time. My stated falsifier has essentially triggered: yield is below 4.35, range_position sits at 0.52 (barely above midpoint, no longer clearly elevated), and 21d change is negative. The longer 252d change (+0.41pp) shows the broader trend over the past year is still up, so this isn't a sharp reversal, but the near-term momentum has clearly shifted lower. The 10s30s slope (+0.69pp) remains steep, consistent with ongoing balance-sheet runoff pressure on the long end, which argues against a large further decline. On net, the level is near the middle of its 12-month range, momentum is modestly negative, and there's no fresh policy language suggesting either acceleration of runoff or flight-to-quality. I was likely wrong last time calling 'up' with the drift now clearly negative over both 21d and 63d windows. Given the balance between persistent supply pressure (steepening slope) and the recent downward drift, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for month end, with modest conviction given the mixed signals.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 4.30 with 21d change turning positive and range_position rising back above 0.55 would signal renewed upward pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has flattened at the bottom of its recent range: the last four readings are all -0.52, with the 4-week change essentially zero (-0.01) and the 13-week change modest (-0.07). The prior easing trend (steadily falling index) has stalled — the series shows no further declines in the last four prints, sitting exactly at the 52-week low with a range position of 0.00. My previous call was for continued easing (down), but the last several observations show that trend has stopped rather than reversed or continued. There's no evidence of tightening (no increases), but there's also no evidence of further easing momentum — the index is pinned flat. Distance from zero remains meaningfully negative (-0.52), so conditions are still looser than average, but the momentum that would justify a directional call for the next month is absent. Absent any policy language addressing this driver, and with the measurement series showing a clear plateau, the most defensible call is flat: conditions are holding at a loose level rather than easing further or tightening. My prior 'down' call was not wrong in direction over the 13-week window but has clearly run out of momentum in the most recent weeks, which is what matters for the one-month-ahead horizon now.
Would change its mind if: A print below -0.52 (new 52w low) would signal renewed easing; a print above -0.50 would signal tightening resuming.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print confirms the prior call: total assets fell again, from 6,618,415 to 6,603,384 ($M), a drop of ~$15.0B in the most recent week and continuing the steady QT trend. The 4-week change is -39.2B and 13-week change is -69.9B, both consistent with ongoing balance-sheet runoff at a pace roughly in line with (or slightly below) the announced caps — no sign of acceleration beyond the ~$25-30B/month threshold I flagged, and no sign of a pause. The series has declined in 9 of the last 12 observations, with only minor upticks, and is now $511.6B below its 52-week high with a persistent negative slope. Policy language is unchanged, reaffirming continued runoff of Treasury and agency holdings with no reinvestment. There is no indication of a pause, taper announcement, or reversal in either the measurements or the stated policy. Given the consistency of the multi-week decline and unchanged guidance, I expect assets to continue falling into month-end, likely by another $15-40B, keeping the trend intact.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print showing assets flat or rising, or a policy statement announcing a pause/taper in runoff, would reverse this call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down/flattening, conviction 0.45) was wrong: the spread has instead steepened, rising from the high-0.50s to 0.64pp, with the last two prints at 0.60 and 0.64. The 21d change (+0.21pp) and 63d change (+0.12pp) both confirm a steepening trend that has accelerated recently, not stalled. The spread now sits at 0.96 of its 12-month range (near the 12m high of 0.67pp), well above the 12m low of -0.04pp, meaning the curve has moved decisively away from inversion toward positive slope. The 10s30s context (+0.69pp) shows the long end also steep, consistent with broad-based steepening rather than a front-end-only move. Policy language shows a dissent (Waller) favoring a 25bp cut, which if anything pressures the front end lower given expectations of easing, supportive of continued steepening as short rates would fall relative to long rates. Given the strong, multi-horizon consistent steepening signal and the spread pushing to a fresh range high, I'm reversing my prior call. The trend has clear momentum, though a move this close to the 12-month high raises some risk of near-term consolidation or pause.
Would change its mind if: A close back below 0.55pp would signal the steepening has stalled; a break below 0.45pp would suggest renewed flattening pressure.

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/size of ongoing runoff would clarify supply pressure on long end going forward.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment would clarify if recent yield decline reflects flight-to-quality.

arbitrated.notes: Inflation is the clearest signal on the panel: headline CPI m/m and yoy both broke to fresh 12-month highs, with core PCE drifting up too, even if core's momentum is milder than headline's. This is a fresh, well-evidenced acceleration call, not a stale prior. Inflation expectations have plateaued at cycle-highs (be_range_position 0.89) after a genuine multi-month uptrend; the analyst's revision from "down" to "flat" is reasonable but the elevated level and positive 63d/252d changes argue the risk is still tilted slightly higher rather than truly neutral — I nudge this modestly positive rather than flat. Labor tightness is genuinely rangebound/noisy (unrate 4.10/4.30 oscillation), but sitting at the top of its 12-month range with no confirmed reversal — I give it a small positive tilt (loosening = unemployment slightly higher) rather than zero, since the conviction offered is low and directionless. Term premium: the 10y has clear negative 21d/63d momentum and the analyst's own falsifier triggered, so I follow this down, tempered by steep 10s30s slope arguing against a large further decline. Financial conditions are pinned flat at the loose end of the range — no momentum either way, take flat at face value. Balance sheet is the highest-conviction, cleanest trend on the panel: multi-week uninterrupted decline, unchanged policy, no signs of pause — follow down with high conviction. Curve slope shows a genuine, multi-horizon steepening reversal from the prior flattening call, corroborated by 10s30s and a dovish dissent — follow up, tempered slightly for proximity to 12m highs raising consolidation risk. Overall this is a fairly high-quality, fresh-data-driven panel with few contradictions; the main tension is inflation firming while term premium eases, which is plausible if growth/labor softening offsets inflation risk in the front end.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is validated: headline CPI y/y rose from 2.94 to 3.02, the 12-month high, and sits at the top of the observed range (yoy_range_position = 1.00). Momentum remains firm—outgoing m/m of 0.30% is right at the 3-month average (0.29%), so no deceleration signal like the sub-0.15% print I flagged as a falsifier. Headline 3m annualized (3.54%) is running well above the y/y rate, implying further upward pressure in the pipeline. Core PCE y/y also ticked up to 2.91 from 2.86, staying above my 2.7% falsifier threshold and near its own local highs, though core's 3m annualized (2.97%) is more moderate and core-minus-headline is now slightly negative (-0.11pp), suggesting energy/food are adding a modest boost to headline versus core. I weight core PCE for persistence but note it hasn't diverged meaningfully from headline's story here—both are drifting up, not down. With m/m prints holding near 0.30% for two straight months and no cooling signal, the acceleration is intact rather than stalling. Level remains ~1pp above target, momentum is modestly positive. I maintain an "up" call for the next headline print, similar conviction, since neither falsifier condition triggered and the trend continued.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below ~0.15%, or core_pce_yoy falling below 2.7%, would signal the acceleration has stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, yoy_range_position, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, core_minus_headline

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call was flat with modest conviction, contingent on watching for a break below 2.38 or renewed negative short-term change. That break has now happened: the series slipped to 2.35 then ticked to 2.36, with 21d change now -0.05pp, confirming the downside move I flagged as the falsifier. That said, the longer lookbacks (63d +0.07, 252d +0.18) remain positive, and the series still sits well above its 12m low (2.15) and in the upper part of its 12m range (range_position 0.68), 0.36pp above the 2% target. So this looks like a small pullback within a still-elevated range rather than a trend reversal — the magnitude is only 2-3bp over the last two prints, within noise for breakevens. Given the 21d signal that was my explicit falsifier has now triggered, I should shift off flat, but the small size of the move and lack of confirmation from medium-term momentum argues against a strong directional call. I'll lean modestly down for the next month, expecting the recent softening to persist marginally rather than snap back, while acknowledging this could easily prove to be noise around a plateau.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back to 2.38+ over the next few observations, or renewed positive 21d change, would indicate the dip was noise, not a trend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target, breakeven_10y

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.40%, a new 12-month high, up from 4.30% the prior two months. This confirms the falsifier I set previously: a print above 4.30% plus what is effectively a run of increases (4.10→4.30→4.30→4.40) rather than range-bound noise. The 3-month change is now +0.30pp and the Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low of 4.00%) stands at +0.33pp — not yet at the classic 0.50 recession-signal threshold, but moving in that direction and accelerating rather than stabilizing. Range position is at 1.00 (sitting at the top of its own 12-month range), and distance from a ~4.0% natural rate is now 0.40pp, the widest of the sample. The 6-month change of only +0.20pp shows this isn't a sudden shock, but the trend within the last three prints is clearly upward and monotonic. My prior flat call was wrong — this is no longer noise around a stable level; it's a genuine, if modest, loosening trend. I don't have enough data yet to call it a sharp deterioration (still under Sahm-rule trigger), so I'll lean up with moderate-high conviction rather than extreme.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.20% would indicate this was noise, not a trend, and restore a flat/range-bound read.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has been essentially flat-to-slightly-up over the last 10 readings, oscillating in a narrow 4.06-4.20 band and closing near 4.16, roughly mid-pack (range_position 0.40) of its 12-month range. My prior "down" call was only partially validated: dgs10_change_21d (-0.07) and dgs10_change_63d (-0.10) are modestly negative, consistent with mild downward drift, but the longer 252d change (+0.37) shows the dominant trend this past year has been upward, and the last two prints (4.15, 4.16) show stabilization rather than continued decline. The 10s30s slope remains positive at +0.57pp, indicating the long end continues to cheapen relative to the belly, consistent with ongoing balance-sheet runoff pressure keeping term premium elevated rather than compressing. Continued QT withdrawal of duration supply-absorption capacity is a structural upward force on the long end that hasn't reversed. Given the flat recent trend, a positive but unremarkable slope, and mid-range positioning, I don't see strong evidence for either a resumption of the decline or a breakout higher. The move has stalled near the top third of its recent range with no clear catalyst either way, so I'm shifting toward flat with low conviction, having been too confident in "down" last time.
Would change its mind if: A break above 4.30 with 21d/63d changes turning clearly positive and range_position above 0.55 would signal renewed upward pressure; a drop below 4.00 would confirm renewed decline.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has been pinned at -0.52 for the last six readings, having drifted down modestly from -0.49 thirteen weeks ago. The 4-week change is essentially zero and the index sits at the very bottom of its 52-week range (range_position 0.01, at the 52w low). This is a picture of conditions that eased over the prior quarter but have since fully stabilized at that looser-than-average level, with no fresh momentum in either direction. My prior flat call was correct: the last several prints came in exactly at -0.52, not below it and not above -0.50, so neither of my stated triggers fired. There is no policy language pointing to a shift. Given the flatline persistence over six consecutive observations, the most probable outcome at next month-end is another reading at or very near -0.52 — a continuation of loose-but-static conditions rather than a resumption of tightening or a new leg of easing. I see no evidence of building pressure in either direction, so I maintain a flat call, with slightly higher conviction than before given the extended run of unchanged prints reinforcing the stability read.
Would change its mind if: A print below -0.52 (new 52w low) would signal renewed easing; a print above -0.50 would signal tightening resuming.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last four weekly prints (6,602,071 → 6,605,962 → 6,608,597 → 6,608,395) show total assets have stabilized and modestly risen, with assets_change_4w now +5,011M, reversing the prior downtrend. This directly contradicts my prior "down" call at 0.60 conviction — the weekly data has printed flat-to-rising for a month, which was my stated falsifier. The longer-window figures (13w change -53,805M, 52w change -471,664M) still show the cumulative effect of QT, and the policy language is unchanged, reaffirming the Committee's stated intent to continue reducing holdings. But the graded measurement is the near-term trajectory, and pace matters more than level per my mandate. Four consecutive weeks of flat-to-slightly-up prints, with the most recent two essentially flat (6,608,597 → 6,608,395, a trivial -202M), suggest either a pause, technical rebound, or a slowing/ending of runoff pace not yet reflected in language. Given policy language hasn't changed to confirm a formal pause, I can't call this confidently "up," but the "down" call is clearly wrong now. This looks like a flattening plateau — I'll call flat with modest conviction, watching for confirmation via either continued upticks or a resumption of decline.
Would change its mind if: A clear resumption of weekly declines over 2-3 consecutive prints, or explicit policy language reaffirming full-pace runoff continuation, would restore a "down" call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (0.56) rose from 0.52 but remains below the prior 0.57-0.59 cluster, so my prior "up" call is only partly validated — the spread has been chopping in a 0.52-0.59 band for the last several readings rather than cleanly extending its steepening trend. The 21-day change is now slightly negative (-0.08pp) even as the 63-day and 252-day changes remain positive (+0.08, +0.30pp), indicating the steepening trend over the past year is intact but has stalled/plateaued over the most recent month. Range position at 0.83 (near the 12-month high of 0.67) shows the curve is historically steep for this cycle, which can cap further near-term steepening as it approaches resistance. The Fed cutting the funds rate (front-end anchor lower) is typically consistent with continued steepening pressure at the margin, and Miran's dissent for a larger cut reinforces a dovish tilt that would pull front-end yields down relative to the long end. On balance, I lean toward continued modest steepening but with less confidence than before given the recent chop and proximity to range highs — this looks more like consolidation before the next leg than a stalled trend, but a close below 0.55 would question that.
Would change its mind if: A close back below 0.50pp would signal the steepening trend has broken down; a push above 0.65pp would confirm renewed clear steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high

arbitrated.notes: The panel paints a labor market that is clearly loosening (unemployment at a 12-month high, monotonic 3-month rise) alongside inflation that remains sticky-to-firm (headline CPI at a 12-month high, core PCE ticking up, no deceleration signal), a combination that is unusual but consistent with a supply-side/stagflation-adjacent tilt rather than clean disinflation. Inflation expectations have broken modestly below their recent floor but the move is tiny (2-3bp) and medium/long-term momentum is still positive, so I read this as a soft, low-conviction dip rather than a genuine anchoring shift — it sits oddly next to the inflation analyst's firm "up" call, and I weight the realized-inflation signal more heavily since expectations often lag realized surprises. On rates, term premium and curve slope both show stalled, plateauing trends after a longer up-move: 10y is mid-range and flat over 10 readings, while 2s10s steepening has stalled over 21 days even as 63d/252d trends stay positive. I read both as consolidation, not reversal, with curve slope retaining a mild upward lean because the labor-loosening/dovish-cut narrative should keep front-end anchored lower relative to the long end. Financial conditions are genuinely flat, pinned at a 52-week low for six straight prints with zero momentum — high confidence flat call. Balance sheet has stopped shrinking on a weekly basis (a clear reversal of the prior "down" call) but longer-window measures and unchanged policy language argue against calling it "up" outright; I keep it flat but acknowledge the near-term data no longer supports continued runoff deceleration in the rates-pressure sense. Overall: firmer inflation and labor tightness are the highest-conviction reads; term premium, curve slope, and balance sheet are muted/consolidating; financial conditions are the cleanest flat call; inflation expectations get a small downward lean I don't fully trust.

discounted.why: Move is within noise (2-3bp) and contradicted by positive medium/long-term breakeven momentum.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 46 days ago; STALE — no fresher read is available.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is validated: headline CPI y/y rose from 2.94 to 3.02, the 12-month high, and sits at the top of the observed range (yoy_range_position = 1.00). Momentum remains firm—outgoing m/m of 0.30% is right at the 3-month average (0.29%), so no deceleration signal like the sub-0.15% print I flagged as a falsifier. Headline 3m annualized (3.54%) is running well above the y/y rate, implying further upward pressure in the pipeline. Core PCE y/y also ticked up to 2.91 from 2.86, staying above my 2.7% falsifier threshold and near its own local highs, though core's 3m annualized (2.97%) is more moderate and core-minus-headline is now slightly negative (-0.11pp), suggesting energy/food are adding a modest boost to headline versus core. I weight core PCE for persistence but note it hasn't diverged meaningfully from headline's story here—both are drifting up, not down. With m/m prints holding near 0.30% for two straight months and no cooling signal, the acceleration is intact rather than stalling. Level remains ~1pp above target, momentum is modestly positive. I maintain an "up" call for the next headline print, similar conviction, since neither falsifier condition triggered and the trend continued.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below ~0.15%, or core_pce_yoy falling below 2.7%, would signal the acceleration has stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, yoy_range_position, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, core_minus_headline

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call has not materialized: breakeven_10y rose to 2.30 in the latest print, the top of the recent 2.26-2.30 range, with the series showing a modest zigzag rather than a sustained decline. The 21d change is still slightly negative (-0.05pp) but the 63d change has nearly flattened (-0.03pp) and the 252d change is now positive (+0.01pp), suggesting the earlier dip was noise rather than a trend, consistent with my stated falsifier condition (positive 21d change reappearing, or a rebound). Range position sits at 0.45, mid-range within the 12-month band (2.17-2.46), showing no clear directional break in either direction. Breakevens remain 0.30pp above the 2% target, a modest but persistent premium that has been stable for months. Given the flattening of the 63d and 252d trends and the bounce to the top of the recent range, I no longer see convincing evidence for continued decline. The series looks more consolidative than trending, oscillating in a tight 4bp band for the last several readings. I'm shifting to a flat call, since neither the up nor down case is compelling from the last ten observations — this looks like noise around a stable level rather than a fresh directional move.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 2.35 or below 2.24 sustained over multiple readings would indicate a genuine directional move rather than range-bound noise.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 53 days ago; STALE — no fresher read is available.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.40%, a new 12-month high, up from 4.30% the prior two months. This confirms the falsifier I set previously: a print above 4.30% plus what is effectively a run of increases (4.10→4.30→4.30→4.40) rather than range-bound noise. The 3-month change is now +0.30pp and the Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low of 4.00%) stands at +0.33pp — not yet at the classic 0.50 recession-signal threshold, but moving in that direction and accelerating rather than stabilizing. Range position is at 1.00 (sitting at the top of its own 12-month range), and distance from a ~4.0% natural rate is now 0.40pp, the widest of the sample. The 6-month change of only +0.20pp shows this isn't a sudden shock, but the trend within the last three prints is clearly upward and monotonic. My prior flat call was wrong — this is no longer noise around a stable level; it's a genuine, if modest, loosening trend. I don't have enough data yet to call it a sharp deterioration (still under Sahm-rule trigger), so I'll lean up with moderate-high conviction rather than extreme.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.20% would indicate this was noise, not a trend, and restore a flat/range-bound read.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year has ticked up over the last three prints (3.99→4.08→4.11→4.11), pushing the level back to the top of its recent micro-range, though it remains low within the 12-month range (range_position 0.17, only 0.14pp above the 12m low of 3.97). The 21d change is essentially flat (-0.01pp) while 63d and 252d changes are still modestly negative, so the medium-term trend remains one of declining or stable term premium, with only a short-term uptick. The 10s30s slope (0.56pp) and 10s2s slope (0.51pp) are moderate and not signaling acute long-end stress. Notably, policy language shifted to announce the conclusion of balance-sheet runoff (ending QT), which removes a structural source of upward pressure on long-end supply/term premium that had been cited previously. This is a mild negative for term premium going forward, offsetting the recent 3-day uptick. My prior flat call was roughly right — the level barely moved on net (3.98→4.11 is only +0.13pp over the window, mostly in the last two prints) and the range position stayed low. Given the QT-ending signal and still-low range position, I lean slightly toward flat-to-down rather than continued rise, though the last two prints show some tick-up momentum worth respecting.
Would change its mind if: A break above 4.30 with 21d/63d turning clearly positive and range_position above 0.55 would confirm renewed upward pressure; a drop below 4.00 confirms decline.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has been pinned at -0.52 for eleven straight readings before a tiny uptick to -0.51 in the latest observation. This is a 0.01 index-point move, essentially noise, and matches my prior flat call - the small print above -0.50 threshold I set was not breached. The index remains deeply below the zero average (distance_from_zero -0.51), sitting near the bottom of its 52-week range (range_position 0.08), confirming financial conditions are much looser than historical average. Both the 4-week and 13-week changes are near-zero, showing no real momentum in either direction. The 52w low equals the most recent prior value, and the new print is barely above it, not a meaningful break. There's no policy language this cycle to suggest a shift in conditions. Given the extreme flatness of this series historically and the marginal nature of the latest tick, I see no basis for calling a directional turn. The single basis-point move is too small to distinguish from measurement noise given a series that had been literally flat for eleven periods. I maintain a flat call, with slightly higher conviction than before since the extended plateau reinforces the case for continued stability absent any new catalyst.
Would change its mind if: A print below -0.52 (new 52w low) signals renewed easing; a print above -0.48 or sustained move above -0.50 signals tightening resuming.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_13w_avg

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 13 weekly prints show total assets essentially flat over the most recent month: -85 $M over 4 weeks, versus -55,544 $M over 13 weeks and -426,456 $M over 52 weeks. That confirms the pace of runoff has decelerated sharply into the latest observations — the last few prints (6590815, 6596454, 6589533, 6587034) are oscillating in a narrow band with no clear trend, unlike the steady declines earlier in the window. My prior "flat" call was directionally correct: the 4-week change was near zero, consistent with a stalling runoff.
  
  The policy language shift is decisive corroborating evidence: the Committee has moved from "will continue reducing its holdings" to explicitly deciding to "conclude the reduction of its aggregate securities holdings." This is an explicit announcement ending QT, which per my mandate is the clearest possible "why" signal — it means the announced monthly cap-driven runoff is being switched off. Combined with the near-zero 4-week change already observed, this strongly supports a flat-to-slightly-up balance sheet over the next month, as no further scheduled shrinkage will occur and any residual maturities/reinvestment could roughly stabilize the total.
  
  I'm calling flat with higher conviction than before, since the policy language now explicitly confirms cessation rather than just ambiguous deceleration.
Would change its mind if: Resumption of consistent weekly declines post-announcement, or reversal of the "conclude reduction" language back to active runoff, would flip this to down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call (0.35 conviction) was roughly right but choppy: slope ticked from 0.49 to 0.51 over the last two prints after dipping to 0.49, so we're back near mid-range, not confirming a clean breakout. The last 10 readings show a saw-tooth pattern (0.54→0.52 dip→0.51 recover) rather than a decisive trend in either direction. Longer-term context (slope_change_252d +0.35pp) confirms the multi-month steepening from deeply inverted levels is intact, and slope_range_position at 0.75 shows we're in the upper part of the 12-month range, well off the 0.02pp low. But short-term momentum is soft: slope_change_21d -0.06pp and slope_change_63d -0.03pp both negative, meaning the last month or so has actually flattened slightly even as the latest print ticked up. The Fed's second consecutive 25bp cut with a dissent for larger cuts suggests continued front-end anchoring/easing bias, which is generally supportive of continued steepening pressure on the curve over time, but the immediate print-to-print evidence is mixed rather than a clear renewed steepening signal. Given the two most recent observations recovered off the 0.49 low without yet reclaiming the 0.54 highs, I lean modestly toward a mild continuation of steepening but with reduced confidence given the choppiness.
Would change its mind if: A close back below 0.49pp would signal renewed flattening; a clean push above 0.55pp would confirm steepening resumption.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Confirm pace/timing of QT conclusion and any reinvestment shift affecting long-end supply.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment could clarify if recent uptick reflects flight-from-quality or risk-on flows.
