arbitrated.notes: Inflation is the clearest signal: headline CPI and core PCE both grinding to fresh highs, momentum intact, no cooling signature — but the read is 46 days stale, so I keep the "up" call but temper conviction for staleness risk. Labor tightness also shows a genuine, monotonic three-print climb to a 12-month high in unemployment, corroborated by Sahm-gap widening (though not yet at recession trigger) — again stale by 53 days, so I trim conviction slightly versus the analyst's own 0.55. Inflation expectations look like range-bound noise around a stable premium above target — breakevens bounced off a shallow dip rather than confirming a downtrend; I'll take a very slight lean up given the highest print and flattening 63d/252d changes, essentially a soft version of the analyst's flat call. Term premium and curve slope are both genuinely mixed: 10y ticked up on the surface but medium-term trend and low range-position argue against a real breakout, and the QT-ending announcement removes a supply-side upward driver — I keep this close to flat with a small downward lean given the structural offset. Curve slope shows steepening intact on a multi-month view but choppy month-to-month; I keep a modest up lean, discounting the analyst's own conviction slightly for the sawtooth pattern. Financial conditions are pinned at an extreme, stable, loose level with a one-tick move that is pure noise — flat, no directional lean. Balance sheet: the explicit "conclude QT" policy language plus already-flat 4-week change is decisive — I take a firm flat call, possibly leaning marginally up given no further scheduled shrinkage. The link the panel flagged between balance sheet cessation and term premium reinforces my slightly-negative term premium lean rather than the analyst's plain flat.

discounted.why: Range-bound noise around stable premium; no strong directional basis beyond mild uptick.
Series pinned flat for eleven readings; single bp move is noise, no view warranted.

arbitrated.falsifier: A fresh CPI/jobs print reversing the stale trends (sub-0.15% CPI m/m or unemployment back to 4.20%), or a 10y break above 4.30 with positive 21d/63d momentum, would flip this read.

risks.text: Inflation and labor calls are 46-53 days old; newer data could already have reversed the trend.
QT conclusion is a fresh regime change whose full effect on term premium/supply dynamics is unconfirmed by data yet.
Curve slope and term premium readings are choppy/sawtooth, raising risk that any directional lean is overfitting short-term noise.

why.inflation: Headline and core both at highs, momentum firm, but call is 46 days stale — trim from 0.55 for freshness risk.
why.inflation_expectations: Bounce to top of range and flattening 63d/252d changes argue against decline; slight lean up over flat.
why.labor_tightness: Three-print monotonic rise to 12m high, Sahm gap widening, but 53-day-stale read tempers conviction.
why.term_premium: Low range position and QT-ending removes structural upward pressure; recent uptick likely transient, lean down.
why.financial_conditions: Eleven flat prints plus a 1bp tick is pure noise; no directional basis.
why.balance_sheet: Explicit QT-conclusion language plus near-zero 4wk change; flat with a marginal upward lean as runoff stops.
why.curve_slope: Multi-month steepening intact but recent prints choppy/sawtooth; keep modest up lean, shaded from analyst's 0.30.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call played out: headline CPI rose from 2.94 to 2.99% y/y, near the top of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.95), with the latest m/m print (0.25%) still above my stated stall threshold of 0.15%. Headline 3m-annualized momentum (3.64%) remains well above target and above the 12-month trend, confirming headline pressure has not faded. However, core PCE tells a different, softer story: it has drifted down from 2.91 to 2.75% y/y over the last three prints, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.11pp and core_3m_annualized at just 2.60%, close to target. Core-minus-headline is now -0.23pp, meaning headline strength is being driven by less persistent components (likely energy/food), while the stickier core measure is decelerating. Given my mandate to weight core for persistence, this is a genuine divergence: headline momentum argues for another firm print, but underlying trend argues acceleration is stalling. Net effect: headline is likely to hold near current elevated levels or tick up marginally next release given m/m momentum, but I lower conviction versus last time since core evidence now cuts against sustained acceleration.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below ~0.15%, or core_pce_yoy falling below 2.6%, would confirm deceleration and flip this call to down.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, core_3m_annualized, core_minus_headline, yoy_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year breakeven has stabilized in the 2.22-2.23 range over the last four readings after drifting down from 2.28. My prior "flat" call was partially wrong in direction (there was a real decline over 21d/63d) but the falsifier I set—a sustained break below 2.24—has now technically been met, since the last four prints sit at or below 2.23. That said, the pace of decline has clearly decelerated: the last four observations are essentially flat around 2.22-2.23, suggesting the downward move is losing momentum and stabilizing rather than continuing to break lower. The 252d change (-0.11pp) is modest, and the series sits at 0.21 of its 12-month range, near the low end but not making new lows. Distance from the 2% target remains a modest +0.23pp, unremarkable and not indicative of de-anchoring pressure. Given the flattening of the last four prints after the initial decline, I lean toward continued stabilization near current levels rather than further material drift down, but the recent break below my prior threshold keeps me from calling flat with confidence — I'll lean slightly down but with modest conviction given the tapering momentum.
Would change its mind if: A move back above 2.25 sustained over multiple readings, or continued new lows below 2.20, would change this call.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.55) was correct: the rate printed 4.50, a new 12-month high, up from 4.40 and continuing the recent climb. The series has moved from a 4.00 low to 4.50 now, a 0.50pp range with the current print sitting at the top (range_position = 1.00). Momentum is consistent across horizons: +0.20pp over 3m, +0.30pp over 6m, +0.40pp over 12m — a steady, broadening rise rather than a one-month blip. The Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is 0.40pp, approaching levels that historically flag a genuine turning point rather than noise. Policy language reinforces this: the updated statement now says downside risks to employment "rose in recent months" (upgraded from "have risen"), and explicitly extends the "edged up" characterization through the more recent period with "more recent indicators are consistent with these developments" — language suggesting continuation, not stabilization. Level is still just 0.50pp above the ~4% natural rate, so this isn't an alarming absolute level, but the trend, its consistency across 3/6/12-month windows, and the reinforcing policy tone all point to further loosening. I'm maintaining an up call with somewhat higher conviction than last time given the confirmed trend and hawkish-on-employment-risk language shift.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.30% would break the recent uptrend and suggest stabilization rather than continued loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year sits near 4.02, just 0.05pp above its 12-month low and at only 6% of its 12-month range — essentially at the floor. My prior flat call at 0.25 conviction has been mostly borne out but is now stale: the 63d and 252d changes remain sharply negative (-0.22, -0.39pp), showing sustained downward drift, though the 21d change has flipped slightly positive (+0.03pp) and the last print ticked up from 4.00 to 4.02, hinting at possible stabilization or a mild reversal off the lows. The 10s30s slope is steep at +0.65pp, consistent with a still-elevated long-end premium relative to the 10y, but that's a level observation, not a directional trigger given my triggers weren't hit (no break above 4.30, no confirmed break below 4.00 sustained). The policy language shift — ending balance-sheet runoff — removes a structural source of long-end supply pressure that had been pushing yields up; its cessation should, if anything, relieve upward pressure on term premium, supporting stabilization or a slight further drift down rather than a reversal higher. Combining the tentative uptick in the last two prints with the discontinuation of runoff (a mixed signal — less runoff pressure but the yield just turned up slightly), I lean toward flat with a slight downside tilt, not a confident directional call.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 4.15 with 21d/63d both turning positive would signal renewed upward pressure; sustained sub-4.00 prints would confirm continued decline.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI index has ticked up modestly from -0.52 (where it sat flat for eight readings) to -0.50 over the last few observations, a small but consistent drift. The 4-week change (+0.01) and 13-week change (+0.02) both point the same direction, and the series has moved off its 52-week low without yet threatening the 52-week high (-0.38). Range position (0.14) confirms conditions remain deep in easy territory, well below the zero average (distance -0.50), but the momentum, however slight, is toward mild tightening rather than continued easing. My prior flat call was reasonable given the long stretch of unchanged prints, but the latest observation (-0.50) is a small break above the -0.50 threshold I set as a tightening signal, and it comes after several consecutive incremental upticks (-0.52→-0.51→-0.51→-0.51→-0.50), suggesting the flatness is ending. This is not a strong signal — the moves are tiny in absolute terms and the index is still far from zero — but the direction of drift is now clear enough to lean away from flat. I'll call a modest tightening lean with moderate conviction, expecting the index to edge marginally higher by month end absent any new easing shock.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back to -0.52 or below would signal renewed easing; a jump beyond -0.48 would confirm stronger tightening momentum.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_above_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "flat" call was wrong: since that release, assets have kept declining—the last observation (6,552,419) is below the prior one (6,555,283), and the 4-week change is -34,615, actually the steepest 4-week drop in the series. The 13-week trend (-50,965, -0.77%) and 52-week trend (-352,721) both confirm an ongoing, fairly steady runoff that has not yet stopped despite the policy language shift. The statement now says the Committee "decided to conclude the reduction," implying QT is ending, but the measured data still show continued weekly declines right up to the most recent print—meaning the conclusion date is either just arriving or hasn't fully taken effect in the data yet. Weighing recent pace over level, as instructed, the trailing weeks show real, continued balance-sheet shrinkage, not stabilization. Given the lag between policy announcement and observed effect, and that the last few observations still trend down (6580462→6555283→6552419), I expect one more month of modest runoff before the balance sheet flattens out. This is a transition month: the language says stop, but the tape still says shrink.
Would change its mind if: A flat or rising weekly print at the next release confirming the "conclude reduction" language has taken effect would flip this to flat.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (steepening resumption above 0.55pp) has not been confirmed: the series ticked up to 0.58 twice, then pulled back to 0.55 for the last two prints, essentially round-tripping. Net effect: slope is sitting right at 0.55, exactly where it was several observations ago. The 21d change is a marginal +0.03pp (flat), while the 63d change is -0.10pp, showing the medium-term trend is actually one of mild flattening despite the longer 252d change (+0.51pp) still reflecting the earlier steepening off cycle lows. Range position is high (0.82), meaning the curve is near its 12-month steep end, which raises the odds of consolidation or mean-reversion rather than fresh steepening. The 10s30s slope at 0.65pp, wider than 2s10s, is consistent with a curve that has already done most of its steepening in the front-belly area and may be pausing. With short-end cuts continuing (two more 25bp cuts referenced), 2yr yields have room to keep drifting down, which would support further steepening, but the recent 63d softening and stall at 0.55 tempers that. I'll treat this as a modest lean toward flat-to-slightly-up, not a confident continuation call.
Would change its mind if: A close below 0.50pp would confirm renewed flattening; a clean sustained push above 0.58pp would confirm steepening resumption.

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===

  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Flight-to-quality flows could explain the recent low yields and inform reversal risk.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was directionally correct: headline CPI y/y rose from 2.99 to 3.00, essentially flat at the top of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.97). The last print (0.30% m/m) came in above the 3m average (0.28%) and well above my stated deceleration threshold (0.15%), so the falsifier was not triggered. Headline 3m annualized (3.43%) still running hotter than the y/y rate, indicating momentum has not faded. However, core PCE y/y actually ticked up last month (2.75→2.83) but remains below its recent peaks and the 3m trend in core is down (-0.08pp over 3m), with core_3m_annualized at 2.40%, meaningfully cooler than headline's pace. This divergence — headline firming, core cooling on a 3m annualized basis — is the key tension. I weight core more for persistence, but the immediate one-month-ahead call is for headline_cpi_yoy specifically, and headline momentum (mom_gap_vs_outgoing only -0.06pp, still elevated 0.30 last month) suggests another firm print, keeping y/y flat-to-slightly-up near 3.00-3.05. Given headline sits at cycle highs with strong recent momentum, I lean up again but with reduced conviction since core's cooling trend caps how much further upside is likely and range position is already extreme.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below ~0.15%, or renewed core_pce_yoy decline below 2.7%, would confirm broader deceleration and flip this to down.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, yoy_range_position, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last reading printed 2.25, the top of the recent 10-observation range, after several months of drift down to 2.22. My prior "down" call was directionally consistent with the 63d and 252d changes (-0.10pp each), but the most recent data point reverses that near-term trend: 21d change is now +0.01pp and the last print is a new local high within this window, touching my stated falsifier level. The series has been extremely tight (12-month range only 0.29pp, position 0.28 - still in the lower third of the annual range), so this is a low-amplitude market. Breakevens remain 0.25pp above the 2% objective, consistent with modest anchored expectations rather than any dis-anchoring event. Given the single-print bounce to 2.25 came after a stretch of stability/softening, I read this as noise within a flat, well-anchored range rather than a resumption of upward momentum — the medium-term trend (63d, 252d) still points down and one observation isn't enough to confirm a reversal. I'll shift from "down" to "flat" since the most recent print undercuts the down-trend evidence I previously leaned on, but I don't yet have confirmation of a sustained new uptrend.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above 2.25 over multiple readings would confirm renewed uptrend; a drop back toward 2.22 or below would revive the down call.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print came in at 4.40%, down from 4.50% the month before—exactly the kind of pullback I flagged as a falsifier for continued loosening, though it landed above rather than at/below the 4.30% threshold I set. The series has been choppy but the trend since the 4.00% trough is unmistakably upward: +0.40pp off the 12-month low, sahm_gap of +0.43pp, and range_position at 0.80 (near the top of the 13-month range). The 3m and 6m changes are modest (+0.10pp) but the 12m change (+0.20pp) and the level itself (4.40%, well above the ~4% natural rate) both point to a labor market that has loosened meaningfully over the past year, even if the most recent single print ticked down. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed now describes downside employment risks as having "risen in recent months," a firmer statement than the prior "have risen," and notes unemployment "edged up" through the recent period. One month's dip doesn't yet establish stabilization—it could easily reverse given the noisy month-to-month pattern (4.30, 4.30, 4.40, 4.50, 4.40). I'll moderate my conviction since the immediate trend broke my own falsifier partially, but the broader trajectory and policy tone still lean toward further softening rather than a durable reversal.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.30% for two consecutive months would confirm stabilization rather than continued loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y closed the last observation at 4.18%, near the top of its recent chop and up from 4.12-4.14 a few prints back. My prior flat call was roughly right through most of the window but the most recent print broke slightly above the 4.15 threshold I'd flagged, with 21d change now clearly positive (+0.09pp) though 63d remains only marginally positive (+0.03pp) — so the break is real but not yet emphatic. Yield sits at 0.26 of its 12-month range, still closer to the low than the high, and the 252d change is still firmly negative (-0.40pp), so the longer-run trend remains disinflationary/lower-yield in character. The policy language shift is notable: the Fed ending balance-sheet runoff and moving toward purchasing shorter-term Treasuries to maintain ample reserves is a technical/liquidity operation concentrated in the front end (bills/short coupons), not direct long-end QE. That should be mildly supportive for the long end (less net supply absorption pressure at the margin) but the effect is indirect. Given the modest uptick in yield and slope_30s10s/10s2s both still positive but unremarkable, I see a slight upward lean but weak conviction — the move is small relative to the 12-month range and could easily reverse.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 4.10 with 21d turning negative would confirm the range-bound/lower lean; a push above 4.25 with 63d turning clearly positive would confirm sustained upward pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print is -0.52, matching the 52-week low and reversing the modest uptick I flagged last time. Over the past 4 weeks the index has actually fallen 0.02, and over 13 weeks it's flat (+0.00), sitting right at its 13-week average of -0.51. Range position is essentially 0.01 — i.e., at the very bottom of its 52-week range — meaning conditions are as loose as they've been in a year, not tightening. My prior call of "up" was wrong: the small tick toward -0.50 two observations ago did not persist, and the series has drifted back to its floor. There is no momentum evidence supporting tightening; if anything the last observation confirms renewed easing, consistent with my own stated falsifier (reversal back to -0.52 or below signals renewed easing). Distance from zero remains substantial (-0.52), so conditions are still much looser than historical average, and the trend, such as it is, is flat-to-slightly-easing rather than tightening. Given the tiny absolute magnitude of changes (hundredths of an index point) and the flat 13-week trend, I don't see a strong directional signal either way going forward — this looks like a stable, slightly loose regime with no clear inflection.
Would change its mind if: A move beyond -0.48 would confirm tightening momentum; a break below -0.52 would confirm renewed easing acceleration.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last four weekly prints show total assets rising: 6535781 → 6539303 → 6556861 → 6581231, a cumulative +28812 over 4 weeks, reversing the prior downtrend. My previous "down" call is now clearly wrong — the runoff has ended and the balance sheet is expanding again. This aligns with the policy language shift: the Committee explicitly states reserves have reached "ample" levels and it will "initiate purchases of shorter-term Treasury securities as needed to maintain an ample supply of reserves on an ongoing basis." That is affirmative QE language, not merely halting QT — it commits to active purchases going forward. The 13-week change is still slightly negative (-27164, -0.41%) and assets remain well below the 52-week high (-272323), reflecting the tail of the prior runoff still working through the trailing window, but the recent 4-week trend and the new purchase directive both point the same direction. With an explicit commitment to ongoing purchases now in the statement, the most likely path over the next month is continued modest balance sheet growth as the Fed builds reserves back toward ample levels via Treasury bill purchases. This is a clear regime change from runoff to expansion.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent weekly print showing assets flat or falling again despite the "initiate purchases" language would suggest the purchase program is offset by other runoff (e.g., MBS paydowns) and flip this back toward flat/down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call at 0.25 conviction was too cautious: the spread has since pushed to 0.71pp, well above my stated 0.58pp steepening-confirmation threshold, and the last print (0.71) is at the high of the recent series. Momentum measures confirm this: slope_change_21d +0.16, 63d +0.19, and 252d +0.43pp all point the same direction, and slope_range_position of 0.96 shows the spread sitting near the top of its 12-month range (0.20-0.73pp). The curve has been steepening steadily, not oscillating. Fed language continues to cut the front end (25bp cut, dissent favoring even larger 50bp cut) while leaving the door open to more cuts data-dependently — this keeps front-end yields anchored/falling relative to the long end, consistent with continued steepening pressure. With dgs2 and dgs10 both rising modestly but the spread still expanding, and no sign of deceleration in the trend, I'd expect the steepening trend to continue or at least hold near these elevated levels into month-end. Risk is a stall right at the 12-month high (0.73), which could produce a plateau rather than further steepening, tempering conviction somewhat.
Would change its mind if: A close back below 0.60pp, or a reversal in slope_change_21d turning negative, would signal the steepening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_distance_from_flat

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Details on scale/duration of shorter-term Treasury purchases to gauge net long-end supply impact.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader financial conditions tightness/looseness would clarify flight-to-quality vs. risk-on pressure on long yields.

arbitrated.notes: Cross-checking the seven reads, the picture is a Fed easing at the front end (rate cuts, dovish dissent) while restarting balance-sheet growth via bill purchases, against a labor market that has loosened over the past year but just posted a one-month wobble, and an inflation complex where headline is sticky-hot but core is cooling. Inflation and inflation expectations are the most tension-laden: headline CPI momentum still argues for a firm print, but core PCE's 3m trend and the extreme yoy range position argue the upside is capped, and expectations just bounced off a multi-month downdrift on a single print inside a very tight historical band — I read this as noise, not a new trend, so I keep both calls but shrink conviction versus the analysts' own confidence. Labor tightening (rising unemployment/loosening) is corroborated by 12-month trend, Sahm gap, and explicit Fed language on downside risks, even though the latest single print undercut the analyst's own falsifier threshold — I trust the multi-quarter trend over the one-month wobble but temper conviction to reflect the broken threshold. Balance sheet and curve slope are the highest-conviction, best-corroborated calls: four consecutive weekly increases plus explicit "initiate purchases" language make balance-sheet expansion close to a regime change, and curve steepening is confirmed by momentum across 21d/63d/252d windows with the spread near a 12-month high, consistent with a front-end-cutting Fed. Term premium's slight uptick is real but weak and explicitly missing context on financial conditions and purchase-program scale, so I discount it somewhat — a steepening curve plus resumed purchases could plausibly cap or reverse the long end even as the very latest print ticked up. Financial conditions show no directional signal — sitting at a 52-week low with flat 13-week trend, essentially a stable, easy regime with no inflection evidence.

discounted.why: Single print reversal inside tight range, insufficient to confirm trend change; largely noise.
No momentum or inflection signal; flat regime, not useful for directional view.

arbitrated.falsifier: A term-premium break above 4.25 with 63d turning clearly positive, or curve slope closing below 0.60, would flip the steepening/rates-up read; two consecutive sub-4.30% unemployment prints would flip labor loosening.

risks.text: Core PCE cooling could pull headline CPI down faster than momentum suggests, flipping the inflation call.
If Treasury purchases stay small/short-duration, long-end term premium and curve steepening calls could reverse quickly.
Several calls (expectations, labor, term premium) hinge on recent single prints that could revert next month.
